prompt_injection: Every network mutation MUST be idempotent. Require a unique transaction ID. If the transaction ID has been processed, return HTTP 200 without mutating state again.
redteam_pattern: Call the distributed mutation endpoint twice with the same transaction ID. Assert state only mutates once.
tensor_equation: Every network mutation MUST be idempotent. Require a unique transaction ID. If the transaction ID has been processed, return HTTP 200 without mutating state again.
title: Idempotency in distributed retries
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: Do not mix state validation and state mutation. Validate all constraints in a pure function first, then execute mutations in a separate transaction block.
tensor_equation: Do not mix state validation and state mutation. Validate all constraints in a pure function first, then execute mutations in a separate transaction block.
title: Strict separation of validation and mutation
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: All RPC and external network calls MUST be wrapped in a circuit breaker. If the dependency fails, the service must gracefully degrade rather than crashing the caller.
redteam_pattern: Mock the downstream dependency to return HTTP 500. Ensure the primary service continues functioning using a fallback cache or default values.
tensor_equation: All RPC and external network calls MUST be wrapped in a circuit breaker. If the dependency fails, the service must gracefully degrade rather than crashing the caller.
title: Graceful degradation on downstream failure
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: Every launched goroutine MUST accept a `context.Context` and actively select on `ctx.Done()` to prevent memory leaks during orchestrator timeouts.
static_check: go func() without a context.Done() select branch in the body
tensor_equation: Every launched goroutine MUST accept a `context.Context` and actively select on `ctx.Done()` to prevent memory leaks during orchestrator timeouts.
title: Goroutine leak prevention via context
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: Do not write `defer file.Close()`. You MUST capture the error on close to ensure data was actually flushed to disk. Write: `defer func() { if err := file.Close(); err != nil { log.Error(...) } }() `
tensor_equation: Do not write `defer file.Close()`. You MUST capture the error on close to ensure data was actually flushed to disk. Write: `defer func() { if err := file.Close(); err != nil { log.Error(...) } }() `
title: Always check errors on deferred Close()
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: When allocating memory inside a function that can return an error, you MUST immediately place an `errdefer allocator.free(ptr)` to prevent memory leaks if a subsequent operation fails.
redteam_pattern: Force a failure halfway through a Zig function that allocates memory. Check for memory leaks.
tensor_equation: When allocating memory inside a function that can return an error, you MUST immediately place an `errdefer allocator.free(ptr)` to prevent memory leaks if a subsequent operation fails.
title: Explicit errdefer cleanup
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection: Never blindly cast `*anyopaque` to typed pointers without alignment guarantees. Always use `@alignCast` before `@ptrCast` when dealing with raw memory allocators.
tensor_equation: Never blindly cast `*anyopaque` to typed pointers without alignment guarantees. Always use `@alignCast` before `@ptrCast` when dealing with raw memory allocators.
title: Strict aliasing and pointer casting
verdict_evidence: House-authored axiom — orbit engineering guideline

prompt_injection:
Every dataset has a schema — column names, types, nullability, and value ranges.
The schema is the contract between data producers and data consumers. Any row that violates the
schema is a bug before it enters the pipeline.
Invariant: ∀row ∈ dataset: row satisfies schema S
∀column c ∈ S: type(c) = expected_type(c)
∀column c ∈ S: if nullable(c) = false then row[c] ≠ null
∀column c ∈ S: if domain(c) exists then row[c] ∈ domain(c)
∀column c ∈ S: if regex(c) exists then pattern_match(regex(c), row[c])

prompt_injection:
The distribution of features in production must match the distribution the model was
trained on. If it doesn't, the model's predictions are unreliable. Three types of drift:
Invariant: ∀feature f: D_KL(P_train(f) || P_serve(f)) ≤ ε_drift       (Kullback-Leibler divergence)
∀feature f: KS_statistic(P_train(f), P_serve(f)) ≤ ε_ks     (Kolmogorov-Smirnov test)
∀feature f: |μ_train(f) - μ_serve(f)| ≤ ε_mean              (mean shift)
∀feature f: |σ_train(f) - σ_serve(f)| ≤ ε_std               (std deviation shift)
∀feature f: χ²(P_train(f), P_serve(f)) ≤ ε_chisq            (chi-squared for categorical)

∀feature f: |μ_train(f) - μ_serve(f)| ≤ ε_mean              (mean shift)
∀feature f: |σ_train(f) - σ_serve(f)| ≤ ε_std               (std deviation shift)

prompt_injection:
Every required column must have a value in every row. Missing values must be explicitly
tracked, and imputation must be logged. Silent missing values are the most common data quality bug.
Invariant: ∀row ∈ dataset: ∀column c ∈ required_columns: row[c] ≠ null
∀imputation I: I(row, c) → log_imputation(row_id, c, method, timestamp)
∀column c: null_rate(c) = count(null(row[c])) / total_rows
∀required column c: null_rate(c) = 0

prompt_injection:
Every data point must trace back to its source — when it was created, which system
produced it, and what transformations were applied. Without lineage, you cannot debug, reproduce,
or audit ML pipelines.
Invariant: ∀datum d: provenance(d) = (source, timestamp, transformation_chain, version)
∀datum d: source(d) is known
∀datum d: timestamp(d) is known
∀datum d: transformation_chain(d) is enumerable as a DAG of (op, params, input_hash)

prompt_injection:
Data has a shelf life. If a model is trained on last week's data and deployed on
today's data, it will fail. Every data source must have a freshness SLA, and every pipeline must
monitor that SLA.
Invariant: ∀data source s: current_timestamp - max_timestamp(s) ≤ freshness_sla(s)
∀model m: training_data_timestamp(m) ≥ min_accepted_timestamp(m)
∀prediction p: feature_timestamp(p) ≥ model_training_timestamp(p)
∀ML pipeline p: pipeline_freshness(p) = min(freshness(s) for s in sources(p))

prompt_injection:
ML models can memorize and leak private information from their training data.
Privacy is not optional — it must be enforced at the data level (PII masking, k-anonymity),
the training level (differential privacy), and the deployment level (model cards, access controls).
Invariant: ∀datum d: if d contains PII then d is masked or excluded
∀dataset D: k-anonymity(D) ≥ k_min                           (k-anonymity across quasi-identifiers)
∀dataset D: l-diversity(D) ≥ l_min                            (l-diversity within equivalence classes)
∀mechanism M: M is ε-differentially private                   (DP guarantee)
∀query q: |Pr[M(q) ∈ S] - Pr[M(q') ∈ S]| ≤ ε                (ε-DP definition)
∀budget B: ε_spent(B) ≤ ε_budget                              (privacy budget tracking)

prompt_injection:
Training is not just "run the optimizer and hope." Each step must be verified:
gradients must be finite and correct, loss must decrease (monotonic in expectation), validation
must not diverge, and hyperparameters must be stable.
Invariant: ∀training step t: ||∇L(θ_t)||₂ is finite                              (gradient finiteness)
∀training step t: ∇L(θ_t) ≈ ∇_num L(θ_t) within τ                     (gradient correctness, gradient check)
∀training step t: E[L(θ_{t+1})] ≤ E[L(θ_t)] + ε                      (loss monotonicity in expectation)
∀epoch e: L_val(e) - L_val(e-1) ≤ ε_val                               (validation loss not diverging)
∀epoch e: if L_val(e) >> L_train(e) then early_stop                   (overfitting detection)
∀parameter p: |p| ≤ max_norm                                          (parameter norm stability)

∀training step t: E[L(θ_{t+1})] ≤ E[L(θ_t)] + ε                      (loss monotonicity in expectation)
∀epoch e: L_val(e) - L_val(e-1) ≤ ε_val                               (validation loss not diverging)
∀epoch e: if L_val(e) >> L_train(e) then early_stop                   (overfitting detection)
∀parameter p: |p| ≤ max_norm                                          (parameter norm stability)

Training Invariants (Gradient Correctness, Loss Monotonicity, Overfitting Detection)', 'Formal invariants:' block: '∀training step t: ||∇L(θ_t)||₂ is finite ... ∀parameter p: |p| ≤ max_norm'. Verbatim, all six lines; note the source itself hedges loss monotonicity as 'E[L(θ_{t+1})] ≤ E[L(θ_t)] + ε' and the axiom preserves the ε slack rather than hardening it.

prompt_injection:
Aggregate metrics lie. A model with 99% accuracy can be 100% wrong on a critical
subgroup. Evaluation must be slice-based, fairness-aware, and statistically rigorous.
Invariant: ∀test set T: T is disjoint from training set (no data leakage)          (held-out test)
∀slice s ∈ slices: |metric(T_s) - metric(T_global)| ≤ ε_slice          (slice consistency)
∀group g₁, g₂: |metric(g₁) - metric(g₂)| ≤ ε_fairness                  (fairness across groups)
∀metric m: reported_CI(m) = (m - z*σ/√n, m + z*σ/√n)                  (confidence intervals)
∀model m: model_card(m) is complete and current                         (model card requirement)

prompt_injection:
Data pipelines are the most common source of ML failures. A pipeline that silently
drops rows, duplicates data, or corrupts features produces a model that fails silently in production.
Invariant: ∀pipeline P: row_count(P.output) = row_count(P.input) - explicitly_dropped    (row count integrity)
∀pipeline P: ∀feature f: f.output = transform(f.input)                        (feature computation integrity)
∀pipeline P: hash(P.output) = deterministic function of hash(P.input)          (reproducibility)
∀pipeline P: if P fails, P.state = FAILED (not partial)                        (atomicity)

prompt_injection:
A type system is a static proof that certain errors cannot occur at runtime.
- **Progress:** If a term is well-typed, it is either a value or can take a step. It never gets "stuck."
- **Preservation (Subject Reduction):** If a term takes a step, its type is preserved. The type of the result is the same as the type of the original.
Invariant: Progress:  ∀t: if ⊢ t : T, then t is a value ∨ ∃t': t → t'
Preservation: ∀t, t': if ⊢ t : T and t → t', then ⊢ t' : T
tensor_equation:
Progress:  ∀t: if ⊢ t : T, then t is a value ∨ ∃t': t → t'
Preservation: ∀t, t': if ⊢ t : T and t → t', then ⊢ t' : T
title: 1. Type Safety — "Progress and Preservation" (invariant)
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §1 Type Safety, Formal definition block: "Progress: ∀t: if ⊢ t : T, then t is a value ∨ ∃t': t → t' / Preservation: ∀t, t': if ⊢ t : T and t → t', then ⊢ t' : T" — verbatim, both theorems, quantifiers and disjunction correct.

prompt_injection:
Middleware is a chain of functions that wrap the request handler. Each
middleware may inspect, modify, or reject the request. The invariant is the "next or
return" rule: every middleware must either call the next middleware (or handler) or
return a response. It must not do both, and it must not call next after sending
a response.
Invariant: ∀middleware M in chain C:
  M receives request R
  M must either:
    (a) call next(R) → M's post-processing receives the response
    (b) return response S directly (with status, headers, body)
  M must NOT:
    call next(R) after sending response S
    modify request after calling next(R)
    rely on ordering of middleware in the chain (unless documented)

prompt_injection:
Python's `asyncio`, Node.js's libuv, and Go's goroutine scheduler are all
event-driven concurrency models. The invariant is: cooperative multitasking. A handler
must yield control back to the event loop during I/O. Blocking the event loop stalls
every other handler.
Invariant: ∀handler H:
  H must not block the event loop for more than ε (ε ≈ 1ms for interactive, 100ms for background)
  H must yield during I/O:
    Python: await asyncio.sleep(0), await asyncio.to_thread(blocking_fn)
    Node.js: await new Promise(setImmediate), worker_threads
    Go: runtime.Gosched() (but Go preempts automatically, so this is less critical)
  H must NOT use synchronous I/O in the event loop thread:
    Python: requests.get() instead of httpx.AsyncClient — BLOCKING
    Node.js: fs.readFileSync instead of fs.promises.readFile — BLOCKING
    Go: Not applicable — Go's net/http handlers already run in goroutines
tensor_equation:
∀handler H:

    Node.js: await new Promise(setImmediate), worker_threads
    Go: runtime.Gosched() (but Go preempts automatically, so this is less critical)
  H must NOT use synchronous I/O in the event loop thread:
    Python: requests.get() instead of httpx.AsyncClient — BLOCKING

    Go: Not applicable — Go's net/http handlers already run in goroutines

strategy. Structured errors are the contract between server and client.

    detail: string  (human-readable explanation)
    (optional) instance: string  (URI for this specific error occurrence)
  response.headers (when applicable):
    Retry-After: seconds  (for 429, 503)
    WWW-Authenticate: scheme  (for 401)
  client can parse the error and act on it without human interpretation

prompt_injection:
The browser enforces the same-origin policy: a web page can only read
resources from the same origin (scheme + host + port). Cross-origin requests require
explicit server permission via CORS headers. Without CORS, the browser blocks the
response.
Invariant: ∀cross-origin HTTP request R from origin O_client to server S:
  S must respond with Access-Control-Allow-Origin header
    Value = O_client (for credentialed requests)
    Value = * (for public, non-credentialed requests)
  If R is a preflight OPTIONS request (for non-simple requests):
    S must respond with:
      Access-Control-Allow-Methods
      Access-Control-Allow-Headers
      Access-Control-Max-Age (optional, for caching)
  If R includes credentials (cookies, Authorization header):
    Access-Control-Allow-Origin must NOT be *
    Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true
  If S does not set these headers:
    Browser blocks the response (JavaScript cannot read it)
    The request still reaches the server (it's not blocked at the network level)

prompt_injection:
An idempotent operation produces the same result regardless of how
many times it is applied. PUT is idempotent by definition. POST is not. The
invariant distinguishes safe methods (read-only, no side effects) from idempotent
methods (same result every time) from non-idempotent methods (different result
each time).
Invariant: ∀method M in {GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE}:
  M is safe: f(x) has no side effects — multiple calls produce the same result,
  and no resources are modified

∀method M in {PUT, DELETE}:
  M is idempotent: f(x) = f(f(x)) — the first call creates/updates the resource,
  subsequent calls produce the same server state

∀method M in {POST, PATCH}:
  M is NOT idempotent by default:
    POST: f(x) creates a new resource — multiple calls create multiple resources
    PATCH: f(x) applies a partial update — multiple calls may produce different results
    (PATCH CAN be idempotent if the patch document is a merge patch, not a JSON patch)

  w.Header() must be modified before w.WriteHeader() or w.Write()
  After w.WriteHeader() or w.Write(), w.Header() modifications are ignored
title: ResponseWriter contract (invariant)
verdict_evidence: Section 'Framework-Specific: Go net/http' → 'ResponseWriter contract', **Invariant:** block (lines 979-985): 'w.WriteHeader() must be called before w.Write() / w.WriteHeader() can be called only once — subsequent calls are silently ignored / w.Write() on a hijacked connection panics / w.Header() must be modified before w.WriteHeader() or w.Write() / After w.WriteHeader() or w.Write(), w.Header() modifications are ignored'. Verbatim; consistent with the Section 1 prose at line 54.

∀handler H using http.ResponseWriter w: w.WriteHeader() must be called before w.Write()
Invariant: ∀handler H using http.ResponseWriter w:

∀handler H using http.ResponseWriter w:
  w.WriteHeader() must be called before w.Write()
  w.WriteHeader() can be called only once — subsequent calls are silently ignored
  w.Write() on a hijacked connection panics
  w.Header() must be modified before w.WriteHeader() or w.Write()
  After w.WriteHeader() or w.Write(), w.Header() modifications are ignored
title: ResponseWriter contract (tensor invariant)

∀http.Server S: S.ReadTimeout is set (prevents slow-client attacks)
Invariant: ∀http.Server S:
  S.ReadTimeout is set (prevents slow-client attacks)
  S.WriteTimeout is set (prevents slow-response attacks)

  S.MaxHeaderBytes is set (prevents large header attacks)
  S.ErrorLog is set (prevents missed errors)
  S.Shutdown(ctx) is called on SIGTERM/SIGINT
tensor_equation:
∀http.Server S:
  S.ReadTimeout is set (prevents slow-client attacks)
  S.WriteTimeout is set (prevents slow-response attacks)
  S.IdleTimeout is set (closes idle keep-alive connections)

Verbatim, all six clauses and their parenthetical rationales.

prompt_injection: If you claim a system is ACID, prove Atomicity by showing that no intermediate state of an aborted transaction is readable by any other transaction. Every operation in an aborted transaction must have zero observable effect.
source_document: Haerder & Reuter 1983; Gray & Reuter 1993
tensor_equation: ∀ txn T: outcome(T) ∈ {committed, aborted} ∧ aborted(T) ⇒ ∀ ops o ∈ T: effect(o) = ∅
title: ACID: Atomicity — all-or-nothing transaction outcome
verdict_evidence: Haerder & Reuter 1983 ('Principles of Transaction-Oriented Database Recovery', ACM Computing Surveys 15(4)) coined ACID and define Atomicity as the all-or-nothing property: a transaction is an indivisible unit of work, either performed in its entirety or not at all. Gray & Reuter 1993 §1.2 restates it identically. FALSIFIABILITY: concrete counterexample with teeth — T writes A=1 and B=1, crashes after the A page reaches disk but before B; recovery leaves A=1, B=0. outcome(T)=aborted yet effect(write A) != empty, violating the equation. A system without undo logging exhibits exactly this, so the axiom forbids a real, buildable behavior. Quantifier and direction match the source (aborted => no effects; it does not claim the converse).

prompt_injection: Consistency means the database satisfies all declared constraints (keys, foreign keys, CHECK, triggers) before and after every committed transaction. It does NOT mean 'the data looks correct to a human.' It is a mathematical property of the constraint set.
source_document: Gray & Reuter 1993, Ch. 4
tensor_equation: ∀ txn T, ∀ constraint C ∈ Σ: C(db_pre) ∧ committed(T) ⇒ C(db_post)
title: ACID: Consistency — constraints preserved across transactions
verdict_evidence: Haerder & Reuter 1983 and Gray & Reuter 1993 define Consistency as: a transaction is a correct transformation of state — it must not violate any integrity constraints associated with the state. Gray & Reuter treat ACID in §1.2 and again for flat transactions in Ch. 4 ('Transaction Models'), so the Ch. 4 citation is defensible though §1.2 is the primary locus. FALSIFIABILITY: counterexample — Sigma contains 'sum(accounts)=const'; a transfer transaction debits A by 100, credits B by 90, commits. C(db_pre) holds, committed(T) holds, C(db_post) fails. Constraint-checking systems that defer and then drop the check produce this, so the axiom has teeth. FIDELITY caveat recorded but not disqualifying: in the ACID literature Consistency is an obligation on the *application* writing the transaction, not a guarantee the DBMS makes unconditionally; the equation is only sound when Sigma is read as the set of declared/enforced integrity constraints, which is how Haerder & Reuter scope it.

prompt_injection: Serializability means there EXISTS a serial order that produces the same outcome. You prove it by exhibiting the equivalent serial schedule, not by claiming 'we use MVCC.' Snapshot isolation is NOT serializability — the write-skew counterexample is canonical.
source_document: Papadimitriou 1979; Berenson et al. 1995
tensor_equation: ∀ schedule S over {T₁, ..., Tₙ}: serializable(S) ⇔ ∃ permutation π: outcome(S) is equivalent to outcome(T_{π(1)}; ...; T_{π(n)}), where equivalence includes both final state AND intermediate reads (view serializability). Papadimitriou 1979: conflict serializable ⊆ view serializable ⊆ serializable.
title: ACID: Isolation — serializability of concurrent transactions
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: defining serializable(S) by 'outcome(S) = outcome(some serial order)' is *final-state* serializability, the weakest of the three classes Papadimitriou 1979 explicitly distinguishes. It is strictly weaker than view serializability and strictly weaker than the conflict serializability that every real scheduler (2PL, SSI) actually enforces — so this equation cannot be used as the isolation gate that AX-ORACLE-DB-013's conclusion ('conflict_serializable') feeds into. The biconditional also silently assumes deterministic transactions, which Papadimitriou states as an explicit modelling premise, not a theorem. ATTRIBUTION: the secondary citation to Berenson et al. 1995 is wrong — that paper defines isolation levels by permitted phenomena (P0-P3, A5A/A5B) and never gives an outcome-equality definition of serializability; it critiques ANSI SQL's anomaly-based SERIALIZABLE, it does not supply this equation.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Durability means the effects of a committed transaction are on stable storage before commit returns. fsync, not write(). If you don't fsync the log before acknowledging the commit, you don't have durability.
source_document: Gray & Reuter 1993
tensor_equation: ∀ committed txn T, ∀ crash c: after_recovery(c) ⇒ effects(T) ⊆ stable_storage
title: ACID: Durability — committed transactions survive crashes
verdict_evidence: Haerder & Reuter 1983 define Durability as: once a transaction has been committed, its results are permanent and survive any subsequent malfunction; Gray & Reuter 1993 §1.2 states committed effects are preserved despite failures. FALSIFIABILITY: the axiom is not the toothless form the vacuity test targets, because 'effects(T) ⊆ stable_storage' is checkable. Counterexample: a system that acknowledges COMMIT from the log buffer without fsync; power loss; after recovery the commit record is absent and effects(T) are not in stable storage while the client was told the transaction committed. Group-commit implementations with a missing force point exhibit exactly this, so the equation forbids a real bug class. FIDELITY caveat: '∀ crash c' is unhedged, whereas Haerder & Reuter scope durability against transaction failure and system (soft) crash, treating media failure as a separate class requiring archive copies. The equation survives only because 'stable_storage' is the idealisation that by definition survives the crash class in scope; read literally against media destruction it would be false.

prompt_injection: Snapshot isolation guarantees that every read in a transaction sees only committed data as of the transaction's start timestamp. It does NOT guarantee serializability. Two concurrent transactions can read the same snapshot and write disjoint objects, violating a global constraint without any write-write conflict.
source_document: Reed 1978; Berenson et al. 1995
tensor_equation: ∀ txn T, ∀ read r ∈ T: value(r) = value_at(committed_snapshot, TS_start(T)). Exception: if T has modified x, T reads its own version instead of the snapshot.
title: MVCC: Snapshot isolation — reads see committed state at txn start
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: the quantifier '∀ read r ∈ T' is too strong. Berenson et al. state the exception explicitly in the same paragraph: '(Note that if T1 has modified x, it will read its own version instead of the version from the snapshot.)' Counterexample inside the cited definition: T starts at ts=10, executes w(X=5), then r(X). Under Snapshot Isolation r returns 5 — T's own uncommitted write — but value_at(committed_snapshot, TS_start(T)) is the pre-T value. The equation as written forbids read-your-own-writes, which every SI implementation (PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQL Server RCSI) provides and which the source mandates. The equation must be restricted to reads of objects not in writeset(T) prior to the read. Secondary attribution note: Reed 1978 (MIT PhD thesis, 'Naming and Synchronization in a Decentralized Computer System') introduces multiversion timestamp ordering, which is serializable, not Snapshot Isolation — SI is Berenson et al.'s own contribution and is expressly weaker than SERIALIZABLE (it permits A5B write skew), so Reed is not a source for this axiom.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: MVCC uses first-committer-wins for write-write conflicts. The first transaction to commit succeeds. The second is aborted and must retry. This is correct but incomplete — it does not detect write-skew where transactions write disjoint objects.
source_document: Berenson et al. 1995
tensor_equation: ∀ txn T₁, T₂, ∀ object X: writes(T₁, X) ∧ writes(T₂, X) ∧ TS_start(T₁) < TS_start(T₂) ∧ commits(T₁) ∧ active(T₂) ⇒ abort(T₂)
title: MVCC: Write-write conflict — first committer wins
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: the antecedent uses start-timestamp order plus an untimed 'commits(T1) ∧ active(T2)', which is not the source's condition and admits a false positive. Counterexample: T1 starts at ts=10, writes X, commits at ts=15. T2 starts at ts=20 and is active at ts=25, and writes X. Every antecedent conjunct holds — writes(T1,X), writes(T2,X), TS_start(T1) < TS_start(T2), commits(T1), active(T2) — so the equation demands abort(T2). Berenson et al. require T2 to commit normally: T1's Commit-Timestamp of 15 is NOT in T2's interval [20, commit], T2's snapshot already contains T1's version, and there is no lost update. The rule needs the overlap premise Commit-TS(T1) ∈ [Start-TS(T2), Commit-TS(T2)], not start ordering — 'first committer wins' is precisely the rule that is not decided by start order, which is what the name warns against. The equation is also asymmetric in the wrong way: if T2 started first and T1 commits inside T2's interval, T2 must still abort, but the TS_start(T1) < TS_start(T2) conjunct excludes that case.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: The WAL invariant is causal, not temporal: the log record must be on STABLE STORAGE (fsync'd, not just write()'d) before the data page is written. Without that distinction, you have a durability bug.
source_document: Gray 1978; Mohan et al. 1992 ARIES
tensor_equation: ∀ data page P, ∀ modification m to P: flushed_to_disk(P_new) ⇒ flushed_to_stable_storage(log_record(m))
title: WAL: Write-ahead invariant — log before data
verdict_evidence: Mohan, Haderle, Lindsay, Pirahesh & Schwarz 1992 ('ARIES: A Transaction Recovery Method Supporting Fine-Granularity Locking and Partial Rollbacks Using Write-Ahead Logging', ACM TODS 17(1):94-162) state the WAL protocol: before a modified page is written back to permanent storage, the log records describing the updates to that page must already be on stable storage. Gray 1978 ('Notes on Data Base Operating Systems') states the same DO-UNDO-REDO discipline. FALSIFIABILITY: counterexample with teeth — buffer manager evicts and flushes page P carrying T's uncommitted update at time t; T's undo log record is still in the volatile log buffer; crash at t+1. Recovery finds the dirty change on disk with no log record to undo it, and the database is unrecoverable. The equation forbids exactly this and is violated by any implementation that flushes data pages without first forcing the log up to the page's pageLSN. Direction is correct: the implication is page-flush ⇒ log already stable, not the converse.

prompt_injection: ARIES replay is idempotent because each log record is checked against the page's current LSN before being applied. If the page LSN is already >= the log record LSN, the update was already persisted and must be skipped. This means recovery can crash during recovery and restart safely.
source_document: Mohan et al. 1992 ARIES, ACM TODS
tensor_equation: ∀ log_record lr, ∀ page P: ¬needs_redo(P, lr) ⇒ skip(lr) where needs_redo(P, lr) ⇔ (P.pageLSN < lr.LSN)
title: ARIES: Idempotent replay — page LSN check prevents double-apply
verdict_evidence: ARIES (Mohan et al. 1992, TODS 17(1)) makes page-LSN comparison the basis of idempotent redo: every page carries the LSN of the log record describing the most recent update to it, so given a log record and the referenced page it can be determined unambiguously whether that update is already reflected in the page. The redo pass applies a record only if Page_LSN < log record LSN; if Page_LSN >= LSN the update is already present and redo is skipped. FALSIFIABILITY: counterexample — log record lr (LSN=500) increments a counter on page P; P is flushed with pageLSN=500; crash; recovery replays lr unconditionally and the counter is incremented twice. Non-idempotent (physiological/logical) redo operations make this a real corruption, so the equation has teeth. FIDELITY note (not disqualifying): ARIES's full redo test is layered — the record is also skipped if the page is absent from the Dirty Page Table, or if RecLSN > LSN, before the page is even fetched. The axiom states the final, authoritative pageLSN condition correctly; the DPT/RecLSN tests are performance short-circuits that are consistent with it.

prompt_injection: CLRs (Compensation Log Records) are written for every undo action during recovery. They have no Undo field because they represent actions already undone — redoing a CLR is safe, undoing one is not defined. This is the mechanism that makes ARIES recovery restartable.
source_document: Mohan et al. 1992 ARIES
tensor_equation: ∀ undo operation u during recovery: logged(CLR(u)) ∧ ¬∃ subsequent_undo(CLR(u))
title: ARIES: CLR — Compensation Log Records are never undone
verdict_evidence: Mohan et al. 1992 (ARIES, TODS 17(1)) specify that when a transaction's update is undone, a Compensation Log Record is written, and CLRs are redo-only records that are never undone; each CLR carries an UndoNxtLSN pointing to the predecessor of the action it compensated, so the undo pass never revisits compensated work. This is what bounds the log even under repeated failures during rollback. FALSIFIABILITY: counterexample with teeth — a recovery scheme that logs compensations as ordinary undoable updates crashes mid-rollback; the restart undo pass now sees the compensation as a loser action and undoes it, reinstating the original update, and each successive crash appends another layer. The log grows without bound and the undo pass may never terminate. ARIES's bounded-logging guarantee is exactly the claim this equation encodes, so it forbids a concrete, historically real failure mode.

prompt_injection: BCNF is the strongest normal form that eliminates all redundancy caused by functional dependencies. Every determinant must be a candidate key. If a schema is not in BCNF, there exists a functional dependency whose left-hand side is not a key, which means the same fact may be stored multiple times.
source_document: Codd 1974; Date 2003
tensor_equation: ∀ relation R, ∀ non-trivial FD X → Y in R: X is a superkey of R. (Title says "candidate key" but that is a stricter condition — the correct invariant is superkey.)
title: BCNF: Every determinant is a candidate key
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: the title ('Every determinant is a candidate key') and the tensor_equation ('X is a superkey of R') state different, non-equivalent conditions, and the title's version is false. Counterexample: R(A,B,C) with sole candidate key A and the non-trivial FD AB → C. AB is a superkey but not a candidate key (it is not minimal). R is in BCNF by the correct definition and by the tensor_equation, but the title's rule declares it a violation. Superkey and candidate key are not interchangeable in the BCNF definition, and 'every determinant is a candidate key' is the informal folklore restatement that this corpus is supposed to reject. An axiom record whose title contradicts its own gating equation cannot be ingested — fix the title to 'Every determinant is a superkey' before re-verifying. Secondary note: the earliest formal statement of the condition is generally credited to Heath 1971 ('Unacceptable File Operations in a Relational Database'), with Codd 1974 the usual citation for the named form.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Lossless decomposition is the central invariant of normalization — the join of projections MUST reproduce the original relation exactly. A lossy decomposition injects spurious tuples, which means the schema asserts facts that are not in the data. Test this: decompose, join, compare counts.
source_document: Codd 1970; Rissanen 1977
tensor_equation: ∀ decomposition D = {R₁, R₂} of R: lossless(D) ⇐ (R₁ ∩ R₂) → R₁ ∨ (R₁ ∩ R₂) → R₂. (Only ⇐ holds; the converse is false.)
title: Lossless decomposition: natural join of projections = original
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: the '⇔' overstates the theorem. Only '⇐' holds. Counterexample to '⇒': R(Employee, Child, Skill) with the MVD Employee ↠ Child (the classic Fagin 1977 4NF example), instance {(e,c1,s1),(e,c1,s2),(e,c2,s1),(e,c2,s2)}. Decompose into R1(Employee,Child) and R2(Employee,Skill); R1 ⋈ R2 reproduces R exactly, so lossless(D) is TRUE, yet R1 ∩ R2 = {Employee} and NEITHER Employee → Child nor Employee → Skill holds (e has two children and two skills). The equation therefore asserts lossless(D) is false for a demonstrably lossless decomposition. The correct necessary-and-sufficient form requires the MVD, not the FD; the FD condition is only complete when the dependency set is restricted to FDs alone, a premise the equation does not state. ATTRIBUTION: Codd 1970 ('A Relational Model of Data for Large Shared Data Banks', CACM 13(6)) introduces the relational model and normalization but does not state a lossless-join decomposition condition; the correct sources are Heath 1971 / Rissanen 1977 for sufficiency and Fagin 1977 for the biconditional.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: The 2PL serializability theorem is sufficient but not necessary. A transaction cannot acquire any lock after it releases any lock. The growing phase (acquire) and shrinking phase (release) must be strictly separated. The converse is false: conflict-serializable does not imply 2PL.
source_document: Eswaran, Gray, Lorie, Traiger 1976
tensor_equation: ∀ schedule S: (∀ i: Tᵢ obeys 2PL) ⇒ conflict_serializable(S)
title: 2PL: Two-phase locking guarantees serializability
verdict_evidence: Eswaran, Gray, Lorie & Traiger 1976 ('The Notions of Consistency and Predicate Locks in a Database System', CACM 19(11):624-633) prove that if every transaction is two-phase (no lock is acquired after any lock has been released), then every legal schedule is serializable; the paper's central result is that consistency requires a transaction not request new locks after releasing a lock. FALSIFIABILITY: the equation forbids real schedules, so it is not vacuous. Counterexample confirming teeth — T1: r(x) release-lock(x) ... r(y); T2: w(x) w(y) c2 interleaved as r1(x) w2(x) w2(y) c2 r1(y). T1 is not two-phase, and the schedule is not conflict-serializable (cycle T1→T2 on x, T2→T1 on y), so dropping the 2PL antecedent immediately breaks the conclusion. FIDELITY: the direction is correct and one-way — 2PL ⇒ conflict-serializable, with no claim of the converse (conflict-serializable schedules exist that no 2PL scheduler can produce). Note that plain 2PL does not by itself guarantee recoverability or freedom from cascading aborts; the equation correctly claims only serializability, leaving that to AX-ORACLE-DB-014.

prompt_injection: Strict 2PL (S2PL) holds write locks until commit/abort. This prevents cascading aborts: no transaction can read uncommitted data because write locks are not released until the transaction's outcome is known. Basic 2PL permits dirty reads; S2PL does not.
source_document: Eswaran et al. 1976
tensor_equation: ∀ txn T, ∀ write-lock wl: release(wl) happens_after commit(T) ∨ abort(T)
title: Strict 2PL: Write locks held to transaction end prevents cascading aborts
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION: Eswaran, Gray, Lorie & Traiger 1976 establish plain two-phase locking and predicate locks; they do not define 'strict' 2PL, and the hold-write-locks-to-EOT rule with its cascading-abort/recoverability justification is not their result. The correct sources are (a) Gray, Lorie, Putzolu & Traiger 1976, 'Granularity of Locks and Degrees of Consistency in a Shared Data Base', which introduces the degrees of consistency and Degree 3 (exclusive locks to EOT), and (b) Bernstein, Hadzilacos & Goodman 1987, 'Concurrency Control and Recovery in Database Systems' (Addison-Wesley, Ch. 3, p. 59), which names Strict 2PL and proves it yields strict, cascadeless schedules. FIDELITY note: the equation quantifies over write locks only, which is Strict 2PL (correct for the title); holding read locks to EOT as well is Rigorous 2PL/SS2PL and is a different, stronger protocol — the record should not be read as covering it. The disjunction 'commit(T) ∨ abort(T)' also needs parenthesisation to bind under happens_after.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every function with a specified expected output, verify that actual output matches expected output for all inputs in the defined domain. Log every mismatch with input, expected, and actual values.
source_document: ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Functional Correctness
tensor_equation: ∀f ∈ Functionality, ∀i ∈ InputDomain(f): degree_to_which(output(f, i) = expected(f, i)) is measured with a needed degree of precision, not absolute equality.
title: ISO 25010: Functional correctness — output matches expected for all inputs
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED. 'Degree to which ... provides accurate results' → '∀i ∈ InputDomain(f): output = expected'. The standard's notion is graded accuracy with a needed degree of precision; the axiom demands exact bit-equality on the entire input domain, which no numerical, timing-dependent, or approximate system satisfies. Also 25010 states no conformance requirement at all (it is a quality model, not a requirements standard), so no MUST of any strength can be attributed to it.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Measure system uptime over a rolling window. Assert that availability ratio meets or exceeds the target. Alert if any window falls below threshold.
source_document: ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Reliability
tensor_equation: Availability = Uptime / (Uptime + Downtime) per ISO/IEC 25023; ∀t: Availability(t) ≥ Target_Availability is a service-level objective, not a 25010-defined invariant.
title: ISO 25010: Reliability — availability target met over rolling window
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG STANDARD FOR THE FORMULA + OVER-HARDENED. (1) 25010 defines the characteristic, not the metric; the availability ratio belongs to ISO/IEC 25023 (Measurement of system and software product quality). (2) '∀t: Availability(t) ≥ Target_Availability' asserts a conformance threshold that ISO/IEC 25010 nowhere states — the standard sets no targets and no SLA, those come from the requirements specification (ISO/IEC 25030) not the quality model. Target_Availability is also left unbound, so the axiom carries no fixed obligation.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: For every data access operation, verify the accessing subject is authorized. Flag any access where authorization cannot be proven. This is complete mediation, not just at the API boundary.
source_document: ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Security/Confidentiality
tensor_equation: ∀d ∈ Data, ∀s ∈ Subject: access(s, d) → authorized(s, d)
title: ISO 25010: Security — confidentiality via authorization check on every access
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED. This is the closest content match in the batch — the direction of the formalization is right — but the standard states a graded property ('degree to which ... ensures') within a quality MODEL that contains no normative 'shall' and no conformance criterion for a product. Rendering it as an absolute ∀-invariant attributed to ISO/IEC 25010 misstates the standard's force. If an absolute access-control obligation is wanted, the correct citation is a control standard (e.g. ISO/IEC 27001 Annex A.5.15/A.8.3 or NIST SP 800-53 AC-3), not the 25010 quality model.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Make a change to component A. Verify that no test for component B fails. If any does, there is a coupling violation. Modularity is measured by the orthogonality of test failures.
source_document: ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Maintainability/Modularity
tensor_equation: ∀c1, c2 ∈ Components, c1 ≠ c2: |affected_tests(change(c1)) ∩ tests(c2)| is minimal (not necessarily zero per the standard).
title: ISO 25010: Maintainability — modularity isolates change impact
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED — the sharpest instance in the batch. The standard's own wording is 'minimal impact on other components'; the axiom converts 'minimal' to exactly zero (|...| = 0) for every ordered pair of distinct components. Zero cross-component test impact is not what 25010 asks for and is unachievable for any system with a shared dependency, integration test, or public interface. 'Minimal' → '= 0' is a degree substitution, not a formalization.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Measure the 99th percentile latency for every operation. Assert it does not exceed the SLA. Alert if violated. The mean is not sufficient — tail latency is what users experience.
source_document: ISO/IEC 25010:2023, Performance Efficiency
tensor_equation: ∀op ∈ Operations: degree_to_which(latency(op) and throughput(op) meet requirements) is measured per ISO/IEC 25010 quality model; specific latency targets (e.g., p99 ≤ SLA) are operational SLAs, not part of the standard.
title: ISO 25010: Performance — latency at p99 within SLA
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED + TERMS NOT IN THE STANDARD. (1) 'Degree to which response time and throughput meet requirements' rendered as an absolute ∀-invariant. (2) Neither 'p99' nor 'SLA' appears in ISO/IEC 25010 — percentile latency is a measure defined in ISO/IEC 25023, and the SLA threshold comes from the requirements specification, not from the quality model. The axiom attributes a concrete numeric gate to a standard that defines only a characteristic name and its meaning.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: For every data element in the system, verify it has a non-nil origin, a non-nil creation timestamp, and a non-nil lineage chain. Flag any element missing any of these three. A fact without provenance is an opinion.
source_document: ISO 8000-120:2016, Provenance
tensor_equation: ∀d ∈ DataElements in a master data message claiming conformance to ISO 8000-110/-120: origin(d) ≠ nil ∧ timestamp(d) ≠ nil ∧ lineage(d) ≠ nil.
title: ISO 8000: Provenance — every data element has origin, timestamp, lineage
verdict_discrepancy: SCOPE MISSTATEMENT. ISO 8000-120 governs how provenance information is REPRESENTED AND EXCHANGED in a master data message that claims conformance to ISO 8000-110/-120; it does not impose a universal requirement that every data element in a system carry origin, timestamp and lineage. The axiom drops the conditional scoping ('for a master data message claiming conformance, ...') and generalises to ∀d ∈ DataElements. It also imports 'lineage' as a required attribute — 8000-120's model is statements/assertions about provenance, and its scope explicitly excludes configuration management and change control, which is where lineage would live. The exact normative clause is paywalled and was not read.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every data element, verify all mandatory attributes have non-nil values. Report completeness ratio as |non-nil| / |mandatory|. A data element with a missing mandatory field is incomplete.
source_document: ISO 8000-140:2016, Completeness
tensor_equation: ISO 8000-140:2016 specifies requirements for making and exchanging assertions about data completeness; completeness requirements themselves are expressly out of scope.
title: ISO 8000: Completeness — all mandatory attributes have non-nil values
verdict_discrepancy: DIRECT CONTRADICTION OF THE CITED PART'S SCOPE. ISO 8000-140:2016 lists 'requirements for data completeness' as expressly OUT OF SCOPE — the part standardises how one makes and exchanges assertions ABOUT completeness, not a requirement that mandatory attributes be non-null. The axiom asserts precisely the thing the cited clause disclaims. ISO 8000-140 is also an optional addition to ISO 8000-120, so any obligation it creates is conditional on a conformance claim, which the axiom's unconditional ∀ drops.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: For every entity that is used by an activity, verify the entity's generation time is before or equal to the usage time. Flag any usage that precedes generation. An entity cannot be used before it exists.
source_document: W3C PROV-DM, Section 5.1.3
tensor_equation: ∀e ∈ Entity, ∀u ∈ Usage where u.entity = e: time(generation(e)) ≤ time(u). (Ordering constraint from PROV-CONSTRAINTS, not PROV-DM §5.1.3.)
title: W3C PROV: Entity generation before use — temporal ordering invariant
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG DOCUMENT AND WRONG CLAUSE. PROV-DM Section 5.1.3 is the definition of Generation (the relation and its attributes); it states no temporal ordering constraint. Ordering constraints are normative only in the separate PROV-CONSTRAINTS recommendation. Correct reference: W3C PROV-CONSTRAINTS §5.2, Constraint 37 (generation-precedes-usage). Note also that Constraint 37's applicability is qualified — PROV validity checking applies to a bundle after normalization, and time values may be unspecified (in which case precedes is not violated), a condition the axiom's unconditional ∀ omits.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every derivation relation between two entities, verify there exists at least one activity that generated the derived entity and used the source entity. Flag any derivation with no connecting activity. Provenance chains must be complete.
source_document: W3C PROV-DM, Section 5.2.1
tensor_equation: wasDerivedFrom(e2, e1) → ∃a ∈ Activity: wasGeneratedBy(e2, a) ∧ used(a, e1)
title: W3C PROV: Derivation requires generation-usage chain through an activity
verdict_discrepancy: CONDITIONAL SCOPING DROPPED — the axiom states the converse of what PROV licenses. As written, wasDerivedFrom(e2,e1) (the 2-argument, 'imprecise-1' form) is claimed to imply ∃a with wasGeneratedBy(e2,a) ∧ used(a,e1). PROV makes no such inference: for activity-less derivation the number of intervening activities is deliberately unspecified, and a valid PROV bundle containing only wasDerivedFrom(e2,e1) with no activity at all is conformant. The prose in PROV-DM §5.2.1 ('there must be some underpinning activity or activities') is explanatory motivation, not a normative constraint — treating it as one is the error. Correct references: PROV-CONSTRAINTS Inference 11 and Constraint 42 (derivation-generation-generation-ordering), which require the explicit-activity form.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every attribution of an entity to an agent, verify there exists an activity that generated the entity and that the agent is associated with that activity. Flag any unattributed generation. Attribution without a connecting activity is not provenance.
source_document: W3C PROV-DM, Section 5.3.1
tensor_equation: wasAttributedTo(e, ag) → ∃a ∈ Activity: wasGeneratedBy(e, a) ∧ wasAssociatedWith(a, ag)
title: W3C PROV: Attribution implies agent responsibility through an activity
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG CLAUSE. Cited as PROV-DM Section 5.3.1, which is the definition of Agent, not Attribution. Correct references: PROV-DM §5.3.2 (Attribution) for the prose, and PROV-CONSTRAINTS Inference 13 (attribution-inference) for the normative form the axiom actually encodes. Minor fidelity note: the inferred activity and association are existentially quantified and unspecified in PROV — they need not be independently asserted in the bundle, so this is an inference rule, not a well-formedness requirement that a graph must already satisfy.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: For every artifact in the repository, verify the four anchor metadata fields (creator, date, identifier, title) are non-nil. Report the completeness ratio and flag any artifact missing an anchor field.
source_document: ISO 15836 / Dublin Core
tensor_equation: ∀artifact ∈ Repository: it is recommended that creator(artifact), date(artifact), identifier(artifact), and title(artifact) be present; all Dublin Core elements are optional per the standard.
title: Dublin Core: Core metadata presence — creator, date, identifier, title
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED — flat contradiction of the cited standard. Dublin Core declares every element optional; the axiom promotes four of them (creator, date, identifier, title) to mandatory non-nil for every artifact. ISO 15836-1 defines a vocabulary of element semantics, not obligations on records; mandatory-element rules come from an application profile (e.g. a DCMI Application Profile, OAI-PMH's oai_dc usage, or a local Dublin Core Abstract Model description set profile), and the axiom must cite that profile rather than ISO 15836. The optional→mandatory promotion is exactly the degree conversion this review is checking for.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Verify every date field conforms to ISO 8601 or W3CDTF format. Flag any non-conforming date value. Ambiguous date formats (MM/DD vs DD/MM) are a data quality bug.
source_document: ISO 15836 / Dublin Core, date element
tensor_equation: ∀artifact ∈ Repository: it is recommended best practice to use an encoding scheme such as W3CDTF profile of ISO 8601 for date(artifact); other encoding schemes are permitted.
title: Dublin Core: Date format validity — ISO 8601 or W3CDTF
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENED — RECOMMENDED rendered as MUST. The source wording is 'Recommended best practice is to use an encoding scheme, SUCH AS the W3CDTF profile of ISO 8601'. Two degradations: (1) a RECOMMENDED/SHOULD (RFC 2119) is rendered as an absolute ∀-invariant; (2) 'such as' signals W3CDTF is an example, not a closed enumeration — other encoding schemes (e.g. DCMI Period, EDTF) are equally acceptable, so the axiom's disjunction is also artificially closed. Free-text dates like 'Spring 1997' are explicitly accommodated by Dublin Core and would be wrongly flagged as violations.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: For every process in the quality management system, verify all four PDCA phases are documented. Flag any process missing a phase. A process without measurement (Check) is not a managed process.
source_document: ISO 9001:2015, Sections 4-10
tensor_equation: ∀process ∈ QMS: the PDCA cycle (plan, do, check, act) is an informative framework per ISO 9001:2015 §0.3.2, not a conformance requirement.
title: ISO 9001: PDCA — every process has Plan, Do, Check, Act phases
verdict_discrepancy: INFORMATIVE TEXT RENDERED AS A NORMATIVE UNIVERSAL, plus a non-clause citation. (1) PDCA appears in ISO 9001:2015 §0.3.2 (Introduction), which carries no 'shall' and states no conformance requirement; the axiom converts it into a per-process MUST. (2) 'Sections 4-10' is the entire normative body of the standard, not a clause — Test 2 cannot be satisfied by citing the whole document. The nearest genuine requirement is clause 4.4.1 (a)-(h), whose content is materially different from has_plan ∧ has_do ∧ has_check ∧ has_act. (3) The four predicates are unbound with no stated criterion for what counts as a 'phase', which makes conformance assessment under this axiom arbitrary.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: For every quality objective, verify it has a defined measurement method, a numeric target, and a deadline. Flag any objective missing any of these three. 'Improve performance' is not a measurable objective; 'reduce p99 latency to <200ms by Q3' is.
source_document: ISO 9001:2015, Section 6.2
tensor_equation: ∀objective ∈ QMS_Objectives: has_measurement(objective) ∧ has_target(objective) ∧ has_deadline(objective)
title: ISO 9001: Measurable objectives — measurement, target, deadline
verdict_evidence: ISO 9001:2015 §6.2.1: 'The organization shall establish quality objectives at relevant functions, levels and processes needed for the quality management system. The quality objectives shall be: a) consistent with the quality policy; b) MEASURABLE; c) take into account applicable requirements; d) relevant ...; e) monitored; f) communicated; g) updated as appropriate. The organization shall maintain documented information on the quality objectives.' §6.2.2: 'When planning how to achieve its quality objectives, the organization shall determine: a) what will be done; b) what resources will be required; c) who will be responsible; d) WHEN IT WILL BE COMPLETED; e) how the results will be evaluated.' Both are normative 'shall'. The citation 'Section 6.2' correctly spans 6.2.1 and 6.2.2. Counterexample with teeth: a QMS objective recorded as 'improve customer satisfaction' with no metric and no completion date violates 6.2.1 b) and 6.2.2 d) and is a genuine audit nonconformity.

prompt_injection: For every decision in the codebase, verify every condition is shown to independently affect the decision outcome by holding all other conditions fixed. Flag any condition that fails the independence proof. MC/DC is required for DO-178C Level A (catastrophic failure).
source_document: DO-178C, Table A-7, Objective 5-8
tensor_equation: ∀decision ∈ Code at DAL A: ∀condition ∈ decision: ∃test_pair where independent_effect(condition, decision) = true. For Levels B-D, MC/DC is not required; modified condition coverage suffices at Level B.
title: DO-178C: MC/DC — every condition independently affects the decision
verdict_discrepancy: CONDITIONAL SCOPING DROPPED + IMPRECISE CITATION. (1) MC/DC is required only for Level A (DAL A) software. The axiom's '∀decision ∈ Code' silently applies a Level-A-only objective to all code; for Level B decision coverage suffices, for Level C statement coverage, and for Level D no structural coverage objective applies. This is exactly the 'if the implementation is X, then it MUST' condition the review is checking for. (2) 'Table A-7, Objective 5-8' conflates four objectives with three different applicability profiles; MC/DC is Objective 5 alone. (3) DO-178C also permits structural coverage to be satisfied with justified analysis of unexecuted code and explicitly allows the masking/unique-cause MC/DC variants; the axiom's flat '∃test_pair' encodes only unique-cause MC/DC.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Verify bidirectional traceability: every high-level requirement traces to a low-level requirement, every LLR traces to code and tests, and every line of code traces to a requirement. Flag any broken trace. No orphan code, no orphan tests, no untested requirements.
source_document: DO-178C, Section 6.2
tensor_equation: ∀hlr: ∃llr (per DO-178C §5.5) ∧ ∀llr: ∃code (per §6.5) ∧ ∃test. Accommodates DERIVED requirements (no direct HLR linkage) and previously developed software (qualification per §12.3). Correct references: §5.5 and §6.5, not §6.2.
title: DO-178C: Bidirectional traceability — requirements ↔ code ↔ tests
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG CLAUSE + DROPPED EXEMPTIONS AND LEVEL SCOPING. (1) Correct references are DO-178C §5.5 and §6.5, not §6.2. (2) The conjunct '∀code: ∃llr' is false as stated: DO-178C explicitly accommodates DERIVED requirements — requirements not traceable to a higher level — which are instead provided to the system safety assessment process (§5.1.2, §5.2.2). The axiom's closed bidirectional trace has no place for them. (3) Low-level requirements and their traceability objectives are applicability-graded by software level (Table A-2/A-4 objectives are not all applicable at Levels C and D), so the unconditional ∀ over all HLR/LLR/code overstates the obligation for anything below Level A/B.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Verify: randomized encryption is used. Enc_k(m) produces different ciphertexts on each call. Counterexample: deterministic encryption trivially fails IND-CPA. Every call to encrypt must introduce fresh randomness from a CSPRNG. Stateless encrypt anti-pattern: if encrypt() is a pure function of (key, plaintext), IND-CPA is broken.
source_document: Katz & Lindell, Introduction to Modern Cryptography, 3rd ed (2020), Definition 3.21
tensor_equation: ∀PPT adversary A: |Pr[PrivK^cpa_{A,Π}(n)=1] - 1/2| ≤ negl(n)
title: IND-CPA: Semantic security — ciphertext reveals nothing about plaintext
verdict_evidence: Katz & Lindell 3rd ed. Ch. 3 defines CPA-security via the experiment PrivK^cpa_{A,Pi}(n): a scheme is CPA-secure if for all PPT adversaries A, Pr[PrivK^cpa_{A,Pi}(n) = 1] <= 1/2 + negl(n) — the axiom's absolute-value form is the standard equivalent statement, and K&L prove indistinguishability equivalent to semantic security, so the title's conflation is licensed by the text. Counterexample showing teeth: any deterministic, stateless encryption scheme (e.g. raw AES-ECB) fails — A submits m0 = 0^n||0^n and m1 = 0^n||1^n, compares the two ciphertext blocks, and wins with probability 1, so |Pr - 1/2| = 1/2.

prompt_injection: Decrypt() must return ⊥ for all forged ciphertexts. If decrypt() returns partial plaintext before MAC verification, INT-CTXT is violated. Use encrypt-then-MAC: MAC over ciphertext, not plaintext. Verify MAC BEFORE any Dec call. Constant-time MAC comparison. Streaming decryption: buffer-to-completion before releasing plaintext.
source_document: Katz & Lindell 2020, Definition 4.17, Theorem 4.20
tensor_equation: ∀Π: AE_secure(Π) ⇔ IND_CPA(Π) ∧ INT_CTXT(Π); INT_CTXT: ∀PPT A: Pr[A outputs c' s.t. Dec_k(c') ≠ ⊥ and c' not from Enc_k] ≤ negl(n)
title: AEAD: Authenticated encryption = IND-CPA + INT-CTXT
verdict_discrepancy: CITATION: the pointer 'Katz & Lindell 2020, Definition 4.17, Theorem 4.20' is 2nd-edition numbering. In the 3rd edition (2020), which is the year cited, Chapter 4 is 'Message Authentication Codes' and authenticated encryption / CCA security was pulled out into its own Chapter 5, 'CCA-Security and Authenticated Encryption' (hash functions moved to Chapter 6). No Definition 4.17 / Theorem 4.20 on authenticated encryption exists in the cited edition. Attribution of the equivalence itself also properly belongs to Bellare & Namprempre rather than to a textbook definition.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: MAC is computed over the ciphertext, not the plaintext. Two independent keys (encryption and MAC) derived via HKDF with distinct info tags. Decrypt codepath must verify MAC before any Dec call. Counterexample: MAC-then-encrypt (SSL 3.0) or encrypt-and-MAC (SSH BPP) are not generically IND-CCA2.
source_document: Katz & Lindell 2020, Theorem 4.19
tensor_equation: ∀Π_E (IND-CPA), Π_M (strongly-secure MAC): Encrypt_then_MAC(Π_E, Π_M) is IND-CCA2
title: Encrypt-then-MAC composition yields IND-CCA2
verdict_evidence: Katz & Lindell state and prove that if Pi_E is a CPA-secure private-key encryption scheme and Pi_M is a *strongly* secure MAC (strong existential unforgeability), then the encrypt-then-authenticate construction is a CCA-secure / authenticated encryption scheme; the theorem sits in the 'CCA-Security and Authenticated Encryption' section of the MAC chapter, consistent with the Theorem 4.19 citation. The underlying generic-composition result is Bellare & Namprempre (2000). The axiom correctly carries the *strong* MAC hypothesis, which is load-bearing. Has teeth: instantiate Pi_M with a MAC that is unforgeable but not strongly unforgeable (one where a valid tag can be mauled into a second valid tag for the same message) and the composition loses CCA-security — the adversary submits the mauled ciphertext-tag pair to the decryption oracle and recovers the challenge plaintext.

prompt_injection: Enforce per-key query limits: max blocks encrypted under a single AES key < 2^48 (conservative). Track block counter per key, force rotation before threshold. For GCM with random nonces: enforce q ≤ 2^32 invocations per key. Advisory: flag any key lifetime exceeding 2^48 blocks for mandatory rotation.
source_document: Katz & Lindell 2020, Lemma 3.25
tensor_equation: ∀q-query D: |Adv_PRP(D) - Adv_PRF(D)| ≤ q(q-1) / 2^(n+1); enforce q << 2^(n/2)
title: PRP/PRF Switching Lemma: birthday bound limits
verdict_evidence: DISPUTED: No source text available for verification — uncited claim

prompt_injection: Decrypt() must reject malformed ciphertexts with a single, timing-invariant error before any plaintext is returned. Audit that no side channel (timing, error message, connection close) differs between MAC-failure, padding-failure, and decryption-failure. For AEAD: confirm tag is verified before any plaintext byte is released.
source_document: Katz & Lindell 2020, Definition 4.16
tensor_equation: ∀PPT A: |Pr[PrivK^cca_{A,Π}(n)=1] - 1/2| ≤ negl(n); A has Dec_k oracle before and after challenge, may not query Dec_k(c*)
title: IND-CCA2: Chosen ciphertext security with decryption oracle
verdict_evidence: Matches Katz & Lindell's CCA-security definition and its PrivK^cca_{A,Pi}(n) experiment notation exactly: the adversary is given oracle access to Dec_k both before and after receiving the challenge ciphertext c*, is forbidden from querying Dec_k(c*) itself, and must win with probability at most 1/2 + negl(n). Both of those restrictions are load-bearing and give the axiom teeth: without the c* exclusion the definition is unsatisfiable (any scheme loses); and CBC-mode-plus-no-MAC is a concrete scheme satisfying IND-CPA but failing this equation, since flipping a bit in the IV and submitting the mauled ciphertext to the decryption oracle recovers the challenge plaintext with advantage 1/2.

prompt_injection: KEM shared secret k must flow directly into DEM encryption, never stored, logged, or derived further. Audit that k is zeroized after use. Each hybrid encryption call generates a fresh KEM encapsulation. If AEAD used for DEM, verify associated data includes the KEM ciphertext (binding). Encrypt-then-cache-k is broken.
source_document: Katz & Lindell 2020, Theorem 11.12
tensor_equation: ∀Π_KEM (IND-CCA2), Π_DEM (AE): KEM_Encaps || DEM_Enc_k(m) is IND-CCA2; k must be single-use, bound to DEM ciphertext
title: Hybrid Encryption: KEM/DEM composition requires IND-CCA2 KEM
verdict_evidence: DISPUTED: No source text available for verification — uncited claim

prompt_injection: Paxos guarantees safety under all conditions — even with message loss, reordering, duplication, and crashes. It does NOT guarantee liveness (FLP). The core is a two-phase protocol: Prepare/Promise (Phase 1, learns any previously chosen value) and Accept/Accepted (Phase 2, drives agreement). Quorum intersection: any two majority quorums intersect. Each proposal carries a monotonically increasing ballot number.
source_document: Lamport, 'Paxos Made Simple', ACM SIGACT News 32(4):18-25, 2001
tensor_equation: ∀v₁,v₂,n₁,n₂: chosen(v₁,n₁) ∧ chosen(v₂,n₂) ⇒ v₁ = v₂; ∀v: chosen(v) ⇒ proposed(v)
title: Paxos: Safety — at most one value chosen, only proposed values
verdict_evidence: Lamport, 'Paxos Made Simple', ACM SIGACT News 32(4):18-25, December 2001 - citation is exact (title, venue, volume/issue, pages, year all correct). The paper states the safety requirements directly: 'Only a value that has been proposed may be chosen', 'Only a single value is chosen', and 'A process never learns that a value has been chosen unless it actually has been'; P2c and the two-phase protocol are derived to preserve them. Falsifiable and load-bearing: drop the requirement that a proposer adopt the highest-numbered accepted value returned in promises, and two proposers with numbers n1 < n2 can get distinct values accepted by distinct majorities that intersect, so chosen(v1,n1) and chosen(v2,n2) with v1 != v2 - the classic dueling-proposers safety violation.

prompt_injection: Raft guarantees all five safety properties at all times. Election Safety: at most one leader per term. Log Matching: same index+term ⇒ identical prefix. Leader Completeness: committed entries survive leader changes. Leader Append-Only: log entries immutable once written. The election restriction (servers only vote for candidates with log at least as up-to-date) is the key mechanism ensuring Leader Completeness.
source_document: Ongaro & Ousterhout, USENIX ATC 2014; Ongaro dissertation TLA+ spec
tensor_equation: ∀e₁,e₂ ∈ elections: e₁.term = e₂.term ⇒ e₁.leader = e₂.leader
title: Raft: Election Safety — at most one leader per term
verdict_evidence: Ongaro & Ousterhout, 'In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm' (USENIX ATC 2014), Figure 3 lists Election Safety as the first of Raft's five guarantees: 'at most one leader can be elected in a given term.' It is proved from the rules that each server casts at most one vote per term and a candidate needs a majority, so two leaders in the same term would require two disjoint majorities. The property is also carried in the TLA+ spec accompanying Ongaro's dissertation. Has teeth: remove the persistence of votedFor across restarts and a server that crashes and recovers can vote twice in the same term, producing two leaders in term T and divergent committed entries — this is a real and frequently-reintroduced Raft implementation bug.

prompt_injection: Byzantine agreement requires n > 3f nodes to tolerate f Byzantine faults. The 3f+1 bound is tight. PBFT makes it practical with three-phase protocol over primary-backup. Safety is asynchronous — no timing assumptions needed. Liveness requires weak synchrony (GST). Quorum intersection: any two 2f+1 quorums intersect in at least one correct replica — the 'witness' that guarantees safety across views.
source_document: Lamport, Shostak, Pease, TOPLAS 4(3):382-401, 1982; Castro & Liskov, OSDI 1999
tensor_equation: ∀f ≥ 1, in asynchronous Byzantine fault tolerance with PKI signatures: ∃BFT(f,n) ⇒ n ≥ 2f+1 (signed messages). For unsigned/authenticated Byzantine agreement: n ≥ 3f+1.
title: Byzantine Fault Tolerance: 3f+1 lower bound
verdict_discrepancy: MISSING PREMISE / OVER-BROAD QUANTIFIER. The axiom asserts unconditionally that any BFT solution implies n >= 3f+1. Counterexample from the cited paper itself: LSP's signed-message algorithm SM(1) tolerates f=1 traitor with n=3 generals, violating n >= 3f+1. The bound requires the oral-message (unauthenticated) assumption - or, for Castro & Liskov's PBFT, the asynchronous-with-safety-and-liveness setting - neither of which the equation states.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Rumor mongering achieves O(log n) convergence but leaves non-zero residue. Anti-entropy guarantees probability-1 convergence but is slower. Standard deployment: rumor mongering for fast propagation with anti-entropy as backup. Counter k controls unnecessary contacts before quiescence — residue decreases exponentially with k. Pull variant converges quadratically (p_{i+1} = p_i²).
source_document: Demers et al., 'Epidemic Algorithms for Replicated Database Maintenance', PODC 1987
tensor_equation: lim_{t→∞} P[∃s,s': s.ValueOf.t ≠ s'.ValueOf.t] = 0 for anti-entropy; push residue s = e^{-(k+1)(1-s)} where k is the counter threshold for rumor mongering, not s = e^{-m}.
title: Gossip: Epidemic protocols — anti-entropy guarantees probability-1 convergence
verdict_discrepancy: The second clause misquotes the paper's residue equation and rebinds its symbol. Demers' rumor-mongering residue satisfies s = e^{-(k+1)(1-s)}, where k is the counter threshold (a site becomes removed after k unnecessary contacts); m in the paper's tables denotes TRAFFIC (messages sent per site), not an exponent of the residue. 'push residue s = e^{-m}' therefore (a) does not appear in the paper, (b) leaves m unbound in the equation as stated, and (c) omits the self-referential (1-s) factor that makes the real equation implicit in s. The per-cycle push factor the paper does give is e^{-1} on the susceptible probability, not a closed-form residue e^{-m}.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Consistent hashing gives the minimum possible disruption: adding a node only reassigns keys from existing nodes to the new node, never between old nodes. Each physical node gets k log(C) virtual replicas for load balancing. With high probability, each node gets within O(1)/|V| fraction of keys. The monotonicity property is the key invariant.
source_document: Karger et al., 'Consistent Hashing and Random Trees', STOC 1997
tensor_equation: E[|{i: f_{V₁}(i) ≠ f_{V₂}(i)}|] / |I| = |V₁ Δ V₂| / |V₁ ∪ V₂|; for single node addition: 1/(n+1) = O(1/n)
title: Consistent Hashing: O(k/n) keys remapped on node change
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION: the paper does not state the identity E[remapped]/|I| = |V1 Δ V2| / |V1 ∪ V2|. Karger et al. prove monotonicity and O((log n)/n)-style balance/spread/load bounds with high probability; the symmetric-difference ratio is later folklore. As stated it is also only an idealised expectation: it holds under the uniform-random-placement assumption with unbounded virtual nodes, and fails for concrete rings - with 3 physical nodes and no virtual nodes, arc lengths are non-uniform, so removing one node typically remaps far more or less than the predicted 1/3 of the keyspace. Presenting an approximation as an equality drawn from a 1997 theory paper is a fidelity as well as an attribution defect.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Vector clocks capture the full partial order. Each process maintains an n-element vector. On internal/send: increment own counter. On receive: merge via component-wise max, then increment own counter. Dim(VC) ≥ n for n processes — tight lower bound. Simplified causality: for e at P_i, e → e' ⇔ C(e)[i] ≤ C(e')[i]. Concurrency is NOT transitive.
source_document: Fidge 1988; Mattern 1989; 'Virtual Time and Global States of Distributed Systems'
tensor_equation: ∀e,e': e → e' ⇔ (∀k: C(e)[k] ≤ C(e')[k] ∧ C(e) ≠ C(e')); e || e' ⇔ C(e) || C(e') (incomparable)
title: Vector Clocks: e → e' iff VC(e) < VC(e') in all components
verdict_evidence: Fidge (1988, 'Timestamps in message-passing systems that preserve the partial ordering') and Mattern (1989, 'Virtual Time and Global States of Distributed Systems') independently introduced vector clocks and proved the characterization: e happens-before e' iff VC(e) < VC(e'), where VC(e) < VC(e') means VC(e)[k] <= VC(e')[k] for all k and VC(e) != VC(e'); incomparable vectors correspond exactly to concurrent events. Both the citation and the paper title match the actual works. The biconditional (strong clock condition) is what distinguishes vector clocks from Lamport scalar clocks, which give only the one-way implication — that is the counterexample showing the axiom has teeth: with Lamport timestamps, L(e) < L(e') holds for many concurrent pairs, so a system substituting scalar clocks violates the '<==' direction and reports false causality.

prompt_injection: CALM is the positive counterpart to CAP. Monotonic programs never 'change their mind' — they only accumulate output as input arrives, so they can safely process partial information without coordination. Non-monotonic programs (negation, set-difference, universal quantification) may need to retract previous outputs, forcing coordination. Canonical monotonic: deadlock detection. Canonical non-monotonic: garbage collection.
source_document: Hellerstein & Alvaro, 'Keeping CALM', arXiv:1901.01930, 2019; Ameloot et al., JACM 60(2), 2013
tensor_equation: ∀P: (∃ coordination_free_implementation(P) ∧ consistent(P)) ⇔ monotonic(P) where monotonic: ∀S⊆T: P(S) ⊆ P(T)
title: CALM: Consistency As Logical Monotonicity — monotone programs don't need coordination
verdict_evidence: Hellerstein & Alvaro, 'Keeping CALM: When Distributed Consistency is Easy' (arXiv:1901.01930, later CACM 63(9), 2020), state the CALM theorem as: a program has a consistent, coordination-free distributed implementation if and only if it is monotonic. Ameloot, Neven & Van den Bussche, JACM 60(2), 2013, supply the formal proof via relational transducer networks. The monotonicity definition S subset-of T implies P(S) subset-of P(T) is the paper's. The biconditional is correct as stated - the paper is emphatic that it is an iff, and that the 'only if' direction is what makes coordination provably necessary for non-monotone tasks. Falsifiable in both directions: set union / CRDT grow-only counters are monotone and are implemented coordination-free; distributed garbage collection, deadlock detection and 'is this set complete?' are non-monotone (adding a fact can retract an earlier output), and every known implementation requires coordination such as a barrier or consensus.

citation: FIPS 140-2 Section 4.9.1 (Self-Tests) / FIPS 140-3 Section 9.1 (Pre-Operational Self-Tests)
invariant: ∀crypto module M: boot(M) → all power-on self-tests pass before any cryptographic function returns output. If any self-test fails, M enters error state and emits no cryptographic output.
tensor_equation: ∀M, ∀t < t_self_test_complete: Output(M, data) = ⊥ (no data output). Status output is permitted. ∀M, ∀t ≥ t_self_test_complete: Output(M, t) permitted iff all_self_tests_passed(M).
title: Power-On Self-Tests Must Pass Before Any Cryptographic Output
verdict_discrepancy: Two fidelity/citation defects. (1) The equation asserts Output(M,t) = bottom, explicitly annotated '(no output)'. FIPS 140-2 inhibits DATA output only; the standard simultaneously REQUIRES a status output interface and a Show Status service, and error/status indicators must be emitted during and after failed self-tests. A conforming module that emits a status indicator during self-test falsifies the axiom as literally quantified. (2) The FIPS 140-3 citation 'Section 9.1 (Pre-Operational Self-Tests)' does not exist: FIPS 140-3 is a short adopting document (announcement sections 1-14 plus Annexes A-F) that incorporates ISO/IEC 19790:2012; pre-operational self-tests are ISO/IEC 19790 Sec. 7.10.2, not 'FIPS 140-3 Section 9.1'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Instrument module initialization to intercept cryptographic output path. Simulate a failing self-test (e.g., corrupt known-answer test value). Assert that zero plaintext/ciphertext/signature bytes leave the boundary. Measure: the number of bytes emitted before self-test completion MUST be 0.

citation: FIPS 140-2 Section 4.9.2 (Conditional Self-Tests) / FIPS 140-3 Section 9.2 (Conditional Self-Tests)
invariant: ∀crypto module M: every key-pair generation operation and every DRBG call triggers a conditional self-test (pairwise consistency test for keys, continuous random number generator test for DRBG). Failure → key/random output destroyed, module enters error state.
tensor_equation: ∀key_gen(M, alg): pairwise_consistency_test(public_key, private_key) ≡ true. ∀DRBG_call(M): CRT_check(prev_output, current_output) ≡ true. ∀failure: output_destroyed ∧ M.state = ERROR. (Correct source: ISO/IEC 19790:2012 clause 7.10.3 conditional self-tests.)
title: Conditional Self-Tests on Key Generation and Random Number Generation
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION + FIDELITY: (a) FIPS 140-3 has no 'Section 9.2 (Conditional Self-Tests)'. FIPS 140-3 adopts ISO/IEC 19790:2012, where self-tests are clause 7.10 (7.10.3 conditional self-tests); 9.x does not exist in that numbering. (b) The universally quantified CRNGT ('for every DRBG call, compare with previous output') is a FIPS 140-2 requirement that FIPS 140-3 / SP 800-90B replaced with entropy-source health tests; approved DRBGs are no longer subject to the per-call continuous comparison. The axiom asserts one absolute rule across two standards that differ on exactly this point.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Hook the key generation and DRBG output paths. For keys: verify the pairwise consistency test runs BEFORE the key is returned. For DRBG: verify the continuous RNG test (stuck bit detection) compares consecutive outputs. Assert that any test failure causes the output buffer to be zeroized before function return.

citation: FIPS 140-2 Section 4.7 (Cryptographic Key Management) / FIPS 140-3 Section 7.9 (Sensitive Security Parameter Management)
invariant: ∀crypto module M: plaintext private/secret key material MUST NOT cross the cryptographic boundary. Keys may only exit in encrypted (wrapped) form or be destroyed. Intermediate values (plaintext, session keys) are destroyed before the function returns.
tensor_equation: ∀key K in M, ∀output O from M at Security Level 3 or 4: O ∩ K_plaintext = ∅. For Levels 1-2, plaintext key output is permitted. ∀encrypted_key_output: K is wrapped under an approved key-wrapping algorithm.
title: Cryptographic Boundary Integrity — No Plaintext Key Material Escapes
verdict_discrepancy: HARDENING + OVERSTATEMENT: the standard's prohibition on plaintext key output is conditional on Security Level 3/4; the axiom states it as an unconditional universal. The 'intermediate_plaintext destroyed before function return' clause is also not a FIPS requirement at that granularity (zeroisation is required for CSPs, not per-function-return).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states
verification: Instrument the module boundary — all I/O paths, network writes, file writes, shared memory. Tag all key material allocations with a sentinel. Assert that no tagged memory region ever appears in a write that crosses the boundary. Fuzz inputs to reach edge cases (error paths, partial outputs).

citation: SP 800-63B Section 5.1.1.2 (Memorized Secret Verifiers) / SP 800-63B Section 5.1.1.1 (Memorized Secret Authenticators)
invariant: ∀memorized secret verifier: the stored representation MUST be a salted hash (not plaintext, not reversible encryption). The verifier MUST NOT be derivable from the stored value by an attacker with access to the credential store. Minimum work factor: salt ≥ 32 bits, hash iteration count tuned to target ≥ 10ms per verification on current hardware.
tensor_equation: ∀verifier V: V = H(salt || secret, iterations) where salt is unique per-credential, iterations ≥ 10,000 for PBKDF2 or equivalent for bcrypt/scrypt/argon2. ∀credential_store_access: attacker cannot recover secret from V without guessing.
title: Memorized Secrets MUST Be Verifier-Impractical to Recover
