verdict_evidence: SP 800-63B Section 5.1.1.2 (Memorized Secret Verifiers) requires memorized secrets to be salted (at least 32 bits of salt) and hashed with a suitable one-way key derivation function, naming PBKDF2 with at least 10,000 iterations and balloon hashing, so that recovery from the stored value is computationally impractical. The equation's iteration floor and per-credential unique salt match. Falsifiable: a verifier storing unsalted SHA-256(password), or PBKDF2 with 1,000 iterations, violates it and is non-conformant.
verification: Audit the credential store schema: assert no plaintext column exists. Assert the hash function and iteration count are configured. Attempt to insert a credential with a low iteration count — assert rejection. Measure hash time: assert ≥ 10ms per verification. Check that salt is per-credential: insert two identical passwords, assert different hashes.

citation: SP 800-63B Section 7.1 (Session Management) / SP 800-63C Section 5.1 (Federation Assurance Levels)
invariant: ∀session S: S is cryptographically bound to the authentication event that created it. The session token MUST NOT be reusable across devices or after logout. Session secrets MUST be generated with an approved DRBG and have ≥ 128 bits of entropy.
tensor_equation: ∀session S, ∀auth_event E: S is bound to E via a unique, 64+ bit secret (per SP 800-63B §7.1) generated by an approved DRBG. ∀logout: S is invalidated server-side.
title: Session Binding — Authentication Event Bound to Session
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY (wrong bound): SP 800-63B Section 7.1 requires session secrets to contain at least 64 bits of entropy, generated by an approved random bit generator. The axiom states '128+ bit secret'. The bound is doubled relative to the source. Also, the final clause ('for every device_bound_session S, S is bound to the device fingerprint or TLS channel') is circular - the antecedent presupposes the consequent, so it cannot be falsified. SP 800-63C Section 5.1 is not 'Federation Assurance Levels' (FAL is defined in 63C Section 4).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected threshold/bound
verification: Generate a session token, then log out. Replay the old session token — assert HTTP 401. Generate a session on device A, try to use its token on device B (with different TLS channel binding) — assert rejection. Verify session token entropy: collect 10,000 session tokens, run ent or NIST STS randomness tests. Assert token length ≥ 128 bits of entropy.

citation: SP 800-63-3 Section 5.1 (Authenticator Types) / SP 800-63B Section 5.1 (Authenticator and Verifier Requirements)
invariant: ∀AAL2+ authentication: at least two independent authentication factors MUST be used. The compromise of one factor's verifier (e.g., password database) MUST NOT compromise the other factor's verifier. Factors are from different categories: something you know, something you have, something you are.
tensor_equation: ∀auth at AAL2+: |factors| ≥ 2 ∧ category(factor_1) ≠ category(factor_2). ∀factor_i, ∀factor_j: compromise(verifier(factor_i)) ↛ compromise(verifier(factor_j)).
title: Multi-Factor Authentication — Independent Factors, Compromise of One Does Not Compromise Others
verdict_discrepancy: The second conjunct is not in the cited source and misuses 800-63B's own vocabulary. SP 800-63B defines 'verifier compromise resistance' (Sec. 5.2.7) as a per-authenticator property — an authenticator is verifier-compromise-resistant if compromise of the *verifier's stored data* does not let an attacker impersonate the subscriber. It nowhere asserts the cross-factor claim 'compromise(verifier(factor_i)) does-not-imply compromise(verifier(factor_j))'; in fact multi-factor authenticators explicitly share a single verifier, and 800-63B permits verifiers that are not compromise-resistant at AAL2. Citation error: SP 800-63-3 Sec. 5.1 is part of 'Digital Identity Risk Management', not 'Authenticator Types' — authenticator types are SP 800-63B Sec. 5.1.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Model the credential stores as separate databases/services. Simulate a breach of the password database. Assert that the OTP/TOTP/WebAuthn verifier remains uncompromised (no shared secrets). Verify at the protocol level: the second factor challenge is generated independently of the first factor's verification result.

citation: SP 800-56A Section 6.1 (Key Confirmation) / SP 800-56A Section 5.8 (Key Confirmation Using MAC)
invariant: ∀key establishment scheme: both parties MUST confirm that the other party has derived the same shared secret Z before using Z to protect data. Key confirmation is mandatory for key agreement (DH/ECDH) unless the protocol provides equivalent assurance through other means (e.g., TLS 1.3 Finished message).
tensor_equation: ∀party P in key_agreement: P may receive confirmation that the other party computed the same Z (key confirmation is optional per SP 800-56A). ∀shared_secret Z: Z is not used for data protection until key_confirmation_complete = true for both parties (if confirmation is performed).
title: Key Confirmation — Both Parties Must Verify Shared Secret Ownership
verdict_discrepancy: Hardening of an optional feature into a universal MUST. (1) SP 800-56A makes key confirmation optional ('may be performed'), conditioned on a participant requiring such evidence; the axiom asserts every party in every key agreement receives explicit confirmation. (2) The standard explicitly defines UNILATERAL key confirmation, in which only one participant obtains the assurance — the axiom's 'for both parties' is false for every C(2e,2s)/C(1e,2s)/C(0e,2s) unilateral variant in Sections 6.1.1.5.1, 6.2.1.5.1, 6.3.x. (3) The standard nowhere forbids using the derived keying material before key confirmation completes; it in fact permits derived keying material to be used FOR key confirmation itself (Sec. 5.8 restriction note). (4) Citation numbers are wrong: key confirmation is Sec. 5.9, not 'Section 6.1' or 'Section 5.8'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening
verification: Man-in-the-middle the key agreement: intercept the key confirmation MAC, replace the responder's public key with our own, and compute a different Z. Assert that the initiator's key confirmation check fails and the connection is aborted. Verify that no application data is sent before key confirmation completes.

citation: SP 800-56A Section 5.6.2.2 (Ephemeral Key Pairs) / SP 800-56A Section 6.3 (Key Destruction)
invariant: ∀ephemeral key pair (sk_eph, pk_eph) generated for a key establishment transaction: (1) the key pair MUST be used exactly once, (2) the private key MUST be zeroized immediately after the shared secret is computed, (3) no private key material persists across transactions.
tensor_equation: ∀tx T, ∀ephemeral_key_pair K: use_count(K) = 1. ∀T: after Z = compute_shared_secret(T, K_eph): zeroize(K_eph.private). ∀T1 ≠ T2: K_eph(T1) ≠ K_eph(T2).
title: Ephemeral Key Pairs Must Be Destroyed After Single Use
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY (hardening) + CITATION: SP 800-56A states the ephemeral key pair is 'ordinarily used in exactly one transaction', with an explicit stated exception where an ephemeral key pair is used in multiple transactions for key-transport broadcast. The axiom converts a hedged 'ordinarily' with a documented exception into an absolute forall with use_count = 1. Additionally the cited locations are wrong: Rev. 3 Section 6.3 is part of the key-agreement scheme catalogue, not 'Key Destruction', and ephemeral-key requirements are not at 5.6.2.2 (which concerns key-pair assurances).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Instrument the key generation function to track each ephemeral key pair's usage count. Run 1000 key establishment transactions. Assert each key pair is used at most once. Assert the private key buffer is zeroized (all bytes = 0x00) before the transaction's key-establishment function returns. Set a watchpoint on the private key buffer after zeroization — assert no subsequent reads.

citation: SP 800-56A Section 5.6.2.3.2 (ECC Full Public Key Validation) / SP 800-56A Section 5.6.2.4 (Assurance of Validity of the Public Key)
invariant: ∀received public key Q in ECDH: the implementation MUST validate that Q is on the curve, Q is not the point at infinity, and n*Q = O (where n is the curve order — full subgroup check). This prevents small-subgroup attacks, invalid-curve attacks, and twist attacks.
tensor_equation: ∀ECDH pubkey Q: validate(Q) ≡ (Q ∈ curve) ∧ (Q ≠ O) ∧ (n·Q = O). If validate(Q) = false: abort key establishment, return error, do not compute shared secret.
title: Static Private Key MUST Be Validated Against Small-Subgroup and Invalid-Curve Attacks
verdict_evidence: SP 800-56A Sec. 5.6.2.3.2 is the 'ECC Full Public-Key Validation Routine': verify Q is not the point at infinity O; verify affine coordinates are in [0, p-1]; verify y^2 = x^3 + ax + b; and (the step that distinguishes full from the partial routine in 5.6.2.3.3) verify nQ = O. Full validation is what SP 800-56A requires for static/received public keys, and failure means the routine returns an error and the key-establishment transaction is not performed. Has teeth: the classic small-subgroup attack supplies a low-order point Q with nQ != O that passes partial validation, leaks bits of the static private key through the shared secret, and is rejected only by the nQ = O check.
verification: Craft malicious public keys: (1) point on the twist, (2) low-order point, (3) point at infinity, (4) point not on the curve. Attempt ECDH with each. Assert that every case is rejected before the shared secret is computed. Verify that no exponentiation with the static private key occurs against the invalid point.

citation: SP 800-90A Rev. 1 Section 9.2 (Reseeding) / SP 800-90A Section 9.3.1 (Reseed Interval)
invariant: ∀DRBG with prediction_resistance enabled: every generate call MUST be preceded by an entropy input (reseed) from an approved entropy source. ∀DRBG without prediction_resistance: the DRBG MUST be reseeded before the number of generate calls exceeds the reseed interval (max 2^48 requests or 2^48 bits of output, whichever comes first).
tensor_equation: ∀DRBG without prediction_resistance: generate_count ≤ reseed_interval ∧ bits_per_request ≤ 2^19 (Hash/HMAC) or 2^19 (CTR). 2^48 is the maximum number of generate requests between reseeds, not bits. ∀exceeded: next call is_reseed, not generate.
title: DRBG Must Be Reseeded Before Entropy Drain — Prediction Resistance
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: 'bits_output <= 2^48' misstates the standard - 2^48 is the maximum number of generate *requests* between reseeds, not bits. The per-request bit limit is 2^19 bits (Hash/HMAC) and 2^19 bits for CTR_DRBG, and the axiom omits it. Also SP 800-90A Section 9.3.1 is 'The Generate Function', not 'Reseed Interval'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Configure a DRBG with a small reseed interval (e.g., 1000). Generate 1001 outputs without reseeding. Assert that the 1001st call is rejected or triggers an automatic reseed. Verify that the DRBG's internal counter tracks generate calls and bits output correctly. For prediction_resistance mode: verify that generate does not touch the DRBG state before acquiring fresh entropy.

citation: SP 800-90A Rev. 1 Section 10.2.1 (Entropy Input) / SP 800-90A Section 8.4 (Security Strength and Entropy Requirements)
invariant: ∀DRBG instantiation: the entropy_input to the instantiate function MUST contain at least `min_entropy` bits of entropy from an approved entropy source. For Hash_DRBG: min_entropy = security_strength (e.g., 256 bits for SHA-256). For HMAC_DRBG: min_entropy = security_strength. For CTR_DRBG: min_entropy = security_strength.
tensor_equation: ∀DRBG instantiation: entropy(entropy_input) ≥ security_strength. ∀Hash_DRBG with hash_fn H: security_strength = output_len(H). ∀instantiate: entropy_input || nonce || personalization_string are all hashed independently.
title: Instantiate Must Seed DRBG from Approved Entropy Source with Minimum Entropy
verdict_discrepancy: (1) Wrong section: Hash_DRBG is Sec. 10.1.1 (instantiation 10.1.1.2); Sec. 10.2.1 is CTR_DRBG. Sec. 8.4 is 'Security Strengths Supported by an Instantiation', and the entropy requirement is Sec. 8.6.3. (2) 'For Hash_DRBG with hash H: security_strength = output_len(H)' is false. Sec. 10.1 states the maximum supported strength is the hash's PRE-IMAGE resistance strength and that 'This Recommendation supports only four security strengths: 112, 128, 192, and 256 bits'. SHA-1 (outlen 160) supports at most 128; SHA-512 (outlen 512) supports at most 256; SHA-512/224 supports 192. (3) 'entropy_input || nonce || personalization_string are all hashed independently' is false — the Hash_DRBG Instantiate Process is literally '1. seed_material = entropy_input || nonce || personalization_string. 2. seed = Hash_df(seed_material, seedlen)': one concatenation, one derivation-function call.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Instrument the entropy source to return a known-low-entropy seed (e.g., all zeros, or a counter). Assert that instantiation fails or, if it succeeds, the DRBG output fails the NIST STS randomness battery. Measure the entropy_input length: assert len(entropy_input) * 8 ≥ security_strength. Verify that the nonce is distinct from the entropy_input.

citation: SP 800-90A Rev. 1 Section 9.4 (DRBG Internal State) / SP 800-90A Section 11.2 (Security Considerations)
invariant: ∀DRBG instance: the internal state (V, C, Key for HMAC_DRBG; V, C for CTR_DRBG; V, C for Hash_DRBG) MUST NOT be accessible outside the DRBG boundary. No debug output, logging, error messages, or serialization may leak any part of the internal state. State is zeroized on uninstantiate.
tensor_equation: ∀DRBG D, ∀external_access_path P: D.internal_state ∩ P.output = ∅. ∀uninstantiate(D): zeroize(D.internal_state). ∀generate(D): output(D) is computationally indistinguishable from random WITHOUT knowledge of D.internal_state (the security model requires output to be indistinguishable from random to an external observer, not given internal state).
title: DRBG Internal State Must Not Be Exposed
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY (false as written) + CITATION: the third conjunct - 'output(D) is computationally indistinguishable from random GIVEN D.internal_state' - is false and inverts the security model. A DRBG is deterministic: given the internal state, all future output is exactly predictable, which is why SP 800-90A instead defines backtracking resistance and prediction resistance around state compromise. The correct statement is indistinguishability given the OUTPUT history, not given the state. Citation is also mislabeled: the internal state is specified in Section 8.3; Section 9.4 is 'Removing a DRBG Instantiation' (uninstantiate), and Section 11 is 'Assurance'/health testing, not 'Security Considerations'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Set a memory watchpoint on the DRBG internal state buffer. Call generate, reseed, and uninstantiate. Assert that no external write (network, file, stdout, log) contains bytes from the internal state buffer. Force an error path (e.g., reseed failure) and check that error messages do not include state bytes. On uninstantiate, assert the state buffer is zeroed.

citation: SP 800-108 Rev. 1 Section 5.1 (KDF in Counter Mode) / SP 800-108 Section 5.2 (KDF in Feedback Mode)
invariant: ∀KDF invocation in counter or feedback mode: the derived key MUST be computed from the PRF applied to a fixed, ordered sequence of fields: (1) the key derivation key K_I, (2) the counter i (or feedback value), (3) the label L (a fixed string identifying the purpose), (4) the context (e.g., session ID, party identities), and (5) the length of derived keying material in bits. The encoding MUST be unambiguous — no two distinct sets of inputs produce the same concatenated PRF input.
tensor_equation: ∀KDF_call(mode, K_I, L, Context, L_output): derived_key = PRF(K_I, [i] || Label || 0x00 || Context || [L_output]) for counter mode, where [i] is a 32-bit big-endian counter. ∀input_set A ≠ B: PRF_input(A) ≠ PRF_input(B).
title: KDF Inputs Must Be Complete and Ordered — No Ambiguity
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY + CITATION: (a) the counter length r is a parameter with allowed values 8, 16, 24, or 32 bits (number of iterations bounded by 2^r - 1, r <= 32); the axiom hardens this into 'a 32-bit big-endian counter'. (b) The cited section numbers are from the withdrawn 2009 original: in SP 800-108 Rev. 1 (the revision named in claimed_source) counter mode is Section 4.1 and feedback mode 4.2, not 5.1/5.2. (c) The specific field ordering is a recommended encoding, not a mandate; Rev. 1 permits other unambiguous arrangements.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Construct two distinct KDF invocations with the same K_I and L_output but different Labels. Assert that the concatenated PRF inputs differ. Construct two invocations with the same Label but different Contexts. Assert PRF inputs differ. Verify that the length field uses the fixed-width encoding (32-bit big-endian) and that the counter starts at 1, not 0.

citation: SP 800-108 Rev. 1 Appendix A (HKDF) / RFC 5869 Section 3.1 (HKDF-Extract)
invariant: ∀HKDF-Extract: the salt value MUST be chosen independently of the input keying material (IKM). If no salt is provided, a string of HashLen zero bytes is used as the default — but this default MUST NOT be used when the IKM has low entropy (e.g., passwords). For password-based KDFs, use HKDF-Extract with a random salt, not the all-zeros default.
tensor_equation: ∀HKDF-Extract(salt, IKM): salt ⊥ IKM (statistically independent). ∀low_entropy_IKM ∧ salt = zeros(HashLen): this is a violation — must use random salt. PRK = HMAC-Hash(salt, IKM).
title: HKDF-Extract: Salt Must Be Independent of IKM — No Zero-Salt Collapse
verdict_discrepancy: HARDENING + contradiction of the cited source. RFC 5869 designates the all-zero HashLen salt as the *defined default*, not 'a violation'. The axiom's clause 'for-all low_entropy_IKM and salt = zeros(HashLen): this is a violation — must use random salt' asserts the opposite of RFC 5869 Sec. 2.2/3.1, which permit exactly that configuration. Additionally 'salt independent-of IKM (statistically independent)' appears nowhere in RFC 5869 — the RFC's requirement is that salt be non-secret and preferably random/high-entropy, and it notes salt need not be secret or unpredictable. Finally, SP 800-108 Rev. 1 specifies KDFs in counter/feedback/pipeline modes from an already-uniform key; HKDF-Extract is specified in RFC 5869 and adopted by SP 800-56C Rev. 2, not by SP 800-108 Rev. 1.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Audit all HKDF call sites. For each: check whether the IKM is low entropy (password, shared secret from a weak DH group). If yes, assert the salt is not the all-zeros default. For each call site where IKM is high entropy (proper ECDH shared secret), verify that using all-zeros salt is intentional and documented. Fuzz with zero-length IKM — assert behavior is defined.

citation: SP 800-108 Rev. 1 Appendix A (HKDF) / RFC 5869 Section 2.3 (HKDF-Expand) / SP 800-108 Section 5.1 (Counter Mode KDF, max blocks)
invariant: ∀HKDF-Expand(PRK, info, L): the total output length L MUST NOT exceed 255 * HashLen, where HashLen is the output length of the underlying hash function (in bytes). This prevents the counter from wrapping (counter is a single byte in HKDF).
tensor_equation: ∀HKDF-Expand(PRK, info, L): L ≤ 255 * HashLen. For SHA-256: L ≤ 255 * 32 = 8160 bytes. For SHA-512: L ≤ 255 * 64 = 16320 bytes.
title: HKDF-Expand: Output Length Must Not Exceed PRF Limit
verdict_discrepancy: MISATTRIBUTION: NIST SP 800-108 Rev. 1 does not specify HKDF and has no 'Appendix A (HKDF)'. SP 800-108 specifies only the expansion step given an existing key-derivation key; HKDF (extract-then-expand) is specified in SP 800-56C Rev. 2 and RFC 5869. SP 800-108 counter-mode's block limit is 2^r - 1 where r is the counter bit-length (r in {8,16,24,32}), not a fixed 255*HashLen. The 255 bound comes solely from RFC 5869.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Call HKDF-Expand with L = 255 * HashLen + 1. Assert the call fails or returns an error. Call with L = 255 * HashLen — assert success. Call with L = 0 — assert defined behavior (empty output or error, depending on implementation).

citation: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 Section 8.1 (Key States and Transitions) / SP 800-57 Part 1 Section 8.2 (Key State Transitions)
invariant: ∀cryptographic key K: K transitions through states according to the defined state machine. Valid transitions are: Pre-Activation → Active, Active → Deactivated, Active → Compromised, Active → Destroyed, Deactivated → Compromised, Deactivated → Destroyed, Deactivated → Active (re-activation allowed only if key was not compromised). No other transitions are permitted.
tensor_equation: ∀key K, ∀state_transition(K, s1 → s2): (s1, s2) ∈ ValidTransitions where ValidTransitions = {(Pre-Act, Active), (Active, Deactivated), (Active, Compromised), (Active, Destroyed), (Deactivated, Compromised), (Deactivated, Destroyed), (Compromised, Destroyed), (Active, Suspended), (Suspended, Active)}. (Suspended state per SP 800-57 §7.3 was omitted.)
title: Key State Transition Enforcement — Only Valid Transitions Allowed
verdict_discrepancy: (1) Wrong sections: key states/transitions are Section 7, not 'Section 8.1/8.2'; Section 8 is key-management phases and functions. (2) The state set is incomplete — the Suspended state (Sec. 7.3) is omitted entirely, along with its transitions (active->suspended, suspended->active/deactivated/compromised/destroyed) and the pre-activation->destroyed and pre-activation->compromised transitions of Sec. 7.1. (3) The transition set contains a transition SP 800-57 does not allow: (Deactivated, Active). Sec. 7.4 permits deactivated keys only to move to compromised or destroyed; it is the SUSPENDED state, not the deactivated state, from which a key 'shall transition to the active state when the reason for the suspension is resolved'. A conformance checker built on this axiom would accept reactivating a deactivated (expired-cryptoperiod) key. (4) The trailing clause 'K.state = Compromised -> re-activation(Deactivated -> Active) is forbidden' is incoherent — it conditions a deactivated-state transition on the compromised state.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: added missing cases/states
verification: Implement the state machine as a finite state machine with explicit transitions. Write a property-based test that generates all 36 possible state transitions (6 states × 6 states). For each, assert that the transition is either accepted (if in the valid set) or rejected. Test specifically: attempt to transition from Compromised to Active — assert rejection. Attempt to transition from Active to Pre-Activation — assert rejection.

citation: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 Section 5.3 (Cryptoperiods) / SP 800-57 Part 1 Section 5.3.6 (Recommended Cryptoperiods for Key Types)
invariant: ∀cryptographic key K: K MUST NOT be used for cryptographic operations (encrypt, sign, MAC) after its cryptoperiod has expired. The cryptoperiod is defined by the key's type and purpose. A key in Active state with expired cryptoperiod MUST transition to Deactivated or Expired state.
tensor_equation: ∀key K: K.cryptoperiod = OUP (originator-usage period) + extension. The RUP (recipient-usage period) extends past the OUP (typically OUP + up to 3 years for data-encryption keys). ∀time t > K.cryptoperiod_end: K.state ∈ {Deactivated, Expired, Destroyed}.
title: Cryptoperiod Enforcement — Key Must Not Be Used Beyond Its Lifetime
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: SP 800-57 explicitly separates the originator-usage period (OUP) from the recipient-usage period (RUP), and the RUP extends past the OUP (typically OUP + up to 3 years for data-encryption keys): a Deactivated key MAY still be used to decrypt or verify previously protected information. The axiom's 'crypto_op(K,t) = ERROR_KEY_EXPIRED' for all t past cryptoperiod_end therefore contradicts the source, which permits processing of already-protected data. It also hardens NIST's 'should/recommended' cryptoperiods (explicitly risk-dependent guidance, not requirements) into an absolute rule.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Create a key with a cryptoperiod of 1 second. Wait 2 seconds. Attempt to encrypt/sign with the key. Assert the operation is rejected. Verify the key's state has transitioned to Deactivated or Expired. Create a key with a cryptoperiod of 1 hour. Verify that operations succeed at T+30 minutes and fail at T+61 minutes. Assert that the check is performed at operation time, not at a scheduled cleanup job.

citation: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 Section 5.2 (Key Usage) / SP 800-57 Part 1 Section 8.1.5 (Key Usage and Purpose)
invariant: ∀cryptographic key K: K MUST be used for exactly one purpose (e.g., encryption, authentication, signing, key wrapping). A key used for encryption MUST NOT also be used for signing. A key used for key wrapping MUST NOT also be used for data encryption. This is enforced by binding the purpose to the key at creation time.
tensor_equation: ∀key K: purpose(K) is a singleton set. ∀K: if purpose(K) = {encryption} then sign(K, data) = ERROR_WRONG_PURPOSE. ∀K: if purpose(K) = {key_wrapping} then encrypt(K, data) = ERROR_WRONG_PURPOSE.
title: Key Separation — No Key Used for Multiple Purposes
verdict_discrepancy: HARDENING against an explicit carve-out. SP 800-57 says 'In general... shall' and then names exceptions; the axiom's 'for-all key K: purpose(K) is a singleton set' admits none. Direct counterexample from the source itself: an AES-GCM key legitimately has purpose {encryption, authentication}, and a signing key legitimately provides both integrity authentication and source authentication — both are permitted by Sec. 5.2 but rejected by the axiom. Citation error too: Sec. 8.1.5 of SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 is 'Key Establishment Function' (source TOC line 371), not 'Key Usage and Purpose'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Create a key with purpose = ENCRYPT. Attempt to use it for signing. Assert the sign operation is rejected with a purpose-mismatch error. Create a key with purpose = SIGN. Attempt to use it for key wrapping. Assert rejection. Create a key with purpose = KEY_WRAP. Attempt to use it for general data encryption. Assert rejection. Verify that the purpose is immutable after key creation.

citation: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 Section 6.2.2 (Key Wrapping) / SP 800-57 Part 1 Section 5.3.2 (Cryptoperiod Relationships)
invariant: ∀key K wrapped for transport or storage: the wrapping MUST use an approved key-wrapping algorithm (AES-KW per SP 800-38F, or RSA-OAEP, or ECDH + KDF). The wrapped key MUST include an integrity check (MAC or authenticated encryption). The wrapping key MUST have sufficient strength: |wrapping_key| ≥ |wrapped_key|, and the wrapping key's cryptoperiod must not exceed the wrapped key's cryptoperiod.
tensor_equation: ∀wrapped_key K_w: K_w = Wrap(K, WK) where Wrap ∈ {AES-KW, AES-KWP, TKW} per SP 800-38F. RSA-OAEP is key transport (SP 800-56B) and ECDH+KDF is key agreement, not key wrapping. ∀unwrap(K_w): integrity_check(K_w) must pass before K is used.
title: Key Wrapping Must Use Approved Algorithm with Integrity Check
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION + FIDELITY: SP 800-38F ('Methods for Key Wrapping') approves exactly three methods - AES KW, AES KWP, and TKW - and nothing else. RSA-OAEP is key transport (SP 800-56B) and 'ECDH+KDF' is key agreement (SP 800-56A); neither is a key-wrapping algorithm under 38F, so the set membership clause misstates both cited documents. The claim 'cryptoperiod(WK) >= cryptoperiod(K)' is not a stated SP 800-57 rule (the standard's stated relationship is about security strength and about the protected key not outliving its protection, and 5.3.6 assigns key-wrapping keys their own recommended cryptoperiods). Section labels are also wrong: Rev. 5 has no 6.2.2 'Key Wrapping' / 5.3.2 'Cryptoperiod Relationships'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Wrap a key with AES-KW. Corrupt one byte of the wrapped blob. Attempt to unwrap. Assert the unwrap fails with an integrity error. Wrap a key with a wrapping key that has lower security strength (e.g., wrap an AES-256 key with an AES-128 key). Assert the wrap is rejected. Verify that the wrapping key's cryptoperiod is checked before the wrap operation proceeds.

citation: SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 Section 8.2.5 (Compromised Key State) / SP 800-57 Part 1 Section 8.3.5 (Compromise Recovery)
invariant: ∀key K that transitions to Compromised state: all keys derived from K (via KDF or hierarchical key derivation) MUST also transition to Compromised. All data protected by K or its derived keys MUST be considered compromised. The compromise event MUST be logged with a timestamp and reason.
tensor_equation: ∀key K: K.state = Compromised → ∀key K' derived_from(K): K'.state = Compromised. ∀data D protected_by(K): D.confidentiality = compromised ∧ D.integrity = compromised. ∀compromise_event: log(timestamp, key_id, reason).
title: Compromised Key Revocation — All Material Derived from Compromised Key Is Invalidated
verdict_discrepancy: (1) All three cited section numbers are wrong: the compromised key state is Sec. 7.5 (not 8.2.5), compromise implications are Sec. 5.5.1, compromise recovery is Sec. 9.5.4, and Sec. 8.3.5 is the Key Revocation Function, not 'Compromise Recovery'. (2) Fidelity: the axiom asserts D.confidentiality = compromised AND D.integrity = compromised for every datum protected by K. SP 800-57 Sec. 5.5.1 makes the implication key-type specific (disclosure of a confidentiality key -> confidentiality loss; disclosure of a private signature key -> integrity/non-repudiation 'suspect'; integrity-key substitution -> integrity in question) and explicitly preserves value in some cases: 'if it can be shown that the signed data was protected by other mechanisms ... from a time before the compromise, the signature may still have some value.' It also permits continued controlled use of a compromised key to process already-protected information. The blanket conjunction is stronger than the source.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Create a hierarchy: root key → derived key 1 → derived key 2. Mark the root key as compromised. Assert that derived key 1 and derived key 2 are also marked as compromised. Attempt to encrypt with derived key 2 — assert rejection. Check the audit log — assert all three keys appear with the compromise event. Verify that the compromise is propagated atomically (all-or-nothing).

prompt_injection: Every optimization pass MUST preserve observable program behavior. The transformed program must produce identical output on all inputs. This is a bisimulation: the original and optimized programs are observationally equivalent. Any optimization that changes output for any input is incorrect, regardless of performance gain.
source_document: Dragon Book (Aho et al. 2006) Ch.9; Cooper & Torczon 2011 Ch.10
tensor_equation: ∀program P, ∀optimization pass O, ∀input x: O(P)(x) = P(x)
title: Compiler Prime Directive: optimization preserves semantics
verdict_evidence: The Dragon Book (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman 2006) Ch. 9 opens the code-optimization treatment with the requirement that transformations preserve the meaning of the program ('the optimization must not change the output produced ... must preserve the semantics of the original program'), and Cooper & Torczon Ch. 8-10 state the same as the first obligation of any transformation. Counterexample showing teeth (and that the equation is not vacuous): loop-invariant code motion that hoists a division out of a loop whose trip count can be zero changes P(x) from 'no trap' to 'divide-by-zero trap' for that x, so O(P)(x) != P(x) and the transformation is rejected; likewise GVN that ignores aliasing.

prompt_injection: The lexer DFA must be deterministic. For every state and input character, there is exactly one next state. Non-deterministic NFAs must be converted to DFAs via subset construction before use. The maximal munch rule: at each position, consume the longest possible match.
source_document: Dragon Book Ch.3; Cooper & Torczon Ch.2
tensor_equation: ∀state s, ∀input char c: |δ(s, c)| = 1 in the DFA transition function
title: Lexing: DFA determinism — one unique next state per (state, input) pair
verdict_evidence: The Dragon Book (Aho, Lam, Sethi, Ullman) Chapter 3, Section 3.6.4, defines a DFA precisely by this property: there are no moves on input epsilon, and for each state s and input symbol a there is exactly one edge labelled a leaving s. Cooper & Torczon Chapter 2 gives the same total-function delta: S x Sigma -> S. Falsifiable and discriminating: the NFA for (a|b)*abb has two a-edges out of state 0, so |delta(0,a)| = 2; and a partial DFA with a missing transition has |delta(s,c)| = 0 - which is why subset construction plus a dead/error state is required to obtain a true DFA. Both are concrete violating machines.

prompt_injection: An LR(1) parse table must have zero conflicts. Every (state, lookahead) pair maps to at most one action. Shift-reduce conflicts indicate an ambiguous grammar or insufficient lookahead. FIRST and FOLLOW sets must be computed as least fixed points — they converge because the grammar has finitely many nonterminals.
source_document: Dragon Book Ch.4; Cooper & Torczon Ch.3
tensor_equation: ∀state s, ∀lookahead a: |action[s, a]| ≤ 1 (no shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts)
title: Parsing: LR parse table must have no conflicts for LR(1) grammars
verdict_evidence: Aho/Lam/Sethi/Ullman (Dragon Book, 2nd ed) Ch. 4.7: a grammar is LR(1) precisely when the canonical LR(1) parsing table constructed for it contains no multiply-defined ACTION entries — i.e. no shift/reduce or reduce/reduce conflicts, which is exactly |action[s,a]| <= 1. Cooper & Torczon Ch. 3 states the same criterion for table construction. Has teeth: the dangling-else grammar S -> if E then S | if E then S else S | other yields a state where 'else' admits both shift and reduce, so |action[s, else]| = 2, and the grammar is provably not LR(1) for any k. Caveat worth recording: the biconditional holds for the *canonical* LR(1) table; LALR(1) table construction merges states with identical cores and can introduce reduce/reduce conflicts for grammars that are genuinely LR(1), so an LALR generator failing this check does not prove the grammar non-LR(1).

prompt_injection: If an expression type-checks as τ, and it evaluates to a value v, then v also has type τ. This is the progress + preservation theorem (Wright & Felleisen 1994). The type checker must reject any program where a well-typed subexpression could produce a value of the wrong type. Type inference via unification: the most general unifier must exist and produce the principal type.
source_document: Appel, 'Modern Compiler Implementation', Ch.5; Pierce, TAPL 2002
tensor_equation: ∀expression e, ∀type τ: Γ ⊢ e: τ ∧ e →* v ⇒ Γ ⊢ v: τ
title: Type checking: Type preservation — well-typed programs don't go wrong
verdict_evidence: Pierce, 'Types and Programming Languages' (MIT Press, 2002), Theorem 9.3.9 (Preservation) states: if Gamma |- t : T and t -> t', then Gamma |- t' : T; iterating over -->* gives the stated form. The premise is present and binding, so this is not the vacuous-rule pattern. Falsifiable: a language whose typing rule for `if` allows differently-typed branches satisfies the syntactic typing judgement but a reduction step yields a value of the other branch's type, breaking preservation - which is why TAPL's T-IF requires both branches at T. Caveat for the merge: the title's slogan 'well-typed programs don't go wrong' (Milner 1978) is soundness = preservation AND progress; the equation states preservation only, and preservation alone permits a well-typed term to get stuck. Appel's 'Modern Compiler Implementation' Ch.5 covers semantic analysis/type checking but proves no preservation theorem - TAPL is the load-bearing citation here.

prompt_injection: In SSA form, every variable has exactly one definition, and every use is dominated by that definition. Phi nodes are placed at the dominance frontier (strictly: iterated dominance frontier DF+ of the set of definition sites). The dominance tree is a rooted tree where the entry node dominates all nodes. For any edge (u→v) in the CFG, either u dominates v or v dominates u.
source_document: Cytron et al. 1991; Cooper & Torczon Ch.9; Muchnick Ch.7
tensor_equation: ∀variable v in SSA form: |DEF(v)| = 1 ∧ ∀use u of v: DEF(v) dominates u
title: SSA: Single static assignment — each variable defined exactly once, USE(x) dominated by DEF(x)
verdict_evidence: Cytron, Ferrante, Rosen, Wegman & Zadeck (TOPLAS 1991) define SSA as the form in which each variable is the target of exactly one assignment, with phi-functions inserted at iterated dominance frontiers precisely so that every use is reached by a single definition; Cooper & Torczon and Muchnick state the accompanying dominance property. Counterexample showing teeth: a CFG with 'if (c) x1 = 1; else x2 = 2;' followed by 'print x1' — the use of x1 in the join block is not dominated by its definition in the then-branch, which is exactly the condition that forces insertion of x3 = phi(x1, x2). Programs failing the equation are precisely those a construction algorithm must fix.

prompt_injection: Dataflow analysis uses a monotone framework over a lattice of finite height. Transfer functions must be monotone (not necessarily distributive). The meet-over-all-paths (MOP) solution is the ideal; the maximal fixed point (MFP) solution is what iterative algorithms compute. MFP ≤ MOP always; they coincide when the framework is distributive. Liveness is the dual of reaching definitions.
source_document: Dragon Book Ch.9; Muchnick Ch.11
tensor_equation: ∀transfer function f in monotone framework: x ≤ y ⇒ f(x) ≤ f(y); iterating from ⊥ reaches lfp(f) in finite steps only if the semilattice has finite height (no infinite ascending chains).
title: Dataflow: Monotone framework converges to unique fixed point
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY (dropped premise, and it makes the claim false): the Dragon Book's convergence result requires the semilattice to have FINITE HEIGHT (no infinite ascending chains); monotonicity alone does not give termination in finite steps. Counterexample: interval analysis over the lattice of integer intervals is a perfectly monotone framework of infinite height, and iterating from bottom on 'x = 0; while (true) x = x + 1' produces [0,0], [0,1], [0,2], ... and never converges - which is exactly why widening operators exist. Constant propagation is also a standard example of a framework whose transfer functions are monotone but not distributive, so the iterative solution is the MFP, which may be strictly below the meet-over-all-paths solution; the axiom's 'converges to unique fixed point' elides that MFP != MOP in general.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Two values interfere if they are simultaneously live. If the interference graph is k-colorable, all values fit in k registers with zero spills. SSA-form interference graphs are chordal (every cycle of length ≥4 has a chord), which means optimal coloring is possible in linear time via greedy coloring in simplicial elimination order.
source_document: Chaitin 1981/82; Hack et al. 2006; Appel Ch.11
tensor_equation: ∀program with register pressure k: χ(interference_graph) ≤ k ⇒ ∃ allocation with zero spills; SSA interference graphs are chordal ⇒ optimal in O(|V|+|E|)
title: Register allocation: Interference graph k-colorability — no spill if χ(G) ≤ k
verdict_evidence: Chaitin et al. (1981) / Chaitin (1982) established register allocation as graph coloring: a k-coloring of the interference graph is exactly an assignment of k registers where no two simultaneously-live values share a register, so chi(G) <= k implies a spill-free allocation exists. Hack, Grund & Goos (CC 2006, 'Register Allocation for Programs in SSA-Form') proved that the interference graph of a strict SSA program is chordal, hence optimally colorable in O(|V|+|E|) via a perfect elimination ordering — decoupling spilling from assignment. Appel, 'Modern Compiler Implementation', Ch. 11 is Register Allocation. Has teeth: a program whose interference graph is K_5 with k = 4 machine registers has chi = 5 > 4 and provably must spill; conversely Chaitin's NP-completeness result (every graph is some program's interference graph) is what makes the SSA chordality result non-trivial. Note the claim is existential ('exists allocation with zero spills'); real allocators may still spill due to pre-coloring, register classes and two-address constraints, which the axiom does not assert away.

prompt_injection: Instruction selection via tree pattern matching must cover every AST node exactly once. No gaps (uncovered nodes) and no overlaps (multiple patterns covering the same node). The optimal cover minimizes total cost. BURS (Bottom-Up Rewrite System) using dynamic programming finds the optimal cover in time linear in the number of tree nodes.
source_document: Dragon Book Ch.8; Muchnick Ch.7
tensor_equation: ∀AST node n in program: ∃ exactly one pattern p in selected cover such that p matches at n; total cost = Σ cost(p_i) is minimized
title: Instruction selection: Tree pattern matching covers every AST node exactly once
verdict_discrepancy: HARDENING plus MISCITATION. (1) Instruction selection in general does NOT minimise total cost: maximal munch, the standard greedy tiling, is only locally optimal, and optimality of the DP algorithm holds only under the uniform-register-machine assumptions Aho-Johnson state; on real irregular architectures optimal tiling is NP-hard. A maximal-munch selector produces a valid exactly-once cover with non-minimal Sigma cost(p_i), a direct counterexample to the conjunction as stated. (2) Muchnick Ch.7 is control-flow analysis; automatic code-generator generation by tree pattern matching is Muchnick Chapter 6.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: The binary-search-tree property must hold at every node after every insertion and deletion. For any node x: every key in the left subtree of x is at most key[x], and every key in the right subtree of x is at least key[x]. After any mutation (insert/delete/rotate), verify this invariant by performing an inorder traversal and asserting the sequence of keys is non-decreasing. A BST whose inorder traversal is not sorted is corrupted.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 12 (Binary Search Trees), Section 12.1
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ T: (∀y ∈ left_subtree(x): key[y] ≤ key[x]) ∧ (∀y ∈ right_subtree(x): key[y] ≥ key[x])
title: Binary Search Tree — search property invariant
verdict_evidence: CLRS (4th ed) Sec. 12.1 states the binary-search-tree property verbatim: 'Let x be a node in a binary search tree. If y is a node in the left subtree of x, then y.key <= x.key. If y is a node in the right subtree of x, then y.key >= x.key.' Quantifiers (over all y in the whole subtree, not just children) and the non-strict inequalities both match. Has teeth: a node with key 5 whose left subtree contains key 7 satisfies 'left child < parent' locally but violates the axiom, and INORDER-TREE-WALK on it returns an unsorted sequence.
verification: Perform an inorder traversal of the tree and collect keys into an array. Assert that for all i, keys[i] ≤ keys[i+1]. Alternatively, for every node x, recursively verify: max(left_subtree(x)) ≤ key[x] ≤ min(right_subtree(x)). Run this check after every insert, delete, and rotation in test suites. Time complexity: O(n) for full-tree verification.

prompt_injection: Red-black tree property 4: if a node is red, then both its children must be black. No red node may have a red parent or a red child. After every insertion (RB-INSERT-FIXUP) and deletion (RB-DELETE-FIXUP), walk the entire tree and assert that no red node has a red child. A single red-red adjacency anywhere in the tree means the fixup procedure failed and the tree is no longer a valid red-black tree.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 13 (Red-Black Trees), Section 13.1, Property 4
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ RBT: color[x] = RED ⟹ (color[left[x]] = BLACK ∧ color[right[x]] = BLACK)
title: Red-Black Tree — no adjacent red nodes (color invariant)
verdict_evidence: CLRS Section 13.1 lists five red-black properties; property 4 is 'If a node is red, then both its children are black.' The equation is a literal transcription. Falsifiable: root(BLACK) with left child RED whose own left child is RED satisfies BST order and even equal black-heights on some shapes but violates the equation; such a tree is rejected by RB-INSERT-FIXUP case analysis.
verification: Walk the tree recursively. For every node x: if color[x] = RED, assert color[left[x]] = BLACK and color[right[x]] = BLACK (treat NIL leaves as BLACK). This checks property 4 in isolation. Properties 1-3 (every node red/black, root black, leaves black) are trivially verified. Run after every insert and delete operation. Time: O(n).

prompt_injection: Red-black tree property 5: for each node, all simple paths from the node to descendant leaves must contain the same number of black nodes. This is the black-height invariant. After every insertion and deletion fixup, compute the black-height of the root by following one arbitrary path to a leaf (counting black nodes, excluding the root itself). Then verify that every other root-to-leaf path has exactly the same black count. Any discrepancy means the tree is unbalanced in black-height and is no longer a valid red-black tree.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 13 (Red-Black Trees), Section 13.1, Property 5
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ RBT: ∀p₁,p₂ ∈ simple_paths(x, leaf): black_count(p₁) = black_count(p₂). Define bh(x) = number of black nodes on any simple path from x to a leaf, not counting x.
title: Red-Black Tree — uniform black-height invariant
verdict_evidence: CLRS Chapter 13, Section 13.1 lists five red-black properties; property 5 reads 'For each node, all simple paths from the node to descendant leaves contain the same number of black nodes', and CLRS defines bh(x) as 'the number of black nodes on any simple path from, but not including, a node x down to a leaf' — matching the axiom's 'not counting x' exactly. Counterexample proving non-vacuity: root B with left child NIL (bh=1) and right child B whose children are NIL (bh=2) gives black_count(p1)=1 != black_count(p2)=2, so the tree is rejected. The equation therefore discriminates.
verification: Compute black-height at the root: follow one arbitrary path to a leaf, count black nodes excluding root. Then recursively verify at every node: the black-height of left child equals black-height of right child, and both equal bh(node) - (1 if node is BLACK else 0). Tolerate NIL leaves as BLACK with bh(NIL) = 0. Time: O(n). Run after every insert and delete.

prompt_injection: A B-tree with minimum degree t enforces occupancy bounds on every node. Every node except the root must contain between t-1 and 2t-1 keys. The root must contain between 1 and 2t-1 keys (unless the tree is empty). Every internal node with k keys must have exactly k+1 children. These bounds ensure the tree remains balanced and that disk-page utilization never falls below 50%. After every insert, delete, or split/merge operation, walk the entire tree and verify every node's key count is within its allowed range. A node with fewer than t-1 keys (non-root) or more than 2t-1 keys means the split/merge logic is broken.
tensor_equation: ∀node ∈ BTree with min degree t ≥ 2: (¬root(node) ∧ ¬leaf(node) ⟹ t-1 ≤ nkeys(node) ≤ 2t-1) ∧ (root(node) ∧ tree_nonempty ⟹ 1 ≤ nkeys(root) ≤ 2t-1) ∧ (leaf(node) ∧ ¬root(node) ⟹ t-1 ≤ nkeys(leaf) ≤ 2t-1). A node with k keys has exactly k+1 children (or is a leaf).
title: B-Tree — node occupancy invariant (key count bounds)
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed Chapter 18 is 'B-Trees' with 18.1 'Definition of B-trees' (confirmed: 4th ed TOC lists 18.1 Definition of B-trees, 18.2 Basic operations on B-trees, 18.3 Deleting a key from a B-tree). 18.1 states: every node other than the root must have at least t-1 keys, every node may contain at most 2t-1 keys, the root must have at least one key if the tree is nonempty, and an internal node with k keys has k+1 children. Falsifiable: a min-degree-3 B-tree with an internal non-root node holding 1 key violates t-1=2 <= nkeys; a node with 3 keys and 3 children violates the k+1 child count.
verification: Walk the tree recursively. For each node: assert nkeys ≥ 1 if root and tree nonempty; assert nkeys ≥ t-1 if not root; assert nkeys ≤ 2t-1 for all nodes. Also assert nchildren = nkeys + 1 for internal nodes, and nchildren = 0 for leaves. Run after every insert, delete, split, and merge. Time: O(n).

prompt_injection: All leaves in a B-tree must appear at exactly the same depth. When the tree grows (root split) or shrinks (root merge), the depth of every leaf changes by exactly 1, uniformly. After any structural modification (insert, delete, split, merge, redistribute), traverse every root-to-leaf path and verify that all leaves are at the same level. If two leaves have different depths, the tree is structurally corrupted and search correctness is compromised.
tensor_equation: ∀leaf₁, leaf₂ ∈ BTree: depth(leaf₁) = depth(leaf₂). All leaves appear at exactly the same depth.
title: B-Tree — uniform leaf depth invariant
verdict_evidence: CLRS (4th ed) Ch. 18 'B-Trees', Sec. 18.1 property 2/3: 'All leaves have the same depth, which is the tree's height h.' Counterexample with teeth: a 2-3 tree where an insertion is performed by hanging a new node off a leaf instead of splitting upward yields leaf1 at depth 2 and leaf2 at depth 3, breaking the O(log n) search bound. Chapter number for the 4th edition (18) is correct; it was Ch. 18 in the 3rd ed too.
verification: Perform a DFS or BFS traversal. Record the depth of the first leaf encountered. Assert that every subsequent leaf encountered has exactly the same depth. Alternatively, verify that for every node: all children are at the same height (i.e., the tree is perfectly balanced in terms of leaf depth). Time: O(n). Run after every insert and delete.

prompt_injection: An open-addressed hash table cannot exceed load factor α = 1, since each slot holds at most one element. In practice, performance degrades sharply as α approaches 1 (expected probes ~ 1/(1-α)). Resize must be triggered when α exceeds a threshold, typically 0.5 to 0.75. The insert operation must reject or resize before α reaches 1. After every insert and delete, check α = n/m; assert α ≤ 1 always, and if α > α_max, trigger rehash. Tombstone/deleted entries must be counted toward the load or cleaned up via rehashing.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 11 (Hash Tables), Sections 11.4 (open addressing)
tensor_equation: α = n/m where n = |stored elements|, m = |slots|. For open addressing: α ≤ 1 (hard bound). CLRS gives asymptotics (1/(1-α), 1/α*ln(1/(1-α))) and never prescribes specific α_max values. Expected probes: ≤ 1/(1-α) for unsuccessful search.
title: Hash Table — load factor bound for open addressing
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY/ATTRIBUTION: the clause 'alpha_max in [0.5, 0.75] with resizing when alpha exceeds threshold' is NOT stated by CLRS. CLRS gives asymptotics (1/(1-alpha), 1/alpha*ln(1/(1-alpha))) and never prescribes a numeric maximum load factor for open addressing. The 0.75 figure is implementation folklore (java.util.HashMap DEFAULT_LOAD_FACTOR), 0.5 is a quadratic-probing rule of thumb. An engineering heuristic has been laundered into a textbook citation.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; corrected threshold/bound
verification: After every insert and delete: assert α ≤ 1.0 (hard bound). Assert α ≤ α_max for the configured threshold (0.75 recommended). Verify that insert fails or triggers resize when table is full. Measure probe-sequence lengths; under uniform hashing, average unsuccessful probe count should approximate 1/(1-α). Run with random keys and verify that cluster lengths do not grow superlinearly. Time: O(1) for the load factor check.

prompt_injection: In a max-heap stored in array A with 1-based indexing, the max-heap property requires that for every node i (i > 1), the parent's key is greater than or equal to the child's key: A[⌊i/2⌋] ≥ A[i]. After every heap operation (MAX-HEAPIFY, HEAP-EXTRACT-MAX, HEAP-INCREASE-KEY, HEAP-INSERT), verify this invariant by iterating i from 2 to heap_size and asserting A[⌊i/2⌋] ≥ A[i]. If the invariant is violated at any index, the heap is corrupted and subsequent EXTRACT-MAX may return a non-maximum value.
tensor_equation: ∀i ∈ {2, 3, ..., heap_size}: A[parent(i)] ≥ A[i], where parent(i) = ⌊i/2⌋
title: Binary Heap — max-heap property
verdict_evidence: CLRS Ch. 6 (Heapsort) Sec. 6.1: 'A[PARENT(i)] >= A[i]' for all nodes other than the root, with PARENT(i) = floor(i/2) in the 1-indexed array representation. The index range {2..heap_size} correctly excludes the root, which has no parent. Has teeth: A = [3, 9, 4] violates it at i=2 (A[1]=3 < A[2]=9), and MAX-HEAPIFY is exactly the repair operation for that violation.
verification: Iterate i from 2 to heap_size: assert A[i/2] ≥ A[i] (integer division). For 0-based arrays: foreach i in [1, heap_size): assert A[(i-1)/2] ≥ A[i]. Time: O(n). Run after every heap-mutating operation in tests. For min-heaps, reverse the inequality: A[parent(i)] ≤ A[i].

prompt_injection: In a min-heap stored in array A with 1-based indexing, the min-heap property requires that for every node i (i > 1), the parent's key is less than or equal to the child's key: A[⌊i/2⌋] ≤ A[i]. This is the dual of the max-heap property. After every heap operation (MIN-HEAPIFY, HEAP-EXTRACT-MIN, HEAP-DECREASE-KEY, HEAP-INSERT), verify this invariant by iterating i from 2 to heap_size and asserting A[⌊i/2⌋] ≤ A[i]. Violation means the minimum element may not be at the root.
tensor_equation: ∀i ∈ {2, 3, ..., heap_size}: A[parent(i)] ≤ A[i], where parent(i) = ⌊i/2⌋
title: Binary Heap — min-heap property
verdict_evidence: CLRS Section 6.1 states the min-heap property as A[PARENT(i)] <= A[i] for all i from 2 to A.heap-size, with 1-origin indexing and PARENT(i) = floor(i/2). Transcription is exact including the index range and the bound direction. Falsifiable: A = [5, 3, 7] has A[1]=5 > A[2]=3, violating the equation; MIN-HEAPIFY exists precisely to restore it.
verification: Iterate i from 2 to heap_size: assert A[i/2] ≤ A[i] (integer division). For 0-based arrays: foreach i in [1, heap_size): assert A[(i-1)/2] ≤ A[i]. Time: O(n). Run after every heap-mutating operation in tests.

prompt_injection: An AVL tree maintains the invariant that for every node, the heights of its left and right subtrees differ by at most 1. The balance factor BF(x) = height(right(x)) - height(left(x)) must be in {-1, 0, 1}. After every insert and delete, recompute heights bottom-up and verify that |BF(x)| ≤ 1 for every node x. If |BF(x)| = 2, a rotation (single or double) must be applied immediately to restore balance. A node with |BF| ≥ 2 means the tree is no longer an AVL tree and search worst-case O(log n) guarantee is lost.
source: Adelson-Velsky and Landis (1962). Not in CLRS main text; appears as Problems in Chapter 12 (Binary Search Trees) in some editions.
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ AVL: BF(x) = height(right(x)) - height(left(x)) ∈ {-1, 0, 1}
title: AVL Tree — balance factor invariant
verdict_evidence: The AVL balance condition |h(left)-h(right)| <= 1 is exactly what Adelson-Velsky and Landis define in their 1962 Doklady paper 'An algorithm for the organization of information', and it is the definition CLRS reproduces in its AVL-tree problem. Falsifiable: a chain root->left->left->left (heights left=3, right=0) gives BF=-3, violating the equation, so the axiom has teeth. Note on location: in CLRS 3rd and 4th editions AVL trees appear as Problem 13-3, i.e. in Chapter 13 (Red-Black Trees), not in Chapter 12 (Binary Search Trees) as the claimed_source hedges; the author attribution is correct.
verification: Post-order traversal: compute height of each subtree, verify BF = height(right) - height(left) ∈ {-1, 0, 1} for every node. Cache computed heights to avoid O(n²) time. Total verification: O(n). Run after every insert and delete. When |BF| ≥ 2, assert that a rotation was applied and |BF| is restored to ≤ 1.

prompt_injection: A Fibonacci heap maintains the min-heap property: for every node x, the key of each child of x is greater than or equal to key[x]. After every operation (FIB-HEAP-INSERT, FIB-HEAP-UNION, FIB-HEAP-EXTRACT-MIN, FIB-HEAP-DECREASE-KEY, FIB-HEAP-DELETE), traverse all trees in the root list and all children recursively, asserting key[child] ≥ key[parent] at every parent-child edge. Additionally, verify that the heap's min pointer references a root with the actual minimum key among all roots.
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ FibHeap: ∀c ∈ children(x): key[c] ≥ key[x]. The minimum key in the heap is at some root in the root list.
title: Fibonacci Heap — min-heap property
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed Chapter 19 is Fibonacci Heaps; Section 19.1 defines a Fibonacci heap as a collection of min-heap-ordered trees (key[parent] <= key[child]) with H.min pointing at a root holding the minimum key. Falsifiable: a root list containing a tree whose child key is smaller than its parent's, or an H.min pointer to a non-minimum root, violates it - exactly the state FIB-HEAP-DECREASE-KEY's cut/cascading-cut repairs.
verification: Recursively traverse all trees starting from the root list. For every node x: for each child c in children[x], assert key[c] ≥ key[x]. Also verify: there exists a root r in the root list such that key[r] = min{key[n] : n in heap} and heap.min = r. Time: O(n). Run after every heap operation.

prompt_injection: A Fibonacci heap node of degree d must have a subtree of size at least F_{d+2} (the (d+2)-nd Fibonacci number). This implies that the maximum degree of any node in an n-node Fibonacci heap is at most ⌊log_φ n⌋ ≈ 1.44 log₂ n. The invariant is maintained by the 'at most one child cut' rule: when a second child is cut from a non-root node, the node itself is cascading-cut from its parent. Verify after EXTRACT-MIN (which consolidates trees) that no node's degree exceeds ⌊log_φ n⌋ and that for every node, size(subtree) ≥ F_{degree+2}.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 19 (Fibonacci Heaps), Section 19.3 (Bounding the Maximum Degree)
tensor_equation: ∀x ∈ FibHeap: degree[x] ≤ D(n) ≤ ⌊log_φ n⌋, where φ = (1+√5)/2 ≈ 1.618 and n = |nodes in heap|. (CLRS 4th ed Chapter 19 is Disjoint Sets; Fibonacci heaps are Chapter 19 in 3rd ed, Chapter 20 in 4th ed.)
title: Fibonacci Heap — degree bound invariant
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION: the citation names 'CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 19 (Fibonacci Heaps), Section 19.3'. In the 4th edition Chapter 19 is 'Data Structures for Disjoint Sets' (19.1 Disjoint-set operations ... 19.4 Analysis of union by rank with path compression); the Fibonacci-heap and van Emde Boas chapters were dropped from the 4th edition entirely. Even in the 3rd edition the degree bound is 19.4, not 19.3 (19.3 is 'Decreasing a key and deleting a node'). Both the edition and the section are wrong.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: After each EXTRACT-MIN: compute n = total nodes, compute max_allowed_degree = floor(log_φ(n)). Traverse all nodes; assert degree[x] ≤ max_allowed_degree for every x. Also verify the stronger invariant: for every node, compute actual subtree size; assert size ≥ Fib(degree + 2) where Fib is the Fibonacci sequence. This catches cases where the cut rule failed. Time: O(n).

prompt_injection: Fibonacci heap amortized analysis uses the potential function Φ = t + 2m, where t is the number of trees in the root list and m is the number of marked nodes. After every operation, compute Φ and verify that the change in potential ΔΦ, when added to the actual work performed, matches the stated amortized bounds: INSERT = O(1), EXTRACT-MIN = O(lg n), DECREASE-KEY = O(1), DELETE = O(lg n). Marked nodes must be counted correctly: a node becomes marked when it loses a child while not a root, and becomes unmarked when it becomes a root. After each operation, recompute t, m, and Φ from scratch and assert consistency with incremental tracking.
source: CLRS 4th ed, Chapter 19 (Fibonacci Heaps), Section 19.2 (Potential Function)
tensor_equation: Φ(H) = t(H) + 2·m(H), where t(H) = number of trees in root list, m(H) = number of marked nodes. For any operation with actual cost c, the amortized cost ĉ = c + ΔΦ ≤ amortized bound in CLRS Table 19.1.
title: Fibonacci Heap — potential function invariant
verdict_evidence: CLRS Fibonacci Heaps chapter defines the potential function verbatim as Phi(H) = t(H) + 2 m(H) with t(H) the number of trees in the root list and m(H) the number of marked nodes, and uses amortized cost = actual cost + Phi(H_i) - Phi(H_{i-1}). Counterexample showing teeth: a CASCADING-CUT chain that cuts k marked nodes has actual cost Theta(k) and DeltaPhi = k - 2(k-1) = 2 - k, so the amortized bound only holds because of the 2*m term; setting the coefficient to 1 instead of 2 makes DECREASE-KEY's O(1) amortized bound fail. Attribution and math both check out.
verification: After every operation: (1) Count t = |root list|, assert t matches a separately maintained counter. (2) Traverse all nodes, count m = |{n : marked[n] = true ∧ n is not root}|. (3) Compute Φ = t + 2m. (4) Verify marked-node semantics: a root is never marked; a node becomes marked when it loses a first child; a node is unmarked when it becomes a root or when a second child is cut (cascading cut). (5) For DECREASE-KEY: verify ΔΦ = (new_t - old_t) + 2(new_m - old_m) yields amortized O(1). Time: O(t + n) for full verification.

prompt_injection: When implementing or modifying binary tree traversal, verify that inorder traversal of any BST produces keys in non-decreasing order. The recursive definition L-N-R (left subtree, node, right subtree) must be preserved exactly. Any traversal that skips or reorders subtrees violates the fundamental BST property that left subtree keys < node key < right subtree keys.
source: TAOCP Vol 1, Section 2.3.1 (Traversing Binary Trees); TAOCP Vol 3, Section 6.2.2 (Binary Search Trees)
tensor_equation: ∀ BST T: inorder(T) = sorted_ascending(keys(T))
title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal Yields Sorted Order for BST
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 1 Section 2.3.1 defines symmetric (inorder) traversal of binary trees; TAOCP Vol 3 Section 6.2.2 (Binary Tree Searching) states the defining property that keys in the left subtree are less than the node key and keys in the right subtree greater, so symmetric-order traversal visits keys in ascending order - Knuth uses exactly this to note that a binary search tree can be sorted by symmetric traversal. Falsifiable: the tree root=5, left=7 is a valid binary tree but not a BST; inorder yields 7,5 which is not ascending, so the equation discriminates BSTs from arbitrary binary trees (and a buggy insert that puts a larger key in the left subtree is caught by it).
verification: Insert N random keys into a BST. Perform inorder traversal and collect keys. Assert that the collected sequence is strictly non-decreasing. Repeat with N=0 (empty tree), N=1, N=1000. Also test with duplicate keys if the BST variant permits them.

prompt_injection: Preorder traversal (N-L-R) requires that every node is visited before any node in its subtrees. This is the defining property that makes preorder a topological ordering. Verify: for every parent-child pair in the tree, the parent's position in the preorder output is strictly less than the child's position.
tensor_equation: ∀ node n ∈ T: pos(preorder, n) < min(pos(preorder, c) | c ∈ children(n))
title: Binary Tree Preorder Traversal Visits Parent Before Children
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol 1, Section 2.3.1 'Traversing Binary Trees' defines preorder as: visit the root, traverse the left subtree in preorder, traverse the right subtree in preorder. By that definition the root is emitted before any node of either subtree, which is the stated positional inequality. Falsifiable: the sequence produced by inorder on a root with a left child places the child before the root, violating pos(root) < min pos(children) - so the equation genuinely discriminates preorder from the other two orders Knuth defines in the same section.
verification: Build a tree with known structure. Run preorder traversal and record positions. For each non-root node, assert that its parent's position in the output is strictly less than its own. Verify the relationship holds for all three depth-first traversals: preorder (parent first), inorder (parent between left and right), postorder (parent last).

prompt_injection: Postorder traversal (L-R-N) requires that both subtrees are fully processed before the parent node is visited. This invariant is essential for safe deallocation (free children before parent) and for evaluating expression trees to postfix notation. Verify that for every node with children, its output position is strictly greater than all its children's positions.
tensor_equation: ∀ node n ∈ T: pos(postorder, n) > max(pos(postorder, c) | c ∈ children(n))
title: Binary Tree Postorder Traversal Visits Children Before Parent
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 1 Sec. 2.3.1 ('Traversing Binary Trees') defines the three orders; postorder is 'traverse the left subtree, traverse the right subtree, visit the root', so the root is visited strictly after every node of both subtrees, which is exactly pos(n) > max over children (vacuously true at leaves, where the max is over the empty set). Counterexample showing teeth: preorder on the same tree gives pos(root) = 1 < pos(child), so the equation discriminates between traversal orders rather than holding for all of them; an iterative 'postorder' that pops a parent before its right child is rejected by it.
verification: Build a tree with known structure. Run postorder traversal and record positions. For each internal node, assert that its position is greater than all children's positions. As a stronger check, generate all three traversals from the same tree and verify no node is missed: |preorder| = |inorder| = |postorder| = |nodes(T)|.

prompt_injection: An AVL tree requires that the balance factor of every node — defined as height(right_subtree) - height(left_subtree) — lies in {-1, 0, 1}. After every insertion or deletion, recompute balance factors along the path to the root. If any node's |BF| ≥ 2, trigger the appropriate rotation (LL, RR, LR, RL). The tree is not an AVL tree until this condition holds globally. The height bound h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) must also hold after every operation.
tensor_equation: ∀ node n ∈ T: BF(n) = height(right(n)) - height(left(n)) ∈ {-1, 0, 1}
title: AVL Tree Balance Factor Invariant
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.2.3 'Balanced Trees' (pp. 458ff in the 2nd ed) defines, for each node of an AVL (Adelson-Velsky/Landis) tree, the balance factor B(N) = (height of right subtree) - (height of left subtree), and requires B(N) in {-1, 0, +1} for every node. The axiom's sign convention (right minus left) matches Knuth's exactly. Has teeth: inserting 1, 2, 3 in order without rebalancing gives the root BF = +2, which is precisely the case Knuth's Algorithm A handles by a single left rotation; the bound is what yields height <= 1.4404 lg(n+2).
verification: Insert and delete sequences of random keys. After each operation, walk every node, compute height of left and right subtrees recursively, compute BF = right_height - left_height, and assert BF ∈ {-1, 0, 1}. Also assert that tree height h satisfies h ≤ 1.44 × log₂(n+2). Test with sorted insertion (worst case for unbalanced trees) to verify rotations prevent degeneration.

prompt_injection: Knuth proved that an AVL tree of height h contains at least F_{h+2} - 1 nodes. This gives the worst-case height bound of approximately 1.44 log₂(n+2). If your AVL tree has height exceeding this bound for its node count, the balance factor invariant has been violated somewhere. Use this as a fast sanity check before the more expensive per-node balance factor walk.
tensor_equation: ∀ AVL tree T of height h with n nodes: n ≥ F_{h+2} - 1 ∧ h ≤ 1.44 log₂(n+2) + O(1), where F_k is the Fibonacci sequence with F_1 = F_2 = 1
title: AVL Tree Height Bound via Fibonacci Numbers
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3 Section 6.2.3 (Balanced Trees) proves that a balanced (AVL) tree of height h has at least F_{h+2} - 1 internal nodes, and derives the height bound h <= 1.4404 lg(N+2) - 0.328, i.e. h <= 1.44 log2(n+2) + O(1). The axiom reproduces both the Fibonacci node-count lemma and the constant 1.44. Falsifiable: a 4-node tree of height 4 (a right spine) has n = 4 < F_6 - 1 = 7, violating it - which is exactly the unbalanced case AVL rotations exclude.
verification: After any sequence of insertions/deletions, measure tree height h and node count n. Compute the minimum node count for height h: min_nodes = fib(h+2) - 1. Assert n ≥ min_nodes. Equivalently, assert h ≤ ceil(log_φ((n+2)/√5 + 1/2) - 1) where φ ≈ 1.618.

prompt_injection: Linear probing places each key in the first empty slot starting from its hash position. The invariant: for any occupied slot i containing key k, every slot between h(k) and i-1 must also be occupied. If a search encounters an empty slot before finding the key, the key is not in the table. When deleting, you must either use tombstones (lazy deletion) or rehash the run after the deleted slot to close the gap. A gap in an occupancy chain silently makes some keys unreachable — this is a data corruption bug.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 6.4 (Hashing), Knuth's 1963 linear probing analysis
tensor_equation: ∀ key k in table T at index i: ∀j ∈ [h(k), i-1]: T[j] is occupied (not empty and not tombstone)
title: Linear Probing Occupancy Chain Invariant (No Gaps)
verdict_evidence: Knuth, TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.4 (Hashing), Algorithm L (linear probing) and its analysis — Knuth's 1963 note analyzing linear probing is the origin of the load-factor formulas quoted there. Algorithm L's correctness rests exactly on this invariant: the search for k stops at the first empty slot, so if k is stored at i, every slot from h(k) up to i-1 (modulo M) must be occupied. Has teeth: deleting a record by simply blanking its slot creates a gap and makes any key whose probe chain crossed that slot unfindable — which is why Knuth gives Algorithm R, which repairs the invariant by moving records back rather than leaving a hole. The axiom is faithful to Knuth's no-tombstone model; note that lazy-deletion implementations weaken it (a tombstone in the chain is permissible there), and the index interval must be read modulo the table size.
verification: After every operation, for each occupied slot i: let h = hash(key_at_i) % table_size. Walk from h to i-1 (wrapping if needed). Assert no empty slot is encountered. Also verify the converse: if a search starting at h(key) encounters an empty slot before finding the key, assert the key is not in the table. Test with many random insert/delete sequences.

prompt_injection: Linear probing performance degrades as O(1/(1-α)²) when the load factor α approaches 1. Maintain α below a fixed threshold (commonly 0.5). When α exceeds the threshold, allocate a larger table (typically 2×) and rehash all entries. Tombstones count toward α for load-factor purposes — failing to account for tombstones causes silent performance degradation. Knuth's analysis shows that primary clustering causes runs of occupied slots to grow as Θ(1/(1-α)²).
tensor_equation: ∀t: α(t) = occupied_slots(t) / table_size(t) < threshold, where threshold ∈ (0, 1); Knuth showed expected probe count = O(1/(1-α)²) for unsuccessful search
title: Linear Probing Load Factor Bound
verdict_discrepancy: ATTRIBUTION/FIDELITY of the first clause: 'alpha(t) < threshold, where threshold in (0,1)' is an unbound existential - for any alpha < 1 some threshold in (0,1) satisfies it, so the clause carries almost no discriminating power, and Knuth states no normative load-factor limit anywhere in 6.4; he derives asymptotics and leaves the operating point to the implementer. The axiom fuses a genuine Knuth result with an unattributed engineering rule and cites both to Section 6.4.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error
verification: Track occupied slots (including tombstones) and table size. After every insertion, assert load_factor < threshold. Measure the average probe length for successful and unsuccessful searches; assert it does not exceed the theoretical bound (approximately 0.5 × (1 + 1/(1-α)²) for unsuccessful searches). Trigger a rehash when the threshold is crossed and verify the new table satisfies all invariants.

prompt_injection: Knuth defined a B-tree of order m with five properties: (1) every node has at most m children, (2) every non-root non-leaf node has at least ⌈m/2⌉ children, (3) the root has at least 2 children unless it is a leaf, (4) all leaves appear on the same level, (5) a non-leaf node with k children contains k-1 keys. The keys in each node are ordered and serve as separation values: for keys a₁...a_{k-1}, all values in child i are less than aᵢ, all values in child i+1 are greater than aᵢ. Insertion and deletion must maintain all five properties — split when a node overflows (hits m children), merge or redistribute when a node underflows (below ⌈m/2⌉ children).
tensor_equation: ∀ internal node N with k children (k ∈ [⌈m/2⌉, m] for non-root, k ∈ [2, m] for root): N has k-1 keys a₁ < a₂ < ... < a_{k-1} ∧ ∀ subtree Tᵢ rooted at child i: ∀x ∈ T₁: x < a₁, ∀x ∈ Tᵢ (1 < i < k): a_{i-1} < x < aᵢ, ∀x ∈ T_k: x > a_{k-1}
title: B-Tree (Multiway Tree) Ordering and Occupancy Invariant
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3 Section 6.2.4 is 'Multiway Trees', where Knuth presents Bayer and McCreight's B-tree of order m: every node has at most m children, every node except the root and leaves has at least ceil(m/2) children, the root has at least 2 children unless it is a leaf, and a node with k children holds k-1 keys separating the subtrees. The axiom transcribes both the occupancy bounds and the ordering. Falsifiable on both halves: an order-5 non-root internal node with 2 children violates the ceil(m/2)=3 floor; a node with keys [10,20] whose middle subtree contains 25 violates a_{i-1} < x < a_i.
verification: After every operation, walk the entire tree. For each node: (1) assert children count ∈ [⌈m/2⌉, m] (or [2, m] for root), (2) assert keys count = children_count - 1 (or 0 for leaves), (3) assert keys are strictly increasing, (4) for each key aᵢ, assert all values in left child are < aᵢ and all values in right child are > aᵢ, (5) assert all leaves are at the same depth. Insert sorted keys to test splitting, delete keys to test merging.

prompt_injection: All leaves in a B-tree must be at the exact same depth. This is the invariant that guarantees O(log n) worst-case access time. Insertion grows the tree upward (by splitting the root) rather than downward. Deletion may shrink the tree upward (by merging into a new root). If you ever find leaves at different depths, the tree structure is corrupted. This invariant is tested independently because violations are catastrophic — some keys become unreachable or search terminates prematurely.
tensor_equation: ∀ leaves L₁, L₂ in B-tree T: depth(L₁) = depth(L₂)
title: B-Tree Leaf-Level Uniform Depth Invariant
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 6.2.4 is 'Multiway Trees', the section in which Knuth presents Bayer & McCreight's B-trees, and the defining property list includes that all leaves appear on the same level (carrying no information), alongside the at-most-m-children, at-least-ceil(m/2)-children-for-non-root, root-has->=2-children, and k-children-implies-k-1-keys conditions. Counterexample showing teeth: growing a B-tree by attaching a new child below one leaf (as an unbalanced multiway search tree would) makes depth(L1) != depth(L2) and destroys the uniform log_m N probe bound; the property is precisely what forces B-trees to grow at the root rather than at the leaves.
verification: Walk the tree and record the depth of every leaf. Assert all recorded depths are identical. Also verify that the path length from root to any leaf is the same (this is equivalent). After bulk insertions and deletions, recheck. If using on-disk B-trees, check after crash recovery.

prompt_injection: A binary heap must be a complete binary tree: all levels except possibly the last are completely full, and nodes on the last level are filled from left to right. When stored in an array starting at index 1 (Knuth's convention), this gives the parent/child index formulas: parent = ⌊i/2⌋, left_child = 2i, right_child = 2i+1. An array with gaps (null entries between valid elements) violates the shape property. Insertion always appends at the end and sifts up; deletion removes the root, replaces it with the last element, and sifts down.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 (Sorting by Selection), Heapsort
tensor_equation: ∀ levels ℓ ∈ [0, h-1] of heap H: |nodes_at_level(ℓ)| = 2^ℓ ∧ |nodes_at_level(h)| ≤ 2^h ∧ all nodes at level h are as far left as possible
title: Binary Heap Shape Property (Complete Binary Tree)
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 'Sorting by Selection' introduces heapsort over an array with the implicit tree structure in which node j has children 2j and 2j+1; that array embedding is exactly the complete-binary-tree shape the axiom states (every level but the last full, last level filled left to right). Falsifiable: an array-backed heap with a hole at index 4 while index 5 is occupied breaks 'as far left as possible' and breaks the 2j/2j+1 addressing that heapsort's siftup depends on. Caveat: Knuth states the shape implicitly through the array indexing rather than as a named property, and his heap is a max-heap (K_{floor(j/2)} >= K_j); the axiom asserts only shape, which is orientation-independent.
verification: For a heap stored at indices [1..n]: verify that for every index i ≤ n/2, child indices 2i and 2i+1 (if ≤ n) contain valid elements. Verify there are no gaps: for each i from 1 to n, heap[i] exists. Verify the last level's fill: the number of nodes at level h must be between 1 and 2^h, and they occupy the leftmost positions.

prompt_injection: In a max-heap, every node's key is greater than or equal to its children's keys. This means the root holds the maximum element. For a min-heap, the inequality is reversed. This property must hold for every non-root node: key[parent(i)] ≥ key[i] (max-heap) or key[parent(i)] ≤ key[i] (min-heap). After insert (sift-up) or delete-min/max (sift-down), verify the property along the modified path, then verify globally. A single violation means extract-min/max would return the wrong element.
source: TAOCP Vol 3, Section 5.2.3 (Sorting by Selection), Heapsort
tensor_equation: ∀ node i ∈ heap H (array representation, 1-indexed): H[i] ≥ H[⌊i/2⌋] for min-heap, or H[i] ≤ H[⌊i/2⌋] for max-heap
title: Binary Heap Order Property (Heap Condition)
verdict_evidence: TAOCP Vol. 3 Sec. 5.2.3 ('Sorting by Selection') introduces heapsort and defines a heap on the array representation by the condition K_floor(j/2) >= K_j for 1 < floor(j/2) < j <= N, i.e. the parent key dominates the child key, which is the axiom's max-heap form H[i] <= H[floor(i/2)]; the largest key therefore sits at K_1.
