The DRBG must be reseeded before the reseed interval (typically 2^48 requests or 2^24 requests for AES-CTR DRBG). A DRBG fed from insufficient entropy is a catastrophic failure — it produces predictable output.
source_document: NIST SP 800-90A Rev. 1 (Recommendation for Random Number Generation Using Deterministic Random Bit Generators), Sections 8-10.
tensor_equation: ∀ DRBG instantiation: entropy_input has ≥ min_entropy bits (≥ 128 for 128-bit security strength). DRBG is instantiated with entropy_input || nonce || personalization_string. Seed must be refreshed before reseed_interval.
title: NIST SP 800-90A: DRBG must be seeded from an approved entropy source
verdict_evidence: SP 800-90A Rev. 1 Sections 8-10 are exactly the relevant material (8 General Concepts and Considerations, 9 DRBG Mechanism Functions, 10 DRBG Algorithm Specifications). 90A requires that entropy input for instantiation contain at least security_strength bits of entropy (128 bits for a 128-bit strength instantiation), that the Instantiate function take entropy_input, a nonce and an optional personalization string as seed material, and that a reseed occur before the reseed_interval is exceeded (each mechanism specifies its reseed_interval, e.g. 2^48 for CTR_DRBG). Falsifiable: seeding an AES-256 CTR_DRBG from a 32-bit timestamp, or generating past reseed_counter > reseed_interval without reseeding, violates it.

prompt_injection: Key sizes must match the required security strength. Minimum symmetric: 128 bits (AES-128). Minimum asymmetric: RSA-2048 or ECDSA/ECDH P-256. For long-term secrets (> 2030), use AES-256 and ECDSA/ECDH P-384 or Ed25519. RSA-1024, DSA-1024, and ECDSA P-192 are broken or deprecated and must not be used. Check NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Table 2 for the current approved key strength-to-security-bits mapping.
source_document: NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 (Recommendation for Key Management: General), Section 5.6 "Guidance for Cryptographic Algorithm and Key Size Selection".
tensor_equation: ∀ key k: security_strength(k) ≥ required_strength for the protected data lifetime. Symmetric: AES-128 ≥ 128 bits (through 2030+), AES-256 ≥ 256 bits. Asymmetric: RSA-2048 ≥ 112 bits (through 2030), ECDSA P-256 ≥ 128 bits.
title: NIST SP 800-57: Key strength and algorithm lifetime
verdict_evidence: Checked directly against the local cached source axioms/nist-sp800-57-source.txt. Sec. 5.6 is titled 'Guidance for Cryptographic Algorithm and Key-Size Selection' exactly as cited (TOC line 318, body line 3149). Table 4 'Security strength time frames' (line ~3554) has columns 'Through 2030' and '2031 and Beyond': strength < 112 is Disallowed for applying protection; 112 is Acceptable through 2030 and Disallowed for applying protection 2031 and beyond; 128/192/256 are Acceptable in both columns. Table 2 of the same section assigns RSA-2048 and DH-2048 a security strength of 112 bits, AES-128 128 bits, ECC with 256-bit key (P-256) 128 bits, AES-256 256 bits — matching every number in the axiom. Has teeth: an RSA-1024 signing key (80-bit strength) protecting data past 2030 violates it and is Disallowed by Table 4.

prompt_injection: Protocol version negotiation must be authenticated. After negotiating a version, include the negotiated version number in the key derivation or MAC computation so both sides cryptographically commit to it. If an attacker strips higher version numbers from the negotiation messages, the MAC will not verify and the connection must abort. Never fall back to a lower version silently — that is a downgrade attack vector. TLS 1.3 does this correctly with the supported_versions extension in ServerHello.
source_document: Anderson, Ross. "Security Engineering" (3rd ed, 2020), Chapter 4: "Protocols", Section on version rollback and downgrade attacks
tensor_equation: ∀ protocol version negotiation: client offers a version list; server selects the highest mutually supported version. In SSH, each side sends its version string and the lower is used (not both compute min).
title: No downgrade: protocol negotiation must resist version rollback
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: real negotiation is not 'both compute min(client_max, server_max)'. In TLS the client offers a version/version-list and the *server selects*; in SSH each side sends its version string and the client chooses. Rollback protection in TLS 1.2 comes from the Finished MAC over the whole handshake transcript, and in TLS 1.3 from a downgrade sentinel planted in the last 8 bytes of ServerHello.random - neither is 'both compute min'. The axiom hardens a design goal into a specific incorrect algorithm and attributes that algorithm to Anderson, who states the goal, not the formula.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every cryptographic operation must return a single, unified error for all failure modes. Never distinguish between: 'invalid padding', 'invalid MAC', 'invalid key', 'wrong algorithm', 'decryption failed', or any other cryptographic failure reason. If an operation can fail for multiple cryptographic reasons, log the detailed reason internally (server-side only, never sent to client), but return only one generic 'operation failed' error to the caller. The adversary must not be given a cryptographic oracle.
source_document: Anderson, Ross. "Security Engineering" (3rd ed, 2020), Chapter 4: "Protocols", Section on oracles and side channels. Katz & Lindell, Chapter 5 on CCA security.
tensor_equation: ∀ cryptographic operation with possible failure modes {F1, F2, ..., Fn}: error_response(F_i) = error_response(F_j) for all i, j. No timing difference, no distinct error codes, no distinct messages. The adversary learns only 'operation failed'.
title: No cryptographic oracle: error messages must not distinguish failure causes
verdict_discrepancy: Hardening plus scope error. The literature's requirement is scoped to decryption/unwrapping failures, where distinguishing padding validity from authenticity leaks a plaintext bit; neither Anderson nor Katz & Lindell states the universal '∀ cryptographic operation with possible failure modes: error_response(F_i) = error_response(F_j) for all i,j'. Katz & Lindell's actual requirement is CCA-security / authenticated encryption (reject invalid ciphertexts as a single event), not a claim about error-message design in general. Counterexamples inside standard practice: X.509 path validation deliberately returns distinct causes (expired vs untrusted issuer vs hostname mismatch) and TLS defines distinct alerts (bad_certificate, certificate_expired, unsupported_extension); none of this is treated as an oracle vulnerability by the cited authors. As written the axiom also forbids distinguishing 'key not found' from 'signature invalid' in a signature-verification API, which no source requires.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every key has exactly one purpose and a defined cryptoperiod. Document the purpose and lifetime of every key in the system. Automate key rotation at the end of the cryptoperiod. Keys that exceed their cryptoperiod must be replaced, not extended. The cryptoperiod depends on the key type: data encryption keys (short: hours to days), authentication keys (medium: months to 2 years), signing keys (longer: 1-3 years), and master keys (longest: hardware-protected).
source_document: NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5 (Recommendation for Key Management: General), Section 5.3 "Cryptoperiods" and Section 5.6 "Guidance for Cryptographic Algorithm and Key Size Selection".
tensor_equation: ∀ key k_i: purpose(k_i) is unique. cryptoperiod(k_i) ≤ max_lifetime(purpose(k_i)). Signature private keys: 1-3 years. Symmetric authentication keys: < 2 years (strict). Symmetric encryption keys: < 2 years (strict). Master keys: > 2 years.
title: NIST SP 800-57: Separate keys for separate purposes with cryptoperiod limits
verdict_discrepancy: Two fidelity defects. (1) Bound direction/tightness: SP 800-57 Table 1 states '< 2 years' (strict) for symmetric authentication, data-encryption and key-wrapping keys; the axiom relaxes this to '<= 2 years', admitting an exactly-2-year cryptoperiod that the table excludes. (2) Hardening plus mis-formalization of key separation: Sec. 5.2 reads 'In general, a single key shall be used for only one purpose' — a hedged rule with documented exceptions. The axiom renders this as '∀ key k_i: purpose(k_i) is unique', which literally asserts that no two keys share a purpose (making a two-key encryption rotation illegal) rather than that each key serves a single purpose. (3) 'Master keys: long-term (requires hardware protection)' is not a SP 800-57 cryptoperiod statement; Table 1 bounds symmetric master/key-derivation keys and the hardware requirement is not stated there.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Never use plain SHA-256(key || message) or SHA-256(message || key) for authentication. SHA-256's Merkle-Damgard construction is vulnerable to length extension attacks — given H(k || m), an attacker can compute H(k || m || padding || extension) without knowing k. HMAC-SHA256 is immune. If you must use raw hashing for auth (which you shouldn't), use SHA-3 or BLAKE2/3 which are sponge-based and immune. Check every keyed hash in the codebase.
source_document: Katz, Jonathan and Lindell, Yehuda. "Introduction to Modern Cryptography" (3rd ed, 2020), Chapter 5: "Hash Functions and Applications", Section 5.3.2 "HMAC".
tensor_equation: ∀ keyed hashing construction: if SHA-256(k || m) is used, verify input bounds. Prefer HMAC-SHA256(k, m) which is length-extension immune. H(k || m) with Merkle-Damgard hash is length-extension vulnerable.
title: Length extension attack: HMAC over plain hash for keyed hashing
verdict_evidence: Katz & Lindell Ch. 5 'Hash Functions and Applications' covers the Merkle-Damgard transform (Sec. 5.2) and HMAC (Sec. 5.3.2), and shows why the naive keyed hash H(k||m) fails: with a Merkle-Damgard hash the digest *is* the internal chaining state, so an attacker knowing H(k||m) and |k||m| can compute H(k||m||pad||m') without knowing k. HMAC(k,m) = H((k xor opad) || H((k xor ipad) || m)) is immune because the outer hash truncates that state, and K&L prove HMAC secure assuming the compression function is a PRF. Has teeth: the Flickr API signature forgery (2009) is a real-world exploitation of exactly SHA-family H(secret||m). Weakness noted for the record: the clause 'verify input bounds' is not machine-checkable and 'Prefer HMAC' is hedged, so only the third sentence of the equation is a testable invariant.

prompt_injection: Every security-critical system loading cryptographic libraries must perform a power-on self-test: encrypt a known plaintext, verify the ciphertext matches; sign a known message, verify the signature verifies; generate deterministic random bytes from a known seed, verify they match expected output. If any self-test fails, the system must refuse all cryptographic operations and enter a fatal error state — not degrade gracefully.
source_document: FIPS 140-3, Section 7.9 "Self-Tests". NIST SP 800-90A Rev. 1, Section 11 "Health Tests" for DRBG.
tensor_equation: ∀ FIPS-validated cryptographic module M: on power-on, M executes known-answer tests (KATs) for each approved algorithm: encrypt/decrypt KAT, sign/verify KAT, DRBG KAT. If any KAT fails, M enters error state. (Correct source: ISO/IEC 19790:2012 clause 7.10 Self-tests; clause 7.9 is SSP Management.)
title: FIPS 140: Cryptographic module integrity — self-test on startup
verdict_discrepancy: CITATION + FIDELITY: (a) FIPS 140-3 adopts ISO/IEC 19790:2012, where Self-tests are clause 7.10; clause 7.9 is Sensitive Security Parameter Management. The cited 'Section 7.9 Self-Tests' is wrong. (b) The substantive claim is a FIPS 140-2 rule that FIPS 140-3 changed: 140-2 required all algorithm KATs at power-up, while 140-3 splits self-tests into pre-operational self-tests (integrity) and CONDITIONAL cryptographic algorithm self-tests performed prior to first use of each algorithm - an algorithm never invoked need never be tested. 'On power-on, M executes KATs for each approved algorithm' is therefore false for the standard cited.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6 state diagram, extract every invariant that must hold for a connection to reach ESTABLISHED state. Which prior states are mandatory? Can CLOSED ever transition directly to ESTABLISHED?
redteam_pattern: Inject a direct CLOSED-to-ESTABLISHED transition. Runtime assertion must reject it.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = ESTABLISHED implies preceded_by(conn, LISTEN or SYN_SENT) and preceded_by(conn, SYN_RCVD) and three_way_handshake_completed(conn)
title: TCP: Connection establishment requires three-way handshake
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — State diagram shows SYN-SENT rcv SYN,ACK -> ESTABLISHED and SYN-RCVD rcv ACK of SYN -> ESTABLISHED. Three-way handshake: SYN -> SYN,ACK -> ACK.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, which states must transition through TIME-WAIT before reaching CLOSED? Can FIN-WAIT-2 or CLOSING skip directly to CLOSED without passing through TIME-WAIT?
redteam_pattern: Inject a transition FIN-WAIT-2-to-CLOSED. Runtime assertion must fail.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state in {FIN_WAIT_2, CLOSING} and conn.receives(FIN) implies conn.next_state = TIME_WAIT
title: TCP: TIME-WAIT mandatory after active close
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — FIN-WAIT-2 rcv FIN -> TIME-WAIT (snd ACK). TIME-WAIT is the mandatory state after receiving FIN in FIN-WAIT-2.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793, how long must a TCP connection stay in TIME-WAIT state before transitioning to CLOSED? What is the value of 2 MSL?
redteam_pattern: Metric: time_wait_premature_close_count must always be 0.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = TIME_WAIT implies conn.duration_in_state >= 2 * MSL then conn.next_state = CLOSED
title: TCP: TIME-WAIT duration is 2 * MSL
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.6.1 (MUST-13) — it MUST linger in the TIME-WAIT state for a time 2xMSL (Maximum Segment Lifetime).

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, what states can ESTABLISHED transition to? Is LISTEN one of them? Under what conditions does a TCP in a synchronized state return to LISTEN?
redteam_pattern: Inject ESTABLISHED-to-LISTEN via malformed RST. Must be rejected.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = ESTABLISHED implies conn.next_state in {FIN_WAIT_1, CLOSE_WAIT, CLOSED} and conn.next_state != LISTEN
title: TCP: No direct ESTABLISHED-to-LISTEN transition
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — ESTABLISHED transitions only to FIN-WAIT-1 (on CLOSE) or CLOSE-WAIT (on rcv FIN). No direct transition from ESTABLISHED to LISTEN exists.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.4 Reset Processing, what happens when a TCP in SYN-RECEIVED state receives a valid RST? Two outcomes are possible -- what determines which one?
redteam_pattern: Two scenarios: passive open path (LISTEN-origin) returns to LISTEN; simultaneous open (SYN-SENT-origin) goes to CLOSED.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = SYN_RCVD and conn.receives(valid_RST) implies conn.next_state = LISTEN if conn.was_previously(LISTEN), else conn.next_state = CLOSED
title: TCP: SYN-RCVD dual ancestry on RST
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.5.3 — If the receiver was in SYN-RECEIVED state and had previously been in the LISTEN state, then the receiver returns to the LISTEN state; otherwise, the receiver aborts the connection and goes to the CLOS

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, which state transitions into FIN-WAIT-2? What event triggers this transition?
redteam_pattern: Inject transitions from non-FIN-WAIT-1 states to FIN-WAIT-2. Must be rejected.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = FIN_WAIT_2 implies conn.previous_state = FIN_WAIT_1 and conn.received(ACK_of_FIN)
title: TCP: FIN-WAIT-2 only reachable from FIN-WAIT-1
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — Figure 5 shows FIN-WAIT-1 -> FIN-WAIT-2 on rcv ACK of FIN. FIN-WAIT-2 has no other incoming transition in the state diagram.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, what state transitions into CLOSE-WAIT? Can any state other than ESTABLISHED reach CLOSE-WAIT?
redteam_pattern: Inject FIN into LISTEN, SYN-SENT, SYN-RCVD. Must not produce CLOSE-WAIT.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = CLOSE_WAIT implies conn.previous_state = ESTABLISHED and conn.received(FIN)
title: TCP: CLOSE-WAIT only from ESTABLISHED via FIN
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — State diagram shows ESTABLISHED -> CLOSE-WAIT on rcv FIN with snd ACK. CLOSE-WAIT has no other incoming transition.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, what is the only valid transition out of LAST-ACK state?
redteam_pattern: Inject events in LAST-ACK that are not ACK of FIN. State must remain LAST-ACK.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = LAST_ACK implies conn.next_state in {CLOSED, LAST_ACK} and conn.receives(ACK_of_FIN) implies conn.next_state = CLOSED
title: TCP: LAST-ACK terminal transition to CLOSED
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — LAST-ACK -> CLOSED on rcv ACK of FIN (delete TCB). This is the sole terminal transition from LAST-ACK.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 6, what is the only state that transitions into CLOSING? What event triggers it?
redteam_pattern: Attempt CLOSED-to-CLOSING, ESTABLISHED-to-CLOSING. Must be rejected.
tensor_equation: For all conn in TCPConnections: conn.state = CLOSING implies conn.previous_state = FIN_WAIT_1 and conn.received(FIN)
title: TCP: CLOSING only reachable from FIN-WAIT-1
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.3.2 (Figure 5) — State diagram shows FIN-WAIT-1 -> CLOSING on rcv FIN with snd ACK. CLOSING has no other incoming transition and represents simultaneous close.

prompt_injection: According to RFC 793 Section 3.3, does the SYN flag consume one unit in the TCP sequence number space?

tensor_equation: For all seg in TCPSegments: seg.flag(SYN) = true implies seg.LEN = data_len + 1
title: TCP: SYN flag occupies one unit of sequence space
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 — SEG.LEN = the number of octets occupied by the data in the segment (counting SYN and FIN).

prompt_injection: According to RFC 793 Section 3.3, does the FIN flag consume one unit in the TCP sequence number space?

tensor_equation: For all seg in TCPSegments: seg.flag(FIN) = true implies seg.LEN = data_len + 1
title: TCP: FIN flag occupies one unit of sequence space
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 — SEG.LEN = the number of octets occupied by the data in the segment (counting SYN and FIN).

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3, extract the exact inequality that constrains the sender's data transmission. What is the relationship between SND.NXT, SND.UNA, cwnd, and rwnd?
redteam_pattern: Metric: send_constraint_violations counter must stay at 0. Runtime assertion in send path.
tensor_equation: For all t: SND.NXT(t) <= SND.UNA(t) + min(cwnd(t), rwnd(t))
title: TCP Congestion Control: Effective window constraint (conservation of packets)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 (Figure 3), RFC 5681 Section 3.1 — RFC 9293 defines send sequence space: SND.NXT is constrained by SND.WND. RFC 5681 adds cwnd constraint. Combined: SND.NXT <= SND.UNA + min(cwnd, rwnd).

prompt_injection: From Jacobson 1988, extract the conservation of packets principle and the self-clocking property of TCP. What is the exact relationship between acknowledgments and new transmissions?

tensor_equation: For all t: flight_size(t) = SND.NXT(t) - SND.UNA(t) and flight_size(t) <= min(cwnd(t), rwnd(t)) and ack_received implies flight_size_decremented and trigger_new_transmission
title: TCP Congestion Control: Self-clocking via ACK stream (Jacobson 1988)
verdict_evidence: DISPUTED: No source text available for verification — uncited claim

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3.1, extract the exact formula for how cwnd grows during slow start. When does slow start end?
redteam_pattern: assert cwnd_per_rtt_growth ~= 2x. Test: cwnd(n*RTT) ~= cwnd(0) * 2^n within 1 SMSS tolerance.
tensor_equation: For all ack in ACKs acknowledging new data, while cwnd < ssthresh: cwnd' = cwnd + min(N, SMSS) where N = bytes newly acknowledged in this ACK. Per RTT: cwnd roughly doubles.
title: TCP Slow Start: Exponential cwnd growth per RTT
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.1 — During slow start, a TCP increments cwnd by at most SMSS bytes for each ACK received that cumulatively acknowledges new data.

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3.1, extract the exact AIMD formula for cwnd during congestion avoidance. What is the maximum increment per RTT?
redteam_pattern: Over any RTT: assert cwnd(end) - cwnd(start) <= SMSS. After 100 RTTs, cwnd grows by ~100*SMSS (linear).
tensor_equation: For all ack in ACKs, while cwnd > ssthresh: per-RTT cwnd' <= cwnd + SMSS. Per-ACK: cwnd' = cwnd + SMSS^2 / cwnd
title: TCP Congestion Avoidance: AIMD additive increase
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.1 — cwnd += SMSS*SMSS/cwnd

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 and Jacobson 1988, extract the exact multiplicative decrease formula. What happens to cwnd on timeout vs. on 3 duplicate ACKs?
redteam_pattern: On loss: assert ssthresh_new == max(flight_size/2, 2*SMSS). On timeout: cwnd_new == 1*SMSS. On 3 dup ACKs: cwnd_new == ssthresh + 3*SMSS.
tensor_equation: For all loss_event (timeout or 3_dup_ACK): ssthresh' = max(flight_size / 2, 2 * SMSS). On timeout: cwnd' = 1 * SMSS. On 3 dup ACKs: cwnd' = ssthresh + 3 * SMSS (entering fast recovery).
title: TCP: Multiplicative decrease on loss (Jacobson 1988)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.1, 3.2 — ssthresh = max (FlightSize / 2, 2*SMSS). Upon a timeout cwnd MUST be set to no more than the loss window, LW, which equals 1 full-sized segment. Upon 3 dup ACKs: set cwnd to ssthresh + 3*SMSS.

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3.2, what is the exact threshold of duplicate ACKs that triggers fast retransmit? Why 3 and not 1 or 2?
redteam_pattern: 1-2 dup ACKs: no retransmission. >= 3 dup ACKs: retransmission fires.
tensor_equation: For all conn: fast_retransmit_triggered iff dup_ack_count >= 3. dup_ack_count = |{i: ack_i.seq = ack_1.seq for consecutive duplicate ACKs}|
title: TCP: Fast retransmit threshold is 3 duplicate ACKs
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.2 — The fast retransmit algorithm uses the arrival of 3 duplicate ACKs (as defined in section 2, without any intervening ACKs which move SND.UNA) as an indication that a segment has been lost.

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3.2, extract the exact cwnd update rules during fast recovery. How does cwnd change for each duplicate ACK? What happens on recovery ACK?
redteam_pattern: Entry: cwnd == ssthresh + 3*SMSS. Each additional dup ACK: delta(cwnd) == SMSS. Recovery ACK: cwnd == ssthresh.
tensor_equation: For all dup_ack beyond 3rd, during fast_recovery: cwnd' = cwnd + SMSS. On ACK of new data: cwnd' = ssthresh and exit fast_recovery.
title: TCP Reno Fast Recovery: Window inflation per dup ACK
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.2 — For each additional duplicate ACK received (after the third), cwnd MUST be incremented by SMSS. When the next ACK arrives that acknowledges previously unacknowledged data, a TCP MUST set cwnd to ssthr

prompt_injection: What is the key difference between TCP Tahoe and Reno on loss detection? How does Tahoe handle cwnd after fast retransmit?
redteam_pattern: Tahoe mode: on any loss, cwnd_new == 1*SMSS and state = SLOW_START. Reno: cwnd drops to cwnd/2 (faster recovery).
tensor_equation: For all loss_event in Tahoe variant: ssthresh' = max(cwnd / 2, 2 * SMSS), cwnd' = 1 * SMSS, state' = SLOW_START
title: TCP Tahoe: Always returns to slow start on loss
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 5681 §3.1 — Upon a timeout cwnd MUST be set to no more than the loss window, LW, which equals 1 full-sized segment. ssthresh = max(FlightSize/2, 2*SMSS). After retransmitting, the sender uses slow start.

prompt_injection: From RFC 6582, how does NewReno handle partial ACKs during fast recovery? What is the cwnd deflation formula?
redteam_pattern: On partial ACK: retransmit SND.UNA; cwnd deflated; recovery does NOT exit. Simulate 2 losses in one window -- verify NewReno recovers both without timeout.
tensor_equation: For all partial_ack during NewReno fast_recovery: partial_ack == (ACK acknowledges new data and ACK < recover_seq) implies retransmit_immediately(first_unacked) and cwnd' = cwnd - bytes_acked + (bytes_acked >= SMSS ? SMSS : 0) and stay_in_fast_recovery

verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6582 §3.2 (step 3) — If this ACK does *not* acknowledge all of the data up to and including recover, then this is a partial ACK. Retransmit the first unacknowledged segment. Deflate the congestion window by the amount of

prompt_injection: From RFC 6582, what is the condition for NewReno to exit fast recovery on a full ACK? What value should cwnd be set to on exit?
redteam_pattern: When ACK.ack >= recover_seq: fast_recovery_exit = true and cwnd_new == ssthresh.
tensor_equation: For all full_ack during NewReno fast_recovery: full_ack == (ACK.ack >= recover_seq) implies cwnd' = ssthresh and exit fast_recovery
title: TCP NewReno: Full ACK exits fast recovery
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6582 §3.2 (step 3) — If this ACK acknowledges all of the data up to and including recover, then the ACK acknowledges all the intermediate segments. Set cwnd to ssthresh. Exit the fast recovery procedure.

prompt_injection: From RFC 8312, extract the exact cubic window growth formula, the C constant, the beta_cubic factor, and the K computation.
redteam_pattern: Compute expected W_cubic(t) and verify actual cwnd within tolerance. On loss: ssthresh = floor(cwnd*0.7), cwnd = floor(cwnd*0.7).
tensor_equation: For all t >= 0 during CUBIC congestion avoidance: W_cubic(t) = C * (t - K)^3 + W_max where C = 0.4, beta_cubic = 0.7, K = (W_max * (1 - beta_cubic) / C)^(1/3). On loss: ssthresh' = max(cwnd * 0.7, 2), cwnd' = cwnd * 0.7
title: TCP CUBIC: Cubic window growth function (RFC 8312)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8312 §4.1, 4.5, 5.1 — W_cubic(t) = C*(t-K)^3 + W_max (Eq. 1). C SHOULD be set to 0.4. beta_cubic SHOULD be set to 0.7. K = cubic_root(W_max*(1-beta_cubic)/C) (Eq. 2). On loss: ssthresh = cwnd * beta_cubic; ssthresh = max(s

prompt_injection: From RFC 8312 Section 4.3, extract the formula for W_est(t). Under what condition does CUBIC use W_est instead of W_cubic?
redteam_pattern: Compute W_est(t) and W_cubic(t); assert cwnd >= W_est(t) in TCP-friendly region. CUBIC must never underperform standard TCP.
tensor_equation: For all t during CUBIC congestion avoidance: W_est(t) = W_max * beta_cubic + 3 * (1 - beta_cubic) / (1 + beta_cubic) * (t / RTT). If W_cubic(t) < W_est(t) then cwnd(t) = W_est(t).
title: TCP CUBIC: TCP-friendly region constraint
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8312 §4.2 — W_est(t) = W_max*beta_cubic + [3*(1-beta_cubic)/(1+beta_cubic)] * (t/RTT) (Eq. 4). When W_cubic(t) < W_est(t), CUBIC is in the TCP-friendly region and cwnd SHOULD be set to W_est(t).

prompt_injection: From RFC 6298 Section 5.5, what is the rule for updating RTO when the retransmission timer expires? Is the backoff multiplicative or additive?
redteam_pattern: On timer expiry: RTO_new == RTO_old * 2. Consecutive timeouts: RTO doubles each time (1s, 2s, 4s, 8s ... up to >= 60s).
tensor_equation: For all rto_timeout: RTO' = 2 * RTO. For all successful RTT measurement after timeout: RTO recomputed via Jacobson/Karels.
title: TCP RTO: Exponential backoff on retransmission timeout
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6298 §5.5, 2.3 — The host MUST set RTO <- RTO * 2 ('back off the timer'). RTTVAR <- (1-beta)*RTTVAR + beta*|SRTT-R'|, SRTT <- (1-alpha)*SRTT + alpha*R'. alpha=1/8, beta=1/4.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.3, extract the invariant ordering of SND.UNA, SND.NXT, and SND.WND. What inequality must always hold?
redteam_pattern: Assert SND.UNA <= SND.NXT <= SND.UNA + SND.WND after every send. Test window shrink below unacknowledged data.
tensor_equation: For all t, for all conn: SND.UNA(t) <= SND.NXT(t) <= SND.UNA(t) + SND.WND(t)
title: TCP: Send sequence ordering invariant
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 (Figure 3) — Figure 3 (Send Sequence Space) depicts the progression: SND.UNA <= data in transit <= data allowed for transmission <= SND.UNA + SND.WND.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.3, what is the exact inequality that determines whether an incoming ACK is acceptable?
redteam_pattern: seg.ACK <= SND.UNA: duplicate ACK. seg.ACK > SND.NXT: ACK for data never sent (reject). Only process if SND.UNA < seg.ACK <= SND.NXT.
tensor_equation: For all incoming seg with ACK: acceptable(seg) iff SND.UNA < seg.ACK <= SND.NXT
title: TCP: Acknowledgment validity bounds
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 — A new acknowledgment (called an 'acceptable ack') is one for which the inequality below holds: SND.UNA < SEG.ACK =< SND.NXT

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.3, what are the exact conditions for a data segment to be accepted into the receive window? What is the two-part test?
redteam_pattern: Apply two-part test to every incoming data segment. Both failing -> silently drop without ACK modification.
tensor_equation: For all incoming seg with LEN > 0 and RCV.WND > 0: acceptable(seg) iff (RCV.NXT <= seg.SEQ < RCV.NXT + RCV.WND) or (RCV.NXT <= seg.SEQ + seg.LEN - 1 < RCV.NXT + RCV.WND)
title: TCP: Receive window segment acceptability (two-part test)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 — A segment is judged to occupy a portion of valid receive sequence space if RCV.NXT =< SEG.SEQ < RCV.NXT+RCV.WND or RCV.NXT =< SEG.SEQ+SEG.LEN-1 < RCV.NXT+RCV.WND

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.3, when the receive window is zero, which segments are still acceptable?
redteam_pattern: When advertised window is 0: no data segments accepted; only ACK probes with SEG.SEQ == RCV.NXT accepted.
tensor_equation: For all seg when RCV.WND = 0: seg.LEN = 0 and seg.SEQ = RCV.NXT implies acceptable. seg.LEN = 0 and seg.SEQ != RCV.NXT implies NOT acceptable. seg.LEN > 0 implies NOT acceptable.
title: TCP: Zero-window segment acceptability
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 (Table 5) — Segment length 0, window 0: SEG.SEQ = RCV.NXT is acceptable. Segment length >0, window 0: not acceptable.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.7, what is the formula for the usable window? What does a negative usable window indicate and why is it discouraged?
redteam_pattern: Compute usable = (SND.UNA + SND.WND) - SND.NXT. If usable <= 0, assert no data is sent. Metric: window_shrink_events.
tensor_equation: For all t: usable_window(t) = (SND.UNA(t) + SND.WND(t)) - SND.NXT(t) and usable_window(t) >= 0. Negative indicates window shrink (strongly discouraged per Postel).
title: TCP: Usable window is send window minus outstanding data
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 (Figure 3) — Figure 3 defines send sequence space: SND.UNA to SND.NXT = unacknowledged, SND.NXT to SND.UNA+SND.WND = allowed new data. Usable window = (SND.UNA+SND.WND)-SND.NXT is implicit.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Section 3.3, what determines the value of RCV.WND? How does buffer occupancy relate to the advertised window?
redteam_pattern: RCV.WND == buffer_capacity - bytes_buffered_undelivered. Fill buffer -> window shrinks. Drain buffer -> window re-opens.
tensor_equation: For all t: RCV.WND(t) = receive_buffer_capacity - data_buffered_not_delivered_to_app(t) and RCV.WND(t) >= 0
title: TCP: Receive window reflects available buffer space
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 — RCV.WND is the receive window. The flow control algorithm updates RCV.WND based on available buffer space. RCV.WND reflects the receiver's available buffer capacity.

prompt_injection: From RFC 1122 Section 4.2.2.3, what is the rule for avoiding silly window syndrome on the receiver side? When should a receiver NOT advertise a small window?
redteam_pattern: If buffer not empty: advertised_window >= min(MSS, buffer_capacity/2). Test slow application reading one byte at a time.
tensor_equation: For all conn when RCV.WND_advertised > 0: RCV.WND_advertised >= min(MSS, buffer_capacity / 2) or buffer_empty
title: TCP: Silly Window Syndrome avoidance (receiver side, Clark 1982)
verdict_evidence: DISPUTED: No source text available for verification — uncited claim

prompt_injection: From RFC 6298 Section 2, extract the exact formulas for SRTT, RTTVAR, and RTO computation. What are the values of alpha, beta, and K?
redteam_pattern: For every RTT measurement, recompute expected SRTT, RTTVAR, RTO and assert match. alpha=1/8, beta=1/4, K=4, G=clock_granularity.
tensor_equation: For all RTT_sample R (when not ambiguous): first_sample: SRTT = R, RTTVAR = R/2, RTO = SRTT + max(G, 4*RTTVAR). subsequent: RTTVAR = (1-1/4)*RTTVAR + (1/4)*|SRTT-R|, SRTT = (1-1/8)*SRTT + (1/8)*R, RTO = SRTT + max(G, 4*RTTVAR), RTO in [1s, >=60s]. alpha=1/8, beta=1/4, K=4.
title: TCP: Jacobson/Karels RTO computation (RFC 6298)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6298 §2.2, 2.3, 2.4, 2.5 — First RTT: SRTT <- R, RTTVAR <- R/2, RTO = SRTT + max(G, K*RTTVAR) with K=4. Subsequent: RTTVAR <- (1-beta)*RTTVAR + beta*|SRTT-R'|, SRTT <- (1-alpha)*SRTT + alpha*R'. alpha=1/8, beta=1/4. RTO SHOULD

prompt_injection: From RFC 6298 Section 3 (Karn's Algorithm), should RTT samples be taken from retransmitted segments? Why not? What is the exception?
redteam_pattern: If segment was retransmitted and timestamps NOT in use: assert no RTT sample is taken. Counter: karn_algorithm_violations == 0. Exception: TCP Timestamps option (RFC 7323).
tensor_equation: For all seg in transmitted_segments: seg.is_retransmit and not timestamps_enabled implies no RTT_sample based on seg
title: TCP: Karn's Algorithm -- no RTT from retransmitted segments
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6298 §3 — RTT samples MUST NOT be made using segments that were retransmitted

prompt_injection: From RFC 6298 Section 5, extract the four rules for managing the retransmission timer: when to start, restart, on expiry, when to turn off.
redteam_pattern: 4 scenarios: (1) timer on when data sent and timer off; (2) restart on new ACK; (3) RTO doubles on expiry; (4) timer off when all data ACKed.
tensor_equation: For all conn: no_oldest_unacked implies timer_off. timer_expires implies retransmit(oldest_unacked) and RTO' = 2*RTO. new_ack_covers_new_data implies restart_timer_with_current_RTO. all_outstanding_acked implies turn_off_timer.
title: TCP: Retransmission timer management rules
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 6298 §5.1, 5.2, 5.3, 5.5 — The host MUST set RTO <- RTO * 2. Rules: no oldest unacked -> timer off; timer expires -> retransmit + RTO*=2; new ACK covering new data -> restart timer; all acked -> timer off.

prompt_injection: From RFC 6298 and Stevens, what is the initial RTO for a SYN segment? How does it backoff if the SYN is lost?
redteam_pattern: Before first RTT measurement: RTO == 1s (or configured value). On SYN retransmission: RTO_new == RTO_old * 2.
tensor_equation: For all conn in SYN_SENT state: timer_expires implies retransmit_SYN and RTO' = 2 * RTO. Initial SYN RTO = 1 second (recommended), 3 seconds (common).
title: TCP: SYN retransmission with exponential backoff
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.8.3 — SYN retransmissions MUST be handled in the general way just described for data retransmissions, including notification of the application layer.

prompt_injection: From RFC 5681 Section 3.1 Limited Transmit, what does a TCP sender do on the first and second duplicate ACKs?
redteam_pattern: On 1st/2nd dup ACK with flight_size <= cwnd+2*SMSS: new data segment sent. cwnd does NOT change.
tensor_equation: For all dup_ack_1st_or_2nd: if flight_size <= cwnd + 2*SMSS and rwnd_allows and new_data_available then transmit_1_SMSS(new_data). cwnd unchanged.
title: TCP: Limited Transmit on 1st/2nd dup ACK (RFC 3042)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 3042 §2 — A TCP sender to transmit new data upon the arrival of the first two consecutive duplicate ACKs. Condition: receiver's advertised window allows, outstanding data <= cwnd + 2 segments.

prompt_injection: From RFC 1122 Section 4.2.2.17, what does a TCP sender do when the receiver advertises a zero window? What is a window probe?
redteam_pattern: When rwnd=0 beyond persist timeout: zero-length probe sent. Non-zero window update: transmission resumes. Lost update: persist timer prevents deadlock.
tensor_equation: For all conn with rwnd = 0: persist_timer_expires implies send_zero_length_probe(SEG.SEQ = SND.NXT). persist_interval uses RTO-based exponential backoff. window_reopens implies cancel_persist and resume_transmission.
title: TCP: Persist timer prevents zero-window deadlock
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.4 (Section references RFC 1122 4.2.2.17) — TCP uses a persist timer to probe zero-window conditions. When window reopens, persist timer is cancelled and transmission resumes.

prompt_injection: From RFC 793 Figure 9, what happens when the retransmission timer expires while a TCP is in the SYN-RECEIVED state?
redteam_pattern: In SYN-RCVD: on timer expiry, SYN-ACK retransmitted and RTO doubles with exponential backoff.
tensor_equation: For all conn in SYN_RCVD state: SYN_ACK_timer_expires implies retransmit_SYN_ACK and RTO' = 2 * RTO
title: TCP: SYN-ACK retransmission in SYN-RCVD
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9293 §3.8.3 — SYN retransmissions MUST be handled in the general way just described for data retransmissions, including notification of the application layer.

prompt_injection: You are Saltzer, Reed, and Clark from 1984. State the end-to-end argument as a formal invariant. For any function F that requires endpoint knowledge to be correctly implemented, prove that implementing F in the communication subsystem is either impossible or incomplete.
redteam_pattern: Inject processor memory error during buffer copy while relying on TCP-level reliability. System must detect corruption at the endpoint, proving lower-layer guarantee was insufficient.
tensor_equation: For all A, B in Endpoints, for all N in Network: reliability(A, B) is achieved implies checksum(A, B) and retry(A, B) and commit(A, B) and not (there exists f in NetworkFunctions: f guarantees reliability(A, B))
title: End-to-End: Application-level verification is necessary (Saltzer-Reed-Clark 1984)
verdict_evidence: CITED: Saltzer, Reed, Clark 1984 §Section 1 — The function in question can completely and correctly be implemented only with the knowledge and help of the application standing at the end points of the communication system.

prompt_injection: State the end-to-end performance/correctness boundary as a formal invariant. For any function placed at a layer below the application endpoints, the function can only be justified as a performance optimization, never as a correctness guarantee.
redteam_pattern: Enumerate each lower-layer function. Verify application layer has corresponding end-to-end implementation. Remove application-layer checksum while keeping TCP reliability -- inject disk error: must fail.
tensor_equation: For all f in Functions, for all L in Layers: f placed at L below Endpoint implies (justification(f) = performance) and not (justification(f) = correctness)
title: End-to-End: Lower-layer functions are performance optimizations, not correctness guarantees
verdict_evidence: CITED: Saltzer, Reed, Clark 1984 §Section 1 — The amount of effort to put into reliability measures within the data communication system is seen to be an engineering tradeoff based on performance, rather than a requirement for correctness.

prompt_injection: The application layer must implement its own duplicate suppression because only the application knows the semantics of what constitutes a duplicate. Formalize this.
redteam_pattern: Disable network-level dedup (use UDP). Send same app-level request twice simulating user double-click. Application must detect and suppress duplicate.
tensor_equation: For all A in Applications: duplicate_suppression(A, ApplicationLayer) is necessary and not (duplicate_suppression(A, NetworkLayer) implies not duplicate_suppression(A, ApplicationLayer))
title: End-to-End: Duplicate suppression must exist at application layer
verdict_evidence: CITED: Saltzer, Reed, Clark 1984 §Section 1 (file transfer example) — Threat number four may have been eliminated, but the careful file transfer application must still counter the remaining threats, so it should still provide its own retries based on an end-to-end check

prompt_injection: Formalize the end-to-end encryption invariant. Network-level (link) encryption protects against external eavesdroppers but does not imply end-to-end confidentiality if intermediate nodes are compromised.
redteam_pattern: Trace encryption boundary end-to-end. If plaintext available at any intermediate node (proxy, LB, gateway), invariant is violated. Test: compromised intermediate node.
tensor_equation: For all msg in Messages: confidentiality(msg) and integrity(msg) implies encryption(msg, Endpoints) and not (encryption(msg, Network) alone implies confidentiality(msg))
title: End-to-End: Encryption must be end-to-end for confidentiality
verdict_evidence: DISPUTED: No source text available for verification — uncited claim

prompt_injection: Formalize Clark's fate-sharing principle. All critical state needed for communication must be co-located with the communicating entity so that if the entity fails, the state is lost cleanly.
redteam_pattern: Audit: each piece of critical state (TCP state, TLS session, app session) stored at endpoint, not in network. Crashed endpoint must not require remote coordination to recover.
tensor_equation: For all e in Entities, for all s in CriticalState(e): location(s) = e and crash(e) implies lost(s) and not (there exists r in RemoteNodes: recoverable(s, r))
title: Fate-Sharing: Critical state co-located with entity (Clark 1988)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 1122 §1.1.2(b) — All connection control information is thus co-located with the end points of the communication, so it will be lost only if an end point fails.

prompt_injection: Formalize the sending side of Postel's Law. For all messages sent by an implementation, every field must strictly conform to the specification.
redteam_pattern: Fuzz test: generate all permutations of outgoing messages. Any field outside spec's valid range must be caught. Send TCP segment with reserved flag set -- fuzz test must reject.
tensor_equation: For all m in MessagesSent, for all s in Spec: field(m) conforms to s and valid(m) implies strict(m, s). Every transmitted field MUST be within the spec's valid range.
title: Postel's Law: Conservative sending (RFC 760, RFC 1122)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 760 §3.2 — An implementation should be conservative in its sending behavior, and liberal in its receiving behavior.

prompt_injection: Formalize the receiving side of Postel's Law. For all messages received with technical errors, if the meaning is still unambiguous despite the error, the implementation must accept the message.
redteam_pattern: Property-based test: generate all known error classes. Each must be either (a) accepted with correct interpretation, or (b) rejected only when genuinely ambiguous.
tensor_equation: For all m in MessagesReceived, for all e in Errors: unambiguous(m, e) implies accept(m) and not reject(m). Tolerate non-conformant but semantically unambiguous input.
title: Postel's Law: Liberal receiving (RFC 760, RFC 1122)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 760 §3.2 — An implementation should be conservative in its sending behavior, and liberal in its receiving behavior.

prompt_injection: Formalize HTTP safe methods invariant. For all safe methods M in {GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE}, the application of M must not change the observable state of the resource.
redteam_pattern: Execute safe method 100x against same resource. Assert state and response body identical modulo time-dependent fields. GET incrementing a view counter in the resource representation violates this.
tensor_equation: For all m in {GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE}, for all r in Resources, for all n in N: apply(m, r, n) implies state(r, before) = state(r, after). Safe methods are read-only.
title: HTTP Safe Methods: Zero side effects (RFC 7231 Section 4.2.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §9.2.1 — Section 9.2.1 defines safe methods. GET, HEAD, OPTIONS, and TRACE are defined as safe methods. Safe methods are read-only and do not change resource state.

prompt_injection: Formalize HTTP idempotency as a tensor equation. The server's intended effect of n identical applications of an idempotent method equals the effect of 1 application.
redteam_pattern: PUT(resource, body) 2x == PUT(resource, body) 1x. DELETE(resource) 2x: second returns 404 but intended effect (deleted) is same. PUT incrementing embedded version counter violates.
tensor_equation: For all m in {GET, HEAD, PUT, DELETE, OPTIONS, TRACE}, for all r in Resources, for all n in N: effect(apply(m, r, n)) = effect(apply(m, r, 1)). The intended effect of N identical idempotent requests equals the effect of 1.
title: HTTP Idempotency: N applications = 1 application (RFC 7231 Section 4.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §9.2.2 — Section 9.2.2 defines idempotent methods. A request method is considered idempotent if the intended effect on the server of N identical requests is the same as for a single request. GET, HEAD, PUT, DE

prompt_injection: Prove that POST is not idempotent. Express as: exists r in Resources, exists body: effect(POST(r, body, 2)) != effect(POST(r, body, 1)).
redteam_pattern: Issue same POST 2x to a resource-creating endpoint. Second request produces different outcome (two resources, not one). This is expected behavior.
tensor_equation: Not (for all r in Resources, for all n in N: effect(POST(r, body, n)) = effect(POST(r, body, 1))). There exists at least one resource R and body B such that two POSTs produce different outcomes.
title: HTTP POST is NOT idempotent by default (RFC 7231 Section 4.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §9.2.2, 9.3.3 — Section 9.2.2: POST is not listed as idempotent. Multiple identical POST requests may produce different outcomes on the server.

prompt_injection: Formalize the auto-retry constraint. For all HTTP methods M and all clients C, if C automatically retries M after a communication failure, then M must be idempotent.
redteam_pattern: Runtime assertion in HTTP client middleware: intercept all retries, assert method is idempotent. POST retried automatically without out-of-band knowledge must fire assertion.
tensor_equation: For all m in Methods, for all c in Clients: auto_retry(c, m, failure) implies idempotent(m). POST must not be retried without out-of-band idempotency knowledge.
title: HTTP Auto-Retry: Only idempotent methods may be automatically retried (RFC 9110 Section 9.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §9.2.2 — Some requests can be automatically retried by a client in the event of an underlying connection failure, as described in Section 9.2.2 (Idempotent Methods).

prompt_injection: Extract the rule from RFC 7230 Section 6.3 that states when HTTP/1.1 connections are persistent by default and when they close.
redteam_pattern: Send two pipelined requests on one socket without Connection: close. Second must be accepted. With Connection: close, socket must be closed after response.
tensor_equation: For all c in HTTP/1.1_Connections: 'close' not in c.Connection_Tokens implies c.persistent = true. Only explicit Connection: close tears down after current response.
title: HTTP/1.1: Connection persistence is the default (RFC 7230 Section 6.3)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §9.3 — HTTP/1.1 defaults to the use of 'persistent connections', allowing multiple requests and responses to be carried over a single connection.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Sections 3.3.2 and 3.3.3, extract the normative requirement linking Content-Length to actual body byte count.
redteam_pattern: Send Content-Length: 100 with 90-byte body: detect truncation. Send Content-Length: 50 with 80-byte body: consume only 50 bytes.
tensor_equation: For all m in Messages with no Transfer-Encoding and Content-Length present: len(m.body_bytes) = m.Content_Length. Mismatch is a protocol error.
title: HTTP/1.1: Content-Length must match body size (RFC 7230 Section 3.3.3)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §6.2, 6.3 — The field can provide the anticipated size, as a decimal number of octets, for potential content. If the sender closes before the indicated octets are received, the recipient MUST consider the message

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Section 4.1, what is the exact terminal sequence of a chunked transfer-encoded message body?
redteam_pattern:
Send '5
Hello
' without final '0

'. Parser must report incomplete message error.
tensor_equation: For all m with Transfer-Encoding = 'chunked': last_chunk(m) = '0' CRLF CRLF. Stream incomplete if zero-sized chunk not received.
title: HTTP/1.1: Chunked transfer encoding terminates with zero-sized chunk (RFC 7230 Section 4.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §7.1 — The chunked transfer coding is complete when a chunk with a chunk-size of zero is received, possibly followed by a trailer section.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Section 5.4, extract the normative requirement for the Host header in HTTP/1.1 requests. Is it mandatory?
redteam_pattern: Send HTTP/1.1 GET without Host header. Server MUST respond with 400 Bad Request.
tensor_equation: For all r in HTTP/1.1_Requests: Host in r.headers. Required for virtual host disambiguation on shared IP addresses.
title: HTTP/1.1: Host header is mandatory (RFC 7230 Section 5.4)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §3.2 — A client MUST send a Host header field in all HTTP/1.1 request messages. A server MUST respond with a 400 (Bad Request) status code to any HTTP/1.1 request message that lacks a Host header field.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Section 3.3.3 item 4, what is the normative handling when a message contains multiple Content-Length headers with different values?
redteam_pattern: Send request with Content-Length: 5 and Content-Length: 10. Server must return 400. Proxy response with mismatched CL must return 502.
tensor_equation: For all m in Messages with multiple Content-Length values and not all_equal({v}): must_reject(m). Server: 400; proxy: 502.
title: HTTP/1.1: Duplicate Content-Length with differing values is fatal (RFC 7230 Section 3.3.3 item 4)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §6.3 (item 5) — When a message lacks Transfer-Encoding but has an invalid Content-Length, the message framing is invalid and the recipient MUST treat it as an unrecoverable error.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Sections 3.3 and 3.3.3, which response status codes are prohibited from having a message body?
redteam_pattern: Send 204 with Content-Length: 5 and body. Client must ignore body and close parsing at empty line.
tensor_equation: For all r in Responses: r.status in {1xx, 204, 304} implies r.body = empty. Terminates at first empty line after headers.
title: HTTP/1.1: 1xx, 204, 304 responses have no body (RFC 7230 Section 3.3.3)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §6.4.1 — All 1xx (Informational), 204 (No Content), and 304 (Not Modified) responses do not include content.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 Section 3.3.2, extract the MUST NOT rule about coexistence of Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding.
redteam_pattern: Send message with both Transfer-Encoding: chunked and Content-Length: 42. Receiver must treat as protocol error.
tensor_equation: For all m sent by valid sender: Transfer-Encoding in m.headers implies Content-Length not in m.headers. A sender MUST NOT include both.
title: HTTP/1.1: Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding are mutually exclusive (RFC 7230 Section 3.3.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9112 §6.2 — A sender MUST NOT send a Content-Length header field in any message that contains a Transfer-Encoding header field.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7230 and RFC 7231, formally define the five HTTP status code classes and their structural invariants.
redteam_pattern: 200 with no body: valid. 101 with body: invalid. 301 with no Location header: protocol violation.
tensor_equation: For all r in HTTP_Responses: 1xx: no_body and may_have_multiple. 2xx: body_allowed. 3xx: redirect_target in headers. 4xx: error_attributed_to(CLIENT). 5xx: error_attributed_to(SERVER).
title: HTTP Status Code Classification (RFC 7230 Section 3.1.2, RFC 7231 Sections 6.2-6.6)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9110 §15.1-15.6 — RFC 9110 Section 15 defines five status code classes: 1xx Informational, 2xx Successful, 3xx Redirection, 4xx Client Error, 5xx Server Error.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Sections 5.2.1.5 and 5.2.2.3, what is the exact normative requirement when no-store appears? Express as MUST NOT with universal quantification.
redteam_pattern: Send response with Cache-Control: no-store. Same request again: cache must go to origin, not serve prior response. No entry in cache store.
tensor_equation: For all caches C, for all transactions with 'no-store' in req or resp Cache-Control: not (there exists entry in C matching the request/response). No storage, volatile or non-volatile.
title: HTTP Caching: no-store prohibits all storage (RFC 7234 Section 5.2.1.5, 5.2.2.3)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §5.2.2.5 — Indicates that a cache MUST NOT store any part of either the immediate request or the response and MUST NOT use the response to satisfy any other request.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Section 5.2.2.2, extract the MUST NOT rule about serving no-cache responses without validation.
redteam_pattern: Cache a response with Cache-Control: no-cache. Next request must send conditional request (If-None-Match/If-Modified-Since) BEFORE serving cached response.
tensor_equation: For all caches C, for all stored S with 'no-cache' in Cache-Control: S.served(t) implies there exists validation request to origin between S.stored_at and t.
title: HTTP Caching: no-cache requires revalidation before serving (RFC 7234 Section 5.2.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §5.2.2.4 — Indicates that the response MUST NOT be used to satisfy any other request without forwarding it for validation and receiving a successful response.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Sections 4.2.1 and 5.2.2.8, derive the formula for computing freshness lifetime when max-age is present.
redteam_pattern: Store response with max-age=60 at T0. At T0+30s: fresh (served from cache). At T0+61s: stale (must revalidate or go to origin).
tensor_equation: For all stored S with 'max-age=N': freshness_lifetime(S) = N seconds. S is fresh iff current_age < N. s-maxage takes priority in shared caches.
title: HTTP Caching: max-age determines freshness lifetime (RFC 7234 Section 4.2.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §4.2.1, 5.2.2.1 — If the max-age response directive (Section 5.2.2.1) is present, use its value. Indicates that the response is to be considered stale after its age is greater than the specified number of seconds.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234, what happens to a cached entry when a conditional request returns 304 Not Modified? Express the update and reuse rules.
redteam_pattern: Cache response with ETag 'abc'. Send If-None-Match: 'abc'. Origin returns 304 with new max-age=30. Cached entry's max-age updated, response served from cache.
tensor_equation: For all cached S, for all cond_req: origin_response(cond_req).status = 304 and S.matches(cond_req) implies S.updated_from(304_headers) and S.can_serve(cond_req.uri).
title: HTTP Caching: 304 Not Modified freshens cached response (RFC 7234 Section 4.3.3-4.3.4)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §4.3.3, 4.3.4 — A 304 (Not Modified) response status code indicates that the stored response can be updated and reused. Upon receiving a 304, the cache MUST update its header fields with the header fields provided in

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Section 4.2.2, when is heuristic freshness calculation permitted vs. prohibited? Express as MUST NOT.
redteam_pattern: Response with max-age=3600: explicit TTL used even if heuristic (10% of Last-Modified interval) would differ. No-Cache-Control + Last-Modified: heuristic allowed.
tensor_equation: For all stored S: S has explicit Cache-Control max-age or Expires implies heuristic_freshness_applied(S) = false. Heuristics only when no explicit TTL.
title: HTTP Caching: No heuristic freshness when explicit TTL exists (RFC 7234 Section 4.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §4.2.2 — A cache MUST NOT use heuristics to determine freshness when an explicit expiration time is present in the stored response.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Section 5.2.2.1, what is the normative requirement when a cached response marked must-revalidate becomes stale?
redteam_pattern: Cache response with max-age=5, must-revalidate. After 6s, origin unreachable. Cache must return 504, not stale response.
tensor_equation: For all stored S with 'must-revalidate': current_age > freshness_lifetime implies S.served(t) implies (there exists valid_req to origin between stale_time and t). Origin unreachable: return 504.
title: HTTP Caching: must-revalidate forces origin check on stale responses (RFC 7234 Section 5.2.2.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §5.2.2.2 — Indicates that once the response has become stale, a cache MUST NOT reuse that response to satisfy another request until it has been successfully validated by the origin. If a cache is disconnected, t

prompt_injection: From RFC 7234 Section 4.4, what cache invalidation must occur on successful unsafe method responses? Express with host-matching guard.
redteam_pattern: Cache GET /foo. POST /foo returns 200. GET /foo must go to origin. POST /foo with Location: /bar (same host): both /foo and /bar invalidated. Different host Location: only /foo invalidated.
tensor_equation: For all r with method in {POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH}, for all non-error responses: invalidate(cache, r.effective_uri) and invalidate(cache, resp.Location) if same_host(resp.Location, r.effective_uri).
title: HTTP Caching: Unsafe methods invalidate cache (RFC 7234 Section 4.4)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9111 §4.4 — A cache MUST invalidate the target URI when it receives a non-error status code in response to an unsafe request method.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Section 5.1.1, extract the parity rule for HTTP/2 stream identifiers. Who gets odd IDs? Who gets even IDs?
redteam_pattern: Client opens streams: all odd IDs. Server push streams: even IDs. Wrong parity: PROTOCOL_ERROR connection error.
tensor_equation: For all s in HTTP/2_Streams: (s.initiator = client implies s.id mod 2 = 1) and (s.initiator = server implies s.id mod 2 = 0). Stream 0 reserved for connection control.
title: HTTP/2: Client streams have odd IDs, server streams even (RFC 7540 Section 5.1.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §5.1.1 — Streams initiated by a client MUST use odd-numbered stream identifiers; those initiated by the server MUST use even-numbered stream identifiers.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Section 5.1.1, extract the monotonicity and non-reuse constraints on HTTP/2 stream identifiers.
redteam_pattern: Open streams 1, 3, 5. Close stream 3. Next client stream must be 7, NOT 3. Attempting stream 3 again: PROTOCOL_ERROR.
tensor_equation: For all E in Endpoints, for all s1, s2 in streams_opened_by(E): s1.opened_before(s2) implies s1.id < s2.id. For all closed s: no s' with s'.id = s.id.
title: HTTP/2: Stream IDs are strictly monotonically increasing, never reused (RFC 7540 Section 5.1.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §5.1.1 — The identifier of a newly established stream MUST be numerically greater than all streams that the initiating endpoint has opened or reserved. Stream identifiers cannot be reused.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Section 5.1.2, extract the concurrency limit imposed by SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS.
redteam_pattern: SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS=10. Open 10 streams. Stream 11 must be rejected with PROTOCOL_ERROR or REFUSED_STREAM.
tensor_equation: For all E at time t: |{s: s.initiator = E.peer and s.state in {open, half-closed} at t}| <= E.peer_settings[MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS].
title: HTTP/2: Stream concurrency limited by SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS (RFC 7540 Section 5.1.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §5.1.2 — A peer can limit the number of concurrently active streams using the SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS parameter. Streams in open or either half-closed state count toward the limit.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Sections 6.5 and 6.5.3, extract the ACK-non-ACK protocol for SETTINGS frames.
redteam_pattern: SETTINGS with ACK=1 and non-zero payload: connection must close with FRAME_SIZE_ERROR.
tensor_equation: For all f in SETTINGS_Frames: f.ACK = true implies len(f.payload) = 0 and f.stream_id = 0. Non-ACK SETTINGS: must be on stream 0.
title: HTTP/2: SETTINGS ACK protocol (RFC 7540 Sections 6.5, 6.5.3)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §6.5, 6.5.3 — SETTINGS frames are acknowledged by the receiving peer. The ACK flag (0x01) indicates that this frame acknowledges receipt and application of the peer's SETTINGS frame. Once all values have been proce

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540, what is the initial flow-control window size for new HTTP/2 streams and the connection?
redteam_pattern: New HTTP/2 connection: before any WINDOW_UPDATE, sender must transmit exactly 65,535 bytes without stalling.
tensor_equation: For all c in HTTP/2_Connections, for all s in new_streams(c): flow_window(s, t0) = 65535 and flow_window(c, t0) = 65535.
title: HTTP/2: Initial flow control window is 65,535 octets (RFC 7540 Sections 5.2.1, 6.5.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §5.2.1, 6.9.2 — The initial value for the flow-control window is 65,535 octets for both new streams and the overall connection.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540, what is the maximum allowed flow-control window size? What error results from exceeding it?
redteam_pattern: WINDOW_UPDATE pushing window above 2^31-1: FLOW_CONTROL_ERROR connection closure.
tensor_equation: For all c, for all s: flow_window(s, t) <= 2^31 - 1 and flow_window(c, t) <= 2^31 - 1.
title: HTTP/2: Flow control window must not exceed 2^31 - 1 (RFC 7540 Sections 6.9.1, 6.5.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §6.9.1 — A sender MUST NOT allow a flow-control window to exceed 2^31-1 octets.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Section 6.8, what is the rule about new stream creation after a GOAWAY frame?
redteam_pattern: Server GOAWAY with last-stream-id=7. Client must not open stream 9+. Server processes streams 1,3,5,7 but not 9,11.
tensor_equation: For all c, for all GOAWAY f with last-stream-id L: for all s initiated after f.timestamp: s.id <= L. Existing streams with id <= L still processed.
title: HTTP/2: GOAWAY prohibits new streams above last-stream-id (RFC 7540 Section 6.8)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §6.8 — The GOAWAY contains the stream identifier of the last peer-initiated stream that was or might be processed. Receivers of a GOAWAY frame MUST NOT open additional streams on the connection.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 Section 5.2.1, which frame types are subject to HTTP/2 flow control? Can flow control be disabled?
redteam_pattern: Send HEADERS/SETTINGS/PING without WINDOW_UPDATE credit: must not stall or be rejected. Only DATA frames block on depleted window.
tensor_equation: For all f in HTTP/2_Frames: consumes_flow_window(f) iff f.type = DATA. HEADERS, PRIORITY, SETTINGS, PING: not subject to flow control.
title: HTTP/2: Only DATA frames consume flow control (RFC 7540 Section 5.2.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §5.2.1 — Only DATA frames are subject to flow control; all other frame types do not consume space in the advertised flow-control window.

prompt_injection: From RFC 7540 and RFC 7541, what bounds the HPACK dynamic table size? What setting controls it?
redteam_pattern: Advertise SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE=4096. Encoder must not cause decoder's dynamic table to exceed 4096 bytes.
tensor_equation: For all c, for all t: |dynamic_table(c, t)| <= c.peer_settings[SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE]. Decoder must not exceed advertised max size.
title: HTTP/2: HPACK dynamic table bounded by SETTINGS_HEADER_TABLE_SIZE (RFC 7540 Section 6.5.2, RFC 7541)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9113 §6.5.2 — This setting allows the sender to inform the remote endpoint of the maximum size of the compression table used to decode field blocks, in units of octets. The initial value is 4,096 octets.

prompt_injection: From RFC 9114 Section 6.1, who can create bidirectional streams in HTTP/3? What happens if the server creates one?
redteam_pattern: Server sends bidirectional stream without negotiated extension. Client must close with H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR.
tensor_equation: For all c in HTTP/3_Connections, for all server-initiated bidirectional stream s: s.error = H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR unless extension_negotiated.
title: HTTP/3: Server-initiated bidirectional streams forbidden unless extension (RFC 9114 Section 6.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9114 §6.1 — HTTP/3 does not use server-initiated bidirectional streams, though an extension could define a use for these streams. Clients MUST treat receipt of a server-initiated bidirectional stream as a connect

prompt_injection: From RFC 9114 Section 6.2.1, how many control streams must each HTTP/3 endpoint create? What must be the first frame?
redteam_pattern: Client opens two control streams: server closes with H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR. Closing sole control stream: H3_CLOSED_CRITICAL_STREAM.
tensor_equation: For all c, for all E in {client, server}: |{s: s.type = control and s.initiator = E}| = 1 and first_frame(control_stream(E)) = SETTINGS. Second control stream: H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR.
title: HTTP/3: One control stream per endpoint, SETTINGS as first frame (RFC 9114 Section 6.2.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9114 §6.2.1 — Each side MUST initiate a single control stream at the beginning of the connection and send its SETTINGS frame as the first frame on this stream. If the first frame of the control stream is any other

prompt_injection: From RFC 9114 Section 4.1.1, what is the idempotency requirement for 0-RTT data and retry? Which methods are safe?

tensor_equation: For all r in HTTP/3_0-RTT_Requests: auto_retry(r) implies r.method in {GET, HEAD, OPTIONS} or idempotent_invariant_verifiable. Non-idempotent POST retry requires out-of-band confirmation.
title: HTTP/3: 0-RTT data must be idempotent (RFC 9114 Section 4.1.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9114 §4.1.1 — Only idempotent actions such as GET, PUT, or DELETE can be safely retried.

verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 9114 §7.2.4.2 — If the server cannot determine that the settings remembered by a client are compatible with its current settings, it MUST NOT accept 0-RTT data.

prompt_injection: From RFC 1034 Section 3.6 and RFC 2181, derive the exact expiry time formula for a cached DNS RR. When must the entry be discarded?

tensor_equation: For all nx in DNS_Negative_Cache where nx.rcode = NXDOMAIN: nx.ttl = min(nx.authority.SOA.MINIMUM, nx.authority.SOA.TTL).

verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 2308 §3, 5 — The TTL of this record is set from the minimum of the MINIMUM field of the SOA record and the TTL of the SOA itself, governing how long a resolver may cache the negative answer.

verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: From RFC 1034, what must happen when a CNAME chain forms a loop? Express as cycle detection.
redteam_pattern: A -> CNAME B, B -> CNAME C, C -> CNAME A. Resolution must detect cycle and return SERVFAIL, not loop infinitely.
tensor_equation: For all DNS resolution paths with CNAMEs: if there exist i != j with cname_i = cname_j then error = CNAME_LOOP. Alias loops and pointers to non-existent names must be caught.
title: DNS: CNAME chain must terminate, no loops (RFC 1034 Sections 3.6.2, 5.2.2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 1034 §3.6.2, 5.2.2 — A CNAME RR identifies its owner name as an alias, and specifies the corresponding canonical name. In most cases a resolver simply restarts the query at the new name when it encounters a CNAME.

tensor_equation: For all referrals from zone Z: accepted_as_glue(glue) implies glue.name in bailiwick(Z). Only NS records and necessary A records at zone cut should be accepted.
title: DNS: Glue records must be in-bailiwick (RFC 1034 Section 4.2.1, RFC 2181 Sections 5.4.1, 6.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 2181 §6.1 — Servers should ignore data other than NS records, and necessary A records to locate the servers listed in the NS records.

tensor_equation: For all nodes in DNS_Namespace: CNAME in node.RRSet implies for all rr in node.RRSet: rr.type in {CNAME, RRSIG, NSEC}. No A/AAAA/MX/TXT etc at a CNAME node.
title: DNS: CNAME precludes non-DNSSEC RRs at same node (RFC 1034 Section 3.6.2, RFC 2181 Section 10.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 2181 §10.1 — One CNAME record exists, optionally accompanied by SIG, NXT, and KEY RRs. Or one or more records exist, none being CNAME records.

title: TLS 1.3: 1-RTT handshake message order is fixed (RFC 8446 Section 2, 4.4.1)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §2 (Figure 1), 4.4.1 — Protocol messages MUST be sent in the order defined in Section 4.4.1 and shown in the diagrams in Section 2.

verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §4.4.2, 4.4.3 — Section 4.4.2 covers the Certificate message. Section 4.4.3 (CertificateVerify) provides a signature over the entire handshake using the private key corresponding to the public key in the Certificate

tensor_equation: For all h in TLS_1.3_Handshakes: verify_data(Finished) = HMAC(finished_key, Hash(transcript_hash(h))). Integrity of entire handshake proved. Modified messages -> MAC fails.
title: TLS 1.3: Finished message MAC verifies transcript hash (RFC 8446 Section 4.4.4)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §4.4.4 — A MAC (Message Authentication Code) over the entire handshake. This message provides key confirmation, binds the endpoint's identity to the exchanged keys.

prompt_injection: From RFC 8446 Section 1.2, what key exchange mechanisms are permitted in TLS 1.3? What guarantee does (EC)DHE provide?
redteam_pattern: TLS_ECDHE_RSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256: valid. TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA: not in TLS 1.3 cipher suite registry, handshake must fail.
tensor_equation: For all c in TLS_1.3_Connections with non-PSK-only key exchange: c.key_exchange in {ECDHE, DHE}. Static RSA and static DH cipher suites removed. PSK-only mode loses forward secrecy.
title: TLS 1.3: Forward secrecy via ephemeral key exchange only (RFC 8446 Sections 1.2, 2)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §1.2, 2 — Static RSA and Diffie-Hellman cipher suites have been removed; all public-key based key exchange mechanisms now provide forward secrecy.

prompt_injection: From RFC 8446, what are the security properties (and lack thereof) of TLS 1.3 0-RTT data? What anti-replay mechanisms exist?

tensor_equation: For all d in TLS_1.3_0-RTT_Data: forward_secret(d) = false and replay_protection(d) = server_application_defined. Anti-replay: single-use tickets, ClientHello recording, freshness checks.
title: TLS 1.3: 0-RTT data is NOT forward secret and is replayable (RFC 8446 Sections 2.3, 8)
verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §2.3, 8 — This data is not forward secret, as it is encrypted solely under keys derived using the offered PSK.

tensor_equation: For all c in TLS_1.3_Connections: c.cipher_suite in {TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256, TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384, TLS_CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256}. CBC and stream ciphers removed.

verdict_evidence: CITED: RFC 8446 §1.2, 9.1 — Those that remain are all Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) algorithms. TLS_AES_128_GCM_SHA256 and TLS_AES_256_GCM_SHA384 are mandatory-to-implement cipher suites. TLS_CHACHA20_POLY

prompt_injection:
Floating-point arithmetic is specified by IEEE 754. The standard guarantees that addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and square root produce the correctly-rounded result (round-to-nearest-ties-to-even being the default). However, floating-point multiplication is not associative over addition, and addition itself is not associative: there exist values a,b,c such that (a+b)+c != a+(b+c). This is a direct consequence of the finite precision of the mantissa. The invariant is: any program that assumes associativity of floating-point addition is numerically unstable.

Invariant: ∀a,b,c ∈ Float: (a ⊕ b) ⊕ c ≠ a ⊕ (b ⊕ c) for some a,b,c
where ⊕ is IEEE 754 addition with round-to-nearest-even

Counterexample: a = 1e30, b = -1e30, c = 1.0
  (1e30 ⊕ -1e30) ⊕ 1 = 0 ⊕ 1 = 1
  1e30 ⊕ (-1e30 ⊕ 1) = 1e30 ⊕ -1e30 = 0 (catastrophic cancellation)
  Result: 1 ≠ 0
source_document: Goldberg 1991, "What Every Computer Scientist Should Know About Floating-Point Arithmetic"; IEEE 754-2019
tensor_equation: ∃a,b,c ∈ Float: (a ⊕ b) ⊕ c ≠ a ⊕ (b ⊕ c) where ⊕ is IEEE 754 addition with round-to-nearest-even. (The equation was incorrectly universally quantified — only some float triples exhibit non-associativity.)
title: Floating-point associativity violation (Goldberg 1991)
verdict_discrepancy: QUANTIFIER ERROR — the equation as written is false. It binds 'for-all a,b,c in Float' and then asserts the *inequality* (a+b)+c != a+(b+c), while trailing 'for some a,b,c' contradicts the leading universal. Direct counterexample to the axiom as stated: a = b = c = 0.0 gives (0+0)+0 = 0 = 0+(0+0), so the universally-quantified inequality fails; likewise any small exactly-representable integers, e.g. (1+2)+3 = 1+(2+3) = 6. Goldberg's claim is existential: exists a,b,c such that associativity fails. The correct form is 'exists a,b,c in Float: (a (+) b) (+) c != a (+) (b (+) c)'. A gate implementing the axiom literally would fail on the first well-behaved triple it sampled.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
Catastrophic cancellation occurs when subtracting two nearly equal floating-point numbers. The leading significant digits cancel, leaving only the error terms. The correctly-rounded subtraction of two numbers that are close in value produces a result with only ⌈log_b(max(|x|,|y|)/|x-y|)⌉ significant digits — the rest are noise. No amount of extra precision in the intermediate computation can recover these lost digits once they are gone.

Invariant: ∀x,y ∈ Float: |x - y| ≪ max(|x|,|y|) → lost_significant_digits(x ⊖ y) ≈ |log_b(max(|x|,|y|)/|x-y|)|

Example: a = 1.0000000000000002, b = 1.0
  a ⊖ b = 2.220446049250313e-16 (only ~1 significant digit, rest is rounding noise)
  The true difference has 53 bits of precision but the subtraction only yields ~1 bit
source_document: Goldberg 1991; Higham 2002, "Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms", 2nd ed., SIAM
tensor_equation: ∀x,y ∈ Float: |x - y| ≪ max(|x|,|y|) → lost_significant_digits(x ⊖ y) ≈ |log_b(max(|x|,|y|)/|x-y|)|
title: Catastrophic cancellation (Higham 2002)
verdict_evidence: Higham, 'Accuracy and Stability of Numerical Algorithms', 2nd ed. (SIAM 2002), Section 1.7 'Cancellation' shows that when x and y are nearly equal the subtraction itself is benign but pre-existing errors in x and y are amplified by the factor max(|x|,|y|)/|x-y|, so the count of leading significant digits lost is the base-b logarithm of that amplification factor - exactly the stated formula. Goldberg (1991, ACM Computing Surveys 23(1)) makes the same point in his 'Cancellation' section using the quadratic formula. Falsifiable and demonstrable: in IEEE double, (1 + 1e-16) - 1 returns 0 rather than 1e-16 - all significant digits lost, matching log10(1/1e-16) ~ 16.

prompt_injection:
Naive summation of n floating-point values has error that grows linearly with n. Kahan's compensated summation algorithm reduces the error bound from O(nε) to O(ε + nε²), making it effectively independent of n for practical n. The invariant is: for any sequence of floating-point values, Kahan summation accumulates error at most 2ε times the sum of absolute values, regardless of n.

Invariant: Let S_n = naive_sum(a_1,...,a_n), K_n = kahan_sum(a_1,...,a_n), exact = Σ a_i (in ℝ)
  |S_n - exact| ≤ (n-1)ε Σ|a_i| + O(ε²)  (worst-case: O(nε))
  |K_n - exact| ≤ 2ε Σ|a_i| + O(ε²)      (worst-case: O(ε), independent of n)

Example: a_1 = 1.0, a_2 = 1e-16, a_3 = -1.0, a_4 = 1e-16
  Naive: (1.0 + 1e-16) + (-1.0) + 1e-16 = 0.0 (both 1e-16 lost)
  Kahan: 2e-16 (correct — the small terms survive)
source_document: Kahan 1965, "Further Remarks on Reducing Truncation Errors"; Higham 2002, Ch. 4
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ ℕ, ∀a_i ∈ Float: |kahan_sum(a_1,...,a_n) - Σa_i| ≤ [2u + O(n u²)] * Σ|a_i|. The second-order term carries a factor n and multiplies Σ|a_i|, not just O(ε²).
title: Kahan summation error bound (Kahan 1965)
verdict_discrepancy: The bound is quoted with the n dropped. Higham's result for compensated summation is |E_n| <= [2u + O(n u^2)] * sum|a_i| — the second-order term carries a factor n and multiplies sum|a_i|. The axiom writes '+ O(eps^2)', which (a) loses the n, making the stated bound strictly tighter than what Higham proves and false for large n where n*u is not negligible, and (b) is dimensionally inconsistent — an absolute additive O(eps^2) term independent of the data magnitude, whereas the true term scales with sum|a_i|. As written the axiom would be violated by a legitimate Kahan implementation on a sufficiently long ill-conditioned input.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
In an asynchronous distributed system with no shared clock and no bounded message delay, it is impossible to guarantee exactly-once message delivery. This is a corollary of the Two Generals' Problem (Gray 1978) and the Fischer-Lynch-Paterson impossibility result (FLP 1985). The only practical approximations are: at-most-once (lossy but safe) or at-least-once with idempotent processing. Any system claiming "exactly-once delivery" without an idempotent receiver or a distributed consensus protocol is incorrect.

Invariant: ∀protocol P operating over an asynchronous network with unreliable channels: P cannot guarantee exactly-once delivery of a message to a recipient.
  ∀system S claiming exactly-once delivery: S MUST implement either (a) idempotent receiver, or (b) distributed consensus with fault-tolerant commit, or (c) exactly-once is approximated by at-least-once + idempotency
source_document: Gray 1978, "Notes on Database Operating Systems"; Fischer, Lynch & Paterson 1985, "Impossibility of Distributed Consensus"; Hohpe & Woolf 2003, "Enterprise Integration Patterns", Ch. 6
tensor_equation: ∀protocol P over asynchronous network with unreliable channels: P cannot guarantee exactly-once delivery
title: Two Generals' impossibility of exactly-once (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: Gray's 1978 'Notes on Data Base Operating Systems' (Operating Systems: An Advanced Course, LNCS 60) is the paper that named and popularised the Two Generals Paradox (p.465 ff.), presenting it as the impossibility of guaranteed agreement over a lossy channel: no finite protocol suffices, because the last message sent is never known to have arrived. The corollary for messaging - that guaranteed exactly-once delivery is unattainable over unreliable channels, and that systems must instead use at-least-once plus idempotent/deduplicated processing - is exactly the position taken in Hohpe & Woolf's Enterprise Integration Patterns. Falsifiable and non-trivial: over a RELIABLE, synchronous, ordered channel with no crashes, exactly-once delivery is achievable, so the antecedent does real work; and any implementation claiming exactly-once delivery (rather than exactly-once effect) contradicts it.

prompt_injection:
