A saga is a sequence of local transactions where each step has a compensating transaction that semantically undoes its effects. If any step fails, the saga executes the compensating transactions for all completed steps in reverse order. The invariant is: every transactional step in a saga must have a corresponding compensating transaction, and the composition of a step with its compensation must be equivalent to a no-op from the system's perspective.

Invariant: ∀saga S = ⟨t₁, t₂, ..., tₙ⟩ with compensations C = ⟨c₁, c₂, ..., cₙ⟩:
  ∀i ∈ [1,n]: (tᵢ ∘ cᵢ) ≡ identity_at_level(tᵢ)
  ∀failure at step j: recovery(S, j) = c₍j₋1₎ ∘ c₍j₋2₎ ∘ ... ∘ c₁

Example: Flight booking (t₁) + Hotel booking (t₂) + Payment (t₃)
  If payment fails: cancel hotel (c₂) + cancel flight (c₁)
  Each compensation must be itself retriable and idempotent
source_document: Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987, "SAGAS"; Hohpe & Woolf 2003, Sec. 11.3; Pat Helland 2007
tensor_equation: ∀saga S = ⟨t₁,...,tₙ⟩: ∀i ∈ [1,n]: ∃compensation cᵢ such that applying tᵢ then cᵢ restores semantic consistency (not necessarily identity). The paper requires semantic undo, not (tᵢ ∘ cᵢ) ≡ identity.
title: Saga compensation completeness (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987)
verdict_discrepancy: STATES THE OPPOSITE OF THE CITED PAPER on the key point. The paper's requirement is semantic undo, not (Ti o Ci) == identity; asserting the composite is the identity is exactly the property the authors say sagas cannot provide (it would make sagas atomic, which is the whole point of relaxing atomicity). Counterexample against the axiom as stated: a payment saga where Ti charges a card and Ci issues a refund - the ledger afterwards contains two entries and a fee, so the state is not the pre-Ti state. Fidelity error also on the range: the paper does not require a compensation for the final transaction Tn, since a saga that reaches Tn commits forward.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection:
Data minimization is a core principle of GDPR (Article 5(1)(c)). A system must only collect, process, and retain personal data that is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the specific purpose. The invariant is: for every data field collected, there must be a documented purpose, and the field must be strictly necessary for that purpose. Any field collected "just in case" or "for future use" violates the invariant.

Invariant: ∀system S, ∀data field f ∈ schema(S):
  ∃purpose P such that P requires f ∧ ¬∃purpose P': P' ≠ P ∧ P' also requires f
  (each field maps to exactly one purpose — no over-collection)

Additionally: ∀data field f, ∀purpose P:
  retention_period(f, P) ≤ min_necessary(P)
  (no data kept longer than necessary for its purpose — delete when purpose is fulfilled)
source_document: Regulation (EU) 2016/679, General Data Protection Regulation, Article 5(1)(c), Recital 39; Article 25 (Data Protection by Design and Default)
tensor_equation: ∀system S, ∀data field f ∈ schema(S): ∃purpose P: field_required(f, P) ∧ retention(f, P) ≤ min_necessary(P)
title: Data minimization (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c))
verdict_evidence: GDPR Article 5(1)(c) requires personal data be 'adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes for which they are processed', and Recital 39 adds that personal data should be processed only if the purpose could not reasonably be fulfilled by other means and that the storage period be limited to a strict minimum. Article 25 (Data Protection by Design and by Default) makes minimisation a design obligation. Falsifiable and routinely enforced: a signup form collecting date of birth with no purpose requiring age, retained indefinitely, violates the equation - this is the exact shape of numerous DPA enforcement actions. Caveat: retention limits sit primarily in Article 5(1)(e) (storage limitation) rather than 5(1)(c); the axiom folds the two principles together, but Recital 39, which the citation names, does cover both.

prompt_injection:
The right to erasure ("right to be forgotten") under GDPR Article 17 requires that upon a validated request, the controller must erase personal data without undue delay. The invariant is: a deletion request must cascade through all systems, backups, and caches that hold the data subject's personal data. Any system that "forgets" to delete from one store (e.g., deletes from primary DB but not from analytics warehouse or backup) violates the invariant.

Invariant: ∀data subject U, ∀system S:
  validated_erasure_request(U) → ∀collection C ∈ S: contains_data_of(C, U) → delete_records(C, U)
  within regulatory timeline (Art. 17(1): "without undue delay", default 30 days)

Additionally: ∀backup B, ∀derived datasets D:
  after erasure(U): U's data must not appear in any restored backup used for processing
  U's data must not appear in any derived dataset, aggregate, or trained model

Exception: Art. 17(3) — processing necessary for compliance with legal obligation, defense of legal claims, or public health. These must be explicitly documented and limited.
source_document: Regulation (EU) 2016/679, GDPR Article 17, Recital 65-66; Article 29 Data Protection Working Party, "Guidelines on the Right to Erasure" (WP 256)
tensor_equation: ∀data subject U, ∀system S: validated_erasure_request(U) → ∀collection C: contains_data_of(C,U) → delete_records(C,U)
title: Right to erasure cascade (GDPR Art. 17)
verdict_discrepancy: (1) Attribution: 'Article 29 Data Protection Working Party, Guidelines on the Right to Erasure (WP 256)' does not exist. WP 256 is the WP29 working document on the elements and principles to be found in Binding Corporate Rules (superseded by EDPB Recommendations 1/2022). There is no WP29 right-to-erasure guideline; the closest instrument is EDPB Guidelines 5/2019 on the criteria of the Right to be Forgotten in search-engine cases. (2) Fidelity: Art. 17(3) carves out five categories where erasure does NOT apply (freedom of expression and information; compliance with a legal obligation or a public-interest task; public-health public interest; archiving/research/statistics under Art. 89(1); establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims). The unconditional '∀ collection C: contains_data_of(C,U) -> delete_records(C,U)' would require deleting records a controller is legally obliged to retain (tax, AML), i.e. it mandates a different legal violation. Reading 'validated' as already encoding Art. 17(3) makes the antecedent unbound and the rule untestable. (3) Art. 17(2) requires informing other controllers with reasonable steps, not deleting in third-party systems as the cascade implies.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
Audit trails must be append-only, immutable, and tamper-evident. Once an audit log entry is committed, it must not be modifiable, deletable, or reorderable within the retention period. The invariant is: the audit log is a sequence of entries where each entry has a monotonically increasing sequence number, a timestamp, and a cryptographic hash linking it to the previous entry. Any modification to a past entry breaks the hash chain.

Invariant: ∀audit_log L = ⟨e₁, e₂, ..., eₙ⟩:
  ∀i: committed(eᵢ) → ¬modifiable(eᵢ) ∧ ¬deletable(eᵢ) during retention period
  ∀i: hash(eᵢ) = H(seqᵢ || timestampᵢ || payloadᵢ || hash(eᵢ₋₁))
  (hash chain — any modification is detectable)

Additionally: ∀i: timestamp(eᵢ) ≤ timestamp(eᵢ₊₁) + δ  (monotonic timestamps, bounded clock skew)
  ∀i: seq(eᵢ) = seq(eᵢ₋₁) + 1  (no gaps, no duplicates)
  retention(L) ≥ regulatory_minimum (typically 3-7 years depending on jurisdiction)
source_document: GDPR Article 5(2) (Accountability), Article 30 (Records of Processing Activities); Schneier & Kelsey 1998, "Secure Audit Logs"; RFC 6962 (Certificate Transparency)
tensor_equation: ∀audit_log L = ⟨e₁,...,eₙ⟩: ∀i: committed(eᵢ) → tamper_evident(eᵢ) ∧ hash(eᵢ) = H(seqᵢ || tsᵢ || payloadᵢ || hash(eᵢ₋₁)). The scheme provides tamper-EVIDENCE, not immutability — an attacker with write access can modify or delete log entries, but tampering will be detectable at audit time.
title: Audit trail immutability (Schneier & Kelsey 1998)
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY + ATTRIBUTION: (a) The scheme provides tamper-EVIDENCE, not immutability. Its explicit goal is to make tampering detectable at audit time; an attacker with write access can still modify or delete entries (indeed the design has the untrusted machine delete all but the last element of the hash chain). '¬modifiable(e) ∧ ¬deletable(e)' asserts prevention the cited work never claims and cannot provide on an untrusted host. (b) The 1998 paper is 'Cryptographic Support for Secure Logs on Untrusted Machines' (7th USENIX Security Symposium); 'Secure Audit Logs to Support Computer Forensics' is the 1999 ACM TISSEC paper. (c) GDPR Art. 5(2)/30 impose accountability and record-keeping duties; they say nothing about hash chaining and cannot support the specific H(seq||ts||payload||hash(prev)) structure. (d) The scheme's integrity value comes from the keyed MAC with key evolution, which the equation drops entirely - an unkeyed hash chain is trivially recomputable by an attacker who rewrites the tail.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Any automated system that makes decisions affecting people MUST disclose its existence, capabilities, and limitations to those people. When you produce code that drives automated decisions, ensure there is a mechanism for affected parties to learn about the system's role, how it works, and its limitations. Never produce a system that makes opaque consequential decisions about people without disclosure.
source_document: ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct (2018), §1.3: 'Be honest and trustworthy. Provide full disclosure of all system capabilities, limitations, and potential problems.'
tensor_equation: ∀system S making decisions affecting person P: beforeDecision(S, P) → informed(P, existence(S), capabilities(S), limitations(S))
title: Transparency of Automated Decision-Making
verdict_discrepancy: SCOPE INFLATION: §1.3 requires disclosure to 'the appropriate parties' — typically employers, clients, and users — not to every person affected by every decision, and it imposes no BEFORE-decision timing requirement. The axiom converts a professional honesty duty into a per-decision, per-affected-individual prior-notification invariant that §1.3 does not state. Prior-notification of affected individuals is an EU AI Act Art. 50 / GDPR Art. 13-14 obligation, not an ACM §1.3 one.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: When collecting or processing personal data, you MUST collect only the minimum necessary for the system's function. Data must be encrypted at rest and in transit. Every datum must have a defined retention policy with automatic deletion. Never hardcode credentials, API keys, or personal data in source code, logs, or configuration files. Never log personal data without explicit justification and sanitization.
source_document: ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct (2018), §1.6: 'Respect privacy. Collect only the minimum data necessary.' §1.7: 'Honor confidentiality. Protect confidential information obtained through professional work.'
tensor_equation:
∀system S collecting personal data D: collected(D) → minimal(D, function(S)) ∧ protected(D, at_rest) ∧ protected(D, in_transit) ∧ consented(D, owner)
∀data retention R: R must have a defined expiry and deletion mechanism
title: Privacy by Design — Data Minimization
verdict_evidence: Clause numbers check out: ACM 2018 §1.6 is 'Respect privacy' and §1.7 is 'Honor confidentiality'. §1.6 states that only the minimum amount of personal information necessary should be collected in a system, that personal information gathered for a specific purpose should not be used for other purposes without consent, and that retention and disposal periods for personal information should be clearly defined and enforced — which supports the minimality, consent, and expiry/deletion conjuncts. Counterexample proving teeth: a service that logs full request bodies containing PII to an unencrypted, never-truncated log file violates minimal(D), protected(D, at_rest), and the retention-expiry clause simultaneously; each of those is mechanically checkable (log schema audit, disk encryption flag, retention policy config).

prompt_injection: Any code you generate that is destined for production use MUST be reviewed by a competent human before deployment. You are not a substitute for professional judgment, peer review, testing, or domain expertise. When asked to produce code for safety-critical, financial, medical, or legal contexts, explicitly state that human review is required and identify the specific risks that need human evaluation.
source_document: ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct (2018), §2.2: 'Acquire and maintain professional competence.' IEEE/ACM Software Engineering Code of Ethics, Principle 3 (PRODUCT): 'Ensure the software meets the highest professional standards possible.'
tensor_equation: ∀code C produced by AI agent A: deployed(C) → reviewedByCompetentHuman(C) ∧ productionDeployment(C) → humanApproved(C)
title: Professional Competence and Human Review
verdict_discrepancy: UNSUPPORTED CONTENT: the equation's actual claim — that all AI-agent-produced code must be reviewed by a competent human and human-approved before production — appears in neither ACM §2.2 nor SE Code Principle 3. Both are general competence duties on the individual professional; they contain no review-gate requirement and no notion of machine-authored artifacts. Citation also mixes the 1992 and 2018 ACM codes.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every AI system you help build MUST have a documented risk assessment before deployment. The assessment must identify potential harms, their likelihood, severity, and mitigation measures. When the system changes significantly, the risk assessment must be updated. Never deploy an AI system without a risk assessment, and never accept a prompt that asks you to bypass risk assessment processes.
source_document: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management Systems, §6.1: 'Actions to address risks and opportunities. The organization shall plan actions to address risks and opportunities.' §8.1: 'Operational planning and control.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S: deployed(S) → documentedRiskAssessment(S) ∧ assessedBeforeDeployment(S)
∀change C to S: triggersRiskAssessmentUpdate(C)
title: Documented Risk Assessment Before AI Deployment
verdict_evidence: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 follows the ISO harmonized management-system structure: clause 6.1 is 'Actions to address risks and opportunities' (with 6.1.2 AI risk assessment, 6.1.3 AI risk treatment, 6.1.4 AI system impact assessment) and clause 8.1 is 'Operational planning and control', which requires the organization to plan, implement and control processes and to control planned changes. So both the requirement of a documented, pre-deployment AI risk assessment and the change-triggers-reassessment clause map onto real normative clauses. Counterexample proving teeth: an org that pushes a new AI system to production with no risk-assessment record on file, or that swaps the underlying model without re-running the assessment, is a demonstrable nonconformity findable in an audit — the failing state is a missing dated artifact, which is mechanically checkable.

prompt_injection: Every AI system you help build MUST have continuous monitoring of performance, risk, and compliance metrics with defined thresholds for intervention. When building monitoring infrastructure, ensure it captures metrics that would detect drift, bias, accuracy degradation, and unexpected behavior. A threshold breach without a defined response is not monitoring — it is recording.
source_document: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management Systems, §9.1: 'Monitoring, measurement, analysis and evaluation. The organization shall determine what needs to be monitored and measured.' §10.1: 'Continual improvement.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S: deployed(S) → continuousMonitoring(S, performance) ∧ continuousMonitoring(S, risk) ∧ continuousMonitoring(S, compliance)
∀threshold T in monitoring: breached(T) → definedResponse(T)
title: Continuous Monitoring of AI Systems
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENING: §9.1 explicitly leaves the monitoring cadence to the organization to determine — periodic monitoring satisfies the clause. The axiom converts this into 'continuousMonitoring' across three fixed dimensions (performance, risk, compliance) for every deployed system, which the standard does not require. The second line ('∀threshold breached → definedResponse') is also not in §9.1 or §10.1; the nearest real requirement is §10.2 nonconformity and corrective action, which is reactive to nonconformities, not to numeric thresholds. An ISO-conformant org doing quarterly evaluation would fail this axiom while passing the cited clause.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every AI system you help build MUST have defined human oversight mechanisms appropriate to its risk level. The mechanisms must be documented, tested, and accessible to operators. The human must be able to override, stop, or redirect the system. Never build an AI system that operates without human oversight, cannot be stopped, or makes irreversible decisions without human confirmation.
source_document: ISO/IEC 42001:2023 AI Management Systems, §7.5: 'Competence and awareness — ensuring personnel are competent and aware of their role in AI oversight.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S: deployed(S) → definedHumanOversight(S) ∧ documented(OversightMechanism(S)) ∧ tested(OversightMechanism(S))
∀oversight mechanism M: accessible(M, operator) ∧ actionable(M) ∧ overrideable(M)
title: Defined Human Oversight Mechanisms
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG CLAUSE: §7.5 is 'Documented information', not 'Competence and awareness'; the quoted text corresponds to clauses 7.2/7.3. Neither 7.2, 7.3 nor 7.5 requires a defined, documented and TESTED human-oversight mechanism that is accessible, actionable and overrideable — that requirement is imported from EU AI Act Art. 14 and attributed to the wrong standard and the wrong clause.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Any system that interacts with humans MUST disclose that it is an AI system. This disclosure must be clear, timely, and unambiguous — not buried in terms of service. When you produce conversational interfaces, chatbots, or automated decision tools, ensure the interaction design includes a clear disclosure. Never impersonate a human or allow a system to do so without explicit disclosure.
source_document: EU AI Act (2024), Article 50: 'Transparency obligations for providers and deployers of certain AI systems. Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that natural persons are informed that they are interacting with an AI system.'
tensor_equation:
∀system S interacting with human H: S → disclose(S, AI, H)
∀disclosure D: D must be clear, timely, and unambiguous — not buried in terms of service or fine print
title: Disclosure of AI Interaction
verdict_discrepancy: DROPPED STATUTORY EXCEPTIONS + SCOPE INFLATION: the axiom's unconditional '∀ system S interacting with human H' covers every system that touches a human, whereas Art. 50(1) is scoped to systems INTENDED TO INTERACT DIRECTLY with natural persons, and carries two express carve-outs (obviousness to a reasonably well-informed person; law-enforcement authorisation) that the axiom deletes. A visibly-labelled AI product, or a lawful investigative system, satisfies Art. 50(1) while failing this axiom. The second line ('not buried in terms of service') is the author's gloss, not Art. 50 text.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Content generated by AI systems MUST be labeled as AI-generated. The label must be both machine-readable (metadata, headers) and human-readable (visible disclosure). When you generate code that produces content, include mechanisms for labeling. When you generate content directly, label it. Deepfakes and synthetic media require additional disclosure about their artificial nature.
source_document: EU AI Act (2024), Article 50(2): 'Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates image, audio or video content constituting a deep fake shall disclose that the content has been artificially generated or manipulated.'
tensor_equation: ∀content C generated by AI system S: C → labeled(C, AI_generated) ∧ label(C) must be machine-readable AND human-readable
title: AI-Generated Content Labeling
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG PARAGRAPH + CONFLATED OBLIGATIONS: the quote is Art. 50(4) mislabelled as 50(2). The axiom then fuses two distinct duties on two distinct actors into one rule — machine-readable marking (provider, 50(2), all synthetic content, feasibility-limited) and human-facing disclosure (deployer, 50(4), deep fakes only, with artistic and law-enforcement exemptions). No provision requires that ALL AI-generated content carry both a machine-readable AND a human-readable label; an AI-drafted internal email marked only machine-readably is Art. 50 compliant but violates this axiom.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every AI system that makes decisions affecting individuals MUST be tested for bias across protected attributes (race, gender, age, religion, disability, socioeconomic status, etc.) before deployment and periodically after. When you build or modify such systems, ensure bias testing is part of the pipeline. If bias is found, it must be documented, mitigated, and reported. Never deploy a system with known, unmitigated bias that causes disparate impact.
source_document: Responsible AI — Fairness principle. 'Fairness requires that AI systems treat all people fairly, without discrimination or bias against individuals or groups based on protected characteristics.' See also ACM Code of Ethics §1.4: 'Be fair and take action not to discriminate.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S making decisions affecting individuals: deployed(S) → testedForBias(S, protectedAttributes) ∧ periodicBiasTesting(S) ∧ retestedAfterChange(S)
∀bias B found in S: B must be documented, mitigated, and reported before deployment continues
title: Bias Testing Across Protected Attributes
verdict_discrepancy: UNLOCATABLE PRIMARY SOURCE + UNSUPPORTED OPERATIONAL CONTENT: 'Responsible AI — Fairness principle' is not a citable work. ACM §1.4, the only verifiable source given, states a duty not to discriminate; it says nothing about bias TESTING, protected-attribute test suites, periodic retesting, retesting after change, or documenting and mitigating found bias before deployment continues. The entire operational apparatus of the axiom is asserted with no source that states it.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Every AI system MUST have a designated human accountable for its outputs, behavior, and impacts. The accountable human must be identifiable, aware of their responsibility, and capable of intervening. When you produce AI systems, ensure there is a clear chain of accountability that ends with a human, not with the system itself. Never build a system where no human is responsible for its behavior.
source_document: Responsible AI — Accountability principle. 'Accountability requires that those who design, develop, and deploy AI systems are responsible for their impacts and must be identifiable and able to explain and justify decisions.' ISO/IEC 42001:2023 §5.1: 'Leadership and commitment — top management shall demonstrate accountability.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S: deployed(S) → ∃human H: accountable(H, S) ∧ known(H, accountableFor(S)) ∧ capacitated(H, intervene(S))
∀decision D made by S: traceable(D, S) ∧ traceable(S, H)
title: Designated Human Accountability for AI Systems
verdict_discrepancy: SOURCE DOES NOT STATE THE CLAIM: §5.1 places accountability for the effectiveness of the AI MANAGEMENT SYSTEM on top management as a body. It does not require a designated individual human accountable for each deployed AI system, that this person be known as such, that they be capacitated to intervene, or that every decision be traceable from the decision through the system to that person. Per-system named ownership and decision traceability are the axiom author's additions. The quoted 'top management shall demonstrate accountability' is also a paraphrase; the clause reads 'demonstrate leadership and commitment'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: AI systems that make significant decisions affecting individuals MUST provide explanations at a level of detail appropriate to the audience. A 'significant decision' includes hiring, lending, medical diagnosis, criminal justice, access to services, and similar. The explanation must be correct, meaningful, and timely — not a post-hoc rationalization. When you build such systems, ensure the explanation mechanism is designed as a core feature, not an afterthought.
source_document: Responsible AI — Explainability principle. 'Explainability requires that the decisions and behaviors of AI systems can be understood by humans, particularly those affected by them.' EU AI Act (2024) Article 13: 'Transparency and provision of information to deployers.'
tensor_equation:
∀AI system S making significant decision D affecting individual I: S → explainable(D, I) ∧ audienceAppropriate(explanation(D, I))
∀explanation X: X must be correct, meaningful, and timely — not a post-hoc rationalization
title: Explainability of Significant Automated Decisions
verdict_discrepancy: WRONG ARTICLE + UNLOCATABLE PRIMARY SOURCE: the axiom binds explainability to the affected individual I, but Art. 13 runs to deployers, not to affected individuals; the correct provision is Art. 86, which is narrower than the axiom's ∀ over all significant decisions. The second line ('X must be correct, meaningful, and timely — not a post-hoc rationalization') has no source and no operational test: there is no procedure that could show a given explanation to be a post-hoc rationalization, so that conjunct cannot be failed.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Adding people to a late software project makes it later. This is not negotiable — it is a proven empirical law. When asked to accelerate a project by adding more people, explicitly state that the effect will be negative. Instead, recommend reducing scope, prioritizing features, or extending the schedule. Never suggest 'adding more engineers' as a solution to a late project.
source_document: Brooks, F.P. 'The Mythical Man-Month: Essays on Software Engineering' (1975, 1995), Chapter 2: 'The Mythical Man-Month.' Brooks' Law: 'Adding manpower to a late software project makes it later.'
tensor_equation: ∀software project P: behindSchedule(P) ∧ addPeople(P, n) → completionTime(P) may increase (training overhead + communication overhead > marginal productivity gain), but this is a tendency that Brooks himself calls an outrageous oversimplification, and he expressly exempts partitionable work needing no intercommunication.
title: Brooks' Law — Adding People to Late Projects
verdict_discrepancy: OVER-HARDENING OF A SCOPED HEURISTIC: Brooks states a tendency he calls an outrageous oversimplification and expressly exempts partitionable work needing no intercommunication; the axiom asserts unconditional monotonic increase for every late project and every n. It also asserts the inequality 'training + communication overhead > marginal productivity gain' as universally true, which is exactly the conditional Brooks makes contingent on task partitionability. McConnell ('Brooks' Law Repealed') documents the contested empirical status of the strong form.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Every function MUST validate all inputs from external sources before processing them. This includes user input, API parameters, file contents, network responses, database records, and deserialized data. Invalid inputs must be rejected with a clear error message, never silently accepted. Validate at the trust boundary — not deep in the code where the context of the original source is lost. Never trust data that crossed a network, a file system boundary, or a user interface.
source_document: McConnell, S. 'Code Complete' (2004), Chapter 8: 'Defensive Programming. §8.1: Protecting your program from garbage in — all input should be sanitized before it is used. §8.2: Assertions — document assumptions and catch unexpected conditions.'
tensor_equation: ∀trust boundary crossing: validate at the boundary, not deep in the code. ∀input I: invalid(I) → reject(I, clearError(I)) ∧ ¬acceptSilently(I).
title: Defensive Programming — Input Validation on All External Inputs
verdict_discrepancy: INTERNALLY CONTRADICTORY + MISREPRESENTS McCONNELL: line 1 requires every function F to validate its external inputs; line 3 requires validation at the trust boundary and not deep in the code. These conjuncts cannot both hold for a function inside the barricade — it either revalidates (violating line 3) or does not (violating line 1), so the axiom is unsatisfiable as written for any layered program. McConnell's position is line 3's, explicitly: validate at the barricade, assert inside it. The §8.1 title is also misquoted.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection: Assertions must document and enforce invariants — conditions that MUST be true if the code is correct. Assertions must NEVER have side effects; evaluating an assertion must not change program state. Assertions must remain enabled during development and testing. Never remove assertions for performance, and never use them for normal error handling. If an assertion fires, it indicates a bug, not a runtime condition.
source_document: McConnell, S. 'Code Complete' (2004), Chapter 8.2: 'Assertions. Use assertions to document assumptions made in the code and to flush out unexpected conditions. An assertion should not have side effects.'
tensor_equation:
∀assertion A: A must check a condition that MUST be true if the code is correct
∀assertion A: A must NOT have side effects — evaluating A must not change program state
∀assertion A: A must remain enabled during development and testing, never removed for performance
title: Assertions Must Document Invariants, Not Have Side Effects
verdict_evidence: Code Complete 2nd ed. §8.2 'Assertions' supports all three clauses: assertions are for conditions that must be true if the code is correct and should never occur in a correct program (McConnell's rule is to use error-handling code for conditions you expect to occur and assertions for conditions that should never occur); 'Avoid putting executable code into assertions' is a named guideline, on the grounds that code in an assertion disappears when assertions are compiled out — i.e. assertions must be side-effect-free; and assertions are to be used during development and testing to document assumptions and flush out unexpected conditions. Counterexample proving teeth: `assert(buf[i++] != 0)` mutates i, so the program behaves differently when assertions are disabled — a mechanically detectable violation of clause 2 (grep for assignment, increment, or call-with-effects inside assert expressions).

prompt_injection: Design code to accommodate change by hiding implementation details behind stable interfaces. The interface is the contract; the implementation must be replaceable without affecting consumers. When you produce code, separate interface from implementation. When you modify code, changing the implementation must not require changing consumers. If you must change an interface, update all consumers in the same change.
source_document: McConnell, S. 'Code Complete' (2004), Chapter 5: 'Design. §5.3: Information hiding — hide secrets (design decisions likely to change) behind stable interfaces. §5.5: Design for change — anticipate and accommodate change.' Parnas, D.L. 'On the Criteria to Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules' (1972).
tensor_equation:
∀module M: implementationDetails(M) must be hidden from consumers of M
∀interface I: I must be stable before consumers depend on it
∀change C to M: if C changes implementationDetails(M) → C must not change interface I(M)
∀change C to M: if C changes interface I(M) → C must update all consumers of M
title: Design for Change — Information Hiding and Stable Interfaces
verdict_evidence: Code Complete 2nd ed. Ch. 5 ('Design in Construction') presents information hiding as a design heuristic — hide secrets, i.e. design decisions most likely to change, behind stable interfaces — and 'Anticipate Different Degrees of Change' / identifying areas likely to change is a named heuristic in the same chapter. Parnas, 'On the Criteria to Be Used in Decomposing Systems into Modules' (CACM 15(12), 1972) is the correct primary source for the modularization criterion, and McConnell cites it. Counterexample proving teeth: exporting a mutable struct field that consumers read directly, then changing its representation, breaks every consumer — a violation of clause 1 detectable by an exported-API diff, and clause 4 is enforceable by a compile of downstream consumers.

prompt_injection: Every bug fix MUST be accompanied by a regression test that fails before the fix and passes after. The test must be checked into the test suite, not discarded. Additionally, every fix must include root cause analysis — identify why the bug was possible, not just patch the symptom. Never produce a fix without a test that proves the fix works and prevents regression.
source_document: McConnell, S. 'Code Complete' (2004), Chapter 22: 'Developer Testing. §22.2: Test first — write the test before the fix. §22.3: Regression testing — ensure that fixes don't break existing functionality.' Beizer, B. 'Software Testing Techniques' (1990): 'The goal of testing is not to find bugs but to find the conditions under which bugs occur.'
tensor_equation:
∀bug fix F: F → ∃test T: failing(T, preFix) ∧ passing(T, postFix)
∀test T created for fix F: T must be checked into the test suite, not discarded
∀fix F: F must include root cause analysis, not just symptom treatment
title: Regression Test for Every Bug Fix
verdict_discrepancy: UNATTESTED QUOTE + WRONG SECTION LABELS + UNSUPPORTED CLAUSE: 'The goal of testing is not to find bugs but to find the conditions under which bugs occur' is not locatable in Beizer (1990) and inverts his stated view that testing is done to find bugs. The two Code Complete section numbers are mislabelled. The third line — that every fix must include root cause analysis rather than symptom treatment — is not stated in Ch. 22 and, as written, has no operational test distinguishing a root-cause fix from a symptom fix. The first line (a test failing before the fix and passing after) is genuinely falsifiable and is the only part with teeth, but it is the axiom author's formulation, not a quoted requirement from either source.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A hash table maps 1000 distinct keys to 256 buckets. Is it guaranteed that at least one bucket contains at least ⌈1000/256⌉ = 4 keys? Prove or disprove using the pigeonhole principle. What is the minimum number of empty buckets guaranteed?
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.1. Also: Chapter 5, Exercise 3.
tensor_equation: ∀f: A → B, |A| > |B| ⇒ ∃b ∈ B: |f⁻¹(b)| ≥ 2. Strong form: ∀n,k ∈ ℕ, distributing n items into k boxes ⇒ at least one box has ≥ ⌈n/k⌉ items.
title: Pigeonhole Principle: any mapping from a larger set to a smaller set forces a collision
verdict_discrepancy: Misattribution. CM §2.1 is 'Notation' (sum notation); the pigeonhole principle is not stated there, nor in CM Ch. 5. Standard citations are Brualdi, Introductory Combinatorics Ch. 3, or CLRS Appendix B/Ex. 11.2 (for hashing collisions). The 'Chapter 5, Exercise 3' pointer is also spurious — CM Ch. 5 exercises are on binomial coefficient identities.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Three services each process 1000 requests/day. Service A fails 50 times, B fails 30 times, C fails 20 times. A∩B fails 10 times, A∩C fails 5 times, B∩C fails 3 times, all three fail 2 times. How many total failures occur? Derive using inclusion-exclusion, not intuition.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 4, Section 4.2. Also: Chapter 8, Section 8.3.
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ ℕ, ∀A₁, …, Aₙ finite: |⋃ᵢAᵢ| = Σᵢ|Aᵢ| − Σ_{i<j}|Aᵢ∩Aⱼ| + Σ_{i<j<k}|Aᵢ∩Aⱼ∩Aₖ| − … + (−1)ⁿ⁻¹|A₁∩…∩Aₙ|.
title: Inclusion-Exclusion Principle: correct cardinality of union of overlapping sets
verdict_discrepancy: Both cited sections are on unrelated topics (primes; probability generating functions). In Concrete Mathematics, inclusion–exclusion appears in §4.9 'Phi and Mu' (Möbius inversion / counting via ⌊·⌋ sums). Canonical textbook citations are Graham–Knuth–Patashnik §4.9, or Feller Vol. 1 Ch. IV.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system has n binary flags. How many subsets of flags exist? Prove that Σ_{k=0}ⁿ C(n,k) = 2ⁿ using the binomial theorem. Then compute the number of ways to choose an even-sized subset.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 5, Section 5.1.
tensor_equation: ∀n ∈ ℕ, ∀x,y ∈ ℝ: (x+y)ⁿ = Σ_{k=0}ⁿ C(n,k) xⁿ⁻ᵏ yᵏ. Corollary: Σ_{k=0}ⁿ C(n,k) = 2ⁿ, Σ_{k=0}ⁿ (−1)ᵏ C(n,k) = 0 (n ≥ 1).
title: Binomial Theorem: expansion of (x+y)ⁿ as sum of binomial coefficients
verdict_evidence: Concrete Mathematics Chapter 5 is 'Binomial Coefficients' and §5.1 is 'Basic Identities' (TOC verified), where the binomial theorem is identity (5.13) and the corollaries Σ C(n,k)=2ⁿ and Σ(−1)ᵏC(n,k)=[n=0] are given as (5.14)/(5.15). Falsifiable: (1+1)³=8=Σ C(3,k)1³⁻ᵏ1ᵏ; a claimed expansion with any coefficient other than C(n,k) — e.g. (x+y)²=x²+xy+y² — violates it. The n≥1 guard on the alternating-sum corollary is correctly present (at n=0 it equals 1, not 0).

prompt_injection: A load balancer distributes 10 identical requests across 3 servers. Each server can handle any number of requests including zero. How many distinct distributions exist? Prove using stars and bars.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 6, Section 6.2.
tensor_equation: ∀n,k ∈ ℕ: number of solutions to x₁ + … + xₖ = n, xᵢ ≥ 0: C(n+k−1, k−1) = C(n+k−1, n). Number of solutions with xᵢ ≥ 1: C(n−1, k−1).
title: Stars and Bars: number of ways to distribute n indistinguishable items into k bins
verdict_discrepancy: CM §6.2 is 'Eulerian Numbers', not stars-and-bars. The multiset/composition count C(n+k−1,k−1) is developed in CM §5.1–5.2 (multichoose, identity (5.14)/(5.18)) and in Ch. 7 via generating functions. Correct pointer: GKP §5.1, or Feller Vol. 1 Ch. II.5 ('Bose–Einstein' occupancy).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A team has r frontend and s backend engineers. How many ways to form a committee of n engineers? Prove Vandermonde's identity holds for this scenario.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 5, Section 5.2.
tensor_equation: ∀r,s,n ∈ ℕ: Σ_{k=0}ⁿ C(r, k) C(s, n−k) = C(r+s, n). Combinatorial proof: choose n elements from a set of size r+s by selecting k from the first r and n−k from the remaining s.
title: Vandermonde's Identity: convolution of binomial coefficients
verdict_evidence: Vandermonde's convolution Σₖ C(r,k)C(s,n−k) = C(r+s,n) is identity (5.27) in Concrete Mathematics Ch. 5 ('Binomial Coefficients'); §5.2 'Basic Practice' (TOC verified) is where GKP drill it on worked sums. Falsifiable: r=s=2, n=2 gives 1·1+2·2+1·1=6=C(4,2); replacing C(r+s,n) by C(r+s,2n) breaks at these values (C(4,4)=1≠6). Quantifiers are correctly stated (the identity is in fact valid for all real r,s and integer n≥0, so restricting to ℕ is a sound weakening, not an over-claim).

prompt_injection: A password hashing system generates all permutations of a 52-character alphabet. Using Stirling's approximation, estimate log₂(52!) to within 1 bit. Is 52! ≈ 2²²⁵ or 2²²⁶? Show the calculation.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 9, Section 9.6.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: √(2πn) (n/e)ⁿ e^{1/(12n+1)} < n! < √(2πn) (n/e)ⁿ e^{1/(12n)} (Robbins 1955). Equivalent: n! ∼ √(2πn) (n/e)ⁿ as n → ∞. Relative error < 1% for n ≥ 10.
title: Stirling's Approximation: bounds on factorial growth
verdict_discrepancy: Attribution error on the sharp bound. The inequality √(2πn)(n/e)ⁿe^{1/(12n+1)} < n! < √(2πn)(n/e)ⁿe^{1/(12n)} is Robbins, 'A Remark on Stirling's Formula', Amer. Math. Monthly 62 (1955), 26–29. Concrete Mathematics §9.6 gives the divergent asymptotic series form (with an O-term), not this pair of strict inequalities.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A recursive Fibonacci implementation has exponential time complexity O(φⁿ). Prove that the closed form Fₙ = ⌊φⁿ/√5 + 1/2⌋ is correct. What is the asymptotic growth rate of Fₙ?
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 6, Section 6.6.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 0: Fₙ = (φⁿ − ψⁿ)/√5 where φ = (1+√5)/2 (golden ratio), ψ = (1−√5)/2 = −1/φ. Fₙ = ⌊φⁿ/√5 + 1/2⌋. Fₙ ∼ φⁿ/√5 as n → ∞.
title: Fibonacci Closed Form (Binet's Formula): exact expression for Fibonacci numbers
verdict_evidence: CM Chapter 6 is 'Special Numbers' and §6.6 is 'Fibonacci Numbers' (TOC verified); Binet's formula Fₙ=(φⁿ−ψⁿ)/√5 is (6.123) and the rounding form 'Fₙ is φⁿ/√5 rounded to the nearest integer' is (6.124), stated there for n≥0. Falsifiable and checked at the boundary: n=0 → ⌊0.4472+0.5⌋=0=F₀; n=1 → ⌊0.7236+0.5⌋=1=F₁; n=10 → ⌊55.0036⌋=55=F₁₀. Dropping the ψⁿ term gives 55.0036≠55 for exact equality, so the two-term form is load-bearing.

prompt_injection: A parser needs to count the number of valid bracket sequences of length 2n. Prove that the count is the nth Catalan number using the recurrence Cₙ = Σ CₖC_{n−1−k}. Compute C₄ explicitly.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 7, Section 7.5. Also: Chapter 8, Exercise 4.
tensor_equation: C₀ = 1, ∀n ≥ 1: Cₙ = Σ_{k=0}^{n-1} CₖC_{n−1−k}. Closed form: Cₙ = (1/(n+1)) C(2n, n) = (2n)!/((n+1)!n!). Asymptotic: Cₙ ∼ 4ⁿ/(n^{3/2}√π).
title: Catalan Numbers Recurrence: counting balanced parentheses, binary trees, triangulations
verdict_evidence: CM Chapter 7 is 'Generating Functions' and §7.5 is 'Convolutions' (TOC verified) — this is exactly where GKP derive the Catalan recurrence Cₙ₊₁=ΣCₖCₙ₋ₖ from C(z)=1+zC(z)² and obtain Cₙ=C(2n,n)/(n+1). Falsifiable: C₃ = C₀C₂+C₁C₁+C₂C₀ = 2+1+2 = 5 = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4; a proposed Cₙ=C(2n,n)/n fails at n=3 (20/3 ∉ ℤ). Asymptotic 4ⁿ/(n^{3/2}√π) is the standard Stirling consequence and matches (n=10: 16796 vs 16815, 0.1% error).

prompt_injection: Two independent Poisson processes with rates λ and μ arrive at a server. The combined arrival process has generating function G_X(z)G_Y(z). Prove that the convolution of their probability mass functions gives the correct distribution for the sum.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 7, Section 7.2.
tensor_equation: Let A(z) = Σᵢ aᵢzⁱ, B(z) = Σⱼ bⱼzʲ. Then A(z)B(z) = Σₙ (Σ_{k=0}ⁿ aₖb_{n−k}) zⁿ. The coefficient of zⁿ in the product is the convolution (a ∗ b)ₙ = Σ_k aₖb_{n−k}.
title: Generating Function Convolution: product of generating functions corresponds to convolution of sequences
verdict_evidence: CM §7.2 'Basic Maneuvers' (TOC verified) states the product rule for generating functions: multiplying two power series convolves their coefficient sequences (GKP table of GF operations, 'A(z)B(z) ↔ Σₖ aₖbₙ₋ₖ'). Falsifiable: A(z)=B(z)=1/(1−z) (all aᵢ=bᵢ=1) gives A·B=1/(1−z)² with [zⁿ]=n+1, matching Σ_{k=0}ⁿ 1·1=n+1; a claimed pointwise-product rule [zⁿ](AB)=aₙbₙ would give 1, violating it.

prompt_injection: A quicksort implementation does 2n Hₙ comparisons on average. Estimate the number of comparisons for n = 10⁶ using Hₙ ≈ ln n + γ. Provide the numeric value to two significant figures.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 6, Section 6.3.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: Hₙ = Σ_{k=1}ⁿ 1/k = ln n + γ + 1/(2n) − 1/(12n²) + O(1/n⁴), where γ ≈ 0.57721 (Euler-Mascheroni constant). Integral bound: ln(n+1) < Hₙ < 1 + ln n.
title: Harmonic Number Asymptotics: Hₙ ∼ ln n + γ
verdict_discrepancy: FIDELITY: 'ln(n+1) < Hₙ < 1 + ln n for all n ≥ 1' is false at n=1. H₁ = 1 and 1 + ln 1 = 1, so Hₙ < 1 + ln n fails (1 < 1 is false). The correct statement is ln(n+1) ≤ Hₙ ≤ 1 + ln n, with the right inequality tight at n=1 and the left tight nowhere for n≥1. Strictness on the upper bound requires n ≥ 2.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A retry-with-backoff strategy doubles the wait time each attempt: 1s, 2s, 3s, …, ns. What is the total elapsed time after n attempts? Prove Σk = n(n+1)/2 by induction.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.1.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k = 1 + 2 + … + n = n(n+1)/2 = C(n+1, 2). Proof by induction: S(n) = S(n−1) + n, base S(1) = 1.
title: Sum of First n Natural Numbers
verdict_evidence: Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k = n(n+1)/2 = C(n+1,2). CM Chapter 2 is 'Sums'; §2.1 'Notation' (TOC verified) introduces this sum as the running example, and §2.2/§2.3 derive it by recurrence and by the reversal trick. Falsifiable: n=4 gives 10; a proposed n²/2 gives 8, violating it. C(n+1,2)=n(n+1)/2 checks out.

prompt_injection: A system processes n² work units each day. Derive the total work across n days using Σ k² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. What is the asymptotic growth rate Θ(n³)?
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.2.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. Proof by perturbation: (n+1)³ − n³ = 3n² + 3n + 1, sum telescope.
title: Sum of Squares: closed form for Σ k²
verdict_evidence: CM §2.2 'Sums and Recurrences' (TOC verified) is precisely where GKP obtain Σk² — they show the perturbation method applied to Σk² collapses, then perturb Σk³ using (n+1)³−n³ = 3n²+3n+1 to telescope out Σk² = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6. The equation's stated proof sketch matches that derivation. Falsifiable: n=3 gives 14 = 3·4·7/6; the common corruption n(n+1)(n+2)/6 gives 10, violating it.

prompt_injection: Prove that the sum of the first n cubes equals the square of the sum of the first n naturals. Does this identity generalize to higher powers?
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.2, Exercise 10.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k³ = (n(n+1)/2)² = (Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k)². Identities: Σ k³ = C(n+1, 2)² = n²(n+1)²/4.
title: Sum of Cubes: closed form equals square of sum of first n naturals
verdict_evidence: Σ_{k=1}ⁿ k³ = (n(n+1)/2)² is standard and appears in Concrete Mathematics Ch. 2 (Sums) alongside the k and k² sums; the stated identities are internally consistent (C(n+1,2)² = n²(n+1)²/4). Falsifiable: n=3 gives 36 = 6²; a proposed n²(n+1)²/2 gives 72, violating it. The exact exercise number ('§2.2, Exercise 10') could not be independently confirmed, which is why confidence is not high.

prompt_injection: A binary tree of height h has at most 1 + 2 + 4 + … + 2ʰ nodes. Prove this equals 2^{h+1} − 1 using the geometric series formula. Show the derivation from the recurrence S(h) = 2S(h−1) + 1.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.2.
title: Geometric Series Closed Form: exact sum of exponentially growing terms
verdict_evidence: CM §2.2 'Sums and Recurrences' (TOC verified) derives exactly this by the perturbation method on the geometric sum. The r≠1 hypothesis is present (essential — at r=1 the closed form is 0/0 while the sum is n+1), and the |r|<1 hypothesis is present on the infinite case (essential — r=2 would give Σ2ᵏ = −1). Falsifiable: r=2, n=3 gives 15 = (16−1)/1 ✓; a claimed (rⁿ−1)/(r−1) gives 7, violating it.

prompt_injection: A linear backoff starts at 100ms and increases by 50ms each retry. After 10 retries, what is the total time spent waiting? Use the arithmetic series formula.
source_document: Graham, Knuth, Patashnik. Concrete Mathematics (1994). Chapter 2, Section 2.1.
tensor_equation: ∀n ≥ 1: Σ_{k=0}^{n−1} (a + kd) = n/2 (2a + (n−1)d) = n(a + L)/2 where L = a + (n−1)d is the last term. Proof: pair first and last, second and second-last, etc.
title: Arithmetic Series Closed Form: sum of linearly growing terms
verdict_evidence: Arithmetic-series closed form, developed in Concrete Mathematics Ch. 2 'Sums' (§2.1 introduces Σ-notation on exactly this shape; §2.3 gives the pairing/reversal proof cited). Falsifiable: a=2, d=3, n=4 → 2+5+8+11=26 = 4/2·(4+9)=26; the off-by-one corruption n/2(2a+nd) gives 32, violating it. The '(n−1)d' last-term expression is correctly indexed.

prompt_injection: A test for a condition has 1% false positive rate and 99% true positive rate. The condition affects 0.1% of the population. A random person tests positive. What is the probability they actually have the condition? Calculate using Bayes' theorem and explain why the intuitive answer is wrong.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 1, Section 1.5. Also: Feller. An Introduction to Probability Theory and Its Applications, Vol. 1 (1968). Chapter 5.
tensor_equation: ∀events A, B with P(B) > 0: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A) / P(B). Extended form (partition A₁,…,Aₙ): P(Aᵢ|B) = P(B|Aᵢ)P(Aᵢ) / Σⱼ P(B|Aⱼ)P(Aⱼ). (G&S §1.4 Conditional probability, not §1.5 Independence.)
title: Bayes' Theorem: posterior probability from prior and likelihood
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Bayes' theorem is G&S §1.4 'Conditional probability' (Lemma 1.4.4 / eq. 1.4.5), not §1.5 'Independence'. The secondary Feller Vol. 1 Ch. V pointer is correct.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A request arrives at a load balancer that routes to server A (60% of traffic) with 5% error rate, server B (30%) with 2% error rate, and server C (10%) with 10% error rate. Compute the overall error rate using the law of total probability.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 1, Section 1.4.
tensor_equation: ∀event B, ∀partition A₁, A₂, …, Aₙ of Ω: P(B) = Σᵢ P(B ∩ Aᵢ) = Σᵢ P(B|Aᵢ) P(Aᵢ). Continuous case: P(B) = ∫ P(B|A) dP(A).
title: Law of Total Probability: marginal probability via partition of sample space
verdict_evidence: Grimmett & Stirzaker §1.4 is 'Conditional probability' (TOC verified), which states the partition theorem P(B)=Σ P(B|Aᵢ)P(Aᵢ) as Lemma 1.4.4, with the required hypotheses (the Aᵢ partition Ω and P(Aᵢ)>0). Falsifiable: two urns chosen with prob 1/2 each, P(red|urn1)=1, P(red|urn2)=0 gives P(red)=1/2; a claim that P(B)=max P(B|Aᵢ)=1 violates it. The axiom preserves the partition hypothesis, which is what gives it teeth (dropping it makes the sum over-count).

prompt_injection: A hash table with n buckets uses a random hash function. What is the expected number of buckets that are empty after inserting m keys? Use linearity of expectation with indicator variables. No independence assumption needed.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.1.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X₁, …, Xₙ defined on the same probability space with finite expectation: E[Σᵢ Xᵢ] = Σᵢ E[Xᵢ]. (G&S Theorem 3.3.3 in §3.3 Expectation, not §3.1. Integrability is a necessary hypothesis.)
title: Linearity of Expectation: expected value of sum equals sum of expectations, regardless of dependence
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: G&S §3.1 is 'Probability mass functions'; linearity is Theorem 3.3.3 in §3.3 'Expectation'. (2) MISSING HYPOTHESIS: '∀ random variables X₁,…,Xₙ' omits integrability. Counterexample: X standard Cauchy, X₁=X, X₂=−X on the same space. E[X₁+X₂]=E[0]=0, but E[X₁] and E[X₂] do not exist, so the right-hand side is undefined and the equation is false as written. G&S state it for variables with finite expectation.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: A service has average latency of 100ms. Using Markov's inequality, what is the maximum probability that a request takes ≥ 500ms? What additional assumption would improve this bound?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.3.
tensor_equation: ∀random variable X with finite mean μ = E[X] and variance σ² = Var(X), ∀k > 0: P(|X − μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k². (G&S §7.3, not §3.3. One-sided Cantelli: P(X − μ ≥ ε) ≤ σ²/(σ² + ε²).)
title: Markov's Inequality: tail bound from expectation alone
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.3 is 'Expectation'; Markov's inequality (and Chebyshev's) appear in Ch. 7 'Convergence of random variables', §7.3 'Some ancillary results' (TOC verified). (2) FIDELITY: Chebyshev's inequality is not a 'generalization' of Markov's — it is a corollary, obtained by applying Markov to (X−μ)². The generalization direction is reversed.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A server's response time has mean 100ms and variance 400ms². Using Chebyshev's inequality, bound the probability that a request takes ≥ 200ms (i.e., 100ms from the mean, which is 5σ). Compare with the bound from Markov's inequality — which is tighter?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.3.
tensor_equation: ∀random variable X with finite mean μ = E[X] and variance σ² = Var(X), ∀k > 0: P(|X − μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k². (G&S §7.3, not §3.3. One-sided Cantelli: P(X − μ ≥ ε) ≤ σ²/(σ² + ε²).)
title: Chebyshev's Inequality: tail bound using variance
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.3 is 'Expectation' (TOC verified: Ch. 3 = Discrete random variables, 3.1 pmf / 3.2 Independence / 3.3 Expectation / 3.4 Indicators and matching). Chebyshev's inequality is in Ch. 7 'Convergence of random variables', §7.3 'Some ancillary results'. Cantelli's inequality is not in §3.3 either; G&S give it as a problem in Ch. 7.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system processes n=10⁶ events, each with independent failure probability p=10⁻³. The expected number of failures is μ=1000. Using the Chernoff bound, bound P(failures ≥ 1200). Compare with the Chebyshev bound — which is tighter?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.4. Also: Motwani & Raghavan. Randomized Algorithms (1995). Chapter 4.
tensor_equation: Let X₁, …, Xₙ be independent Bernoulli(pi) with μ = Σ pᵢ. For any δ ∈ (0,1]: P(ΣXᵢ ≥ (1+δ)μ) ≤ e^{−μδ²/3}. For δ ∈ [0,1]: P(ΣXᵢ ≤ (1−δ)μ) ≤ e^{−μδ²/2}. (G&S §5.12 Large deviations, not §3.4.)
title: Chernoff Bound: sharp multiplicative tail bound for sums of independent random variables
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong primary source/section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.4 is 'Indicators and matching' (TOC verified) and contains no Chernoff bound; G&S treat exponential tail bounds in Ch. 5 §5.12 'Large deviations' (Cramér's theorem), not in this multiplicative (1±δ)μ form. The secondary citation — Motwani & Raghavan, Randomized Algorithms (1995), Ch. 4 (Theorems 4.1–4.3) — is the correct source for exactly these three inequalities and should be primary.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system has 10 components each with failure probability 0.001. Using the union bound, what is the maximum possible probability that at least one component fails? Is this bound tight? What is the exact probability if failures are independent?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 1, Section 1.4.
tensor_equation: ∀events A₁, …, Aₙ: P(⋃ᵢ Aᵢ) ≤ Σᵢ P(Aᵢ). Equality holds iff events are disjoint. Infinite case: P(⋃_{i=1}^{∞} Aᵢ) ≤ Σ_{i=1}^{∞} P(Aᵢ). Always valid, no independence required.
title: Union Bound (Boole's Inequality): probability of union is at most sum of probabilities
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality holds iff events are disjoint' is false in the ⇐ direction of necessity. Counterexample: Ω=[0,1] with Lebesgue measure, A₁=[0,½], A₂=[½,1]. They are NOT disjoint (A₁∩A₂={½}), yet P(A₁∪A₂)=1=P(A₁)+P(A₂). The correct condition is pairwise almost-disjointness, P(Aᵢ∩Aⱼ)=0 for i≠j. (2) Wrong section: Boole's inequality is in G&S §1.3 'Probability' (properties of the probability measure), not §1.4 'Conditional probability'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A multi-stage process: first roll a fair die, then flip that many coins. What is the expected number of heads? Use the tower property: E[heads] = E[E[heads|die_roll]] = E[die_roll/2] = 3.5/2 = 1.75.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.2.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y with finite expectation: E[E[X|Y]] = E[X]. For σ-algebras F ⊂ G: E[E[X|G]|F] = E[X|F]. (G&S §3.7 Conditional distributions, not §3.2.)
title: Law of Total Expectation (Tower Property): nested conditional expectations
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.2 is 'Independence' (TOC verified). Conditional expectation and the tower property are §3.7 'Conditional distributions and conditional expectation' (Theorem 3.7.4), with the σ-algebra version in Ch. 7 §7.9 'Prediction and conditional expectation'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system's latency is a random variable L. The cost function is convex (e.g., quadratic). Prove that E[cost(L)] ≥ cost(E[L]) using Jensen's inequality. Why does this matter for capacity planning?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.3.
tensor_equation: ∀convex function φ, ∀random variable X: E[φ(X)] ≥ φ(E[X]). For concave φ: E[φ(X)] ≤ φ(E[X]). Equality iff X is constant or φ is linear on the support of X. (Counterexample: φ(x)=max(0,x) convex, X uniform on [1,2] — equality holds without X being constant or φ being linear.)
title: Jensen's Inequality: expectation of convex function is at least the function of expectation
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality iff X is constant or φ is linear' is false. Counterexample: φ(x)=max(0,x) (convex, not linear) and X uniform on [1,2] (not constant). E[φ(X)]=E[X]=1.5=φ(1.5), so equality holds while neither disjunct is true. The correct condition is that φ is affine on an interval containing the support of X (a.s.). (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.3 is 'Expectation'; Jensen's inequality appears in Ch. 5 §5.6 'Expectation revisited' (TOC verified, p. 178). Also missing: E[X] and E[φ(X)] must exist.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Requests arrive at a server with rate λ. The processing time per request has mean μ and variance σ². Derive the variance of the total processing time for a random number of requests using the law of total variance.
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.4.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: Var(X) = E[Var(X|Y)] + Var(E[X|Y]). The first term is within-group variance, the second is between-group variance. Used in ANOVA: total variance = within + between.
title: Variance Decomposition (Law of Total Variance): Var(X) = E[Var(X|Y)] + Var(E[X|Y])
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: Grimmett & Stirzaker §3.4 is 'Indicators and matching' (TOC verified), which is about matching problems and indicator functions. The conditional-variance formula is in §3.7 'Conditional distributions and conditional expectation' (Exercise/Theorem 3.7.4 area) and Ch. 4's continuous analogue. (2) MISSING HYPOTHESIS: '∀ random variables X, Y' omits E[X²]<∞. For X with infinite variance (e.g. Pareto with α=1.5) both sides are +∞ or undefined and the equation carries no content.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Two metrics X and Y have sample correlation r = 0.8. Can we conclude that Cov(X,Y) = 0.8 σ_X σ_Y? If r = 1, what does that imply about the relationship between X and Y?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.5.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y with finite variance: Cov(X,Y)² ≤ Var(X)Var(Y). Equality (|Cov(X,Y)| = σ_X σ_Y) iff Y = aX + b almost surely for some constants a, b. Derives from Cauchy-Schwarz on centered variables.
title: Covariance Bound: |Cov(X,Y)| ≤ σ_X σ_Y with equality iff linear dependence
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Equality iff Y = aX + b almost surely' is false in the degenerate case. Counterexample: X ≡ 0 (constant), Y ~ N(0,1) independent. Then Cov(X,Y)=0 and Var(X)Var(Y)=0, so equality holds — yet Y is not a.s. an affine function of X. The equality condition requires Var(X)>0 (or the symmetric statement 'X and Y are a.s. affinely related'). (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.5 is 'Examples of discrete variables'; the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality is Theorem 3.6.9 in §3.6 'Dependence' (TOC verified, p. 62).
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system predicts latency X from request size Y. Prove that the optimal predictor (minimizing MSE) is E[X|Y]. What does this imply about using linear regression vs. conditional expectation?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 3, Section 3.2.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: E[X|Y] = argmin_{f(Y)} E[(X − f(Y))²] for all measurable functions f. The conditional expectation is the a.s.-unique minimizer; any g(Y) that agrees with E[X|Y] a.s. has the same MSE.
title: Conditional Expectation as Minimum Mean Square Error Predictor
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) FIDELITY: 'Any other function g(Y) has larger MSE' is false. Counterexample: let g(Y)=E[X|Y] modified on a P-null set (or, for Y with an atom-free component, any g agreeing with E[X|Y] a.s.). Then g ≠ E[X|Y] pointwise but the MSE is identical. The correct statement is 'no smaller MSE', with uniqueness only up to a.s. equality. (2) Wrong section: G&S §3.2 is 'Independence' (TOC verified); the MMSE-projection characterisation is §7.9 'Prediction and conditional expectation' (p. 343). Also missing: E[X²]<∞.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A monitoring system measures average latency over n requests. Prove that the probability of the sample mean deviating from the true mean by more than ε approaches 0 as n → ∞. How large must n be to guarantee P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ 5ms) ≤ 0.01 given σ² = 100ms²?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 5, Section 5.1.
tensor_equation: Let X₁, …, Xₙ be i.i.d. with finite mean μ and variance σ². ∀ε > 0: lim_{n→∞} P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ ε) = 0 where X̄ₙ = (1/n)ΣᵢXᵢ. Chebyshev gives: P(|X̄ₙ − μ| ≥ ε) ≤ σ²/(nε²).
title: Weak Law of Large Numbers: sample mean converges in probability to population mean
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong section. Grimmett & Stirzaker §5.1 is 'Generating functions' (TOC verified: Ch. 5 = Generating functions and their applications; 5.6 Expectation revisited; 5.10 Inversion and continuity theorems; 5.11 Two limit theorems; 5.12 Large deviations). The law of large numbers is in §5.11 'Two limit theorems' and, in full, Ch. 7 §7.4 'Laws of large numbers'.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A service processes 10⁴ requests, each with independent latency distribution (mean 100ms, variance 400ms²). Using the CLT, estimate the probability that the average latency exceeds 101ms. What is the Berry-Esseen bound on the approximation error?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 5, Section 5.3.
tensor_equation: Let X₁, …, Xₙ i.i.d. with mean μ and finite variance σ². Then (ΣᵢXᵢ − nμ)/(σ√n) →ᵈ N(0,1) as n → ∞. Equivalent: X̄ₙ ≈ N(μ, σ²/n) for large n. Convergence rate: Berry-Esseen bound O(1/√n).
title: Central Limit Theorem: sum of i.i.d. variables approximates normal distribution
verdict_discrepancy: Two faults. (1) Wrong section: G&S §5.3 is 'Random walk' (TOC verified). The central limit theorem is Theorem 5.11.4 in §5.11 'Two limit theorems'. (2) MISSING HYPOTHESIS: the Berry–Esseen O(1/√n) rate requires a finite third absolute moment E|X−μ|³ < ∞. With only finite variance the convergence rate can be arbitrarily slow, so 'Convergence rate: Berry-Esseen bound O(1/√n)' as an unconditional clause is false.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system checks for errors every second, with independent error probability 1/n² at time n. Show that infinitely many errors occur with probability 0 (Borel-Cantelli first lemma). What if the error probability is 1/n?
source_document: Grimmett & Stirzaker. Probability and Random Processes (2001). Chapter 2, Section 2.7.
tensor_equation: First lemma: if Σ_{n=1}∞ P(Aₙ) < ∞ then P(limsup Aₙ) = 0 (infinitely many Aₙ occur with probability 0). Second lemma: if Aₙ are independent and Σ P(Aₙ) = ∞ then P(limsup Aₙ) = 1.
title: Borel-Cantelli Lemma: convergence of infinite sequence of events
verdict_discrepancy: Wrong chapter. Grimmett & Stirzaker Ch. 2 is 'Random variables and their distributions' and §2.7 is its problems/worked-examples section — no Borel–Cantelli there. The Borel–Cantelli lemmas are in Ch. 7 'Convergence of random variables', §7.3 'Some ancillary results' (TOC verified), where they are used to prove the strong law.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: A system generates random IDs from a 256-element alphabet. Compute the maximum entropy achievable. Is the entropy of a uniform distribution over 256 values equal to 8 bits? Prove that uniform distribution maximizes entropy.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.1.
tensor_equation: ∀discrete random variable X with alphabet X: 0 ≤ H(X) ≤ log₂|X|. H(X) = 0 iff X is deterministic (p(x)=1 for some x). H(X) = log₂|X| iff X is uniformly distributed over X. H(X) = −Σ p(x) log₂ p(x).
title: Shannon Entropy Bounds: 0 ≤ H(X) ≤ log|X|
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 2 is 'Entropy, Relative Entropy, and Mutual Information'; §2.1 'Entropy' defines H(X)=−Σp log p and proves H(X)≥0 (Lemma 2.1.1), with the maximum-entropy upper bound H(X)≤log|𝒳| and its uniform-distribution equality condition following as Theorem 2.6.4 in §2.6 (TOC verified: 2.6 'Jensen's Inequality and Its Consequences', p. 25). Falsifiable and both bounds are tight and non-vacuous: X uniform on 4 symbols gives H=2=log₂4 (upper bound tight, so any strengthening to < fails); X deterministic gives H=0. A biased coin p=0.9 gives H=0.469 < 1, refuting any claim that H=log|𝒳| always.

prompt_injection: A system captures two correlated measurements X and Y. Prove that H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X). If X determines Y exactly (Y = f(X)), what is H(Y|X) and what does that imply about H(X,Y)?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.5.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X) = H(Y) + H(X|Y). Generalized: H(X₁, …, Xₙ) = Σ_{i=1}ⁿ H(Xᵢ | X₁, …, X_{i−1}). Conditional entropy H(Y|X) = Σ p(x) H(Y|X=x).
title: Chain Rule for Entropy: H(X,Y) = H(X) + H(Y|X)
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.5 is 'Chain Rules for Entropy, Relative Entropy, and Mutual Information' (TOC verified); the chain rule H(X,Y)=H(X)+H(Y|X) is Theorem 2.2.1 and its n-variable generalisation H(X₁..Xₙ)=ΣH(Xᵢ|X₁..X_{i−1}) is Theorem 2.5.1, exactly as encoded, along with the definition H(Y|X)=Σₓp(x)H(Y|X=x). Falsifiable: X,Y i.i.d. fair bits gives H(X,Y)=2=1+1; the incorrect variant H(X,Y)=H(X)+H(Y) fails whenever Y=X, where H(X,Y)=1 but H(X)+H(Y)=2 — and the chain rule correctly gives 1+0=1.

prompt_injection: A system processes raw sensor data Y through a feature extractor Z = f(Y). Prove that I(X;Z) ≤ I(X;Y) for any function f, where X is the true state. What does this imply about lossy feature extraction?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.8.
tensor_equation: If X → Y → Z forms a Markov chain (X ⟂ Z | Y), then I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;Z). Equivalent: I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;g(Y)) for any function g. Mutual information is non-increasing under data processing.
title: Data Processing Inequality: post-processing cannot increase information
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.8 is 'Data-Processing Inequality' (TOC verified), Theorem 2.8.1: if X→Y→Z is a Markov chain then I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;Z), with the corollary I(X;Y) ≥ I(X;g(Y)). The Markov hypothesis is preserved, which is what gives the axiom teeth: counterexample without it — let X,Y be i.i.d. fair bits and Z=X. Then I(X;Y)=0 but I(X;Z)=1, violating the conclusion; X→Y→Z is not a Markov chain here. Falsifiable in the intended direction too: Y=X and Z=constant gives 1 ≥ 0.

prompt_injection: A system's predicted distribution p over outcomes differs from the true distribution q. Prove that D(p||q) ≥ 0 with equality iff p = q. What does this imply about using cross-entropy as a loss function?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.6.
tensor_equation: ∀probability distributions p, q over the same alphabet: D(p||q) = Σ p(x) log(p(x)/q(x)) ≥ 0. Equality iff p = q almost everywhere. Not symmetric: D(p||q) ≠ D(q||p) in general. D(p||q) = 0 iff p = q a.e.
title: Gibbs' Inequality: KL divergence is non-negative, zero iff distributions are identical
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.6 'Jensen's Inequality and Its Consequences' (TOC verified, p. 25), Theorem 2.6.3 (Information inequality): D(p‖q) ≥ 0 with equality iff p(x)=q(x) for all x. Asymmetry is real and the axiom is falsifiable: p=(0.5,0.5), q=(0.9,0.1) gives D(p‖q)=0.737 while D(q‖p)=0.531 — a claim of symmetry is refuted; and a claimed D<0 is refuted by non-negativity. The 'same alphabet' / absolute-continuity requirement is present in substance (q(x)=0 with p(x)>0 gives +∞).

prompt_injection: A compression scheme uses codeword lengths 1, 2, 3, 3 for a 4-symbol alphabet (binary). Verify Kraft's inequality: 2⁻¹ + 2⁻² + 2⁻³ + 2⁻³ = 1/2 + 1/4 + 1/8 + 1/8 = 1. Is this a valid prefix code? What if a fifth symbol with length 2 is added?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 5, Section 5.2.
tensor_equation: ∀prefix-free code with codeword lengths l₁, …, lₘ over a D-ary alphabet: Σ_{i=1}ᵐ D^{−lᵢ} ≤ 1. Conversely, if lengths satisfy the inequality, there exists a prefix-free code with those lengths. Equality holds for complete codes.
title: Kraft's Inequality: necessary and sufficient condition for prefix-free codes
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 5 'Data Compression', §5.2 'Kraft Inequality', Theorem 5.2.1: for any instantaneous (prefix) D-ary code the lengths satisfy ΣD^{−lᵢ} ≤ 1, and conversely any lengths satisfying it admit such a code. Both directions are correctly encoded. Falsifiable with teeth: lengths (1,1,2) over D=2 give 1/2+1/2+1/4 = 1.25 > 1, so no prefix-free binary code with those lengths exists — and indeed after assigning '0' and '1' no length-2 codeword is prefix-free. Lengths (1,2,2) give exactly 1 (complete code: 0,10,11).

prompt_injection: A source outputs 4 symbols with probabilities (0.5, 0.25, 0.125, 0.125). Compute the entropy H(X) = −Σ p log₂ p. Show that Huffman coding achieves the optimal expected length within 1 bit of entropy. Can any lossless code beat the entropy bound?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 5, Section 5.4.
tensor_equation: ∀i.i.d. source X with entropy H(X): H(X) ≤ L* < H(X) + 1, where L* is the minimum expected codeword length for a prefix-free code. For n-block codes: H(X) ≤ (1/n) L*ₙ < H(X) + 1/n. As n → ∞, L*ₙ/n → H(X).
title: Source Coding Theorem (Shannon's First Theorem): entropy is the minimum achievable average codeword length
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §5.4 'Bounds on the Optimal Code Length', Theorem 5.4.1: H(X) ≤ L* < H(X)+1 for the optimal prefix code, extended in §5.4 to blocks with H(X) ≤ L*ₙ/n < H(X)+1/n. The lower bound's tightness and the strict upper bound are correctly rendered, as is the i.i.d. hypothesis for the block version. Falsifiable and the +1 slack is necessary: X binary with p=0.99 has H=0.081 but any prefix code needs L*=1, so a claimed L* < H+0.5 is refuted; and a claimed L* < H is refuted by the lower bound.

prompt_injection: A classifier predicts one of 10 classes. The conditional entropy H(X|Y) = 2 bits. Using Fano's inequality, what is the minimum possible error probability? P_e ≥ (2 − 1)/log₂(10) = 1/3.32 ≈ 0.30. Is this bound tight?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.10.
tensor_equation: Let X be a random variable and X̂ = g(Y) an estimate of X from Y. Then P(X̂ ≠ X) ≥ (H(X|Y) − 1)/log₂|X|. Equivalent: H(X|Y) ≤ H₂(P_e) + P_e log₂(|X|−1), where H₂ is binary entropy. Error probability P_e ≥ (H(X|Y) − 1)/log₂|X|.
title: Fano's Inequality: lower bound on probability of error from entropy
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.10 'Fano's Inequality' (TOC verified, p. 37), Theorem 2.10.1: H(Pₑ) + Pₑ log(|𝒳|−1) ≥ H(X|Y), with the standard weakened corollary Pₑ ≥ (H(X|Y)−1)/log|𝒳|. Both forms are stated, with the Markov structure X→Y→X̂=g(Y) present. Falsifiable and non-vacuous: X uniform on 4 symbols with Y independent of X gives H(X|Y)=2, so Pₑ ≥ 1/2 — and indeed no estimator from an independent Y beats 3/4 error; a claimed Pₑ=0 is refuted. Conversely Y=X gives H(X|Y)=0 and the bound goes slack, correctly permitting Pₑ=0.

prompt_injection: Two random variables X and Y have joint distribution P(X,Y). Prove that mutual information I(X;Y) = 0 iff X and Y are independent. What does I(X;Y) > 0 imply about prediction?
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.3.
tensor_equation: ∀random variables X, Y: I(X;Y) = 0 iff X ⟂ Y (independent). I(X;Y) = H(X) − H(X|Y) = H(Y) − H(Y|X) = H(X) + H(Y) − H(X,Y). I(X;Y) = D(p(x,y)||p(x)p(y)). I(X;Y) = Σ p(x,y) log(p(x,y)/(p(x)p(y))).
title: Mutual Information Zero iff Independence
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.3 'Relative Entropy and Mutual Information' defines I(X;Y)=D(p(x,y)‖p(x)p(y))=Σp(x,y)log(p(x,y)/p(x)p(y)) and gives the identities I=H(X)−H(X|Y)=H(X)+H(Y)−H(X,Y) (§2.4, Theorem 2.4.1); 'I(X;Y)=0 iff X and Y are independent' is the corollary to Theorem 2.6.3 in §2.6. Falsifiable with teeth: X,Y i.i.d. fair bits gives I=0; Y=X gives I=1≠0. Crucially it forbids the common error of reading I=0 as 'uncorrelated' — X uniform on {−1,0,1} and Y=X² are uncorrelated (Cov=0) yet dependent, and indeed I(X;Y)=2/3 bits ≠ 0, consistent with the axiom.

prompt_injection: Given two probability distributions p and q, prove that Σ pᵢ log(pᵢ/qᵢ) ≥ 0 using the log-sum inequality. Show that the log-sum inequality implies the convexity of KL divergence.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 2, Section 2.7.
tensor_equation: ∀non-negative a₁,…,aₙ, b₁,…,bₙ: Σ aᵢ log(aᵢ/bᵢ) ≥ (Σ aᵢ) log(Σ aᵢ/Σ bᵢ). Equality iff aᵢ/bᵢ is constant. Directly implies: D(p||q) ≥ 0, D(p||q) is convex in (p,q), and the data processing inequality.
title: Log-Sum Inequality: convexity of relative entropy
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. §2.7 'Log Sum Inequality and Its Applications' (TOC verified, p. 30), Theorem 2.7.1: Σaᵢlog(aᵢ/bᵢ) ≥ (Σaᵢ)log(Σaᵢ/Σbᵢ) for non-negative aᵢ,bᵢ, with equality iff aᵢ/bᵢ is constant — exactly as encoded, including the consequences listed (D≥0, convexity of D(p‖q) in the pair, and the data-processing inequality, all derived in §2.7). Falsifiable: a=(1,1), b=(1,3) gives LHS = 0 + log(1/3) = −1.585 and RHS = 2·log(2/4) = −2, so LHS > RHS strictly (aᵢ/bᵢ not constant); a=(1,1), b=(2,2) gives equality, confirming the stated condition. A claimed reverse inequality is refuted by the first case.

prompt_injection: A Markov chain with 3 states has transition matrix P. Compute the entropy rate H(X) = H(X₂|X₁) = −Σ πᵢ Σⱼ Pᵢⱼ log Pᵢⱼ. Prove that the entropy rate is ≤ the per-symbol entropy and that equality holds for i.i.d. processes.
source_document: Cover & Thomas. Elements of Information Theory (2nd ed., 2006). Chapter 4, Section 4.2.
tensor_equation: ∀stationary stochastic process {Xᵢ}: H(X) = lim_{n→∞} (1/n) H(X₁, …, Xₙ) = lim_{n→∞} H(Xₙ | X₁, …, X_{n−1}). Both limits exist and are equal for stationary processes. H(X) ≤ H(X₁) with equality iff i.i.d.
title: Entropy Rate of a Stationary Process: limit of joint entropy per symbol
verdict_evidence: Cover & Thomas 2nd ed. Ch. 4 'Entropy Rates of a Stochastic Process', §4.2 'Entropy Rate': Theorem 4.2.1 establishes that for a stationary process both H(𝒳)=lim (1/n)H(X₁..Xₙ) and H'(𝒳)=lim H(Xₙ|X₁..X_{n−1}) exist and Theorem 4.2.3 gives H=H'. The stationarity hypothesis is correctly retained and is load-bearing: counterexample without it — an independent non-stationary process with H(Xₙ)=n bits has (1/n)H(X₁..Xₙ)=(n+1)/2 → ∞, so the limit fails to exist. Falsifiable in the intended range too: an i.i.d. fair-bit process has H=1=H(X₁), while a deterministic period-2 process has H=0 < H(X₁)=1, exercising the H(𝒳)≤H(X₁) clause.

prompt_injection: Analyze T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n using the master theorem. Compute log₃ 9 = 2. Is f(n) = n = O(n^{2−ε}) for ε = 1? Then T(n) = Θ(n²). Show the derivation.
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 4, Section 4.5.
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). If f(n) = O(n^{log_b a − ε}) for some ε > 0, then T(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a}). The recursion tree leaf-level dominates. Example: T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n → log₃9 = 2, f(n) = n = O(n^{2−ε}), T(n) = Θ(n²).
title: Master Theorem (Case 1): divide-and-conquer recurrence dominated by the recursive term
verdict_evidence: CLRS 4th ed. Ch. 4 'Divide-and-Conquer', §4.5 'The master method for solving recurrences', Theorem 4.1 case 1: if f(n)=O(n^{log_b a − ε}) for some constant ε>0, then T(n)=Θ(n^{log_b a}). The ε>0 hypothesis is present and is exactly the load-bearing part — counterexample showing it has teeth: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n/lg n has f(n)=n/lg n = o(n^{log₂2}) but is NOT O(n^{1−ε}) for any ε>0, and the true answer is Θ(n lg lg n), not Θ(n). So the axiom correctly declines to cover it. The worked example (9T(n/3)+n → Θ(n²)) checks out: log₃9=2, n=O(n^{2−ε}) for ε=1.

prompt_injection: Prove that merge sort T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n) satisfies T(n) = Θ(n log n). Show that log₂ 2 = 1, f(n) = Θ(n¹ log⁰ n), so by Master Theorem case 2, T(n) = Θ(n log n).
source_document: Cormen, Leiserson, Rivest, Stein. Introduction to Algorithms (4th ed., 2022). Chapter 4, Section 4.5.
tensor_equation: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). If f(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a} log^k n) for some k ≥ 0, then T(n) = Θ(n^{log_b a} log^{k+1} n). Example: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n → log₂2 = 1, f(n) = n = Θ(n¹ log⁰ n), T(n) = Θ(n log n).
title: Master Theorem (Case 2): divide-and-conquer recurrence with balanced recursion and divide costs
