A key consequence of Alpern and Schneider's topological characterization is that safety properties are exactly the properties that can be enforced by a runtime monitor. The monitor observes a finite prefix of the execution and can reject it if the prefix already violates the safety property. (You cannot do this for liveness — any finite prefix might still be extended to satisfy a liveness property.) This is why runtime assertions, circuit breakers, rate limiters, and sandbox path checks all work: they enforce safety properties by rejecting bad prefixes. A runtime monitor for a safety property is: observe the prefix, check if it's still extensible to a good behavior, and if not, halt/reject.

Invariant: ∀safety_property S: ∃runtime_monitor M such that ∀prefix p: M(p) = reject iff p cannot be extended to satisfy S

In orbit: every TestAX gate enforces a safety property. Every runtime assertion enforces a safety property. The circuit breaker, token router, and sandbox all enforce safety properties by rejecting bad prefixes.
source_document: Alpern, B. and Schneider, F.B. "Defining Liveness", Information Processing Letters, 21(4), October 1985, pp. 181-185.
tensor_equation: ∀safety_property S: ∃runtime_monitor M such that ∀prefix p: M(p) = reject if p cannot be extended to satisfy S
title: Safety properties are enforceable by runtime monitors that reject bad prefixes (Alpern & Schneider 1985)
verdict_discrepancy: Quantifier/connective weakened: the source states a biconditional (reject iff the prefix cannot be extended), the axiom states only the one-way implication "M(p) = reject if p cannot be extended". The 'only if' half is what rules out a trivially rejecting monitor; dropping it makes the axiom satisfiable by a monitor that rejects everything.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
Leveson's STPA (System-Theoretic Process Analysis) frames safety as a control problem: hazards arise when safety constraints are not adequately enforced. A safety constraint is a condition on system behavior that, if violated, could lead to a hazardous state. Every safety constraint must be monitored at runtime — not just at design time — and the response to a violation must be fail-closed (halt the system, block the operation, degrade gracefully) rather than fail-open (continue and hope). The monitoring must be independent of the system being monitored (no self-monitoring for critical constraints).

Invariant: ∀hazard H: ∃safety_constraint C such that C prevents H; ∀safety_constraint C: monitored_at_runtime(C) ∧ violation(C) → fail_closed

In orbit: the circuit breaker fails closed (blocks traffic when Open). The sandbox fails closed (rejects paths outside worktree). The token router fails closed (skips keys in cooldown). Every safety-critical component must fail closed, not open.
source_document: Leveson, N.G. "Engineering a Safer World: Systems Thinking Applied to Safety", MIT Press, 2011. (Also: Leveson, N.G. "Software System Safety", forthcoming.)
tensor_equation: ∀hazard H: ∃safety_constraint C such that C prevents H; ∀safety_constraint C: monitored_at_runtime(C) ∧ violation(C) → fail_closed
title: Safety constraints must be monitored at runtime with fail-closed response (Leveson, Software System Safety)
verdict_evidence: §4 Leveson: "∀hazard H: ∃safety_constraint C: C prevents H / monitored_at_runtime(C) ∧ violation(C) → fail_closed"; key principle "Fail closed: A safety constraint violation must block the operation, not allow it."

prompt_injection:
Leveson emphasizes that safety monitoring must track leading indicators — measurable conditions that precede and predict hazards — not just lagging indicators (the hazards themselves). A leading indicator provides early warning: if the error rate is rising, the circuit breaker is approaching its trip threshold, or the token bucket is depleting faster than expected, you have time to act before the hazard materializes. Leading indicators are monitored continuously and trigger alerts before the safety constraint is violated, not after.

Invariant: ∀hazard H: ∃leading_indicator L such that L(t) → ◇H(t+δ) for some δ > 0

In orbit: the circuit breaker's failure count is a leading indicator for the Open state. The token bucket's fill rate is a leading indicator for rate limit exhaustion. The error budget burn rate is a leading indicator for SLO violation.
source_document: Leveson, N.G. "Engineering a Safer World: Systems Thinking Applied to Safety", MIT Press, 2011, Chapter 9 (STPA: A New Hazard Analysis Technique).
tensor_equation: ∀hazard H: ∃leading_indicator L such that L(t) → ◇H(t+δ) for some δ > 0. (The requirement that L is monitored continuously is not in the source.)
title: Leading indicators measure precursors to hazards, not just hazard occurrences (Leveson)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom appends the conjunct "L is monitored continuously", a normative requirement that appears nowhere in the source. The source requires only that a leading indicator exist and temporally precede the hazard; it says nothing about continuous (as opposed to sampled or event-driven) monitoring.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection:
The four golden signals are the minimum set of metrics every service must export. Latency: the time it takes to service a request, measured as a distribution (p50, p95, p99), not an average. Traffic: the demand on the system, measured in requests per second. Errors: the rate of requests that fail, explicitly (HTTP 5xx) or implicitly (HTTP 200 with wrong content). Saturation: how full the service is, measured on the most constrained resource (CPU, memory, I/O, goroutine count). If you only have four metrics, these are the four. Every alert must be derivable from one of these signals.

Invariant: ∀service S: monitored(S, {latency, traffic, errors, saturation})

In orbit: every package that serves requests (dispatch, tokenrouter, ggrind) must export these four signals. The dispatch package already reports HTTP status codes; it needs latency distribution, traffic rate, and saturation metrics.
source_document: Beyer, B., Jones, C., Petoff, J., and Murphy, N.R. "Site Reliability Engineering: How Google Runs Production Systems", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems).
tensor_equation: ∀service S: monitored(S, {latency, traffic, errors, saturation}). (The server-side/client-side measurement distinction is not discussed in the source.)
title: Four golden signals: latency, traffic, errors, saturation (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom appends "∧ ∀signal: signal is measured at the server side, not client side". The cached source never discusses server-side vs client-side measurement anywhere. This conjunct is unsupported and, as an absolute ∀, is stronger than anything the source states.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
The SRE book's core monitoring philosophy: alert on what users experience (symptoms), not on what might go wrong internally (causes). A symptom is 'the p99 latency is above 2 seconds' — users are experiencing slowness. A cause is 'the database connection pool is at 80%' — that might lead to slow queries, but users aren't affected yet. Pages (waking someone up) must be reserved for symptoms. Causes get tickets (investigated during business hours). This prevents alert fatigue: if you page on causes, you'll page on things that never become user-visible problems, and people will start ignoring pages.

Invariant: ∀alert A: trigger(A) = symptom(user_visible) ∧ pages are symptoms only

In orbit: dispatch alerts on HTTP 5xx rates (symptom: users see errors). Token router alerts on rate limit rejections (symptom: users see 429s). Circuit breaker alerts on Open state duration (symptom: users see failures).
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems), §6.3 (Why Monitor?).
tensor_equation: ∀alert A: trigger(A) = symptom(user_visible). Pages are symptoms; tickets are causes. (The restriction that no alert may ever be triggered by an internal cause is not in the source — the source restricts only pages to symptoms, not all alerts.)
title: Alert on symptoms, not causes (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom adds "¬∃alert A': trigger(A') = cause(internal)", i.e. no alert may ever be triggered by an internal cause. The source restricts only *pages* to symptoms and explicitly keeps cause-based signals as tickets ('ticket on what might go wrong'). The axiom's own trailing clause 'tickets are causes' contradicts its formal part.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection:
The error budget burn rate tells you how fast you're consuming your error budget. A burn rate of 1 means you'll exhaust the budget exactly at the end of the SLO window. A burn rate of 2 means you'll exhaust it in half the window. The SRE book recommends multi-window, multi-burn-rate alerting: burn rate > 14.4 over a short window (1 hour) means the budget will be exhausted in 1 hour — page immediately. Burn rate > 2 over a long window (6 hours) means the budget will be exhausted in 3 days — ticket. This prevents both false positives (transient spikes) and false negatives (slow leaks).

Invariant: ∀service S, ∀window W: burn_rate(S, W) = error_rate(S, W) / error_budget_rate(S); burn_rate > 14.4 → page; burn_rate > 2 → ticket

In orbit: the token router's rate limit enforcement should track burn rate. The circuit breaker should track failure rate vs. error budget. The dispatch pipeline should track error budget consumption per endpoint.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 4 (Service Level Objectives), §4.5 (Error Budgets).
tensor_equation: ∀service S, ∀window W: burn_rate(S, W) = error_rate(S, W) / error_budget_rate(S); burn_rate > 1 → budget exhausted before SLO window ends; burn_rate > 14.4 → budget exhausted in 1 hour → page
title: Error budget burn rate determines alerting urgency (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_discrepancy: Two mismatches: (a) the axiom drops the source's second threshold rule 'burn_rate > 2 → ticket'; (b) the axiom invents two claims not in the source — 'burn_rate > 1 → budget exhausted before SLO window ends' and the gloss 'burn_rate > 14.4 → budget exhausted in 1 hour'. The source states 14.4 only as a paging threshold, with no exhaustion-time semantics.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
A counter is a cumulative metric that represents a single monotonically increasing value. It can only increase (or be reset to 0 on process restart). Counters are used for counting events: total requests, total errors, total bytes sent. The rate() function computes the per-second average rate of increase over a time window. Do not use a counter for a value that can decrease — that's a gauge. Do not use rate() on a gauge. The monotonicity of counters is a safety property: if a counter decreases without a restart, the monitoring pipeline is broken.

Invariant: ∀counter c, ∀timestamps t1 < t2: c(t2) ≥ c(t1) except across process restarts

In orbit: every event count (requests, errors, token acquisitions, circuit breaker trips) must be a counter. The invariant c(t2) ≥ c(t1) must be verified by the metric export pipeline.
source_document: Prometheus documentation: 'Metric Types' (https://prometheus.io/docs/concepts/metric_types/); OpenMetrics specification, §5 (Metric Types).
tensor_equation: ∀counter c, ∀timestamps t1 < t2: c(t2) ≥ c(t1) except across process restarts; rate(c[window]) = (c(t_now) - c(t_now - window)) / window
title: Counter is monotonically increasing; rate(counter[window]) gives per-second average (Prometheus/OpenMetrics)
verdict_evidence: §8 Prometheus: "∀counter c, ∀t1 < t2: c(t2) ≥ c(t1) except across restarts / rate(c[window]) = (c(t_now) - c(t_now - window)) / window". Restart exception and the ≥ direction both match.

prompt_injection:
A gauge is a metric that represents a single numerical value that can arbitrarily go up and down. Gauges are used for current state: current memory usage, current goroutine count, current queue depth, current circuit breaker state. Unlike counters, gauges have no monotonicity guarantee. You cannot meaningfully compute rate() on a gauge — rate() assumes monotonic increase. Instead, use the gauge value directly or compute deltas manually. The distinction between counter and gauge is a type safety property: using the wrong type for a metric produces misleading results.

Invariant: ∀gauge g: g(t) ∈ ℝ is a snapshot; ¬monotonic(g); rate(g) is undefined and produces garbage

In orbit: use gauges for: circuit breaker state, worker pool size, token bucket fill level, sandbox process count, goroutine count. Use counters for: total requests, total errors, total tokens acquired.
source_document: Prometheus documentation: 'Metric Types' (https://prometheus.io/docs/concepts/metric_types/); OpenMetrics specification, §5 (Metric Types).
tensor_equation: ∀gauge g: g(t) ∈ ℝ is a snapshot; g(t2) may be <, =, or > g(t1); use rate() only on counters, not on gauges
title: Gauge represents a value that can increase or decrease; use for current state, not cumulative events (Prometheus/OpenMetrics)
verdict_evidence: §8: "∀gauge g: g(t) ∈ ℝ is a snapshot; ¬monotonic(g); rate(g) is undefined"; key principle "Gauge: Can go up and down... Never call rate() on a gauge." The axiom's phrasing (may be <, =, or >) is an equivalent rendering of ¬monotonic.

prompt_injection:
A histogram samples observations and counts them in configurable buckets. It also tracks the total count and sum of all observed values. Histograms are used for latency distributions, request sizes, and any value where you need percentiles. The bucket boundaries must be chosen carefully: too few buckets lose precision; too many buckets increase cardinality. A good rule of thumb: choose buckets that cover the expected range with logarithmic spacing. Always check histogram_quantile against the actual bucket boundaries — if the φ-th percentile falls in the +Inf bucket, your buckets are too small.

Invariant: ∀histogram h: h has buckets b_1,...,b_n; ∀observation x: x is counted in all buckets where b_i ≥ x; p99 is computed from bucket distribution, not from h_sum/h_count

In orbit: dispatch latency must be a histogram with buckets at 1ms, 5ms, 10ms, 25ms, 50ms, 100ms, 250ms, 500ms, 1s, 2.5s, 5s, 10s. Token acquisition latency must be a histogram.
source_document: Prometheus documentation: 'Histograms and Summaries' (https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/histograms/); OpenMetrics specification, §5.3 (Histogram).
tensor_equation: ∀histogram h: h has buckets b_1,...,b_n with b_1 < b_2 < ... < b_n; h_count = total observations; h_sum = sum of observed values; p99 ≈ histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(h_bucket[5m]))
title: Histogram measures distributions; histogram_quantile(φ, rate(bucket[window])) gives φ-th percentile (Prometheus/OpenMetrics)
verdict_evidence: §8: "∀histogram h: h has buckets b_1,...,b_n / p99 ≈ histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(h_bucket[5m]))", plus "_bucket/_count/_sum for histograms" in the naming principle. The added h_count/h_sum definitions are definitional, not new requirements.

prompt_injection:
Havelund and Rosu introduced rewriting-based runtime verification: specifications are expressed as rewrite rules that reduce the execution trace, and violations are detected when the rewrite system reaches a contradiction. A critical property of any runtime monitor is soundness: if the monitor says the spec is violated, the spec is truly violated. A monitor that reports false positives (violations that aren't real) is worse than no monitor — it erodes trust and causes operators to ignore alerts. Soundness is a safety property of the monitor itself.

Invariant: ∀monitor M, ∀spec φ, ∀trace σ: M(σ, φ) = violation → σ ⊭ φ

In orbit: every TestAX gate must be sound — if the test fails, the invariant is truly violated. Every runtime assertion must be sound — if it panics, the condition is truly false. The grind pipeline's severity classification must be sound — a P1 finding must truly be a P1 violation.
source_document: Havelund, K. and Rosu, G. "Monitoring Programs Using Rewriting", in Proceedings of the 16th IEEE International Conference on Automated Software Engineering (ASE 2001), pp. 135-143.
tensor_equation: ∀monitor M, ∀spec φ, ∀trace σ: M(σ, φ) = violation → σ ⊭ φ (soundness: no false positives)
title: Runtime verification is sound: if the monitor reports violation, the trace truly violates the spec (Havelund & Rosu 2001)
verdict_evidence: §6 Havelund & Rosu: "M(σ, φ) = violation → σ ⊭ φ (soundness: no false positives)"; key principle "Soundness: If the monitor reports violation, the trace truly violates the spec." Direction of the implication matches.

prompt_injection:
Completeness in runtime verification means: if the observed trace violates the specification, the monitor must eventually detect it. It must detect the violation at the earliest observable point — the shortest prefix that already determines the violation. This is the dual of soundness. A monitor that misses violations (false negatives) provides a false sense of security. Completeness is limited to the observed prefix: you cannot detect violations that haven't happened yet (future-time LTL requires anticipation), and you cannot detect violations that require infinite traces (pure liveness).

Invariant: ∀monitor M, ∀spec φ, ∀trace σ: σ ⊭ φ → ∃prefix p ≤ σ: M(p, φ) = violation

In orbit: every TestAX gate must be complete over the test cases. The circuit breaker must detect state invariant violations at the earliest point. The grind pipeline must detect all P0/P1 findings in the code it analyzes.
source_document: Havelund, K. and Rosu, G. "Monitoring Programs Using Rewriting", in ASE 2001, pp. 135-143.
tensor_equation: ∀monitor M, ∀spec φ, ∀trace σ: σ ⊭ φ → ∃prefix p ≤ σ: M(p, φ) = violation (completeness: no false negatives up to the observed prefix)
title: Runtime verification is complete up to the observed prefix: violations are detected at the earliest observable point (Havelund & Rosu 2001)
verdict_evidence: §6: "σ ⊭ φ → ∃prefix p ≤ σ: M(p, φ) = violation (completeness up to prefix)"; key principle "no false negatives (up to observed prefix)".

prompt_injection:
Havelund and Rosu distinguish between past-time and future-time temporal logic for runtime monitoring. Past-time LTL uses operators like 'previously' (●), 'sometime in the past' (◆), and 'always in the past' (■). These are always monitorable on finite traces because the past is known. Future-time LTL uses operators like 'next' (○), 'eventually' (◇), and 'always' (□). These require either bounded anticipation (lookahead window) or can only be evaluated on completed traces. The distinction matters for runtime monitoring: use past-time LTL for online monitoring (detect violations as they happen), use future-time LTL for offline analysis (check completed traces).

Invariant: ∀past_time_LTL φ: online_monitorable(φ); ∀future_time_LTL ψ: online_monitorable(ψ) only with bounded lookahead

In orbit: the circuit breaker's state transition monitoring uses past-time LTL ('was the previous state Open and now we got a success?'). The grind pipeline uses future-time LTL on completed traces.
source_document: Havelund, K. and Rosu, G. "Monitoring Programs Using Rewriting", in ASE 2001, pp. 135-143.
tensor_equation: ∀past_time_LTL_formula φ: φ is monitorable on any finite trace; ∀future_time_LTL_formula ψ: ψ is monitorable only with bounded lookahead or on completed traces
title: Past-time LTL is monitorable on finite traces; future-time LTL requires bounded anticipation (Havelund & Rosu 2001)
verdict_evidence: §6: "∀past_time_LTL φ: online_monitorable(φ) / ∀future_time_LTL ψ: online_monitorable(ψ) only with bounded lookahead"; key principle adds "or offline analysis", which the axiom's 'or on completed traces' matches.

prompt_injection:
Bartocci et al.'s 2018 survey of runtime verification identifies overhead as a critical design constraint. A monitor that introduces unbounded overhead is itself a reliability risk: it can cause the system it's monitoring to miss deadlines, exceed SLOs, or run out of memory. The overhead must be bounded (O(1) per event, not O(n)), predictable (the same regardless of input), and configurable (you can trade completeness for lower overhead). Monitoring that causes more problems than it prevents is worse than no monitoring.

Invariant: ∀monitor M, ∀program P: overhead(M, P) ≤ ε for some fixed, known ε; M's overhead is O(1) per event

In orbit: every runtime assertion, metric export, and invariant check must be O(1). The token router's bucket check must be O(1). The circuit breaker's state check must be O(1). The sandbox's path check must be O(1). No monitoring code path should allocate per-request or walk a data structure of unbounded size.
source_document: Bartocci, E., Falcone, Y., Francalanza, A., and Reger, G. "Introduction to Runtime Verification", in Lectures on Runtime Verification, LNCS 10457, Springer, 2018, pp. 1-33.
tensor_equation: ∀monitor M, ∀program P: overhead(M, P) ≤ ε for some fixed, known ε; M's overhead is O(1) per event, not O(n) or worse
title: Monitoring overhead must be bounded and predictable (Bartocci et al. 2018)
verdict_evidence: §7 Bartocci: "overhead(M, P) ≤ ε for fixed, known ε; M's overhead is O(1) per event"; key principle "Bounded overhead: Monitoring must be O(1) per event."

prompt_injection:
Bartocci et al. distinguish online and offline monitoring. Online monitoring runs concurrently with the system, checking each event as it occurs. It detects violations at the earliest possible point — the shortest prefix that already determines the violation. Online monitoring is used for safety properties that must be enforced immediately (circuit breaker trips, rate limit rejections, sandbox path blocks). Offline monitoring runs after execution completes, checking the full trace. It is used for properties that require the full trace to evaluate (future-time LTL, statistical properties) or when monitoring overhead must be zero during execution.

Invariant: online_monitor detects violation at min prefix p where σ ⊭ φ; offline_monitor checks σ after execution

In orbit: the circuit breaker, token router, and sandbox use online monitoring. The grind pipeline uses offline monitoring. The axiom-ingestor uses offline monitoring (it processes completed grind runs).
source_document: Bartocci, E. et al. "Introduction to Runtime Verification", in Lectures on Runtime Verification, LNCS 10457, Springer, 2018, pp. 1-33.
tensor_equation: ∀spec φ, ∀trace σ: online_monitor detects violation at min prefix p where σ ⊭ φ; offline_monitor checks σ after execution completes
title: Online monitoring detects violations at the earliest observable point; offline monitoring checks completed traces (Bartocci et al. 2018)
verdict_evidence: §7: "online_monitor detects violation at min prefix p where σ ⊭ φ / offline_monitor checks σ after execution completes". Verbatim match.

prompt_injection:
A runtime monitor that fails open (allows the system to proceed when the monitor is unavailable) provides no protection. The monitor is on the critical path for safety: if the monitor can't check, the operation must not proceed. This is the same principle as a circuit breaker failing closed, a firewall default-deny, and a sandbox default-reject. The trade-off is availability: a fail-closed monitor reduces availability if the monitor itself is unreliable. The solution is to make the monitor simple, fast, and co-located with the system it monitors (no network dependency for critical checks).

Invariant: ∀monitor M, ∀system S: M_unavailable → S is blocked

In orbit: the circuit breaker's Allow() check is in-process — no network call. The token router's bucket check is in-process. The sandbox's path validation is in-process. If any of these checks can't execute (e.g., mutex deadlock), the system must fail closed by not returning from the check.
source_document: Bartocci, E. et al. "Introduction to Runtime Verification", in Lectures on Runtime Verification, LNCS 10457, Springer, 2018, pp. 1-33.
tensor_equation: ∀monitor M, ∀system S: M_unavailable → S is blocked (fail-closed), not S proceeds unchecked (fail-open)
title: Monitor must fail closed: if the monitor is unavailable, the system is blocked, not allowed to proceed unchecked (Bartocci et al. 2018)
verdict_evidence: §7: "∀monitor M, ∀system S: M_unavailable → S is blocked (fail-closed)"; key principle "Monitor unavailable = system blocked. Never allow unchecked execution."

prompt_injection:
Bartocci et al.'s survey catalogues the specification languages for runtime verification: state machines for safety properties (circuit breaker states), LTL (past-time or future-time) for temporal properties, regular expressions for pattern matching, context-free grammars for nested structures, first-order logic for data-dependent properties, and quantitative/statistical languages for probabilistic properties. Using the wrong language makes the specification either too weak (cannot express the property) or too strong (over-specifies, causing false positives). The specification language must be adequate for the property you're checking.

Invariant: ∀property P: ∃spec_language L such that L is adequate for P

In orbit: circuit breaker uses state machine specifications. Token router uses arithmetic invariants (first-order). Grind pipeline uses a combination of structural patterns and severity classification rules. Sandbox uses regular expressions for path patterns.
source_document: Bartocci, E. et al. "Introduction to Runtime Verification", in Lectures on Runtime Verification, LNCS 10457, Springer, 2018, §3 (Specification Languages).
tensor_equation: ∀property P: ∃spec_language L such that L is adequate for P. safety → state machines; temporal → LTL; data → first-order. (The source enumerates exactly three pairs; "statistical → quantitative" is not in the source.)
title: Specification language for runtime monitoring must match the property being checked (Bartocci et al. 2018)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom adds a fourth mapping, "statistical → quantitative", which does not appear in the source's equation or key principles. The source enumerates exactly three property/language pairs.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
A metric that does not move under a known stimulus is not wired. This is the metric movement proof: (1) record the metric value before the action, (2) execute the action, (3) record the metric value after, (4) verify delta > 0. If the counter doesn't move, either the metric is not exported, the action doesn't trigger the metric, or the metric is measuring the wrong thing. This is a runtime analogue of the 'counter that does not move' invariant from AX-009/AX-010 in orbit's CLAUDE.md. Every metric must have at least one TestAX gate that proves it moves.

Invariant: ∀metric m, ∀action a that should trigger m: m(t_before) < m(t_after); delta > 0 proves m is wired

In orbit: TestAX gates for circuit breaker must prove the failure counter increments under failure. TestAX gates for token router must prove the request counter increments under Acquire. TestAX gates for dispatch must prove the error counter increments under HTTP 5xx.
source_document: Prometheus best practices: 'Instrumenting Your Code' (https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/instrumentation/); orbit CLAUDE.md, AX-009/AX-010 (counter movement proof).
tensor_equation: ∀metric m, ∀action a that should trigger m: m(t_before) < m(t_after) where t_after = t_before + execute(a); delta > 0 proves m is wired
title: Metric movement proof: before action, after action, delta > 0 (counter must move under known stimulus)
verdict_evidence: §8: "∀metric m, ∀action a that should trigger m: m(t_before) < m(t_after); delta > 0 proves m is wired"; key principle "Metric movement proof: Before → action → after, delta > 0. A counter that doesn't move isn't wired."

prompt_injection:
Prometheus and OpenMetrics enforce a strict naming convention. Every metric name follows the pattern namespace_subsystem_name_unit. The namespace is the application or organization (e.g., orbit). The subsystem is the component (e.g., circuitbreaker, tokenrouter, dispatch). The name describes what is being measured. The unit is a base unit suffix (e.g., _seconds, _bytes, _requests). Counters must end in _total. Histograms expose _bucket, _count, and _sum time series. Deviation from this convention breaks dashboards, alerting rules, and the PromQL query model.

Invariant: ∀metric m: name(m) = namespace_subsystem_name_unit; counter → _total suffix; histogram → _bucket/_count/_sum suffixes

In orbit: all metrics must follow this convention. Example: orbit_circuitbreaker_failures_total, orbit_tokenrouter_requests_total, orbit_dispatch_latency_seconds (histogram).
source_document: Prometheus documentation: 'Naming Practices' (https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/naming/); OpenMetrics specification, §4 (Metric Names and Labels).
tensor_equation: ∀metric m: name(m) = namespace_subsystem_name_unit; counter names end in _total; histogram names end in _bucket/_count/_sum
title: Metric naming convention: namespace_subsystem_name_unit (Prometheus/OpenMetrics)
verdict_evidence: §8: "name(m) = namespace_subsystem_name_unit / counter → _total suffix; histogram → _bucket/_count/_sum". Verbatim match.

prompt_injection:
Lamport's assertional reasoning method annotates program points with assertions — predicates that must hold whenever execution reaches that point. The assertions form an inductive invariant: if the initial state satisfies the assertion at the entry point, and every atomic action preserves the invariant (if the assertion at the source point holds, the assertion at the target point holds after the action), then the assertion holds at every program point in every execution. This is the foundation of Hoare logic, Owicki-Gries, and modern runtime assertions.

Invariant: ∀program P, ∀program_point p, ∀execution: assertion_A(p) must hold whenever execution reaches p

In orbit: every goroutine entry point should have a runtime assertion. Every mutex-protected critical section should have an invariant assertion at entry and exit. Every function that takes a pointer should assert non-nil. Every function that takes an index should assert bounds.
source_document: Lamport, L. "Proving the Correctness of Multiprocess Programs", IEEE TSE, SE-3(2), March 1977, pp. 125-143.
tensor_equation: ∀program P, ∀program_point p, ∀execution: assertion_A(p) must hold whenever execution reaches p; if assertion_A(p) is false, P has a bug
title: Assertional reasoning: program points annotated with assertions that must hold at that point (Lamport 1977)
verdict_evidence: §2: "∀program P, ∀program_point p, ∀execution: assertion_A(p) must hold whenever execution reaches p"; key principle "Assertional reasoning: Prove an inductive invariant". The trailing 'if false, P has a bug' is a restatement, not a new requirement.

prompt_injection:
Saturation measures how 'full' a service is. It must be measured on the most constrained resource — the bottleneck that will degrade first under load. For CPU-bound services, this is CPU utilization. For memory-bound services, this is memory usage. For I/O-bound services, this is disk or network I/O. For goroutine-based services (like orbit), this is goroutine count and channel buffer depth. Measuring the wrong resource gives a false sense of headroom. The saturation metric should be expressed as a fraction of capacity: 0.0 (idle) to 1.0 (fully saturated). Above 1.0, the service is overloaded and queuing.

Invariant: ∀service S: saturation(S) = max_{resource r} utilization(r) / capacity(r)

In orbit: saturation for ggrind is goroutine count / GOMAXPROCS. Saturation for dispatch is active HTTP connections / max connections. Saturation for tokenrouter is current bucket level / bucket capacity.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems), §6.4 (The Four Golden Signals).
tensor_equation: ∀service S: saturation(S) = max_{resource r ∈ resources(S)} utilization(r) / capacity(r); the bottleneck resource determines the saturation level
title: Saturation metric must measure the most constrained resource (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_evidence: §5: "saturation(S) = max_{resource r} utilization(r)/capacity(r)"; orbit relevance "Saturation must measure the bottleneck resource". The max over resources is exactly the bottleneck claim.

prompt_injection:
The SRE book emphasizes that latency must be measured as a distribution, not an average. The average (mean) hides tail latency: a service with 99% of requests at 10ms and 1% at 10s has an average of ~110ms, which looks fine, but 1% of users are experiencing 10-second delays. Report p50 (median), p95, and p99 at minimum. The p99 is the most important: it tells you what the worst 1% of users experience. If p99 is acceptable, the service is acceptable. If you only report the mean, you are blind to the long tail where most user pain lives.

Invariant: ∀service S: latency(S) is reported as histogram with p50, p95, p99; ¬use_mean(latency)

In orbit: every latency metric (dispatch, token acquisition, sandbox operations, ggrind task execution) must be a histogram with p50/p95/p99. The mean is computed for informational purposes only, never used for alerting.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems), §6.4 (The Four Golden Signals).
tensor_equation: ∀service S: latency(S) is reported as histogram with p50, p95, p99; ¬use_mean(latency); mean hides tail latency
title: Latency must be measured as a distribution, not an average (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_evidence: §5: "latency(S) reported as p50/p95/p99 histogram; ¬use_mean(latency)"; orbit relevance "Latency must be histogram, never mean." The added 'mean hides tail latency' is rationale, not a new obligation.

prompt_injection:
The SRE book distinguishes two types of errors. Explicit failures: the server returns an error code (HTTP 5xx, gRPC error). These are easy to measure. Implicit failures: the server returns HTTP 200 but the response is wrong, incomplete, or too slow. These are hard to measure but equally damaging to users. A service that returns HTTP 200 with stale data is failing implicitly. A service that returns HTTP 200 after 60 seconds is failing implicitly (the user has already left). Error rate must account for both types, or the metric is incomplete.

Invariant: ∀service S: error_rate(S) = rate(explicit_failures) + rate(implicit_failures)

In orbit: dispatch already tracks explicit failures (HTTP 4xx/5xx). Implicit failures include: responses that exceed the latency SLO (HTTP 200 but too slow), empty responses when data is expected, and responses that fail schema validation.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems), §6.4 (The Four Golden Signals).
tensor_equation: ∀service S: error_rate(S) = rate(explicit_failures) + rate(implicit_failures); explicit = HTTP 5xx; implicit = HTTP 200 with wrong/incomplete content
title: Error rate must distinguish explicit and implicit failures (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom redefines the terms: "explicit = HTTP 5xx; implicit = HTTP 200 with wrong/incomplete content". The cached source characterizes an implicit failure as HTTP 200 that is too slow (a latency failure), not as wrong or incomplete content, and never defines explicit failures as HTTP 5xx. The sum formula itself is faithful; the definitions are substituted.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection:
Meyer's Design by Contract allows the class invariant to be temporarily violated during method execution, as long as it is restored before the method returns. This is necessary because methods often need to break the invariant to make progress: a stack's pop method temporarily violates the 'size equals element count' invariant while removing the element, but restores it before returning. The invariant is checked at method entry and exit, not at every intermediate step. This is a practical compromise: checking at every step would be too expensive and would prevent many correct implementations.

Invariant: ∀class C, ∀method m, ∀instance o: inv_C(o) holds at entry(m) ∧ exit(m); may be false during body(m)

In orbit: runtime assertions at function entry/exit follow this pattern. Internal consistency checks happen at public API boundaries, not at every internal step. This is the orbit equivalent of checking invariants at package boundaries.
source_document: Meyer, B. "Object-Oriented Software Construction", 2nd ed., Prentice Hall, 1997, Chapter 11 (Design by Contract), §11.9 (The Class Invariant).
tensor_equation: ∀class C, ∀method m, ∀instance o: inv_C(o) holds at entry(m) ∧ exit(m); inv_C(o) may be temporarily false during body(m) if restored before exit(m)
title: Design by Contract: invariant holds at method entry and exit, but may be temporarily violated during method body (Meyer 1997)
verdict_evidence: §1: "inv_C(o) holds at entry(m) ∧ exit(m); may be temporarily false during body(m)". Axiom adds only 'if restored before exit(m)', which is entailed by the entry/exit clause.

prompt_injection:
Meyer's Design by Contract specifies the rules for inheritance: a subclass may only weaken the precondition (require less) or strengthen the postcondition (deliver more). It may never strengthen the precondition (require more than the parent) or weaken the postcondition (deliver less than the parent). This is the Liskov Substitution Principle expressed in contract terms: a subclass must be substitutable for its parent — it must accept all inputs the parent accepts and produce outputs at least as good as the parent's. Violating this rule means code written against the parent's contract breaks when given a subclass instance.

Invariant: ∀subclass S, ∀parent P, ∀method m: pre_S(m) ⇒ pre_P(m) ∧ post_P(m) ⇒ post_S(m)

In orbit: Go doesn't have inheritance, but interface implementations follow the same rule. An interface implementation must satisfy the interface's contract. If the interface says 'returns nil on error', the implementation must not return a non-nil value on error.
source_document: Meyer, B. "Object-Oriented Software Construction", 2nd ed., Prentice Hall, 1997, Chapter 11 (Design by Contract), §11.11 (Inheritance and Contracts).
tensor_equation: ∀subclass S, ∀parent P, ∀method m: pre_S(m) ⇒ pre_P(m) (weaker or equal precondition) ∧ post_P(m) ⇒ post_S(m) (stronger or equal postcondition). Inheritance weakens preconditions and strengthens postconditions only.
title: Inheritance strengthens preconditions or weakens postconditions only (Meyer 1997)
verdict_discrepancy: The title inverts the source: it says "Inheritance strengthens preconditions or weakens postconditions only", the exact opposite of Meyer/LSP and of this axiom's own equation and parenthetical glosses (which correctly read weaker precondition / stronger postcondition). Strengthening a precondition in a subclass is precisely the LSP violation the source forbids. The formal part is faithful; the title as shipped states the rule backwards.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: reversed polarity/contradiction

prompt_injection:
Leveson's STPA models safety as a control problem: a controller monitors the controlled process (plant) via sensors, compares the sensed state to the safety constraints, and applies corrective action via actuators when constraints are violated. This is a closed-loop control system. The controller must be independent of the plant (no self-monitoring). The sensor must be reliable (no single point of failure). The actuator must be fail-safe (default to the safe state). In software, this maps to: the monitor is the controller, the monitored system is the plant, metrics are sensors, and circuit breakers/throttles/kill switches are actuators.

Invariant: ∀safety_constraint C: ∃controller K such that K monitors C, detects violations, and applies corrective action; K is independent of the plant

In orbit: the circuit breaker is a controller (monitors failure count, trips to Open). The token router is a controller (monitors bucket level, rejects when exceeded). The sandbox is a controller (monitors path resolution, rejects escapes).
source_document: Leveson, N.G. "Engineering a Safer World: Systems Thinking Applied to Safety", MIT Press, 2011, Chapter 9 (STPA).
tensor_equation: ∀safety_constraint C: ∃controller K such that K monitors C, detects violations, and applies corrective action; K → plant → sensor → K is a closed loop
title: Control structure must enforce safety constraints with feedback loops (Leveson, STPA)
verdict_evidence: §4: "∃controller K: K monitors C, detects violations, applies corrective action"; key principle "Control loops: Monitor (sensor), compare to constraint, correct (actuator)." The axiom's K → plant → sensor → K loop restates that principle.

prompt_injection:
Prometheus metric labels create separate time series for each unique combination of label values. If a label's cardinality is unbounded, the number of time series grows without bound, consuming memory and slowing queries. The classic antipattern is using a user ID, request ID, or URL path (with path parameters) as a label. Each unique value creates a new time series. The cardinality must be bounded and small: endpoint name (handful of values), HTTP method (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE), status code class (2xx/4xx/5xx). Never use request-scoped values as labels.

Invariant: ∀metric m, ∀label L of m: |values(L)| ≤ K for some small, fixed K; cardinality does not grow with traffic

In orbit: dispatch metrics must not label by request URL (unbounded). Instead, label by endpoint name (bounded). Token router metrics must not label by API key (unbounded). Instead, label by key tier (bounded).
source_document: Prometheus documentation: 'Instrumentation — Things to Watch Out For' (https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/instrumentation/#things-to-watch-out-for); OpenMetrics specification, §4.2 (Labels).
tensor_equation: ∀metric m, ∀label L of m: |values(L)| ≤ K for some small, fixed K; |values(L)| must not grow with traffic or time
title: Metric labels must be bounded in cardinality; unbounded label values cause metric explosion (Prometheus/OpenMetrics)
verdict_evidence: §8: "∀metric m, ∀label L of m: |values(L)| ≤ K for small, fixed K; cardinality does not grow with traffic"; key principle "Bounded label cardinality: No user IDs, request IDs, or URL paths as labels."

prompt_injection:
Leveson emphasizes that safety analysis must consider failure modes, not just normal operation. A component that works correctly 99.9% of the time but fails silently 0.1% of the time is more dangerous than a component that fails loudly 10% of the time. Every failure mode must be: (1) identified — what can go wrong? (2) detected — how do we know it went wrong? (3) mitigated — what do we do when it goes wrong? A failure mode without a detection mechanism is a silent failure. A failure mode without a mitigation is an unhandled failure.

Invariant: ∀component C: failure_mode_analysis(C) is complete; ∀failure_mode F: F has detection ∧ F has mitigation

In orbit: every package must document its failure modes. Circuit breaker: what if the state mutex deadlocks? Token router: what if the clock jumps backward? Sandbox: what if the worktree root is deleted? Dispatch: what if the HTTP client pool is exhausted?
source_document: Leveson, N.G. "Engineering a Safer World: Systems Thinking Applied to Safety", MIT Press, 2011, Chapter 8 (Hazard Analysis).
tensor_equation: ∀component C, ∀failure_mode F of C: F is documented ∧ F has a detection mechanism ∧ F has a mitigation. (The timing gate "failure_mode_analysis is complete before code is accepted" is not in the source for this invariant.)
title: Failure mode enumeration: every component must be analyzed for how it fails, not just how it works (Leveson)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom adds a timing gate not present for this invariant — "failure_mode_analysis(C) is complete before code(C) is accepted". The source ties 'before code' only to hazard analysis (MONITOR-032), not to per-component failure mode analysis. Detection + mitigation are faithful; the acceptance gate is an addition that hardens the invariant into a merge blocker the source does not state.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection:
When a runtime assertion (precondition, postcondition, or class invariant) fails, the only safe response is to halt. The program's state is inconsistent — the invariant that guarantees correctness has been violated. Continuing from an inconsistent state is undefined behavior: anything can happen, including data corruption, security vulnerabilities, and cascading failures that are impossible to debug. There is no recovery path from a broken invariant. The assertion failure message is the forensic evidence; it must include the invariant that was violated, the values that caused the violation, and the location.

Invariant: ∀assertion A: A is false → halt(program); ¬∃recovery_path from broken invariant

In orbit: Go's panic on assertion failure is correct. Do not recover from assertion panics in production. The panic message must include the invariant, the violating values, and the file:line. The circuit breaker, token router, and sandbox must all panic on internal invariant violations.
source_document: Meyer, B. "Object-Oriented Software Construction", 2nd ed., Prentice Hall, 1997, Chapter 11 (Design by Contract), §11.8 (When an Assertion Is Violated).
tensor_equation: ∀assertion A: A is false → halt(program); ¬∃recovery_path from broken invariant; continuing after invariant violation is undefined behavior
title: Assertion failure must halt the program; continuing with a broken invariant propagates corruption (Meyer 1997)
verdict_evidence: §1: "∀assertion A: A is false → halt(program); ¬∃recovery_path from broken invariant"; key principle "Fail fast: A broken invariant means the program state is corrupt. Continue = undefined behavior."

prompt_injection:
The SRE book's most important alerting principle: thresholds must be derived from SLOs, not from intuition or 'what feels right'. An SLO says '99.9% of requests succeed within 100ms'. The alert threshold is derived from that: 'page when the error rate over 1 hour will exhaust the error budget if it continues at this rate'. The alternative — setting thresholds by intuition — leads to thresholds that are either too tight (alert fatigue) or too loose (missed outages). Every alert must have an associated SLO, and the threshold must be a mathematical consequence of that SLO.

Invariant: ∀alert A: threshold(A) is derived from SLO; ∃SLO S: threshold(A) = f(S)

In orbit: dispatch alert thresholds must be derived from the dispatch SLO. Circuit breaker alert thresholds must be derived from the availability SLO. Token router alert thresholds must be derived from the rate limit SLO.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 4 (Service Level Objectives), §4.5 (Error Budgets).
tensor_equation: ∀alert A: threshold(A) is derived from SLO; ∃SLO S such that threshold(A) = f(S); ¬∃alert A where threshold(A) is set by intuition
title: Alerting thresholds must be set from SLOs, not from intuition (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_evidence: §5: "∀alert A: threshold(A) derived from SLO; ∃SLO S: threshold(A) = f(S)". The axiom's third clause is the contrapositive restatement of the first.

prompt_injection:
Every alert must be backed by a measurable signal (a metric, a log count, a trace span). An alert without a metric is a phantom alert — it fires based on something that can't be measured, so it can't be tuned, can't be verified, and can't be trusted. Conversely, every metric must have a consumer: either it feeds an alert, it appears on a dashboard, or it's used for capacity planning. A metric collected without a consumer is waste — it costs storage, memory, and CPU, and provides no value. This is the 'no dead metrics, no phantom alerts' rule.

Invariant: ∀alert A: ∃metric m such that A fires ⇔ m exceeds threshold; ∀metric m: ∃consumer C such that C uses m

In orbit: every metric exported by a package must be consumed by an alert, dashboard, or capacity planning pipeline. Every TestAX gate that checks a counter must have a corresponding alert threshold.
source_document: Beyer, B. et al. "Site Reliability Engineering", O'Reilly, 2016, Chapter 6 (Monitoring Distributed Systems), §6.5 (Avoiding Magic Numbers).
tensor_equation: ∀alert A: ∃metric m such that A fires iff m exceeds threshold. ∀metric m: ∃consumer C ∈ {alert, dashboard, capacity_planning} such that C uses m.
title: No alert should fire without a metric; no metric should be collected without a consumer (Google SRE 2016)
verdict_discrepancy: Two changes: (a) the source states a biconditional (A fires ⇔ m exceeds threshold); the axiom weakens it to "A is triggered by m", dropping the 'only when the threshold is exceeded' half; (b) the axiom invents a closed enumeration of legitimate consumers, C ∈ {alert, dashboard, capacity_planning}. The source leaves the consumer unconstrained ('∃consumer C such that C uses m'), so the enumeration is an added restriction.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: added missing cases/states

prompt_injection:
The RED method (Rate, Errors, Duration), originated by Tom Wilkie, is the minimum instrumentation for every service endpoint. Rate: the number of requests per second. Errors: the number of failed requests per second. Duration: the distribution of request latency (p50, p95, p99). With these three metrics, you can answer: Is the service getting traffic? (Rate) Is it failing? (Errors) Is it fast? (Duration). The RED method is the service-level complement to the four golden signals (which are system-level). Every endpoint must export RED metrics.

Invariant: ∀service_endpoint E: monitored(E, {rate, errors, duration})

In orbit: dispatch must export RED metrics per endpoint. Token router must export RED metrics per key tier. Sandbox must export RED metrics per operation type. Ggrind must export RED metrics per task type.
source_document: Wilkie, T. "The RED Method: How to Instrument Your Services" (2015); Beyer, B. et al. "The Site Reliability Workbook", O'Reilly, 2018, Chapter 5 (Monitoring).
tensor_equation: ∀service_endpoint E: monitored(E, {rate, errors, duration}); rate = requests/sec; errors = failed/sec; duration = p50/p95/p99 latency
title: Instrumentation must cover the RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration (Tom Wilkie/Google SRE)
verdict_evidence: §5: "∀service_endpoint E: monitored(E, {rate, errors, duration})  [RED method]"; key principle "RED method: Rate, Errors, Duration — per-endpoint minimum instrumentation"; registry attributes it to "Google SRE/Wilkie", matching the axiom's attribution. Duration as p50/p95/p99 is consistent with MONITOR-028 in the same source.

prompt_injection:
Both Bartocci et al. and Leveson emphasize that the monitor must be independent of the system it monitors. A system that monitors itself has a conflict of interest: the code that violates the invariant is the same code that checks the invariant. The monitor must be in a separate process, a separate goroutine with its own error handling, or at minimum a separate code path that doesn't share state with the monitored code. The monitor's correctness must be verified independently. The monitor's failure must not cause the system's failure (the monitor is not on the critical path for normal operation — only for safety enforcement).

Invariant: ∀monitor M, ∀system S: M is independent of S; M's failure does not propagate to S

In orbit: the circuit breaker's state is monitored by the circuit breaker itself (in-process), which is acceptable because the check is simple and co-located. But the circuit breaker's metrics should be exported to an external monitoring system (Prometheus) that is independent. The grind pipeline is an offline monitor, independent of the code it analyzes.
source_document: Bartocci, E. et al. "Introduction to Runtime Verification", LNCS 10457, Springer, 2018; Leveson, N.G. "Engineering a Safer World", MIT Press, 2011, Chapter 9 (STPA).
tensor_equation: ∀monitor M, ∀system S: M is independent of S; M's failure does not cause S's failure (isolation). (The correctness verification obligation is not in the source — the source's independence claim is structural.)
title: Runtime monitor must be isolated from the system it monitors; self-monitoring is a conflict of interest (Bartocci et al. 2018, Leveson)
verdict_discrepancy: The axiom appends "M's correctness is verified independently of S" — a verification obligation on the monitor that the source never states. The source's independence claim is structural (failure isolation / no propagation), not a claim about how the monitor's correctness is established.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening

prompt_injection: Two-phase commit safety invariant (Gray 1978 §4): All participants MUST reach the same decision. The coordinator commits only if ALL participants voted YES. If ANY participant votes NO, the coordinator MUST abort. There is no scenario where some participants commit and others abort. This is the fundamental safety guarantee of atomic commit protocols.
redteam_pattern: Simulate a 2PC execution where coordinator crashes after sending COMMIT to P₁ but before sending to P₂. Assert that P₁ cannot commit unilaterally (the protocol must block). Assert that in the absence of a coordinator failure, P₁ and P₂ always reach the same decision.
source_document: Gray, J. (1978). Notes on Database Operating Systems. In Operating Systems: An Advanced Course, Springer-Verlag. §4: Two-Phase Commit.
tensor_equation:
∀participants P₁, P₂ in transaction T: decision(P₁, T) = decision(P₂, T)
∀T: decision(T) = COMMIT ⇒ ∀P ∈ participants(T): vote(P, T) = YES
∀T: ∃P ∈ participants(T) with vote(P, T) = NO ⇒ decision(T) = ABORT
title: 2PC Safety: Uniform Agreement — All participants reach the same decision (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: Gray, 'Notes on Data Base Operating Systems' (Operating Systems: An Advanced Course, LNCS 60, 1978, pp. 393-481) presents the two-phase commit protocol in section 5.8 (Recovery Management; 5.8.1 model of errors, 5.8.5.x recovery algorithms). Uniform agreement plus 'COMMIT only if all voted YES' and 'any NO forces ABORT' is exactly the 2PC safety contract stated there. FALSIFIABLE: coordinator sends COMMIT to P1, crashes; P2 times out in the prepared state and unilaterally aborts -> decision(P1)=COMMIT, decision(P2)=ABORT, equation violated. The axiom therefore has teeth: it is precisely what forbids unilateral resolution after a YES vote. Locator error: the citation says '§4: Two-Phase Commit'; in Gray 1978 2PC is §5.8, not §4. Also the work's exact title is 'Notes on Data Base Operating Systems'. Assumption not stated but required: non-Byzantine participants and durable logs; 2PC safety (not liveness) does hold under crash-recovery.

prompt_injection: Write-ahead logging invariant (Gray 1978 §4.2): Before a participant sends its vote, it MUST force-write a log record containing the vote (undo/redo data) to stable storage. Before the coordinator sends the global decision, it MUST force-write that decision to its log. After crash recovery, each participant replays its log to determine the transaction outcome. Without WAL, crash recovery cannot guarantee atomicity.
source_document: Gray, J. (1978). Notes on Database Operating Systems. §4.2: Recovery and Write-Ahead Logging.
tensor_equation:
∀P ∈ participants(T): send(vote, P, T) → durable(wal_record, P, vote)
∀coordinator(T): send(decision, T) → durable(wal_record, coordinator, decision)
∀P after crash recovery: replay(wal, P) → recover(decision, T)
title: 2PC Write-Ahead Logging: Votes and decisions MUST be durable before messages are sent (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: Gray 1978 §5.8 states the write-ahead-log discipline for the recovery manager: a participant force-writes its prepared/vote record before replying, and the coordinator force-writes the decision before broadcasting it; restart replays the log to re-derive the outcome. This is the standard, unmodified 2PC/WAL rule and Mohan et al. 1986 (TODS 11(4)) restates it as the baseline ('2P') protocol against which PA/PC economize forced writes. FALSIFIABLE: participant sends YES, crashes before the record hits disk; on restart it finds no record, presumes ABORT, while the coordinator commits -> agreement broken. Locator note: citation says '§4.2: Recovery and Write-Ahead Logging'; the material is Gray §5.8.

prompt_injection: 2PC liveness constraint (Gray 1978 §4.4): After a participant sends a YES vote, it enters a BLOCKED state. The participant cannot unilaterally commit or abort — it MUST wait for the coordinator's decision. This is the fundamental trade-off: 2PC sacrifices liveness (it can block) for safety (no inconsistent decisions). Any system claiming to implement 2PC MUST handle this blocking window: the participant must not proceed without the coordinator's decision.
source_document: Gray, J. (1978). Notes on Database Operating Systems. §4.4: Blocking and Recovery.
tensor_equation:
∀P ∈ participants(T) where vote(P, T) = YES ∧ ¬received(decision, T): P is BLOCKED
P remains BLOCKED until received(COMMIT, T) ∨ received(ABORT, T) ∨ timeout_and_query_coordinator(T)
If coordinator fails permanently after collecting votes, ∃P that remains BLOCKED forever
title: 2PC Blocking Property: Participant blocks after YES vote until coordinator decision arrives (Gray 1978)
verdict_evidence: The blocking (in-doubt) window is the defining weakness of 2PC as described by Gray 1978 §5.8 and restated by Skeen 1981 ('protocols that allow operational sites to continue processing even though site failures have occurred are called nonblocking' — 2PC is not one). A participant that has voted YES holds its locks and cannot unilaterally decide. FALSIFIABLE and non-vacuous: if the coordinator crashes after collecting all YES votes but before broadcasting, every prepared participant is stuck — no cooperative-termination partner knows the decision — so the third clause ('∃P blocked forever') is an existence claim that a working 3PC deployment would refute. Locator note: cited '§4.4: Blocking and Recovery'; actual material is Gray §5.8.

prompt_injection: Presumed Abort optimization (Mohan, Lindsay, Obermarck 1986): If a participant has no log record for a transaction, it assumes the transaction was ABORTED. This eliminates the need for the coordinator to force-write an ABORT log record — the coordinator can forget about aborted transactions immediately. The invariant: every participant that has no durable record for transaction T MUST behave as if T was aborted. This is the default in most production 2PC implementations.
source_document: Mohan, C., Lindsay, B., & Obermarck, R. (1986). Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System. ACM Transactions on Database Systems, 11(4), 378-396. §3: Presumed Abort.
tensor_equation:
∀participant P with no log record for transaction T: decision(T) = ABORT
∀P after crash where ¬∃wal_record(P, T): P replies ABORT to coordinator queries
Presumed Abort eliminates forced log writes at the coordinator for ABORT; only COMMIT is force-logged
title: R* Presumed Abort: In the absence of transaction outcome information, assume ABORT (Mohan et al. 1986)
verdict_evidence: Mohan, Lindsay & Obermarck, 'Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System', ACM TODS 11(4):378-396, 1986, introduces Presumed Abort (PA): absence of any log information about a transaction is interpreted as ABORT; the coordinator force-writes only commit records (abort records need not be forced), no ACKs are required for aborts, and the coordinator forgets an aborted transaction immediately after deciding. All three clauses of the equation match. FALSIFIABLE: a recovering participant with no record for T that replied COMMIT (or blocked) instead of ABORT would break the protocol's economization — that is the exact behaviour PA forbids. Section locator '§3' is approximate (PA/PC occupy the later protocol sections of the paper), but the content attribution is correct and the paper is the primary source.

prompt_injection: Hierarchical 2PC (Mohan, Lindsay, Obermarck 1986): In R*, the coordinator tree mirrors the process tree that executed the transaction. Each node votes YES only after all its children have voted YES. The decision propagates top-down. This reduces message complexity from O(n²) to O(n) compared to flat 2PC. The invariant: each intermediate coordinator MUST aggregate votes from its subtree before sending its own vote upward.
source_document: Mohan, C., Lindsay, B., & Obermarck, R. (1986). Transaction Management in the R* Distributed Database Management System. ACM TODS 11(4). §4: Hierarchical 2PC.
tensor_equation:
∀subtree rooted at coordinator C: vote(C, T) = YES iff ∀P ∈ children(C): vote(P, T) = YES
∀C at level L > 0: C acts as participant to its parent, coordinator to its children
Decision propagates top-down: root → children → grandchildren
title: R* Hierarchical 2PC: A tree of coordinators propagates prepare/commit; each subtree participant communicates only with its parent coordinator (Mohan et al. 1986)
verdict_evidence: R* commits over a tree of processes (Mohan & Lindsay 1983, 'Efficient commit protocols for the tree of processes model of distributed transactions', and Mohan et al. 1986 TODS 11(4)): an intermediate node propagates PREPARE to its subordinates and may vote YES to its own coordinator only if all of its subordinates voted YES; on receiving the decision it force-writes its own commit/abort record, ACKs its coordinator, and propagates the decision downward. That is clause-for-clause the axiom. FALSIFIABLE: an intermediate node that voted YES upward while one child voted NO would produce a subtree that must abort under a global COMMIT — a concrete violation.

prompt_injection: Three-phase commit non-blocking property (Skeen 1981): 3PC adds a pre-commit phase between prepare and commit. The invariant: no participant enters the commit phase until ALL participants have acknowledged readiness to commit (pre-commit). This eliminates the 2PC blocking window — if the coordinator fails, a new coordinator can query surviving participants: if ANY is in COMMIT state, the outcome is commit; if NONE have received pre-commit, the outcome is abort. The protocol ALWAYS terminates with bounded delays.
source_document: Skeen, D. (1981). Nonblocking Commit Protocols. In Proceedings of the 1981 ACM SIGMOD International Conference on Management of Data, 133-142. §3: Three-Phase Commit Protocol.
tensor_equation:
∀execution with bounded network delay D and bounded node response time R: ∃finite time t such that ∀P ∈ participants(T): decision_known(P, T) at time t
∃no participant state S such that S is ambiguous after any single node failure
Pre-commit state eliminates the blocking window: no participant can commit until ALL have acknowledged pre-commit
title: 3PC Non-Blocking Property: Protocol always terminates with bounded network delay and node response times (Skeen 1981)
verdict_evidence: Skeen, 'Nonblocking Commit Protocols', SIGMOD 1981, pp. 133-142, gives necessary and sufficient conditions for nonblocking commit and derives 3PC. Critically, the failure-model preconditions SURVIVED extraction: the axiom explicitly assumes bounded network delay D and bounded node response time R, which is exactly the synchronous model 3PC requires (3PC is nonblocking for site failures, not for partitions — a partition is unbounded delay and is excluded by the stated hypothesis). The PRECOMMIT state removes the ambiguous state that makes 2PC block. FALSIFIABLE: drop the bounded-delay assumption and 3PC blocks/diverges under partition — the axiom is exactly the claim that fails when its hypothesis is dropped, so it is not vacuous.

prompt_injection: 3PC recovery invariant (Skeen 1981): When a new coordinator takes over after a failure, it queries surviving participants for their state. The recovery decision is deterministic based on the highest state observed: COMMITTED forces commit (at least one participant already committed), PRECOMMIT with no COMMITTED forces commit (all participants were ready), neither PRECOMMIT nor COMMITTED forces abort (no participant could have committed). The invariant: these rules are exhaustive and always produce a safe, unambiguous decision.
source_document: Skeen, D. (1981). Nonblocking Commit Protocols. ACM SIGMOD 1981. §4: Recovery and Termination.
tensor_equation:
∀recovery after coordinator failure where S = {state(P) | P ∈ surviving_participants(T)}:
  COMMITTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = COMMIT
  PRECOMMIT ∈ S ∧ ¬∃ABORTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = COMMIT
  ¬∃PRECOMMIT ∈ S ∧ ¬∃COMMITTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = ABORT
  ABORTED ∈ S ⇒ decision(T) = ABORT
title: 3PC Recovery: New coordinator determines safe outcome from surviving participant states (Skeen 1981)
verdict_evidence: Skeen 1981's termination protocol: a newly elected coordinator polls survivors — if any has COMMITTED, commit; if any has ABORTED, abort; if some are in the pre-commit (PrepareToCommit) state and none aborted, drive the decision to COMMIT; if no survivor received PrepareToCommit, the previous coordinator cannot have committed anywhere, so ABORT is safe. Secondary sources (Springer 'Three-Phase Commit' encyclopedia entry) state the last rule verbatim. FALSIFIABLE: a recovery rule that aborted while a survivor held COMMITTED would violate uniform agreement — the axiom's case split is what rules that out. Note the state-set cases are mutually exclusive by construction (PRECOMMIT is only reachable after a unanimous YES), so no case overlaps.

prompt_injection: Chubby lock service mutual exclusion invariant (Burrows 2006, OSDI §2.1): The fundamental safety guarantee of Chubby is that at most one client holds any lock at any time. This is enforced by: (1) all lock state is replicated via Paxos, (2) lock acquisition requires a majority of replicas to agree, (3) session leases are time-bounded. If a client's lease expires, the lock is released. No two clients can simultaneously believe they hold the same lock.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service for Loosely-Coupled Distributed Systems. OSDI '06. §2.1: Design Overview — Locks and Sessions.
tensor_equation: ∀lock L held by client C with lease expiry E: holds(C, L, t) ⇒ t < E. ∀C: ¬renew_lease(C, E) before E ⇒ ∀lock L held by C: released(L) at time E. (The lease boundary is not absolute: if the master fails before receiving the KeepAlive, the lease may extend past E.)
title: Chubby Mutual Exclusion: At most one client holds any lock at any time (Burrows 2006)
verdict_discrepancy: Hedge-hardening plus dropped precondition. (1) 'Lock acquisition is linearizable across all clients' is not Burrows's claim; the paper offers no linearizability guarantee for lock operations. (2) The axiom presents mutual exclusion as an unqualified system property, dropping that Chubby locks are advisory and that mutual exclusion of the PROTECTED RESOURCE is not guaranteed without sequencers or lock-delay (§2.4). Counterexample from the paper itself: C1 holds L, is descheduled, its session lease lapses, C2 acquires L, then C1's in-flight request reaches the resource — two clients act as holders. The extracted equation only survives if 'holds' is silently redefined as the master's view, a restriction the axiom does not state.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Chubby session lease invariant (Burrows 2006, OSDI §2.2-2.3): Every lock is held under a time-bounded lease. A client that fails to renew its lease (via KeepAlive RPCs) before expiry automatically loses ALL its locks. Chubby guarantees this via a Paxos-replicated master that maintains lease timers. The invariant: there is a hard upper bound on how long a lock can be held without an active, healthy client. This prevents indefinite lock holding after client failures.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service. OSDI '06. §2.2: Sessions and Leases.
tensor_equation: ∀lock L held by client C with lease expiry E: holds(C, L, t) ⇒ t < E. ∀C: ¬renew_lease(C, E) before E ⇒ ∀lock L held by C: released(L) at time E. (The lease boundary is not absolute: if the master fails before receiving the KeepAlive, the lease may extend past E.)
title: Chubby Session Lease: Lock ownership is time-bounded; a client MUST renew its lease before expiry or lose the lock (Burrows 2006)
verdict_discrepancy: The equation hardens the lease boundary. '¬renew_lease(C,E) before E ⇒ ∀L held by C: released(L) at time E' is false in Chubby. Counterexample straight out of §2.9: the master fails; C's KeepAlive is not answered before E; C enters jeopardy, retries against replicas, and a new master responds with a new epoch during the 45s grace period — C never renewed before E, yet it still holds every lock after E and the session is 'safe'. Symmetrically, 'holds(C,L,t) ⇒ t < E' is contradicted by the grace period, and 'released at E' is contradicted by lock-delay, during which the lock is held by nobody and grantable to nobody.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: corrected over-hardening; added missing cases/states

prompt_injection: Chubby replication consistency (Burrows 2006, OSDI §3): All lock state is replicated across 5 replicas using a Paxos-based consensus protocol. The invariant: all replicas agree on the lock state after every committed operation. Lock acquisition, release, and lease renewal are linearizable operations — they appear to execute atomically at a single point between invocation and response. This is the foundation that makes Chubby safe to use as a distributed lock service.
source_document: Burrows, M. (2006). The Chubby Lock Service. OSDI '06. §3: System Structure — Replication and Paxos.
tensor_equation:
∀replicas R₁, R₂ in Chubby cell: state(R₁, lock L) = state(R₂, lock L) after all committed operations
∀lock operation O: ∃linearization point between invocation and response such that O is globally ordered
Chubby cell uses Paxos to replicate a write-ahead log; lock state is derived from the replicated log
title: Chubby Paxos Consistency: All replicas agree on lock state; lock operations are linearizable (Burrows 2006)
verdict_evidence: Burrows OSDI '06 §2.2 ('System structure'): a Chubby cell is typically five replicas; they use a distributed consensus (Paxos) protocol to elect a master and to replicate a write-ahead log/database from which all state — including lock state — is derived; a majority is required and the master holds a master lease, so all client reads and writes go through one master. The axiom's first clause is properly guarded by 'after all committed operations' (a lagging replica is not a counterexample), and its third clause restates the paper's mechanism accurately. FALSIFIABLE: a replica that applied committed log entries out of order, or a second master serving concurrently, would violate it — precisely what the master lease and Paxos log exist to prevent. Locator note: cited '§3'; §3 is 'Mechanisms for scaling', the replication/Paxos description is §2.2. The word 'linearizable' is the extractor's, not Burrows's, though it is a fair description of single-master-lease operation.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper total order invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.1): The ZAB protocol guarantees that all state updates are applied in the exact same order on every replica. Each update is assigned a monotonically increasing zxid. The invariant: any two replicas that have applied the same set of updates will have identical state. This is the foundation of ZooKeeper's linearizable write semantics and is stricter than FIFO ordering.
source_document: Hunt, P., Konar, M., Junqueira, F.P., & Reed, B. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination for Internet-scale Systems. USENIX ATC '10. §2.1: Service Overview — ZAB.
tensor_equation:
∀replicas R₁, R₂, ∀update sequence U = [u₁, u₂, ..., uₙ]: applied(R₁, U) ⇔ applied(R₂, U)
∀uᵢ, uⱼ where i < j: applied(R, uᵢ) before applied(R, uⱼ) on every replica R
ZAB (ZooKeeper Atomic Broadcast) guarantees total order of state updates across all replicas
title: ZooKeeper Total Order: All updates are applied in the same sequence on every replica (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_evidence: Hunt, Konar, Junqueira & Reed, 'ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination for Internet-scale Systems', USENIX ATC '10. ZAB (atomic broadcast, described in §4.2 of the paper) delivers state-changing requests in a single total order and every replica applies them in that order; the paper's guarantee list gives 'linearizable writes' — one global order of updates. FALSIFIABLE: two replicas applying u_i and u_j in opposite orders would produce divergent trees under a non-commuting pair (setData on the same znode), which ZAB's zxid ordering forbids. Fidelity notes: clause 1 ('applied(R1,U) ⇔ applied(R2,U)') is loosely written — a lagging follower has applied only a prefix — but clause 2 is the correct and load-bearing statement. Locator: ZAB is §4.2 ('Atomic Broadcast'), not §2.1.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper FIFO client order invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.2): Operations issued by a single client are applied in the exact order the client issued them. This is guaranteed by the client's monotonically increasing zxid counter and the session mechanism. Even if the client reconnects to a different server after a failure, the new server will apply pending operations in the client's original order. This is essential for building correct coordination primitives (locks, barriers, queues) on top of ZooKeeper.
source_document: Hunt, P. et al. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination. USENIX ATC '10. §2.2: Client API — Ordering Guarantees.
tensor_equation:
∀client C, ∀operations op₁, op₂ issued by C where issue_time(op₁) < issue_time(op₂): applied(op₁) before applied(op₂) in the global order
FIFO client order is guaranteed even if the client reconnects to a different server
title: ZooKeeper FIFO Client Order: Updates from a single client are applied in the order they were sent (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_evidence: Hunt et al. 2010 §2.3 ('ZooKeeper guarantees') lists exactly two ordering guarantees: linearizable writes and 'FIFO client order: all requests from a given client are executed in the order that they were sent'. The paper's session/zxid mechanism preserves this across reconnection — a client presents the last zxid it has seen and a server whose view is older than the client's refuses the session, so a reconnected client cannot regress. FALSIFIABLE: a client issuing create(/a) then create(/a/b) whose second request was ordered first would fail with NONODE; FIFO client order is what makes the pipelined-async API usable. Locator note: cited '§2.2 Client API'; the ordering guarantees are §2.3.

prompt_injection: ZooKeeper linearizable write invariant (Hunt et al. 2010, USENIX §2.3): Every write operation is linearizable — it appears to execute atomically at some point between its invocation and response. Once a write completes, all subsequent reads (from any client, to any server) will observe that write's effects. This is enforced by the ZAB protocol: the leader proposes writes, and a write is only committed after a majority of followers acknowledge it. Reads may be stale (served locally) unless the client uses sync().
source_document: Hunt, P. et al. (2010). ZooKeeper: Wait-free Coordination. USENIX ATC '10. §3: Zab Protocol — Leader and Commit.
tensor_equation: ∀write operation W: ∃linearization point t between W.invoke and W.response such that W is globally ordered. (ZooKeeper does NOT guarantee that reads after W completes observe W's effects — reads may be served from a follower that has not yet received the write.)
title: ZooKeeper Linearizable Writes: All write operations appear to execute atomically at a single point in time (Hunt et al. 2010)
verdict_discrepancy: '∀reads that execute after W completes: read observes W's effects (or later state)' is false for ZooKeeper. Counterexample: client A writes /x=1 to the leader and receives the response; client B, connected to a lagging follower, reads /x and gets the old value, because followers serve reads from local state without contacting the leader. The paper documents this and provides sync() as the opt-in fix. The axiom has silently promoted 'linearizable writes' to full linearizability of the register — the single most common misquotation of this paper.
verdict_evidence: Fixed: fixed fidelity error

prompt_injection: Saga compensation invariant (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987, ACM SIGMOD §2): Every step in a saga MUST have a compensating transaction defined in advance. A compensating transaction is NOT a database rollback — it is a new transaction that semantically reverses the effects of a previously committed transaction. When a step fails, compensations execute in REVERSE chronological order (most recent first). The invariant: for every forward step Tᵢ, there exists a compensating transaction CTᵢ such that executing CTᵢ after Tᵢ leaves the system in a semantically consistent state.
source_document: Garcia-Molina, H. & Salem, K. (1987). Sagas. In Proceedings of the 1987 ACM SIGMOD International Conference on Management of Data, 249-259. §2: The Saga Model.
tensor_equation:
∀saga S = [T₁, T₂, ..., Tₙ]: ∀i ∈ [1, n]: ∃CTᵢ such that CTᵢ semantically undoes Tᵢ
∀Tᵢ that fails: execute compensation sequence CTᵢ₋₁, CTᵢ₋₂, ..., CT₁ in REVERSE order
∀Tᵢ: CTᵢ is a NEW transaction, not a database rollback (Tᵢ is already committed)
title: Saga Compensation: Every step MUST have a corresponding compensating transaction defined in advance (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987)
verdict_evidence: Garcia-Molina & Salem, 'Sagas', SIGMOD 1987, pp. 249-259 (read in full). §1: 'To amend partial executions, each saga transaction T_i should be provided with a compensating transaction C_i. The compensating transaction undoes, from a semantic point of view, any of the actions performed by T_i, but does not necessarily return the database to the state that existed when the execution of T_i began.' The guaranteed executions are 'T1,T2,...,Tn' or 'T1,T2,...,Tj,Cj,...,C2,C1' — reverse order, exactly as the axiom states. §2: each end-transaction call carries the identification of the compensating transaction, i.e. compensation is declared in advance. §4 (Backward Recovery) shows the SEC aborting the in-flight T3 conventionally and then running C2, C1 — matching the axiom's 'compensate the already-committed prefix, roll back the failing step'. FALSIFIABLE: compensating in forward order (C1 then C2) breaks the airline example when C2 depends on state C1 removed. Minor fidelity note: the paper defines C_1..C_{n-1} and states there is no need for a compensating transaction for the last transaction when end-saga ends both; the axiom's '∀i ∈ [1,n]' is one broader than the paper's construction.

prompt_injection: Saga isolation limitation (Garcia-Molina & Salem 1987, ACM SIGMOD §3): Sagas do NOT provide ACID isolation. Each local transaction Tᵢ commits immediately, making its effects visible to other transactions before the overall saga completes. This creates a window where other sagas or transactions can observe partially-completed work.
