prompt_injection:
The untyped lambda calculus has three constructs: variables, abstractions, and applications. Every programming language is syntactic sugar over this core. The invariant is that every program reduces to a term via substitution, and computation is beta-reduction. The minimal syntax: t ::= x | λx.t | t t.

Invariant: ∀program P: P is a λ-term t, and reduction t →* t' proceeds by substitution alone. No runtime errors exist because there are no types — every term is syntactically valid.

Corollary for practical engineering: when designing a new feature, ask "what's the lambda calculus of this?" — strip to the minimal core before adding sugar.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 5: The Untyped Lambda-Calculus.
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002.
tensor_equation:
∀t: t is a lambda-term → t ::= x | λx.t | t t
∀t, t': t → t' → t' = [x→t']t (beta-reduction via capture-avoiding substitution)
∀feature F: λ-calculus(F) = minimal core {x, λx.t, t t} that expresses F
title: Lambda Calculus: Computation is substitution — three constructs (variable, abstraction, application) are the minimal core of every language
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §2 "The Lambda Calculus — The Smallest Language", Formal definition block: 't ::= x | λx.t | t t'. The three-construct grammar is verbatim from Pierce Chapter 5. Beta-reduction as the sole reduction rule is the standard definition in TAPL §5.2 and Barendregt (1984).
counterexample: A Go program with side effects (I/O, mutation) cannot be expressed as a pure lambda term — side effects are outside the lambda calculus core. A function that prints to stdout is not a lambda abstraction because its reduction depends on external state.

prompt_injection:
A type is a predicate on values. `int` means "this value is an integer." `string → int` means "this is a function from string to int." The type system checks that predicates are consistent: if f : T₁ → T₂ and x : T₁, then f(x) : T₂.

Invariant: Γ ⊢ f : T₁ → T₂ ∧ Γ ⊢ x : T₁ → Γ ⊢ f x : T₂

Newtypes are types as documentation enforced by the compiler. Named types (type UserID string) are predicates that the compiler checks at every call site. A function signature is a theorem; every call site is a proof obligation.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 8: Typed Arithmetic Expressions. Chapter 9: Simply Typed Lambda-Calculus.
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002.
tensor_equation:
∀f, x, T₁, T₂: (Γ ⊢ f : T₁ → T₂ ∧ Γ ⊢ x : T₁) → Γ ⊢ f x : T₂
∀type T, value v: T(v) is a predicate — the type determines which values satisfy it
∀named type N: N is a new predicate, checked at compile time, distinct from its underlying type
title: Simple Types: Types are predicates on values — the application typing rule enforces predicate consistency at every call site
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §3 "Simple Types — Types as Predicates", Formal definition block: simple typing rule for application 'Γ ⊢ f : T₁ → T₂, Γ ⊢ x : T₁ / Γ ⊢ f x : T₂'. The premise-conclusion form matches TAPL §9.3 (Simply Typed Lambda-Calculus). The untyped lambda calculus analogue (TAPL §5.2) has no such restriction, confirming that the typing rule IS the added predicate check.
counterexample: Calling fmt.Sprintf("%d", "hello") in Go passes at compile time because Sprintf takes interface{} — the type predicate is bypassed. This is exactly what simple types prevent: if the parameter were string, the compiler would reject the call.

prompt_injection:
A type S is a subtype of T if a value of type S can be used wherever a value of type T is expected. In Go this is structural interface satisfaction, not nominal inheritance.

Subsumption rule: Γ ⊢ t : S ∧ S <: T → Γ ⊢ t : T

Liskov Substitution Principle is the behavioral corollary: S <: T means S can do everything T can, and nothing that contradicts T's contract. Prefer composition over inheritance ("has-a" over "is-a"). Small interfaces (1-3 methods) are ideal.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 15: Subtyping. Liskov & Wing (1994). A Behavioral Notion of Subtyping.
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002. Liskov & Wing, ACM TOPLAS 16(6), 1994.
tensor_equation:
∀S, T, t: (Γ ⊢ t : S ∧ S <: T) → Γ ⊢ t : T
∀S, T: S <: T → (∀property P provable about T: P also holds for S)  [Liskov & Wing behavioral subtyping]
∀S, T: S <: T → methods(T) ⊆ methods(S)  [structural subtyping: S must have at least T's methods]
title: Subtyping: Subsumption rule — S <: T means any S value can be used where T is expected; Liskov substitution is the behavioral corollary
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §4 "Subtyping — Is-A vs. Has-A", Formal definition block: 'Γ ⊢ t : S, S <: T / Γ ⊢ t : T' (subsumption rule). The rule is verbatim from TAPL §15.1. Liskov & Wing behavioral subtyping is cited in the source document field. TAPL §15.2 gives the record subtyping rules (width, depth, permutation) that instantiate this schema.
counterexample: A Square type that extends Rectangle and overrides SetWidth to also set height violates Liskov: a caller that sets width expecting height unchanged will see both dimensions change. Square <: Rectangle is false behaviorally even if true structurally.

prompt_injection:
A recursive type is one that refers to itself. Lists, trees, and graphs are all recursive types. List A = Nil | Cons A (List A).

Iso-recursive types: μα.T ≅ [α↦μα.T]T

Structural recursion must be well-founded: the recursive call must be on a structurally smaller value. Termination follows from the well-foundedness of the recursive structure. In Go, recursive structs (type Node struct { Left, Right *Node }) are the primary mechanism.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 20: Recursive Types.
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002.
tensor_equation:
∀recursive type μα.T: μα.T ≅ [α↦μα.T]T  (unfolding isomorphism)
∀list A: List A = μα. (Unit + A × α)
∀recursive function f on type T: the recursion must be structural — f(t) calls f on subterms of t only
∀tree T: depth(T) is finite → structural recursion on T terminates
title: Recursive Types: Lists, trees, and graphs are recursive types — the unfolding isomorphism μα.T ≅ [α↦μα.T]T is the foundation of all data structures
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §6 "Recursive Types — Data Structures", Formal definition block: 'μα.T ≅ [α↦μα.T]T' (iso-recursive types) and 'List A = μα. (Unit + A × α)'. The iso-recursive formulation is from TAPL §20.2; the equi-recursive alternative (§20.1) makes the isomorphism implicit. The structural-recursion termination property is from TAPL §20.1 and Harper (2016) PFPL §7.
counterexample: A linked list with a cycle (last node points back to itself) is not a valid recursive type List A = Nil | Cons A (List A) because it has no Nil base case. Traversing it would not terminate, violating the well-foundedness requirement.

prompt_injection:
The type system can deduce types from usage without explicit annotations. Hindley-Milner (HM) type inference is the gold standard: it is sound, complete, and always finds the most general type.

Given: Γ ⊢ t : ? (unknown type). Find: the most general type T such that Γ ⊢ t : T. HM: unify type constraints, solve for type variables, produce principal type.

Type inference reduces boilerplate: the programmer writes the logic, the compiler figures out the types. If the compiler infers a type that surprises you, your design might be wrong. Gradual typing: annotate public interfaces, infer private ones.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 22: Type Reconstruction. Hindley (1969), Milner (1978), Damas & Milner (1982).
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002. Damas & Milner, POPL 1982.
tensor_equation:
∀expression e, context Γ: HM(Γ, e) = most general T such that Γ ⊢ e : T if such T exists; otherwise REJECT
∀type variable X in inferred type: X is universally quantified — the type is polymorphic over X
∀principal type T: any other valid type T' for e is an instance of T (T' = [X↦U]T for some U)
∀inferred type T that surprises the programmer: the design may be wrong
title: Type Inference: Hindley-Milner finds the most general type via unification — if the inferred type surprises you, the design may be wrong
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §7 "Type Inference — Let the Compiler Figure It Out", Formal definition block: 'Given: Γ ⊢ t : ? (unknown type) Find: the most general type T such that Γ ⊢ t : T / HM: unify type constraints, solve for type variables, produce principal type'. The HM type inference algorithm is defined in TAPL §22.3 (Algorithm W) and Damas-Milner (1982). The principal type property is Theorem 22.3.3 in TAPL.
counterexample: Go's := inference works at the expression level but cannot infer generic type parameters from return values alone: x := Map(...) fails because Go's type inference does not support Hindley-Milner's let-polymorphism. A Haskell expression like \x -> x infers type ∀a. a → a; Go's equivalent func(x any) any loses the polymorphism.

prompt_injection:
To understand a language feature, strip it to its essence. Featherweight Java (FJ) is Java without assignment, without interfaces, without generics — just classes, inheritance, and method dispatch. The core calculus is small enough to prove properties about, and the full language is a conservative extension.

Before implementing: write the minimal calculus (1 page) that captures the idea. Prove progress and preservation for the calculus. The full implementation is a conservative extension of the calculus. If the calculus has a problem, the implementation will too.

This is the same principle as the tensor equation: strip to the invariant, prove it, then implement.
source: Pierce, B.C. (2002). Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press. Chapter 18: Featherweight Java (with Igarashi, Pierce, Wadler, 1999/2001).
source_document: Pierce, Benjamin C. Types and Programming Languages. MIT Press, 2002. Igarashi, Pierce, Wadler. Featherweight Java: A Minimal Core Calculus for Java and GJ. OOPSLA 1999 / ACM TOPLAS 23(3), 2001.
tensor_equation:
∀language feature F: featherweight(F) is a minimal calculus that expresses F without extraneous syntax
∀featherweight calculus C: C has a small-step operational semantics with progress and preservation theorems
∀full language L that extends C: L is a conservative extension of C — every property proved in C holds in L for the subset of programs representable in C
∀P1 violation in C: the violation exists in L unless C's semantics was contradicted by L's extension
title: Featherweight Calculi: Strip a language feature to its essence (1 page), prove progress + preservation, then extend — the full language is a conservative extension of the minimal calculus
verdict_evidence: tapl-oracle.md §8 "Featherweight Calculi — The Essence of a Language". The Featherweight Java calculus is defined in Igarashi, Pierce, Wadler (1999/2001, OOPSLA/TOPLAS). TAPL §18.1 gives the FJ syntax and reduction rules; §18.2 proves type soundness via progress and preservation. The conservative extension claim is from Igarashi et al. §4 (FJ is a true subset of Java — every well-typed FJ program is a well-typed Java program with the same reduction behavior).
counterexample: Adding generics with erasure (Java's type erasure) to FJ changes runtime behavior: FJ's class casts are identity operations, but with erasure, a cast like (List<String>) x inserts a runtime check. This contradicts FJ's semantics and is not a conservative extension — it requires a new calculus (FGJ, Featherweight Generic Java) to prove soundness.

prompt_injection:
Error severity tiers form a strict hierarchy: PANIC (shuts down cluster → Go: panic()), FATAL (terminates backend → log.Fatal()), ERROR (aborts transaction → returned error), WARNING (logged, continues → log.Warn()). Each tier corresponds to a Go error-handling pattern.

Error message structure: Primary (telegram style, lowercase, no period: "could not open file: permission denied"), Detail (complete sentence, capitalized: "The file was created by user root."), Hint (complete sentence, suggestion: "Run chown on the data directory.").

Tense rule: "could not" = retriable (disk full, network timeout); "cannot" = permanent (type mismatch, invalid input).
source: PostgreSQL source conventions, Developer FAQ. PostgreSQL Documentation Chapter 18: Error Reporting.
source_document: PostgreSQL Global Development Group. PostgreSQL Documentation — Error Reporting and Logging. PostgreSQL source: src/backend/utils/error/elog.c, src/include/utils/elog.h.
tensor_equation:
∀error e: severity(e) ∈ {PANIC, FATAL, ERROR, WARNING}
severity(e) = PANIC → process exits with pgpanic() — equivalent to Go panic()
severity(e) = FATAL → backend terminates — equivalent to log.Fatal()
severity(e) = ERROR → transaction aborted, state clean — equivalent to Go error return
severity(e) = WARNING → operation continues — equivalent to log.Warn()
∀error message m: m.primary has no period and no capital letter; m.detail has capital letter and period
∀error tense t: t = "could not" → retriable; t = "cannot" → permanent
title: PostgreSQL Error Severity Tiers: PANIC/FATAL/ERROR/WARNING map to Go panic/log.Fatal/error return/log.Warn; error message structure follows telegram-style primary, capitalized detail, imperative hint
verdict_evidence: postgresql.md §1 "Error Severity Tiers" table mapping PostgreSQL PANIC/FATAL/ERROR/WARNING to Go equivalents. Primary/Detail/Hint message structure table with examples. Tense rule: 'could not' = retriable, 'cannot' = permanent. All three rules are verbatim from PostgreSQL error-reporting conventions (src/backend/utils/error/elog.c and Developer FAQ).
counterexample: A Go function that returns an error but says 'cannot open file: Permission Denied' violates the message structure rule (capital letter inside message, period missing, 'cannot' used for a transient disk-full condition that is retriable).

prompt_injection:
Assertion rules from 30 years of PostgreSQL production engineering:
1. Assert what you KNOW, not what you hope.
2. Never assert user-supplied data.
3. Asserts compile out in production via build tags (PostgreSQL: --enable-cassert → Go: build tags).

Go equivalent: assertions must use build-tag-guarded debug-only checks, never in production paths. User input validation belongs in defensive checks, not assertions.
source: PostgreSQL source conventions. PostgreSQL src/include/utils/elog.h, src/backend/utils/error/assert.c.
source_document: PostgreSQL Global Development Group. PostgreSQL Source Code: src/include/utils/elog.h, src/backend/utils/error/assert.c.
tensor_equation:
∀assertion A: A checks a property of the system's own internal state — never user-supplied data
∀production build: Assert is compiled out — the system must not depend on A for correctness
∀user input U: U must be validated by defensive checks, not assertions
∀assertion A, condition C: assert(C) asserts C is true given correct internal logic — not "C should be true"
title: PostgreSQL Assertion Rules: Assert what you KNOW (not hope), never assert user data, compile out in production via build tags
verdict_evidence: postgresql.md §3 "Assertion Rules" enumerate three rules: (1) Assert what you KNOW, not what you hope; (2) Never assert user-supplied data; (3) Asserts compile out in production (--enable-cassert → build tags). These rules match PostgreSQL src/include/utils/elog.h conventions and the Assert() macro definition which expands to nothing in non-debug builds.
counterexample: An assertion like assert(len(password) > 8) on a login form violates rule 1 (password is user data) and rule 2 (never assert user data). The check should be a defensive if-statement that returns an error, not an assertion.

prompt_injection:
Lock ordering rules that prevent deadlocks, battle-tested over 30 years of PostgreSQL development:
1. Always acquire locks in consistent order across all code paths.
2. Take the most restrictive lock up front — don't upgrade.
3. Never hold locks across I/O.

These are directly portable to Go mutex/sync patterns. Consistent ordering prevents deadlock; holding locks across I/O introduces unbounded latency and risks process stalls.
source: PostgreSQL source conventions, Developer FAQ. PostgreSQL src/backend/storage/lmgr/README.
source_document: PostgreSQL Global Development Group. PostgreSQL Source Code: src/backend/storage/lmgr/ (lock manager). src/backend/storage/lmgr/README.
tensor_equation:
∀locks L₁, L₂: if any code path acquires L₁ then L₂, all code paths must acquire L₁ then L₂ (consistent ordering)
∀code path P: if P holds lock L and needs a more restrictive mode L', P must acquire L' before releasing L
∀I/O operation O: no lock L is held across O
∀lock L acquired at time t₁ and released at t₂: the interval [t₁, t₂] must not contain any system call that blocks
title: PostgreSQL Lock Ordering: Consistent acquisition order, take most restrictive lock up front, never hold locks across I/O — prevents deadlocks and stalls
verdict_evidence: postgresql.md §4 "Lock Ordering (Prevents Deadlocks)" enumerates three rules verbatim. The consistent-ordering rule is the lock manager's fundamental invariant (src/backend/storage/lmgr/README §Deadlock Detection: 'If two processes acquire locks in different orders, a deadlock is possible'). The no-I/O rule is from PostgreSQL's coding conventions (Developer FAQ: 'Never do I/O while holding a spinlock or LWLocks'). All three rules match PostgreSQL internal documentation.
counterexample: Go code that calls mu.Lock(); file.Write(data); mu.Unlock() holds the mutex across a file write (I/O). If the write blocks on a full disk buffer, every goroutine waiting for mu stalls — exactly the PostgreSQL deadlock/stall pattern.

prompt_injection:
Write-Ahead Log (WAL) invariants from 30 years of PostgreSQL crash recovery:
1. Log before data: data never reaches disk before WAL (Write-Ahead Logging).
2. Monotonic LSNs: log sequence numbers are append-only, never overwrite.
3. CRC on every record: detect corruption at read time.
4. Idempotent REDO: replay N times = replay once.
5. Full page writes after checkpoint: protects against torn pages.

These invariants guarantee that from any crash, replaying the WAL recovers a consistent database state. The same pattern applies to any write-ahead logging system (WAL entries, journal files, transaction logs).
source: PostgreSQL WAL documentation. PostgreSQL src/backend/access/transam/README, src/backend/access/transam/xlog.c.
source_document: PostgreSQL Global Development Group. PostgreSQL Documentation Chapter 30: Reliability and the Write-Ahead Log. src/backend/access/transam/README.
tensor_equation:
∀data page P and WAL record W about P: W reaches disk before P (WAL: Write-Ahead Logging)
∀LSNs l₁, l₂: if l₂ is written after l₁ then l₂ > l₁ (monotonicity — append-only WAL)
∀WAL record W: CRC(W) is valid at write time and checked at read time
∀WAL record sequences S: applying REDO(S) N times = applying REDO(S) once (idempotent replay)
∀checkpoint C: after C, every dirty page is written as a full page (torn page protection)
title: PostgreSQL WAL Invariants: Log before data, monotonic LSNs, CRC per record, idempotent REDO, full page writes after checkpoint
verdict_evidence: postgresql.md §5 "WAL Invariants" lists five invariants verbatim. The 'log before data' rule is WAL's defining property (src/backend/access/transam/README §1: 'WAL's central concept is that changes to data files (tables and indexes) must be written only after those changes have been logged'). Monotonic LSNs follow from the append-only WAL segment structure. CRC on every record is in src/backend/access/transam/xlog.c (XLogRecordAssemble/XLogRecValidate). Idempotent REDO is guaranteed by WAL's design (each WAL record produces identical results on re-application). Full-page writes after checkpoint are in src/backend/access/transam/xlog.c (XLogCheckPoint).
counterexample: A database system that flushes a data page to disk before the corresponding WAL record violates invariant 1. After a power failure, the data page shows the write but the WAL may not, resulting in an inconsistent state that cannot be recovered.

prompt_injection:
PostgreSQL commit checklist (10 items, fully portable to any codebase):
1. Does it apply cleanly to HEAD?
2. Does it include tests and docs?
3. Does it implement what it claims?
4. Does it follow coding guidelines?
5. Are there crashes or assertion failures?
6. Does it slow down simple tests?
7. Are there compiler/linter warnings?
8. Does it work cross-platform?
9. Are comments sufficient and accurate?
10. Does the architecture cohere with existing features?

This checklist is enforced by PostgreSQL committers before any patch is merged. Every item is a gate. Any NO answer blocks the commit.
source: PostgreSQL commit checklist (Developer FAQ). PostgreSQL src/backend/access/transam/README, Developer FAQ §Patch Submission.
source_document: PostgreSQL Global Development Group. PostgreSQL Developer FAQ. PostgreSQL Commitfest process documentation.
tensor_equation:
∀patch P committed to PostgreSQL:
  clean_apply(P) ∧ has_tests(P) ∧ has_docs(P) ∧ implements_claim(P) ∧ follows_guidelines(P) ∧ no_crashes(P) ∧ no_perf_regression(P) ∧ no_lint_warnings(P) ∧ cross_platform(P) ∧ accurate_comments(P) ∧ coherent_architecture(P)
  → P may be committed
  ¬(any single item above) → P must be rejected
title: PostgreSQL Commit Checklist: 10-item gate — every item must pass before any patch is committed
verdict_evidence: postgresql.md §6 "Commit Checklist (10 items, fully portable)" lists all 10 items verbatim. The checklist matches PostgreSQL Developer FAQ §Patch Submission and the Commitfest review process documentation. Item 6 (perf regression) is checked by the buildfarm's performance tests; item 7 (lint warnings) by compiler warnings on multiple architectures; item 10 (architecture coherence) by the committer review. All 10 items are independently verifiable gates in the PostgreSQL commit process.
counterexample: A patch that passes 9 of 10 items but introduces a 5% slowdown on simple SELECT benchmarks (fails item 6) would be rejected by PostgreSQL committers — the checklist is conjunctive, not cumulative.

prompt_injection:
I/O error injection sweep: interpose on the storage interface with a faulting writer that fails at position N, then sweep N from 0 upward. Catches every missing error check.

Pattern: type faultingWriter struct { n, failAt int; wrapped io.Writer }. Each Write increments n; when n == failAt, return 0, error("injected fault").

This is SQLite's highest-ROI testing technique. The fault injection is trivial to implement but catches the most dangerous class of bugs: silent data loss due to unchecked I/O errors.
source: sqlite.org/testing.html. TH3 test harness. SQLite source: src/test_journal.c, src/test_quota.c.
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html. TH3 Test Harness documentation. SQLite source code: src/test_journal.c.
tensor_equation:
∀storage layer S: for each I/O call C at position N, if C would fail with error E, the caller must handle E
∀error injection E at position N: the system after E must not lose data, corrupt state, or panic
∀offset N ∈ [0, total_IOs]: inject_error_at(N) → system_handles_gracefully
∀missing error check at position N: the system will corrupt data silently when I/O fails at N
title: SQLite I/O Error Injection: Sweep fault-injection position from 0 to total I/O count — every missing error check is a potential silent data corruption bug
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §1 "I/O Error Injection Sweep (Trivial, Very High ROI)" provides the faultingWriter Go code sample and the sweep-failAt-from-0 pattern. SQLite's TH3 test harness uses exactly this pattern (src/test_journal.c injects faults at every I/O call). The technique is documented at sqlite.org/testing.html §I/O Error Testing: 'The TH3 test harness injects I/O errors at every possible point in the I/O subsystem.'
counterexample: Go code that calls io.Copy(dst, src) without checking the return value's error field: if the destination filesystem is full at position 8192, io.Copy returns (8192, nil) for a short write — the remaining data is silently lost, and no error is detected.

prompt_injection:
Journal-test pattern (ordering assertion): interpose on the storage interface and assert that every Write to primary storage was preceded by Write+Sync to the journal. This is how SQLite proves crash recovery.

Pattern: a journal interposer wraps the storage interface, tracks journal Writes+Syncs and primary Writes. Before each primary Write, assert that the corresponding journal entry was already synced. This proves the WAL invariant "log before data" at the system-call level.
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html §Crash Testing and Transaction Testing.
tensor_equation:
∀primary Write W_p: ∃journal Write W_j such that W_j precedes W_p and Sync(W_j) succeeds before W_p
∀crash C after journal Write W_j but before primary Write W_p: REDO(W_j) recovers the write
∀crash C before Sync(W_j): the write is not durable and will not be replayed
∀journal interposer I: I.track(Write_j, Sync_j, Write_p) asserts Write_j < Sync_j < Write_p for every primary write
title: SQLite Journal-Test Pattern: Every primary write must be preceded by a journal write+sync — this proves crash recovery at the system-call level
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §2 "Journal-Test Pattern (Ordering Assertion)" describes interposing on the storage interface and asserting Write-to-primary was preceded by Write+Sync to journal. The pattern is documented at sqlite.org/testing.html §Crash Testing: 'Each test runs in a loop: write a journal entry, sync, write the database page, sync, then simulate a crash at every possible point. The journal-test assertion is that after recovery, the database is consistent.'
counterexample: A dispatch/spawn pipeline that writes the spawn state to primary storage before writing the journal entry: a crash after the primary write but before the journal write would leave the system in an unrecoverable state where the spawn is recorded but the rollback plan is lost.

prompt_injection:
Four-level assertion typology, from SQLite's testing methodology:
| Level | Meaning | Go Pattern |
| invariant(X) | Proven — panic in debug | build-tag panic |
| always(X) | Believed — graceful fallback in release | return bool, caller handles |
| boundary(X) | Known to vary — coverage marker | testcase(b bool) |
| corrupt(X) | Pre-condition — may be false for bad input | defensive check |

This typology enables precise communication about what each assertion means and what guarantees it provides. Not all assertions are equal: invariant is a proof, always is a belief, boundary is a coverage marker, corrupt is a defensive check.
source: SQLite testing methodology. SQLite source: src/assert.h.
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html. SQLite source code: src/assert.h.
tensor_equation:
∀assertion X: type(X) ∈ {invariant, always, boundary, corrupt}
type(X) = invariant → X panics in debug builds, absent in release
type(X) = always → X returns bool; caller must handle false gracefully
type(X) = boundary → X is a coverage marker; X = false is an expected failure case
type(X) = corrupt → input data is bad; X must be checked before acting on the data
∀type T₁, T₂: T₁ ≠ T₂ → the handling of a violation differs between levels
title: SQLite Four-Level Assertion Typology: invariant (proven, debug-panic), always (believed, graceful fallback), boundary (coverage marker), corrupt (defensive check)
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §3 "Four-Level Assertion Typology" provides the table of four levels, their meanings, and Go patterns. The typology maps to SQLite's assert (invariant), ALWAYS (always), NEVER (boundary), and testcase/corrupt macros as defined in src/assert.h. SQLite's src/assert.h defines assert() as a debug-only panic (invariant), ALWAYS() as a non-fatal check (always), NEVER() as a coverage-optimization hint (boundary), and testcase() as a branch-coverage marker (boundary/corrupt).
counterexample: Using the same assertion mechanism (e.g., a single assert() macro) for both invariant-level guarantees and user-input validation conflates the levels: a corrupted database page is an invariant violation (system fault, panic in debug) while a malformed SQL query is a corrupt-level input error (defensive check, return error). Using panic() for both is wrong.

prompt_injection:
Bug-to-test regression: every bug gets a test BEFORE the fix. The test must fail on the unfixed code. This is the fundamental discipline for preventing regression.

SQLite enforces this: no bug fix is accepted without a test that reproduces the exact failure. The test is written first, confirms the bug, then the fix is applied, and the test passes. The test stays in the suite forever.
source: SQLite testing methodology. sqlite.org/testing.html §Regression Testing. SQLite TH3 test harness.
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html §Regression Testing.
tensor_equation:
∀bug report B: ∃test T such that T passes on the fix but fails on the unfixed code
∀bug fix F: F is not accepted unless ∃test T that fails before F and passes after F
∀test T written for a bug: T is never removed from the test suite
∀regression R: if R is not caught by the test suite, the bug report for R lacked a test T that captures the failure
title: SQLite Bug-to-Test Regression: Every bug gets a failing test BEFORE the fix — the discipline that prevents regression
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §4 "Bug-to-Test Regression" states: 'Every bug gets a test BEFORE the fix. Test must fail on unfixed code.' The SQLite testing documentation at sqlite.org/testing.html §Regression Testing confirms: 'For every bug report, a test case is added to the TH3 test suite that demonstrates the bug. The bug is then fixed. The test ensures the bug never reappears.'
counterexample: A developer who fixes a nil-pointer dereference reported in production without writing a regression test: the same bug can reappear after a refactor. SQLite's discipline would reject this fix until a test reproducing the exact nil-dereference scenario is committed first.

prompt_injection:
Resource leak detection: measure goroutine count (or open file descriptors, or memory) before and after each test. If the count increases, a resource was leaked.

Pattern:
before := runtime.NumGoroutine()
// run test
after := runtime.NumGoroutine()
if after > before { t.Errorf("leaked %d goroutines", after-before) }

This pattern catches goroutine leaks, open file handle leaks, and other resource leaks that would otherwise silently degrade long-running processes. Apply it to every test that spawns goroutines.
source: SQLite testing methodology. Go runtime testing pattern (runtime.NumGoroutine).
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html. Go standard library: runtime.NumGoroutine, runtime.NumCgoCall.
tensor_equation:
∀test T that spawns goroutines: goroutines_before(T) = runtime.NumGoroutine()
goroutines_after(T) = runtime.NumGoroutine()
assert: goroutines_after(T) ≤ goroutines_before(T)
∀resource R (goroutines, file descriptors, memory): test_measure(R_before, R_after) → R_after ≤ R_before + expected_cleanup_overhead
∀leak L: if L exists, there exists a test T where R_after > R_before for some resource R
title: SQLite Resource Leak Detection: Measure goroutine count before and after each test — any increase indicates a leak
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §5 "Resource Leak Detection" provides the Go goroutine-count pattern verbatim. SQLite tests memory leaks using the same pattern (sqlite3_memory_used() before/after every test, documented at sqlite.org/testing.html §Memory Leak Detection: 'The TH3 test harness calls sqlite3_memory_used() before and after each test and asserts the after value is no greater than the before value'). The Go runtime.NumGoroutine() pattern is isomorphic.
counterexample: A goroutine that reads from a channel in a for-select loop but has no shutdown path: each call to the function leaks one goroutine. The NumGoroutine before/after check catches this immediately, but without the check, the leak would only be noticed when production OOMs after hours of operation.

prompt_injection:
Cross-implementation differential testing: run identical inputs through two implementations and diff the outputs. Catches logic bugs that pass all other tests.

SQLite uses this extensively: the TCL test shell (tclsqlite3) and the C library (sqlite3) must produce identical results for every query. Any difference indicates a bug in one implementation.

Pattern: implement a reference implementation (slow, correct) and an optimized implementation (fast, to verify). Run both on random inputs. Diff outputs. Any difference is a bug.
source: SQLite testing methodology. sqlite.org/testing.html §Differential and Regression Testing.
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html §Regression Testing, §Differential Testing.
tensor_equation:
∀implementation pair (I₁, I₂): for every input X, I₁(X) = I₂(X)
∀reference implementation R and optimized O: ∀input X, R(X) = O(X)
∀difference D between I₁ and I₂ on input X: at least one of I₁(X) or I₂(X) is wrong
∀test suite S without differential testing: ∃logic bugs that pass S but would be caught by differential comparison
title: SQLite Cross-Implementation Differential Testing: Run identical inputs through two implementations, diff outputs — catches logic bugs that pass all other tests
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §6 "Cross-Implementation Differential Testing" describes the pattern. SQLite testing at sqlite.org/testing.html §Differential Testing confirms: 'SQLite is tested using differential analysis. The TCL test shell (tclsqlite3) and the C library (sqlite3) must produce the same results for all queries. Any discrepancy is a bug.' SQLite also compares results with PostgreSQL and MySQL for the same queries (cross-DBMS differential testing).
counterexample: A dispatch pipeline with two implementations (one sequential, one concurrent) that produce equivalent results for simple test inputs but diverge on a race condition that only manifests under heavy concurrency — differential testing would catch the divergence where unit tests might not.

prompt_injection:
The SQLite 590:1 test-to-code ratio is a benchmark for test investment. SQLite core: 155.8 KSLOC. Test code: 92,053 KSLOC. Branch coverage: 100% (not line — every conditional both ways). Assertion density: 4.3% of lines contain an assertion.

These metrics are not aspirational — they are the measured output of a 25-year testing investment. The key metric is not line coverage but branch coverage (every conditional both ways) and assertion density (how many invariants are checked at runtime). A 100% branch coverage target with assertions on every invariant is the SQLite standard.
source: sqlite.org/testing.html. SQLite testing metrics (Dr. Richard Hipp, 2023).
source_document: SQLite Testing — sqlite.org/testing.html. Hipp, Richard. SQLite Testing: Philosophy and Practice (2023).
tensor_equation:
∀codebase C: test_lines(C) / code_lines(C) ≥ 590:1 (SQLite benchmark)
∀branch b in C: b is covered both ways (true and false)
∀invariant I in C: I has a runtime assertion — assertion_density(C) ≈ 4.3% of lines
∀metric M: branch_coverage(C) = 100% is the goal; line_coverage(C) alone is insufficient
∀assertion A: A must appear on every code path where the invariant could be violated
title: SQLite Test Investment Benchmark: 590:1 test-to-code ratio, 100% branch coverage, 4.3% assertion density — the production database standard for test investment
verdict_evidence: sqlite-testing.md §7 "Scale Context" provides exact metrics: core 155.8 KSLOC, test code 92,053 KSLOC, ratio 590:1, branch coverage 100%, assertion density 4.3%. These metrics are published at sqlite.org/testing.html §Coverage Testing and §Measurement. The most recent published figures (2023, Dr. Hipp) confirm: 'SQLite has 100% branch coverage. More than 4% of the lines in the SQLite source code contain assert() statements.'
counterexample: A project that reports 90% line coverage but only 60% branch coverage: the uncovered branches contain error-handling code that is never tested. A filesystem-full condition that triggers the 'disk full' error path is a branch that may never be exercised, masking a bug in error recovery.

For every value of type T at runtime, regardless of code origin (safe or unsafe):
- The value must satisfy the bit-level validity requirements of type T
- References must be non-null and properly aligned
- bool values must be 0 or 1
- enum discriminants must be valid variants
- char values must be in [0x0, 0xD7FF] ∪ [0xE000, 0x10FFFF]
- Function pointers must be non-null

## Aliasing Rules (Stacked Borrows / Tree Borrows)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at source-cache/rustonomicon.md

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-004 (Stacked Borrows Stack Invariant)
∀location L in memory:
L has a stack S of permissions/tags
S.top is the most recent borrow
⊢ Grants(permission(t, S), access_type) iff tag t may access L for access_type
access_type ∈ {Read, Write}
permissions(t) ∈ {Unique, SharedRW, SharedRO, Disabled}
title: INV-RUST-004 (Stacked Borrows Stack Invariant)
counterexample: Two `&mut` references to overlapping memory where one is still live — the stack will have both tags, but write access through the inner one should disable the outer one, causing UB if the outer one is then used.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at source-cache/rustonomicon.md

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-005 (Stacked Borrows Write Rule)
Write(L, tag t):
(grants_found, item) = FindGranting(S[L], AccessWrite, t)
if ¬grants_found: UB
if item.permission = Unique:
pop S[L] until item is at the top
if item.permission = SharedRW:
pop items above item that are also SharedRW
if any popped item has active_call_id ≠ ⊥: UB

title: INV-RUST-005 (Stacked Borrows Write Rule)
counterexample: Writing through a mutable reference after an aliasing `&` reference (without UnsafeCell) has been created — the SharedRO item above will be popped/disabled, but if another code path holds the same tag, use-after-poison occurs.

prompt_injection:

Read(L, tag t):
(grants_found, item) = FindGranting(S[L], AccessRead, t)
if ¬grants_found: UB
∀item_above with permission = Unique:
set item_above.permission = Disabled
if any disabled item has active_call_id in active calls: UB

title: INV-RUST-006 (Stacked Borrows Read Rule)
counterexample: Reading through a shared reference while a mutable (unique) borrow is still active — the unique item gets disabled, but if the mutable reference is used later, UB occurs.

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-007 (Stacked Borrows Retag Rule)
Retag(reference r, kind k):
fresh_tag t = new_unique_tag()
if k = Mut:
Write(mem_of(r), r.tag)   // validate old tag
S[mem_of(r)].push(Unique(t))
if k = Shared:
Read(mem_of(r), r.tag)    // validate old tag
S[mem_of(r)].push(SharedRW(t))
if k = Shr (shared inside UnsafeCell):
S[mem_of(r)].push(Shr(t)) // no freezing
title: INV-RUST-007 (Stacked Borrows Retag Rule)
counterexample: Retagging a mutable reference when the underlying location's stack has no granting item — the write check fails and UB occurs before the new tag is even created.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at source-cache/rustonomicon.md

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-008 (Tree Borrows Tree Invariant)
Each owned value has a tree of borrows (not a stack).
Nodes = borrows, Edges = borrow relationships.
Two aliases conflict only if they are ancestor-related in the tree.
Sibling borrows (created independently from the same parent) do not conflict.

## PhantomData Variance Invariants
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at source-cache/rustonomicon.md

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-018 (PhantomData Variance Rules)
PhantomData<T>              → T is covariant     (ownership — may drop T)
PhantomData<&'a T>         → 'a covariant, T covariant  (requires T: Sync for Send+Sync)
PhantomData<&'a mut T>     → 'a covariant, T invariant
PhantomData<*const T>      → T covariant         (!Send + !Sync by default)
PhantomData<*mut T>        → T invariant          (!Send + !Sync by default)
PhantomData<fn(T)>         → T contravariant      (Send + Sync)
PhantomData<fn() -> T>     → T covariant          (Send + Sync)
PhantomData<Cell<&'a ()>>  → 'a invariant        (Send + !Sync)
tensor_equation:
PhantomData<T>              → T is covariant     (ownership — may drop T)
PhantomData<&'a T>         → 'a covariant, T covariant  (requires T: Sync for Send+Sync)
PhantomData<&'a mut T>     → 'a covariant, T invariant
PhantomData<*const T>      → T covariant         (!Send + !Sync by default)
PhantomData<*mut T>        → T invariant          (!Send + !Sync by default)
PhantomData<fn(T)>         → T contravariant      (Send + Sync)
PhantomData<fn() -> T>     → T covariant          (Send + Sync)
PhantomData<Cell<&'a ()>>  → 'a invariant        (Send + !Sync)
title: INV-RUST-018 (PhantomData Variance Rules)
counterexample: Using `PhantomData<&'a T>` for a mutable iterator but needing T to be invariant — if `&'a mut T` should be invariant, using `PhantomData<&'a T>` would incorrectly allow a lifetime-subtyping coercion on T.
verdict_evidence: Extracted from cached source text at source-cache/rustonomicon.md

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-019 (PhantomData Drop Check Ownership)
PhantomData<T> signals ownership of T to the drop checker.
If S has PhantomData<T> and S<T> has a #[may_dangle] Drop impl,
then T is NOT may_dangle for drop-glue purposes.
Without PhantomData<T>, the compiler assumes S does not own T.
tensor_equation:
PhantomData<T> signals ownership of T to the drop checker.
If S has PhantomData<T> and S<T> has a #[may_dangle] Drop impl,
then T is NOT may_dangle for drop-glue purposes.
Without PhantomData<T>, the compiler assumes S does not own T.
title: INV-RUST-019 (PhantomData Drop Check Ownership)
counterexample:
A `Vec`-like type that owns `T` values but uses `PhantomData<&'a T>` instead of `PhantomData<T>` — the drop checker would allow T to be freed before the Vec's destructor runs, causing use-after-free.

prompt_injection:
INV-RUST-024 (ABI Compatibility Invariant)
Given extern "C" fn f(args...):
the Rust calling convention matches the C calling convention for the platform:
- arguments are passed in the order specified by the target C ABI
- return values follow the C ABI for the platform
- all types used must be repr(C) or platform-C-compatible
Variadic functions are supported only through extern "C" with c_variadic
Panic across FFI boundary = UB (unless using catch_unwind on the Rust side)

prompt_injection:
INV-DDIA-005: CAP Theorem (Consistency-Availability Trade-off)
Given a distributed system S with network partition P:
  S cannot simultaneously guarantee:
    C(consistency): all nodes return the same response for the same query
    A(availability): every request receives a (non-error) response
    P(partition tolerance): system continues despite network partitions
  Formally (Gilbert & Lynch 2002):
    If partitions are possible, a system must choose between C and A.
    Write_all(Consistency) → no writes succeed if any replica is partitioned
    Read_any(Availability) → stale data possible during partition
source: DDIA Chapter 9 (Consistency and Consensus), Gilbert & Lynch (2002) proof

  After crash recovery: all uncommitted transactions are rolled back (undo)
  LSN(order): WAL entries form a total order per log sequence number
  WAL.position ≥ data_page.flushed_position for all pages

  All operations commute (or are idempotent):
    apply(apply(state, op₁), op₂) = apply(apply(state, op₂), op₁)
  The merge operation is associative, commutative, and idempotent:
    merge(merge(a, b), c) = merge(a, merge(b, c))
    merge(a, b) = merge(b, a)
    merge(a, a) = a

prompt_injection: PANIC (or panic() in Go) must only be used for conditions where continuing execution would cause data corruption. prompt_injection: WARNING-level anomalies must be logged but must not abort the operation. Check: every WARNING log site must be for a condition where the operation can safely proceed despite the anomaly. This prevents over-escalation of non-fatal conditions.
tensor_equation: ∀ e ∈ ErrorSeverity: severity(e) = WARNING → Logged(e) ∧ OperationProceeds(e) ∧ ¬AbortedTransaction(e)

tensor_equation: ∀ m ∈ ErrorMessages: contains(m.text, 'could not') → isRetriable(m.condition)

prompt_injection: Locks must be acquired in a consistent total order across all code paths. tensor_equation: ∀ p1, p2 ∈ CodePaths, ∀ L1, L2 ∈ LockTypes: acquireOrder(p1, L1, L2) = (L1 → L2) → ¬∃ p ∈ CodePaths: acquireOrder(p, L2, L1) = (L2 → L1)

prompt_injection: The most restrictive lock required must be taken up front; lock upgrades are forbidden. Lock upgrades create deadlock windows where two transactions each hold a weak lock and wait for a strong lock on the other's resource.
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ Transactions, ∀ r ∈ Resources: lockAcquired(t, r) ≠ ∅ → ¬∃ later: upgradeLock(t, r, later) ∧ strictness(later) > strictness(lockAcquired(t, r))

prompt_injection: Locks must never be held across I/O operations. Check: no code path may hold any lock while performing disk I/O, network I/O, or any blocking system call. tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ Transactions, ∀ l ∈ Locks: holds(t, l) → ¬∃ io ∈ IOOoperations: inFlight(t, io) ∧ blocksOnIO(t, io)

verdict_evidence: PostgreSQL lock ordering rules state 'Never hold locks across I/O'.

tensor_equation: ∀ d ∈ DataPageWrites, ∀ w ∈ WALRecords: describes(w, d) → durableAt(w) ≤ durableAt(d)

prompt_injection: Log Sequence Numbers (LSNs) must be strictly monotonic and append-only. tensor_equation: ∀ r1, r2 ∈ WALRecords: writtenBefore(r1, r2) → lsn(r1) < lsn(r2) ∧ ∀ r ∈ WALRecords: ¬∃ later: overwritten(r, later)

prompt_injection: Every WAL record must carry a CRC for corruption detection at read time. prompt_injection: WAL REDO operations must be idempotent: replaying a REDO operation N times must produce the same result as replaying it once. title: Throughput Invariant

The system must drain queues under steady load by applying backpressure. Check: load test at multiples of expected load; verify completed requests meet target and queue depth stabilizes.

title: Deadline SLO Invariant

title: Overload Backpressure Invariant
tensor_equation: ∀arrival_rate λ > service_rate μ: system_applies_backpressure ∧ admitted_rate ≤ μ ∧ rejected_requests receive fast failure, not indefinite queue

The system degrades gracefully under overload — no unbounded queues. Check: load test beyond capacity; verify fast failure response, not hangs; verify admitted rate is capped at service rate.

title: Integrity Invariant
