title: Preservation (Subject Reduction)
tensor_equation: ∀e, e', τ, Γ: (Γ ⊢ e : τ ∧ e ⟼ e') ⇒ Γ ⊢ e' : τ
verdict_evidence: Stated as the Preservation component of the Safety Theorem in PFPL Chapter 4 (Statics and Dynamics).
prompt_injection: For any well-typed expression e of type τ in context Γ, if e reduces in one step to e', then e' must also have type τ in the same context. This is the first half of type safety: evaluation preserves typing. Violation means the type system does not actually constrain runtime behavior. Check that every reduction rule in the language preserves the type of the term being reduced.

title: Progress (Canonical Forms)
verdict_evidence: Stated as the Progress component of the Safety Theorem in PFPL Chapter 4 (Statics and Dynamics).
prompt_injection: For any closed, well-typed expression e, either e is already a value of its type, or there exists some e' to which e can take a step. This is the second half of type safety: well-typed programs do not get stuck. A stuck term (neither a value nor able to step) indicates a missing case in the type system or dynamics. Verify that every syntactic form has a reduction rule for all well-typed configurations.

title: Type Safety (Progress + Preservation)
verdict_evidence: Stated as Theorem 4.1 (Safety) in PFPL Chapter 4, combining Progress and Preservation.
prompt_injection: Every closed, well-typed expression either evaluates to a value of the same type, or takes a step to another well-typed expression. This is the fundamental safety theorem for typed languages: the type system guarantees that evaluation never reaches a stuck or ill-typed state. A counterexample is a term that type-checks but after some number of reduction steps reaches an ill-typed state, such as an ill-formed typecase reaching a dead branch.

title: Structural Dynamics Determinism
tensor_equation: ∀e, e₁, e₂: (e ⟼ e₁ ∧ e ⟼ e₂) ⇒ e₁ ≡ e₂
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Structural Dynamics section as a property of deterministic transition systems.
prompt_injection: The transition relation is a partial function: if an expression e can step to e₁ and also to e₂, then e₁ and e₂ must be syntactically identical. This ensures that evaluation is deterministic — at most one reduction rule applies at each step. Violation means the semantics admits nondeterministic evaluation order, which can produce different results. Verify that for every syntactic form, at most one inference rule in the dynamics can match a given configuration.

title: Substitution Principle
tensor_equation: ∀e, e', Γ, τ, τ': (Γ ⊢ e : τ ∧ Γ, x:τ ⊢ e' : τ') ⇒ Γ ⊢ [e/x]e' : τ'
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 3, Hypothetical Judgments as the Substitution Principle.
prompt_injection: If a term e has type τ in context Γ, and a term e' has type τ' in context Γ extended with x:τ, then substituting e for x in e' yields a term of type τ' in context Γ. This is the fundamental lemma that makes hypothetical judgments compositional. Substituting a term of type τ into a position expecting a different type would violate this principle. Verify that the substitution operation is type-preserving for all syntactic forms.

title: Weakening Property
tensor_equation: ∀e, Γ, τ, τ', x ∉ dom(Γ): (Γ ⊢ e : τ) ⇒ Γ, x:τ' ⊢ e : τ
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 3, Structural Properties of Judgments as the Weakening property.
prompt_injection: Adding an unused assumption to the typing context does not change the type of any expression. The type of e depends only on the free variables it actually uses. Violation occurs when shadowing or name capture changes the meaning of an expression — for instance, if a newly introduced variable x shadows a free variable reference in e, altering its type. Verify that the type system is stable under context extension for all syntactic forms.

title: Mode Correctness for Judgments
tensor_equation: ∀r ∈ Rules: mode(r) is valid ∧ (∀premise ∈ r: outputs(premise) ⊆ inputs(conclusion))
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 2, Inductive Definitions as a constraint on inference rule mode assignments.
prompt_injection: Every inference rule in an inductive definition must have a valid mode assignment: each premise's output positions must be determined by the inputs of the conclusion. A rule whose premises introduce variables not determined by the conclusion's inputs is uncomputable — the variables cannot be resolved during proof search. Verify that every rule in a judgment definition has a mode assignment where all bound variables appear only in positions that respect their input/output mode.

title: Rule Induction Principle
tensor_equation: ∀Φ: (Φ closed under all rules of inductive definition D) ⇒ (∀J ∈ D: Φ(J))
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 2, Inductive Definitions as the Rule Induction Principle.
prompt_injection: If a property Φ is closed under all the rules of an inductive definition (i.e., it holds for all axioms and is preserved by every inference rule), then Φ holds for every derivable judgment. This is the principle of rule induction, which states that the least fixed point of the rule operator is exactly the set of all derivable judgments. A property that holds for all axioms and is preserved by all rules but fails for some derivable judgment would indicate the definition is not the least fixed point.

title: Well-foundedness of Inductive Definitions
tensor_equation: ∀J ∈ D: ∃ finite derivation tree D' concluding J with all leaves being axioms of D
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 2, Inductive Definitions as the well-foundedness property of inductive definitions.
prompt_injection: Every derivable judgment in an inductive definition must have a finite derivation tree whose leaves are axioms. Equivalently, the relation defined by the rules is well-founded under the sub-derivation ordering — there are no infinite descending chains. An inductive definition with a rule that dispatches to itself without consuming any structure would violate well-foundedness and admit no finite derivations for any judgment.

title: Iterated Inductive Definitions
tensor_equation: ∀J(i) ∈ D, ∀r deriving J(i): ∀premise with index j in r: j < i under well-founded ordering <
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 2, Iterated Inductive Definitions as the indexing constraint for well-founded orderings.
prompt_injection: For an inductive definition indexed by a well-founded ordering, if a judgment J(i) holds at index i, then all premises of the rule used to derive J(i) must have indices strictly less than i. This ensures the definition is well-founded and computable. A type system where defining type A requires type B and defining type B requires type A without a well-founded measure on the definitions would violate this constraint.

title: Simultaneous Inductive Definitions
tensor_equation: ∀P, Q mutually defined by rules R: (P ∪ Q) = lfp(R) ∧ each rule for P may use P or Q in premises
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 2, Simultaneous Inductive Definitions as the least fixed point property for mutual definitions.
prompt_injection: For two mutually-defined judgments P and Q, the combined relation (P ∪ Q) must be the least fixed point of the combined set of rules for both. Each rule for P may reference P or Q in its premises. Violation occurs when the mutual recursion is not well-founded, such as mutually recursive types where the positivity condition is violated (e.g., type T = T -> int without a recursive type constructor).

title: Typing of Variables
tensor_equation: ∀x, τ, Γ: Γ, x:τ ⊢ x : τ
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 3, Statics as the Variable typing rule (projection from context).
prompt_injection: A variable x is assigned type τ if and only if the typing context Γ contains the binding x:τ. This is the base case of the type system — the variable rule is how type information flows from the context into expressions. A variable used with a type different from its declaration in the context would violate this rule. Verify that variable lookup always returns the type declared at the binding site.

title: Substitution Property of Typing
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 3, Substitution Lemma as the Substitution Property of Typing.
prompt_injection: If e has type τ under context Γ, and e' has type τ' under context Γ, x:τ, Γ', then substituting e for x in both e' and the context suffix Γ' preserves typing: the result has type τ' under context Γ, [e/x]Γ'. This is the full substitution lemma that handles contexts with bindings both before and after x. A type system that allows a term to change type after substitution due to losing type information would violate this property.

title: Transitivity of Hypothetical Judgments
tensor_equation: ∀e, e', Γ, τ, τ': (Γ ⊢ e : τ ∧ Γ, x:τ ⊢ e' : τ') ⇒ Γ ⊢ let x = e in e' : τ'
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 3, Hypothetical Judgments as the transitivity of hypothetical judgments.
prompt_injection: If e has type τ and e' has type τ' under the hypothesis that x has type τ, then the let-binding 'let x = e in e'' has type τ' (without the hypothesis). This is the typing rule for let, which internalizes the substitution principle as a language construct. A binding construct that does not properly substitute the value and thus type-checks incorrectly would violate this transitivity.

title: Uniqueness of Typing
tensor_equation: ∀e, Γ, τ, τ': (Γ ⊢ e : τ ∧ Γ ⊢ e : τ') ⇒ τ ≡ τ'
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Statics as the uniqueness of typing in the simply-typed setting.
prompt_injection: In the simply-typed setting, every expression has at most one type in a given context. If an expression can be assigned two different types τ and τ' under the same context Γ, then τ and τ' must be syntactically identical. This property holds for simply-typed languages but may fail in systems with subtyping or polymorphism. A term that type-checks as both Int and Bool in the same context would violate uniqueness.

title: Product Type Elimination
tensor_equation: ∀e, e₁, e₂, Γ, τ₁, τ₂: (Γ ⊢ e : τ₁ × τ₂ ∧ e ⟼⁎ ⟨e₁, e₂⟩) ⇒ (e·fst ⟼⁎ e₁ : τ₁ ∧ e·snd ⟼⁎ e₂ : τ₂)
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 6, Finite Product Types as the product type elimination rule.
prompt_injection: If a product-typed expression evaluates to a pair of values ⟨e₁, e₂⟩, then projecting the first component yields e₁ of type τ₁ and projecting the second yields e₂ of type τ₂. The elimination forms (fst and snd) must respect the structure of the pair. A product where projecting the first component returns a value of the wrong type would violate this invariant.

title: Sum Type Case Analysis
tensor_equation: ∀e, e₁, e₂, e₁', e₂', Γ, τ₁, τ₂: (Γ ⊢ e : τ₁ + τ₂) ⇒ ((e ⟼⁎ l·e₁ ⇒ case(e; x.e₁'; y.e₂') ⟼⁎ [e₁/x]e₁') ∧ (e ⟼⁎ r·e₂ ⇒ case(e; x.e₁'; y.e₂') ⟼⁎ [e₂/y]e₂'))
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 7, Finite Sum Types as the sum type case analysis rule.
prompt_injection: Case analysis on a sum type must dispatch to the correct branch based on the injection tag. If the sum value is a left injection l·e₁, the case expression reduces to the left branch with e₁ substituted for the bound variable. If it is a right injection r·e₂, the right branch is taken. Injecting a value of type τ₁ into the left of a τ₁ + τ₂ and having the case-expression dispatch to the right branch would violate this invariant.

title: Function Application Dynamics
tensor_equation: ∀e₁, e₂, e₁', v₂, Γ, τ₂, τ: (Γ ⊢ e₁ : τ₂ → τ ∧ Γ ⊢ e₂ : τ₂ ∧ e₁ ⟼⁎ λ(x:τ₂.e₁') ∧ e₂ ⟼⁎ v₂) ⇒ e₁(e₂) ⟼⁎ [v₂/x]e₁' : τ
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Function Types as the function application dynamics.
prompt_injection: When a function e₁ of type τ₂ → τ is applied to an argument e₂ of type τ₂, and both evaluate to a lambda abstraction and a value respectively, the application reduces to the body of the lambda with the argument substituted for the parameter, yielding a result of type τ. An application that applies a function expecting τ₂ to an argument of type τ₁ where τ₁ ≠ τ₂ would violate this invariant.

title: Canonical Forms for Functions
tensor_equation: ∀v, τ₂, τ: (· ⊢ v : τ₂ → τ ∧ v val) ⇒ (∃x, e: v ≡ λ(x:τ₂.e))
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Canonical Forms Lemma as the canonical forms property for function types.
prompt_injection: Every closed value of function type τ₂ → τ must be syntactically a lambda abstraction λ(x:τ₂.e). This is the canonical forms lemma for function types, which is essential for proving type safety. A value of function type that is not syntactically a lambda abstraction (e.g., an integer erroneously labeled as a function type) would violate this lemma.

title: Termination for Simply-Typed Lambda Calculus
tensor_equation: ∀e, τ: (· ⊢ e : τ in STLC with Nat) ⇒ ∃v: e ⟼⁎ v
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 10, Termination Theorem (Tait's Method) as the normalization property of STLC.
prompt_injection: Every well-typed closed term in the simply-typed lambda calculus (with natural numbers) evaluates to a value — all well-typed terms normalize. This is proved via Tait's computability method. The property holds only for the pure simply-typed lambda calculus without general recursion; adding a fixpoint operator (as in PCF) admits divergent terms. Verify that the language does not include unrestricted recursion if termination is required.

title: Hereditarily Defined Reducibility
tensor_equation: ∀e, τ: (e : τ) ⇒ e ∈ Red(τ) ∧ Red(τ₁ → τ₂) = {e | e ⟼⁎ λx.e' ∧ ∀e₁ ∈ Red(τ₁): [e₁/x]e' ∈ Red(τ₂)}
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 10, Tait's Computability Method as the definition of hereditary reducibility.
prompt_injection: Every well-typed term is reducible at its type, where reducibility is defined by induction on the type structure. For base types, reducibility means normalization to a value. For function types, a term is reducible if it evaluates to a lambda whose body, when applied to any reducible argument, produces a reducible result. A term in Red(τ₁ → τ₂) that when applied to a reducible argument of type τ₁ produces a result not in Red(τ₂) would violate this definition.

title: Unique Evaluation Context Decomposition
tensor_equation: ∀e (not a value): ∃!E, r: e = E[r] where E is a unique evaluation context and r is a unique redex
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Contextual Dynamics as the unique decomposition property.
prompt_injection: Every non-value expression can be uniquely decomposed into an evaluation context E and a redex r such that e = E[r]. The evaluation context specifies where the next reduction step occurs (e.g., leftmost-outermost for call-by-name, leftmost-innermost for call-by-value). An expression that can be decomposed as two different evaluation contexts each with a different redex would give ambiguous evaluation order and violate determinism.

title: Contextual Dynamics Preservation
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Contextual Dynamics as the preservation property under evaluation contexts.
prompt_injection: If an expression e decomposes as E[r] and the redex r reduces to r', then filling the evaluation context with r' must produce a well-typed expression of the same type τ. This is contextual preservation: reducing a subterm inside an evaluation context preserves the type of the whole expression. Filling a well-typed evaluation context with a properly reduced redex that produces an ill-typed expression would violate this invariant.

title: Equational Theory Reflexivity Symmetry Transitivity
tensor_equation: ∀e: e ≡ e ∧ ∀e₁, e₂: (e₁ ≡ e₂ ⇒ e₂ ≡ e₁) ∧ ∀e₁, e₂, e₃: ((e₁ ≡ e₂ ∧ e₂ ≡ e₃) ⇒ e₁ ≡ e₃)
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Equational Dynamics as the reflexivity, symmetry, and transitivity of the equational theory.
prompt_injection: The equational theory of a language must be an equivalence relation: reflexive (every expression equals itself), symmetric (if e₁ equals e₂ then e₂ equals e₁), and transitive (if e₁ equals e₂ and e₂ equals e₃ then e₁ equals e₃). An equational theory where a reduces to b and b reduces to c but a is not equated to c would violate transitivity. Verify that the equational theory is closed under these three properties.

title: Stability Under Reduction Contexts
tensor_equation: ∀e₁, e₂, C[·]: (e₁ ≡ e₂) ⇒ C[e₁] ≡ C[e₂]
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Equational Dynamics as the stability of equivalence under reduction contexts.
prompt_injection: If two expressions e₁ and e₂ are equated, then replacing one with the other inside any context C[·] preserves equivalence. This is the congruence property: the equational theory is a congruence with respect to all expression-forming operations. Replacing a subterm with a β-equivalent term that changes the overall meaning due to non-confluent reduction would violate this invariant.

title: Cost Preservation
tensor_equation: ∀e, e', τ, Γ, k: (Γ ⊢ e : τ ∧ e ⟼ₖ e') ⇒ k ∈ ℕ₀
verdict_evidence: Stated in PFPL Chapter 4, Cost Dynamics as the cost preservation property.
prompt_injection: Every reduction step carries a non-negative integer cost k representing the number of primitive computation steps. The total cost of evaluating a closed, well-typed expression to a value is the sum of the costs of each step. An infinite loop that claims to terminate in k steps would violate cost-monotonicity. Verify that cost annotations are non-negative and that the cost of a multi-step evaluation equals the sum of individual step costs.

title: Types are preserved across evaluation (Preservation)
tensor_equation: ∀ t, t', T: if ⊢ t : T and t → t', then ⊢ t' : T
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), lines 15 and 20: formal definition of Preservation (Subject Reduction) from TAPL — the type of a term is invariant under evaluation.
prompt_injection: Verify that every evaluation step preserves the type of the expression. If a term of type T steps to a term of a different type, the type system is unsound. In Go, check that type assertions, type switches, and reflection do not silently change the effective type of a value without a corresponding type guard.

title: Pattern matching must be exhaustive
tensor_equation: ∀ pattern match expressions over type T: ∀ constructors C of T, ∃ a case branch handling C, so that ∄ unhandled constructors at runtime
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 27: enforcement pattern specifies pattern matching exhaustiveness — the compiler checks that all cases are handled. A missing case is a Progress violation.
prompt_injection: Every pattern match (switch, type switch, match expression) must handle all possible constructors of the matched type. A missing case is a Progress violation — the program can get stuck at runtime when it encounters an unhandled constructor. In Go, type switches must have a default case; value switches over enums must cover all enum members.

title: Nil-capable types must be guarded before dereference
tensor_equation: ∀ expressions e of nullable type: dereference of e is only valid after nil-check(e) yields non-nil; equivalently, use Option<T> to statically distinguish nullable from non-nullable types
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 28: enforcement pattern specifies null safety — Option<T>/Maybe T/nil-checked types. Nil dereference is a Progress violation (the program gets stuck).
prompt_injection: Any type that can be nil (pointers, interfaces, maps, slices, channels, functions in Go) must be checked for nil before dereference. The type system should distinguish nullable from non-nullable types (Option<T>/Maybe T pattern). In Go, nil dereference is a runtime panic, not a compile-time error — use nil guards on all pointer/interface/map/slice/chan/func values before use.

title: Resources should be used exactly once (ownership)
tensor_equation: ∀ resources r: useCount(r) should = 1; use-after-free and double-free are violations (affine: at most once; linear: exactly once)
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 29: enforcement pattern specifies resource linearity — a resource is used exactly once (Rust's ownership, linear types).
prompt_injection: Resources (file handles, locks, network connections, memory allocations) should be used at most once (affine) or exactly once (linear). Use-after-close and double-close are violations of resource linearity. In Go, defer is the primary mechanism for ensuring resources are released, but the compiler does not enforce linearity — audit Close() calls and resource lifecycle manually.

title: Public interfaces should not use untyped escape hatches
tensor_equation: ∀ functions f in PublicAPI: ∀ parameters p of f, type(p) should ≠ void* ∧ type(p) should ≠ Object ∧ type(p) should ≠ any; use generics instead
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 33 and Section 3 (Simple Types), line 98: enforcement patterns state interface{}/any bypasses type checking, and no void*/Object/any in public interfaces — use generics instead.
prompt_injection: Public API signatures must not use untyped escape hatches (void*, Object, any, interface{}). These bypass the type checker and void the safety guarantees provided by the type system. Use generics/type parameters instead. In Go, audit all exported functions for interface{} or any parameters — replace with type parameters where possible to restore compile-time type safety.

title: Type switches should include a default case
tensor_equation: ∀ type switch expressions: ∃ a default case branch, so that ∄ unhandled dynamic types at runtime
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 34: enforcement pattern specifies type switch with default case for exhaustiveness check.
prompt_injection: Every type switch should include a default case to ensure exhaustiveness. Without a default case, a type switch over an interface value may encounter an unexpected concrete type at runtime, causing the switch to silently do nothing — a Progress violation. The default case should either handle the unexpected type gracefully or return an error.

title: Static analysis should catch type errors before runtime
tensor_equation: ∀ codebases: ∃ static analysis that catches common type errors (nil deref, missing cases, type mismatches) before runtime execution
verdict_evidence: Section 1 (Type Safety), line 35: enforcement pattern specifies go vet — static analysis for common type errors. The principle generalizes: tooling should exist to catch errors the compiler misses.
prompt_injection: Static analysis tooling should catch common type errors (nil dereference potential, missing switch cases, type mismatches) before the program reaches runtime. The compiler alone may not catch all errors — dedicated static analysis tools fill the gap between what the type system proves and what can go wrong at runtime. In Go, go vet and staticcheck are the minimum bar; integrate them into CI.

title: Function composition should be preferred over mutation
tensor_equation: ∀ computations producing value v: prefer compose(f, g)(x) over mutate(x); where compose builds new values rather than modifying existing state in place
verdict_evidence: Section 2 (Lambda Calculus), line 64: enforcement pattern specifies function composition over mutation.
prompt_injection: Prefer function composition and immutable data transformations over in-place mutation. Composed pure functions are easier to reason about, test, and parallelize — they have no side effects and their output depends only on their input. In Go, prefer returning new values over modifying pointer receiver state when possible; use value receivers for types that should be treated as immutable.

title: Non-TCO languages should use explicit loops over deep recursion
tensor_equation: ∀ recursive functions in non-tail-call-optimized languages: recursion depth should be bounded by explicit iteration (loop) when input size is unbounded
verdict_evidence: Section 2 (Lambda Calculus), lines 65 and 71: enforcement pattern specifies tail-call optimization or explicit loops for non-TCO languages; Go has no tail-call optimization — use loops instead of deep recursion.
prompt_injection: In languages without tail-call optimization (Go, Python, JavaScript without strict mode), deep recursion on unbounded input will overflow the stack. Use explicit loops for unbounded iteration. Reserve recursion for cases where the depth is provably bounded (e.g., tree depth limited by data structure design). Audit all recursive functions for stack safety.

title: Function application preserves type consistency (Arrow Elimination)
tensor_equation: ∀ Γ, f, x, T₁, T₂: if Γ ⊢ f : T₁ → T₂ and Γ ⊢ x : T₁, then Γ ⊢ f x : T₂
verdict_evidence: Section 3 (Simple Types), lines 88-92: formal definition of the typing rule for application (arrow elimination) from TAPL.
prompt_injection: The type application rule is the fundamental operation of typed computation: if a function expects type T₁ and returns type T₂, and an argument has type T₁, then the application has type T₂. Every call site must satisfy this rule — the argument type must match the parameter type. In Go, this is enforced by the compiler for all function calls, but type assertions (x.(T)) and reflection bypass it — those bypass points are where type safety violations occur.

title: Domain concepts should use named types over primitives
tensor_equation: ∀ domain concepts D with underlying representation R: define type D R (newtype/named type) rather than using R directly, so that D₁ ≠ D₂ even when R₁ = R₂
verdict_evidence: Section 3 (Simple Types), line 99: enforcement pattern specifies newtypes — type UserID string (not just string) — the type IS the documentation.
prompt_injection: Domain concepts with the same underlying primitive type (e.g., UserID string and SessionID string) should use distinct named types. This prevents accidental argument swapping — the compiler will reject passing a UserID where a SessionID is expected. In Go, use type UserID string rather than string directly; the type system will prevent cross-assignment without an explicit conversion.

title: Public function signatures must carry explicit type annotations
tensor_equation: ∀ functions f in PublicAPI: all parameters and return values of f must have explicit type annotations; type inference may be used for local/private bindings only
verdict_evidence: Section 3 (Simple Types), line 97: enforcement pattern specifies type annotations on all function signatures.
prompt_injection: Every public function, method, and interface must have explicit type annotations on all parameters and return values. Type inference is acceptable for local variables and private helper functions, but public API surfaces must be explicitly typed. This serves as enforced documentation — the compiler checks that callers conform, and readers understand the contract without tracing through inference.

title: Phantom types should enforce compile-time invariants
tensor_equation: ∀ compile-time invariants: phantom type parameters (present at type level, absent at runtime) should be used to enforce invariants with zero runtime cost
verdict_evidence: Section 3 (Simple Types), line 100: enforcement pattern specifies phantom types — type parameters that do not appear in the runtime representation but enforce invariants at compile time.
prompt_injection: Phantom type parameters (type variables that appear in type signatures but not in the runtime data representation) should be used to encode and enforce invariants at compile time with zero runtime cost. They allow the type checker to prove properties (e.g., units of measure, state machine phases, access control levels) without adding runtime overhead. In Go, phantom types can be modeled with unused type parameters in generic structs.

title: Subtype values usable wherever supertype expected (Subsumption)
tensor_equation: ∀ Γ, t, S, T: if Γ ⊢ t : S and S <: T, then Γ ⊢ t : T
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), lines 121-125: formal definition of the subsumption rule from TAPL.
prompt_injection: If type S is a subtype of T, any value of type S can be used in any context expecting type T. This is the semantic foundation of polymorphism through subtyping. It must hold without runtime surprises — the subtype must not weaken preconditions or strengthen postconditions relative to the supertype. In Go, interface satisfaction is structural: any type with the required methods automatically satisfies the interface, enabling subsumption.

title: Composition (has-a) should be preferred over inheritance (is-a)
tensor_equation: ∀ type relationships between A and B: prefer A has-a B (composition) over A is-a B (inheritance)
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), line 131: enforcement pattern specifies prefer composition over inheritance — the 'has-a' over 'is-a' principle.
prompt_injection: Composition (embedding one type within another) should be preferred over inheritance (extending a base class). Inheritance creates fragile base class problems and deep coupling; composition provides the same code reuse with looser coupling. In Go, struct embedding is composition, not inheritance — promoted methods can be overridden by the embedding type, avoiding the diamond problem.

title: Interfaces should be small (1-3 methods)
tensor_equation: ∀ interfaces I: |methods(I)| should ≤ 3; prefer single-method interfaces for maximum composability
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), line 132: enforcement pattern specifies interface segregation — small interfaces (1-3 methods) over large ones.
prompt_injection: Interfaces should be small, ideally 1-3 methods. Large interfaces violate the Interface Segregation Principle: clients are forced to depend on methods they do not use. Small interfaces are easier to implement, test, and compose. In Go, the standard library exemplifies this: io.Reader (1 method), io.Writer (1 method), http.Handler (1 method). Split large interfaces into smaller, focused ones.

title: Subtypes must satisfy Liskov substitution principle
tensor_equation: ∀ S <: T: ∀ properties φ(x) provable for objects x of type T, φ(y) must be provable for objects y of type S; S must not require more from callers (weaken preconditions) nor guarantee less to callers (strengthen postconditions) than T
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), line 133: enforcement pattern specifies Liskov substitution principle — S <: T means S can do everything T can, and nothing that contradicts T's contract.
prompt_injection: The Liskov substitution principle (LSP) is the semantic contract of subtyping: a subtype must be usable in all contexts where the supertype is expected, without surprising behavior. The subtype must not strengthen preconditions (require more), weaken postconditions (guarantee less), or violate invariants of the supertype. In Go, any type implementing an interface must satisfy the interface's documented behavioral contract, not just its method signatures.

title: Inheritance depth should not exceed 2-3 levels
tensor_equation: ∀ class hierarchies: max depth from root to leaf should ≤ 3
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), line 134: enforcement pattern specifies no deep inheritance hierarchies — max depth 2-3.
prompt_injection: Deep inheritance hierarchies (more than 2-3 levels) are brittle, hard to understand, and prone to the fragile base class problem. Each level of inheritance adds coupling between the base and derived classes. Prefer shallow hierarchies with composition. In Go, this is naturally enforced by the lack of class inheritance — struct embedding provides delegation without deep nesting.

title: Interface satisfaction must be compile-time verifiable
tensor_equation: ∀ types T that claim to implement interface I: compile-time check var _ I = (*T)(nil) must succeed, proving T satisfies I at compile time rather than failing at runtime
verdict_evidence: Section 4 (Subtyping), line 140: enforcement pattern specifies compile-time interface satisfaction check — var _ Interface = (*Impl)(nil).
prompt_injection: Every concrete type that is intended to implement an interface should have a compile-time assertion proving satisfaction. This converts a potential runtime error (interface not satisfied) into a compile-time error. In Go, use var _ Interface = (*Impl)(nil) at the package level to verify that *Impl satisfies Interface. This assertion has zero runtime cost and guards against accidental interface breakage during refactoring.

title: Polymorphic functions abstract over types (∀-Introduction, System F)
tensor_equation: ∀ Γ, X, t, T: if Γ, X ⊢ t : T, then Γ ⊢ ΛX.t : ∀X.T
verdict_evidence: Section 5 (Parametric Polymorphism), lines 156-159: formal definition of type abstraction (∀-introduction) from System F.
prompt_injection: A polymorphic function (one that works uniformly for all types) is formed by abstracting over a type variable. If a term t has type T when X is a free type variable, then the type abstraction ΛX.t has type ∀X.T — it works for any type substituted for X. In Go, generic functions with type parameters implement this: func Map[T, U any](xs []T, f func(T) U) []U abstracts over T and U.

title: Polymorphic functions instantiated by type application (∀-Elimination, System F)
tensor_equation: ∀ Γ, t, U, X, T: if Γ ⊢ t : ∀X.T, then Γ ⊢ t[U] : [X↦U]T
verdict_evidence: Section 5 (Parametric Polymorphism), lines 161-164: formal definition of type application (∀-elimination) from System F.
prompt_injection: A polymorphic term of type ∀X.T can be instantiated with a concrete type U, yielding a term of type T with X replaced by U. This is how generics are used at call sites — the polymorphic function is applied to a concrete type argument. In Go, generic function instantiation (e.g., Map[int, string](...)) is the practical implementation of type application. The compiler checks that the type argument satisfies all constraints.

title: Recursive types unfold via fixed-point operator μ
tensor_equation: ∀ recursive types: μα.T ≅ [α ↦ μα.T]T (unfolding the fixed-point yields the body with the recursive reference substituted, and folding is the inverse)
verdict_evidence: Section 6 (Recursive Types), lines 195-198: formal definition of iso-recursive types — μα.T ≅ [α ↦ μα.T]T.
prompt_injection: Recursive types are defined via the fixed-point operator μ. A recursive type μα.T is isomorphic to its one-step unfolding [α ↦ μα.T]T — meaning the recursive reference α is replaced by the full recursive type itself. This is the formal foundation for all recursive data structures (lists, trees, graphs). In Go, recursive structs like type Node struct { Left, Right *Node } are the practical embodiment of iso-recursive types.

title: Pattern matching on recursive types must cover base and recursive cases
tensor_equation: ∀ pattern matches on recursive type μα.T: the match must cover (a) all base case constructor(s) and (b) all recursive case constructor(s), so that ∄ well-typed values with no matching branch
verdict_evidence: Section 6 (Recursive Types), line 205: enforcement pattern specifies pattern matching exhaustiveness check on recursive types — both base cases and recursive cases must be covered.
prompt_injection: Pattern matching on recursive types must handle both base cases and recursive cases. For a list type (Nil | Cons(A, List A)), a match must cover Nil (base) and Cons (recursive). For a tree, it must cover Leaf (base) and Node (recursive). Missing a case is a Progress violation — a well-typed value has no matching branch and the program gets stuck. In Go, recursive type dispatch via type switches must include the base case (nil pointer check for linked structures).

title: Recursive calls must be structurally smaller than input
tensor_equation: ∀ recursive functions f on data type D: each recursive call f(x') must satisfy x' <_struct x, where <_struct is the structural subterm ordering (x' is a proper subterm of x)
verdict_evidence: Section 6 (Recursive Types), line 206: enforcement pattern specifies structural recursion — the recursive call is on a structurally smaller value.
prompt_injection: Every recursive call must operate on a structurally smaller value than the original input. For a list, the recursive call processes the tail; for a tree, it processes a subtree. This guarantees that the recursion makes progress toward a base case. In Go, verify that every recursive function call reduces some measure of the input (list length, tree depth, counter value) toward the termination condition.

title: Recursion must be well-founded (guaranteed termination)
tensor_equation: ∀ recursive definitions: the recursion must be well-founded — ∃ measure function m: D → ℕ such that ∀ recursive call f(x') from f(x), m(x') < m(x), guaranteeing termination for all inputs
verdict_evidence: Section 6 (Recursive Types), line 207: enforcement pattern specifies termination — the recursion must be well-founded (no infinite recursion).
prompt_injection: All recursive functions must be provably terminating for all inputs. The recursion must be well-founded: there exists a strictly decreasing measure (natural number) from each call to the next, ensuring the base case is always reached. Infinite recursion is a Progress violation — the program gets stuck. In Go, stack overflow from unbounded recursion is a runtime crash; audit all recursive functions and prove termination or bound recursion depth.

title: Type inference should find the most general (principal) type
tensor_equation: ∀ expressions e where type inference is applied: the inferred type T should be the principal (most general) type of e, such that any other valid type for e is an instance of T via substitution
verdict_evidence: Section 7 (Type Inference), lines 227 and 240: the source states Hindley-Milner type inference is sound, complete, and always finds the most general (principal) type.
prompt_injection: When type inference is used, the inferred type should be the most general (principal) type — not an over-specialized one. If the compiler infers a type that is more specific than expected, your design might be wrong. In Go, := infers the type from the right-hand side; if the inferred type is more specific than expected, consider whether an explicit annotation or a more general return type is warranted.

title: Gradual typing: annotate public interfaces, infer private code
tensor_equation: ∀ codebases: public API surfaces should carry explicit type annotations; private/local bindings may rely on type inference — the boundary between annotated and inferred regions is the module/package interface
verdict_evidence: Section 7 (Type Inference), line 241: enforcement pattern specifies gradual typing — annotate public interfaces, infer private ones.
prompt_injection: Apply gradual typing: annotate all public interfaces (exported functions, methods, types) with explicit types for documentation and contract enforcement; use type inference for private/local variables where the type is obvious from context. The module/package boundary is the annotation boundary. In Go, exported identifiers must have explicit types; := is appropriate for local variables with obvious initializers.

title: Define minimal core calculus before implementing features
tensor_equation: ∀ language features or system components: before implementation, define a featherweight (minimal) core calculus that captures the essential semantics; prove progress and preservation for the core
verdict_evidence: Section 8 (Featherweight Calculi), lines 259-261 and 265-266: the principle of stripping a language feature to its essence, writing the minimal calculus first, and proving it works before adding syntactic sugar.
prompt_injection: Before implementing any non-trivial feature or component, write the 'featherweight' version first — the smallest core that captures the essential idea. Prove it works (progress and preservation hold). Then extend with syntactic sugar and convenience features. If the minimal core has a flaw, the full implementation will inherit it. In orbit, the congestion VM IS a featherweight calculus (4 opcodes); the circuit breaker state machine IS a featherweight calculus (3 states, 3 events).

title: Full implementation must conservatively extend core calculus
tensor_equation: ∀ language implementations: the full implementation must be a conservative extension of its core calculus — for all terms t expressible in the core calculus, eval_full(t) must produce the same result as eval_core(t)
verdict_evidence: Section 8 (Featherweight Calculi), line 268: enforcement pattern states the full implementation is a conservative extension of the calculus — no new evaluation paths contradict the core semantics.
prompt_injection: The full implementation of a system must be a conservative extension of its core calculus: every property proven about the core must hold in the full system, and no new evaluation paths must contradict the core semantics. If the core calculus guarantees termination, the full implementation must also guarantee termination. In orbit, the congestion VM's full interpreter must not introduce evaluation paths that violate the 4-opcode core semantics.

title: Core calculus flaws propagate to full implementation
tensor_equation: ∀ language features: if the core calculus has a soundness problem (∃ t: ⊢ t : T ∧ t gets stuck), then the full implementation also has that problem — soundness bugs are monotonic with respect to feature extension
verdict_evidence: Section 8 (Featherweight Calculi), line 269: enforcement pattern states if the calculus has a problem, the implementation will too.
prompt_injection: If the core calculus of a system has a soundness violation (a well-typed term that gets stuck, a type that is not preserved across steps, an infinite recursion), the full implementation inherits that violation — adding features never fixes core soundness bugs. This is monotonic: features can only add new bugs, never remove existing ones. Fix core calculus problems before building features on top, or the bugs will persist in every extension.

title: Causality: happens-before ordering enforced
tensor_equation: ∀ A, B: A happens-before B ⇒ system enforces A completes before B starts
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Causality row: violation is race condition, uninit memory.
prompt_injection: Verify for every happens-before pair that ordering primitives (locks, channels, barriers) guarantee A completes before B. Violation: race condition or uninitialized memory.

title: Ownership: exactly one owner per resource
tensor_equation: ∀ resource R, ∀ time t: |owners(R, t)| = 1
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Ownership row: 'Only X owns R', violation is double-free, leaked resource.
prompt_injection: Verify each resource has exactly one owner responsible for its lifecycle. Violation: double-free or resource leak.

title: Containment: values stay within declared bounds
tensor_equation: ∀ value v with declared bounds [min, max]: v ∈ [min, max]
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Containment row: 'Value stays inside bounds', violation is path traversal, buffer overflow.
prompt_injection: Verify every value stays within its declared bounds. Check array indices, buffer sizes, path constraints. Violation: path traversal, buffer overflow.

title: Correspondence: mirrored states are consistent
tensor_equation: ∀ states Sa, Sb where Sb mirrors Sa: Sa ≡ Sb within freshness window
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Correspondence row: 'State A mirrors State B', violation is cache inconsistency, stale replica.
prompt_injection: Verify that when state B mirrors state A, the two are consistent. Check cache invalidation, replica synchronization. Violation: stale cache, inconsistent replica.

title: Exclusion: mutually exclusive states cannot co-occur
tensor_equation: ∀ states A, B where A excludes B: ¬(A ∧ B) at any time t
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Exclusion row: 'A and B cannot both hold', violation is deadlock, split-brain.
prompt_injection: Verify that mutually exclusive states never co-occur. Check lock ordering, leader election, mutual exclusion primitives. Violation: deadlock, split-brain.

title: Rate: events bounded per time window
tensor_equation: ∀ event type E, ∀ window W: count(E, W) ≤ N
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Rate row: '≤ N events per window', violation is DoS, retry storm, token exhaustion.
prompt_injection: Verify event rates are bounded. Check rate limiting, token buckets, backpressure. Violation: DoS, retry storm, token exhaustion.

title: Liveness: required events eventually occur
tensor_equation: ∀ event E that must occur: ◇E (eventually E)
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Liveness row: 'Event E eventually happens', violation is goroutine leak, starvation.
prompt_injection: Verify that required events eventually occur. Check goroutine lifecycle, progress guarantees, starvation prevention. Violation: goroutine leak, starvation.

title: Freshness: data served is not stale
tensor_equation: ∀ data d served to consumer: age(d) ≤ max_staleness
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Freshness row: 'Data has timestamp T', violation is stale data served as current.
prompt_injection: Verify data served to consumers is not stale. Check timestamps, TTLs, cache invalidation. Violation: stale data served as current.

title: Uniqueness: IDs appear exactly once
tensor_equation: ∀ identifier id in system: count(id) = 1
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Uniqueness row: 'ID appears exactly once', violation is duplicate, missing entry.
prompt_injection: Verify every identifier appears exactly once. Check primary keys, UUID generation, idempotency keys. Violation: duplicate or missing entry.

title: Persistence: data survives crash or restart
tensor_equation: ∀ data d marked durable: d survives crash/restart cycle
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Persistence row: 'Survives crash/restart', violation is lost data, corrupt state.
prompt_injection: Verify durable data survives crash/restart. Check fsync, WAL, journaling, commit protocols. Violation: lost data, corrupt state.

title: Atomicity: operations are all-or-nothing
tensor_equation: ∀ operation O: either all effects of O apply, or none apply
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Atomicity row: 'All-or-nothing', violation is partial update, torn write.
prompt_injection: Verify operations are atomic. Check transactions, rollback, error handling that unwinds partial state. Violation: partial update, torn write.

title: Idempotency: repeated execution equals single execution
tensor_equation: ∀ operation O: exec(O, 1) ≡ exec(O, N) for N ≥ 1
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Idempotency row: 'Do it once = do it N times', violation is double-charge, replay attack.
prompt_injection: Verify idempotent operations produce the same result when executed once or N times. Check idempotency keys, deduplication. Violation: double-charge, replay attack.

title: Isolation: concurrent execution equals sequential
tensor_equation: ∀ concurrent operations O1..On: result ≡ some serial execution of O1..On
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Isolation row: 'Concurrent = sequential', violation is dirty read, lost update.
prompt_injection: Verify concurrent operations produce results equivalent to some serial order. Check isolation levels, locking, MVCC. Violation: dirty read, lost update.

title: Non-interference: operations do not disturb each other
tensor_equation: ∀ operations A, B where A ⊥ B: exec(A) does not affect exec(B) outcome
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Non-Interference row: 'Operation A doesn't disturb B', violation is clipboard destroyed by paste.
prompt_injection: Verify independent operations do not interfere. Check shared mutable state, global variables, side effects. Violation: one operation corrupts another's state.

title: Reversibility: operations can undo to prior state
tensor_equation: ∀ operation O with undo capability: undo(O) restores state to pre-O condition
verdict_evidence: Lens 1, Reversibility row: 'Can undo to prior state', violation is failed migration leaves corrupt DB.
prompt_injection: Verify operations that claim reversibility can actually undo to the prior state. Check migrations, rollbacks, compensating transactions. Violation: corrupt state after failed operation.

title: Deep modules: large behavior behind small interface
tensor_equation: ∀ module M: interface_size(M) ≪ complexity(M) where interface conveys behavior
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Depth row: 'Large behavior behind small interface', anti-pattern is shallow interface as complex as code.
prompt_injection: Verify modules provide large behavior through a small interface. Check whether interface complexity is proportional to implementation complexity. Anti-pattern: shallow module where interface is as complex as the code.

title: Information hiding: callers do not need implementation details
tensor_equation: ∀ module M, ∀ caller C: C's correctness does not depend on M's internal implementation
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Information Hiding row: 'Caller doesn't need to know', anti-pattern is leaky abstraction.
prompt_injection: Verify callers are not coupled to implementation details. Check if changing internal representation breaks callers. Anti-pattern: leaky abstraction where callers depend on internals.

title: Seam: behavior alterable without editing source
tensor_equation: ∀ module M: ∃ seam S such that behavior of M can be altered through S without editing M's source
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Seam row: 'Can alter behavior without editing there', anti-pattern is hardcoded dependency.
prompt_injection: Verify modules have seams that allow behavior alteration without editing the module. Check for dependency injection, interfaces, configuration. Anti-pattern: hardcoded dependency.

title: Locality: changes confined to one place
tensor_equation: ∀ change C: C requires modification in exactly one place
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Locality row: 'Change in one place', anti-pattern is same change across N files.
prompt_injection: Verify that a single logical change requires modification in only one place. Check for duplication, scattered concerns. Anti-pattern: same change across N files.

title: Leverage: one implementation serves N callers
tensor_equation: ∀ common behavior B: B is implemented once and reused by all callers
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Leverage row: 'One implementation, N callers', anti-pattern is every caller reimplements.
prompt_injection: Verify common behavior is implemented once and reused. Check for copy-pasted code, duplicated logic. Anti-pattern: every caller reimplements the same behavior.

title: Test surface equals interface
tensor_equation: ∀ module M: tests for M exercise M through the same interface callers use
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Test surface = Interface row: 'Tests cross the same seam as callers', anti-pattern is testing internals, brittle tests.
prompt_injection: Verify tests exercise modules through the same interface callers use. Check for tests that depend on internal state. Anti-pattern: testing internals causes brittle tests.

title: Deletion test: module removal removes complexity
tensor_equation: ∀ module M: deleting M reduces total system complexity
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Deletion test row: 'Delete module → complexity vanishes or spreads?', anti-pattern is pass-through modules.
prompt_injection: Verify that deleting a module removes complexity rather than spreading it. Check if the module does real work or just passes through. Anti-pattern: pass-through modules that add no value.

title: Accept dependencies, do not create them
tensor_equation: ∀ function f: f accepts its dependencies as parameters, does not construct them internally
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Accept don't create dependencies row: 'fn(order, gateway) not fn(order) that news Stripe', anti-pattern is untestable code.
prompt_injection: Verify functions accept dependencies as parameters rather than constructing them internally. Check for new, direct construction of external services. Anti-pattern: untestable code that constructs its own dependencies.

title: Return values, do not side-effect
tensor_equation: ∀ function f: f returns its result, does not mutate input state
verdict_evidence: Lens 2, Return don't side-effect row: 'fn(cart) → Discount not fn(cart) mutates cart', anti-pattern is hidden mutation.
prompt_injection: Verify functions return results rather than mutating input state. Check for functions that modify their arguments. Anti-pattern: hidden mutation of input parameters.

title: Main thread UI: all UI updates on main queue
tensor_equation: ∀ UIKit/AppKit update operation U: U executes on main queue/thread
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Main thread UI row: 'All UIKit/AppKit updates on main queue', enforced by MainActor and runtime assertion.
prompt_injection: Verify all UI framework updates execute on the main thread. Check for background thread UI mutations. Enforcement: MainActor annotation, runtime main-thread assertion.

title: View lifecycle order is enforced
tensor_equation: ∀ view V: V.viewDidLoad → V.viewWillAppear → V.viewDidAppear in strict order
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, View lifecycle row: 'viewDidLoad → viewWillAppear → viewDidAppear', order enforced by UIKit.
prompt_injection: Verify view lifecycle methods are called in the correct order. Check that code does not depend on out-of-order lifecycle calls. Enforcement: UIKit guarantees call order.

title: Memory: no retain cycles in ARC-managed code
tensor_equation: ∀ object graph under ARC: no retain cycles exist
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Memory row: 'ARC retain cycles → leak', detected by Xcode memory graph and instruments.
prompt_injection: Verify no retain cycles exist in ARC-managed object graphs. Check for strong reference cycles, especially with closures capturing self. Detection: Xcode memory graph, instruments.

title: Background tasks: limited time, specific task types
tensor_equation: ∀ background task T: T has finite time budget and uses declared task type
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Background row: 'Limited time, specific task types', enforced by BackgroundTasks framework.
prompt_injection: Verify background tasks respect time limits and use proper task types. Check background URL sessions, processing, fetch tasks. Enforcement: BackgroundTasks framework.

title: Sandbox: no filesystem access outside container
tensor_equation: ∀ filesystem operation F from app A: F's target path is within A's container
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Sandbox row: 'No filesystem access outside container', OS-enforced like gVisor L1.
prompt_injection: Verify no filesystem access outside the app container. Check file paths, entitlements, sandbox configuration. Enforcement: OS-level sandbox.

title: Accessibility: VoiceOver and Dynamic Type supported
tensor_equation: ∀ UI element E: E is accessible via VoiceOver and responds to Dynamic Type
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Accessibility row: 'VoiceOver, Dynamic Type', via UIAccessibility protocol.
prompt_injection: Verify all UI elements support VoiceOver and Dynamic Type. Check accessibility labels, traits, dynamic font sizing. Enforcement: UIAccessibility protocol.

title: Animation: 60fps with no main thread blocking
tensor_equation: ∀ animation frame: frame renders in ≤ 16.67ms with no main thread blocking
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, iOS/macOS, Animation row: '60fps, no main-thread blocking', detected by CADisplayLink and instruments.
prompt_injection: Verify animations run at 60fps without main thread blocking. Check frame budget, off-main-thread work. Detection: CADisplayLink, instruments.

title: Event loop: do not block the main thread
tensor_equation: ∀ operation on main thread: duration ≤ T where T does not block the event loop
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, Web, Event loop row: 'Don't block the main thread', enforced via async/await and Web Workers.
prompt_injection: Verify no operation blocks the main thread. Check for synchronous I/O, long-running computation on the main thread. Enforcement: async/await, Web Workers.

title: DOM consistency: render is function of state
tensor_equation: ∀ render: Render = f(state), never mutate DOM directly
verdict_evidence: Lens 3, Web, DOM consistency row: 'Render = f(state), never mutate DOM directly', enforced by React/Vue/Svelte.
prompt_injection: Verify DOM rendering is a pure function of state. Check for direct DOM mutations outside the framework. Enforcement: React, Vue, Svelte declarative rendering.

title: Same-origin: script access limited to same origin

verdict_evidence: Lens 3, Web, Same-origin row: 'Script can only access same origin', browser-enforced.
prompt_injection: Verify scripts only access resources from their same origin. Check CORS configuration, cross-origin requests. Enforcement: browser same-origin policy.

title: CSP: only allowed script sources execute

verdict_evidence: Lens 3, Web, CSP row: 'Only allowed script sources execute', enforced by Content-Security-Policy header.
prompt_injection: Verify only allowed script sources execute per the CSP header. Check for inline scripts, eval usage, untrusted sources. Enforcement: Content-Security-Policy header.

title: Safety: bad thing never happens

verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Safety row: 'Bad thing never happens', example: □(state ≠ Corrupted).
prompt_injection: Verify that bad states are never reachable. Model check: prove □(state ≠ Corrupted) holds in all reachable states. Use TLA+/PlusCal model checking.

tensor_equation: ◇(request → response) — every request eventually gets a response
verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Liveness row: 'Good thing eventually happens', example: ◇(request → response).
prompt_injection: Verify that every request eventually receives a response. Model check: prove ◇(request → response) holds. Use TLA+/PlusCal model checking.

tensor_equation: ∀ action A: if A is enabled infinitely often, then A eventually executes
verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Fairness row: 'If enabled infinitely often, eventually executes', weak/strong fairness.
prompt_injection: Verify that actions enabled infinitely often eventually execute. Check for scheduling fairness, starvation prevention. Model with weak/strong fairness conditions.

title: Refinement: concrete impl implies abstract spec
tensor_equation: ∀ concrete implementation C: C ⇒ Spec (C refines abstract specification)
verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Refinement row: 'Concrete impl ≤ Abstract spec', formula: Impl ⇒ Spec.
prompt_injection: Verify the concrete implementation refines the abstract specification. Model check: prove that every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the spec. Use TLA+ refinement mapping.

title: Invariant: always-true property holds
tensor_equation: □(lock_count ≥ 0) — invariant holds in all reachable states
verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Invariant row: 'Always true', example: □(lock_count ≥ 0).
prompt_injection: Verify that invariants hold in all reachable states. Model check: prove the invariant is never violated. Use TLA+/PlusCal invariant checking.

title: Temporal: eventually always leader elected
tensor_equation: ◇□(leader_elected) — eventually always a leader is elected
verdict_evidence: Lens 4, Temporal row: 'Eventually always', example: ◇□(leader_elected).
prompt_injection: Verify that eventually and always a leader is elected. Model check: prove ◇□(leader_elected) holds. Use TLA+ temporal property checking.

title: Schema: column type and nullability enforced
tensor_equation: ∀ column C in dataset D: C has declared type T and nullability constraint N
verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Schema row: 'Column X is type Y, not null', detected by Great Expectations and schema validation.
prompt_injection: Verify every column conforms to its declared type and nullability constraint. Check schema validation at ingestion boundaries. Detection: Great Expectations, schema validation.

verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Distribution row: 'Feature Z ∈ [0, 1] with μ=0.5', detected by drift detection and KS test.
prompt_injection: Verify feature distributions stay within expected ranges. Check for data drift, distribution shift. Detection: drift detection, KS test.

verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Completeness row: 'No missing values in required columns', detected by null check and imputation.
prompt_injection: Verify required columns have no missing values. Check null counts, completeness ratios. Detection: null check, imputation monitoring.

verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Lineage row: 'This data came from that source at time T', tracked via data catalog and metadata.
prompt_injection: Verify every data record has traceable lineage to its source. Check metadata, provenance tracking, data catalog entries. Detection: data catalog, metadata audit.

title: Freshness: data age within SLA bounds
tensor_equation: ∀ data d in dataset D: age(d) ≤ N hours
verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Freshness row: 'Data is ≤ N hours old', detected by timestamp check and SLA monitor.
prompt_injection: Verify data freshness does not exceed SLA bounds. Check timestamps, pipeline latency. Detection: timestamp check, SLA monitoring.

title: Privacy: PII is masked or absent
tensor_equation: ∀ PII data in dataset D: PII is masked or absent
verdict_evidence: Lens 5, Privacy row: 'PII is masked or absent', enforced by differential privacy and k-anonymity.
prompt_injection: Verify PII is masked or absent from datasets. Check for unmasked identifiers, re-identification risk. Enforcement: differential privacy, k-anonymity.

title: Network-to-process: input is adversarial
tensor_equation: ∀ input I from network boundary: I is validated before processing
verdict_evidence: Lens 6, Network → Process row: 'Input is adversarial', enforced by input validation and fuzzing.
prompt_injection: Verify all network input is treated as adversarial and validated before processing. Check input validation, sanitization, fuzzing coverage. Enforcement: input validation, fuzzing.

title: Process-to-filesystem: path is contained
tensor_equation: ∀ filesystem operation from process P: path is within declared container
verdict_evidence: Lens 6, Process → File System row: 'Path is contained', enforced by sandbox, chroot, seccomp.
prompt_injection: Verify all filesystem paths are contained within allowed boundaries. Check for path traversal, symlink attacks. Enforcement: sandbox, chroot, seccomp.

title: User-to-kernel: syscall args are validated
tensor_equation: ∀ syscall from user space: kernel validates all arguments before acting
verdict_evidence: Lens 6, User → Kernel row: 'Syscall args are validated', enforced by kernel validation and CAP_SYS_* capabilities.
prompt_injection: Verify syscall arguments are validated by the kernel before acting. Check for unchecked pointer dereferences, buffer overflows. Enforcement: kernel validation, capability checks.

title: Tenant isolation: no cross-tenant data access
tensor_equation: ∀ tenants A, B where A ≠ B: A cannot access B's data
verdict_evidence: Lens 6, Tenant A → Tenant B row: 'No cross-tenant data access', enforced by namespace isolation and authz.
prompt_injection: Verify no cross-tenant data access is possible. Check namespace isolation, authorization checks, data partitioning. Enforcement: namespace isolation, authorization.

title: Admin access: auth required for privileged ops
tensor_equation: ∀ privileged operation O: authN + authZ + audit log required
verdict_evidence: Lens 6, Public → Admin row: 'Auth required for privileged ops', enforced by AuthN + AuthZ + audit log.
prompt_injection: Verify privileged operations require authentication, authorization, and audit logging. Check for unprotected admin endpoints, missing audit trails. Enforcement: AuthN + AuthZ + audit log.

title: Memory resource: bounded allocation and cleanup
tensor_equation: ∀ allocation: bytes allocated ≤ N and freed when done
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, Memory row: '≤ N bytes allocated, freed when done', detected by allocations instrument and heap profiles.
prompt_injection: Verify memory allocation is bounded and freed when done. Check for leaks, unbounded growth. Detection: allocation instrumentation, heap profiles.

title: CPU resource: operation completes within time bound
tensor_equation: ∀ operation O: duration(O) ≤ T ms
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, CPU row: 'Operation completes in ≤ T ms', detected by timeout and p99 latency tracking.
prompt_injection: Verify operations complete within their time budget. Check for timeouts, p99 latency. Detection: timeout mechanism, p99 latency tracking.

title: File descriptors: open then close, max concurrent N
tensor_equation: ∀ fd: open(fd) → eventually close(fd), and |open_fds| ≤ N at any time
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, File descriptors row: 'Open → close, max N concurrent', detected by lsof and FD leak detection.
prompt_injection: Verify file descriptors are opened and closed, with a maximum of N concurrent. Check for FD leaks. Detection: lsof, FD leak detection.

title: Connections: pooled with bounded size and lifecycle
tensor_equation: ∀ connection: connect → use → close, and pool_size ≤ N
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, Connections row: 'Connect → use → close, pool size ≤ N', detected by connection pool metrics.
prompt_injection: Verify connections follow the connect-use-close lifecycle with bounded pool size. Check for connection leaks, pool exhaustion. Detection: connection pool metrics.

title: Goroutines: start then exit, no leaks
tensor_equation: ∀ goroutine G: start(G) → eventually exit(G), and no goroutine leaks
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, Goroutines row: 'Start → exit, no leaks', detected by runtime.NumGoroutine before/after.
prompt_injection: Verify goroutines start and exit without leaking. Check goroutine count before and after operations. Detection: runtime.NumGoroutine before/after.

title: Disk resource: bounded write and fsync on durable
tensor_equation: ∀ write: bytes ≤ N, and fsync called when data must be durable
verdict_evidence: Lens 7, Disk row: 'Write ≤ N bytes, fsync when durable', detected by disk usage monitoring.
prompt_injection: Verify disk writes are bounded and fsync is called when durability is required. Check for unbounded log growth, missing fsync after critical writes. Detection: disk usage monitoring.

title: Symbol Resolution with Declaration Before Use
tensor_equation: ∀ identifier use at program point p in scope S: ∃! declaration D such that D is the innermost declaration of that identifier visible at p ∧ D is lexically before p (or in a mutually recursive group with p)
prompt_injection: You are a symbol resolution verifier. Walk every identifier use in the AST. Each use must resolve to exactly one declaration. The resolution follows lexical scoping rules: inner scopes shadow outer scopes. Verify that the symbol table correctly implements this with a stack of scopes (enter/exit) and a hash map per scope. After type-checking, there must be zero 'unresolved identifier' errors.
verdict_evidence: INV-SEM-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 6.2 (symbol tables), Appel Chapter 5, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 4.

title: Type Unification by Structural Induction
tensor_equation: ∀ types T₁, T₂: compatible(T₁, T₂) ⇔ T₁ = T₂ ∨ T₁ = [N]T₁' ∧ T₂ = [M]T₂' ∧ compatible(T₁', T₂') ∨ T₁ = record{fᵢ: Sᵢ} ∧ T₂ = record{gⱼ: Tⱼ} ∧ ∀fᵢ: ∃!gⱼ with same name ∧ compatible(Sᵢ, Tⱼ) ∨ T₁ = T₁' → S₁ ∧ T₂ = T₂' → S₂ ∧ compatible(T₁', T₂') ∧ compatible(S₁, S₂) ∨ T₁ <: T₂ ∨ T₂ <: T₁
prompt_injection: You are a type-unification verifier. Verify the type checker's unification algorithm. For recursive types, the unification must terminate (occurs check). For nominal type systems, name equality suffices. For structural type systems, check the full recursive structure. Unification failure must produce an error message naming both types and the location.
verdict_evidence: INV-SEM-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Appel Chapter 5, Dragon Book Chapter 6.3, and Pierce TAPL (2002) for subtyping.

title: Bottom-Up Expression Type Inference
tensor_equation: ∀ operator op with signature f: (T₁, ..., Tₙ) → T_result: ∀ operands e₁:t₁, ..., eₙ:tₙ: if compatible(tᵢ, Tᵢ) for all i: type(op(e₁, ..., eₙ)) = T_result else: type error
prompt_injection: You are an expression-type checker. Walk the AST bottom-up. For every expression node, verify the inferred type against the operator's signature. If an operand type is incompatible with the operator's expected operand type, reject with a precise error. If coercion rules exist, verify they are applied correctly and deterministically.
verdict_evidence: INV-SEM-004 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapters 6.3-6.4 (type expressions, type checking of expressions) and Appel Chapter 5.

title: AST-to-IR Semantics Preservation
tensor_equation: ∀ program P, ∀ input i: eval_AST(P, i) = eval_IR(translate(P), i)
prompt_injection: You are an IR correctness verifier. For a set of test programs, evaluate the AST interpreter and the IR interpreter on the same inputs. They must produce identical results. Verify that the IR linearization of expressions respects operator precedence and evaluation order. Temporary names must be unique (SSA property or virtual register property). Every high-level construct must have a deterministic IR translation.
verdict_evidence: INV-IR-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 6.4-6.6 (three-address code), Appel Chapter 7 (translation to IR trees), and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 5.

title: Basic Block Partition of CFG
tensor_equation: ∀ CFG (V, E, entry, exit): V is partitioned into basic blocks B₁, ..., Bₖ such that ∀ block B = [i₁, i₂, ..., iₙ]: (1) Only iₙ may be a branch/jump; (2) Only i₁ may be a branch target; (3) No other instruction in B is a branch or branch target; (4) ∀ edge (Bᵢ, Bⱼ) ∈ E: the last instruction of Bᵢ can transfer to the first instruction of Bⱼ
prompt_injection: You are a basic block invariant checker. Partition every IR sequence into maximal straight-line code segments. Verify: no internal labels (all labels are at block start), no internal jumps (all branches are at block end), no fall-through ambiguity (each block has at most one fall-through successor). The CFG edge condition must hold: for every edge, the source's last instruction can branch to the target's first instruction.
verdict_evidence: INV-IR-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 8.4 (basic blocks and flow graphs), Appel Chapter 8, and Muchnick Chapter 7.

title: Dominator Tree Correctness
tensor_equation: ∀ CFG with entry node r: DOM(n) = {m | every path from r to n includes m}; ∀ n ≠ r: r ∈ DOM(n); ∀ n: n ∈ DOM(n); ∀ n ≠ r: |IDOM(n)| = 1; The dominator relation forms a rooted tree with r as root
prompt_injection: You are a dominator invariant verifier. Compute DOM sets from the definition (intersection of all-path dominators) and from the iterative dataflow algorithm. They must agree. Every non-entry node must have exactly one immediate dominator. The IDOM edges must form a tree (no cycles, root at entry). Verify that the dominance frontier is computed correctly.
verdict_evidence: INV-IR-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.2 (dominance), Muchnick Chapter 7, and Dragon Book Chapter 8.4.

title: SSA Single Definition with Dominating Reach
tensor_equation: ∀ SSA-form program: ∀ variable v, ∀ use of v at instruction i: ∃! definition d of v such that d dominates i
prompt_injection: You are an SSA invariant verifier. Walk every variable use. Verify it has exactly one reaching definition. That definition must dominate the use in the CFG. For φ-function operands, verify that the i-th operand corresponds to the i-th CFG predecessor. No variable is defined more than once in the source (SSA names are distinct for distinct definitions).
verdict_evidence: INV-SSA-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Cytron, Ferrante, Rosen, Wegman, Zadeck (ACM TOPLAS 1991), Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.3, and Muchnick Chapter 7.

title: Dominance Frontier φ-Function Placement
tensor_equation: ∀ variable v with definitions at nodes D = {d₁, ..., dₖ}: A φ-function for v is placed at node n ⇔ n ∈ DF⁺(D) where DF⁺(D) = the fixed point of: DF₁ = DF(D), DF_{i+1} = DF(D ∪ DF_i) ∪ DF_i
prompt_injection: You are a φ-function placement verifier. Given the set of definition blocks for each variable, compute DF⁺. Verify that φ-functions are placed exactly at those nodes. Verify that no φ-function is unnecessary (every φ has at least two distinct reaching definitions along different incoming paths). Verify that no merge of definitions is missing a φ.
verdict_evidence: INV-SSA-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Cytron et al. (1991), Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.3, and Muchnick Chapter 7.4.

title: SSA Interference Graph is Chordal
tensor_equation: ∀ SSA-form program: The interference graph IG = (V, E) where V = {virtual registers / SSA names}, E = {(u, v) | live ranges of u and v overlap} is chordal: every cycle of length ≥ 4 has a chord
prompt_injection: You are a chordal graph invariant checker. Build the interference graph from the SSA program. Verify it is chordal: for every cycle of length 4 or more, there must be an edge connecting two non-adjacent vertices on the cycle. If the graph is not chordal, the program is not in strict SSA form (or live ranges were computed incorrectly).
verdict_evidence: INV-SSA-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Hack, Grund, Goos (CC 2006), Pereira & Palsberg (APLAS 2005), and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 13.

title: Monotone Dataflow Framework Convergence
tensor_equation: ∀ dataflow analysis (L, ⊓, F) where L is a finite-height semilattice with top ⊤ and bottom ⊥, and F = {f₁, ..., fₙ} are monotone transfer functions (x ⊑ y ⇒ f(x) ⊑ f(y)): the iterative algorithm IN[n] = ⊓_{p ∈ preds(n)} OUT[p], OUT[n] = fₙ(IN[n]) converges to the MFP (maximal fixed point) solution in O(|V| × |L|) iterations
prompt_injection: You are a dataflow framework verifier. For a given analysis, verify that (1) the lattice is finite-height (no infinite ascending chains), (2) transfer functions are monotone, (3) the iterative solver terminates when a fixed point is reached. Compare the computed solution against the MOP (meet over all paths) solution for small programs: MFP ⊑ MOP always holds. If transfer functions are distributive, MFP = MOP.
verdict_evidence: INV-DF-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 9.3 (iterative dataflow analysis), Muchnick Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.

title: Reaching Definitions Correctness
tensor_equation: ∀ definition d of variable v: d ∈ RD_in(p) ⇔ ∃ path from d to p along which v is not redefined; d ∉ RD_in(p) ⇔ ∀ paths from d to p: v is redefined before reaching p
prompt_injection: You are a reaching-definitions verifier. Compute RD from the definition (all-paths enumeration for small programs) and from the iterative dataflow solver. Verify they agree. For every use of v at point p, the reaching definitions determine which definition(s) could provide v's value. If exactly one definition reaches, that use has a unique definition (use-def chain). If multiple reach, there is ambiguity.
verdict_evidence: INV-DF-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 9.2, Muchnick Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.

title: Liveness as the Dual of Reaching Definitions
tensor_equation: ∀ variable v, ∀ program point p: v is live-out at p ⇔ ∃ use of v at q reachable from p along a v-clear path; v is dead at p ⇔ no use of v is reachable from p (or every path redefines v before any use)
prompt_injection: You are a liveness verifier. Compute live-in/live-out from the definition (backward path enumeration) and from the dataflow solver. Verify they agree. A variable is live at a use point (use → gen). A variable is dead after its last use. The live range of v is the set of program points between v's definition and its last use along any path.
verdict_evidence: INV-DF-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 9.2, Appel Chapter 10, and Muchnick Chapter 11.

title: Optimization Semantics Preservation (Prime Directive)
tensor_equation: ∀ optimization pass P, ∀ program π, ∀ input i: ⟦P(π)⟧(i) = ⟦π⟧(i)
prompt_injection: You are an optimization correctness verifier. For a given optimization pass, construct a proof that it preserves semantics. The proof must be staged: (1) local correctness — for a single basic block, (2) regional correctness — extended basic blocks, (3) global correctness — whole CFG with loops. Test on a corpus of programs: run before and after optimization on the same inputs, verify identical outputs. Fuzz the optimizer: random IR, apply optimization, verify round-trip equivalence.
verdict_evidence: INV-OPT-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 10, Dragon Book Chapter 9.1, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10.

title: Constant Propagation Soundness
tensor_equation: ∀ variable v at program point p: if constant-prop(v, p) = c (constant value c): then on every execution path reaching p, v has value c; if constant-prop(v, p) = ⊤ (unknown/not-constant): then no claim is made; if constant-prop(v, p) = ⊥ (unreachable): then p is unreachable
prompt_injection: You are a constant-propagation verifier. For every claim that v = c at point p, verify by exhaustive path enumeration (for small programs) that every definition of v reaching p has value c. If any reaching definition has a different value or is unknown, v must not be claimed constant. Assert that replacing a variable use with a claimed-constant value does not change program output.
verdict_evidence: INV-OPT-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 12, Dragon Book Chapter 9.4, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10.

title: Dead Code Elimination Soundness
tensor_equation: ∀ instruction i removed by DCE: The value computed by i is dead (no live use of i's destination exists) ∧ i has no side effects that are observable by the program
prompt_injection: You are a DCE verifier. Verify every eliminated instruction: (1) compute liveness; (2) the destination variable is dead after this instruction; (3) the instruction has no side effects (no stores, no calls, no volatile operations). If any of these fails, the elimination is unsound. Run the program before and after DCE with identical inputs; verify identical output, side effects, and IO.
verdict_evidence: INV-OPT-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10, Muchnick Chapter 10, and Dragon Book Chapter 9.4.

title: Available Expressions Correctness
tensor_equation: ∀ expression e (e.g., x + y) at program point p: e ∈ AE_in(p) ⇔ along EVERY path from entry to p: (1) e was computed at some point q before p on that path, (2) neither x nor y was redefined between q and p on that path
prompt_injection: You are an available-expressions verifier. For every expression claimed available, verify on every path from entry that it was computed and its operands are unchanged. Never claim an expression is available on a path where an operand was overwritten. The available set shrinks when an operand is redefined (kill) and grows when the expression is computed (gen). Verify: AE_in(p) = ∩_{q∈preds(p)} (gen[q] ∪ (AE_in(q) − kill[q])).
verdict_evidence: INV-OPT-004 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 9.2, Muchnick Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10.

title: Loop-Invariant Code Motion Correctness
tensor_equation: ∀ expression e computed inside loop L: e can be hoisted to L's preheader ⇔ (1) e is loop-invariant: all operands of e are constant or defined outside L, (2) e dominates all loop exits where e's value is live, (3) e is in a block that dominates all exits (or guarantee execution)
prompt_injection: You are a LICM verifier.
