For every hoisted expression, verify: (1) operands are defined outside the loop or are constants; (2) the expression was originally executed on every iteration (dominates all exits) OR the loop is guaranteed to execute at least once (preheader dominates the first back-edge); (3) no side effect in the loop between the original position and the hoisted position could affect the result. Run before/after: identical outputs for all inputs.
verdict_evidence: INV-OPT-005 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 14, Dragon Book Chapter 10, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10.

title: Interference Graph Correctness for Register Allocation
tensor_equation: ∀ register allocation with k available registers (R0 ... R{k-1}): ∀ edge (u, v) in interference graph IG: live ranges of virtual registers u and v overlap at some program point ⇒ u and v cannot be assigned the same physical register; ∀ valid coloring col: V → {0, ..., k-1}: (u, v) ∈ IG ⇒ col(u) ≠ col(v)
prompt_injection: You are a register allocation verifier. Build the interference graph from liveness information. Verify: if two variables are simultaneously live at any program point, they must have an edge in IG and must be assigned different registers. After allocation, no physical register holds two live values simultaneously. For spilled variables, verify that every use is preceded by a reload and every definition is followed by a store.
verdict_evidence: INV-REG-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Chaitin (PLDI 1982), Chaitin et al. (1981), Muchnick Chapter 8, Appel Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 13.

title: Spill Correctness with Inserted Loads and Stores
tensor_equation: ∀ spilled virtual register v with spill slot at [fp + offset]: ∀ use of v in original IR: in modified IR, load v_tmp ← [fp + offset]; use v_tmp; ∀ definition of v in original IR: in modified IR, compute result into v_tmp; store [fp + offset] ← v_tmp; After spill insertion, the program produces identical results to before
prompt_injection: You are a spill-correctness verifier. Walk the spilled program. Every original virtual register use must have a corresponding load from the correct spill slot. Every definition must have a corresponding store. No load/store is inserted for a non-spilled register. The spill slot must be large enough for the variable's type. Run the program before and after spilling with identical inputs; verify identical outputs.
verdict_evidence: INV-REG-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 8, Appel Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 13.

title: Coalescing Correctness for Copy Elimination
tensor_equation: ∀ copy instruction u ← v (or u = v): if u and v do not interfere (no edge in IG): u and v can be coalesced (assigned the same physical register); the copy instruction can be eliminated; After coalescing, the program produces identical results
prompt_injection: You are a coalescing verifier. For every eliminated copy u ← v, verify that u and v did not interfere before coalescing. After coalescing, verify that all uses of u and v now refer to the same physical register. The copy instruction must be removed. The program must produce identical results. For Briggs coalescing, verify the coalesced node's degree is less than k before accepting the merge.
verdict_evidence: INV-REG-003 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Chaitin et al. (1981), Appel Chapter 11, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 13.2.

title: SSA-Based Register Allocation Optimality
tensor_equation: ∀ SSA-form program with interference graph IG: IG is chordal (every cycle of length ≥ 4 has a chord) ⇒ color(IG) can be found optimally in O(|V| + |E|) using perfect elimination order ⇒ If IG is k-colorable, the optimal coloring uses exactly k registers with no unnecessary spilling
prompt_injection: You are an SSA-register-allocation optimality verifier. Verify the interference graph is chordal. Verify the perfect elimination order succeeds. Verify the coloring uses ω(IG) colors (the maximum clique size, which equals the chromatic number for chordal graphs). Compare the SSA allocation result against a brute-force optimal allocation for small programs. Spill decisions must be minimal: no spill inserted unless ω(IG) > k.
verdict_evidence: INV-REG-004 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Hack, Grund, Goos (CC 2006), Pereira & Palsberg (APLAS 2005), and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 13.4.

title: Tree-Pattern Coverage Completeness in Instruction Selection
tensor_equation: ∀ IR expression tree T: instruction_selection(T) produces a sequence of machine instructions I₁...Iₙ such that: (1) Every IR node in T is covered by exactly one instruction pattern; (2) The instruction sequence computes the same value as T; (3) The pattern tiles are valid: each tile corresponds to a single machine instruction
prompt_injection: You are an instruction selection verifier. Walk the tiling of the IR tree. Every IR node must be covered by exactly one pattern. Every pattern must correspond to a valid machine instruction in the architecture description. The resulting instruction sequence, when executed, must compute the same result as the IR. For optimal tiling (dynamic programming), verify the cost of the chosen tiling is minimal among all valid tilings.
verdict_evidence: INV-ISEL-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Dragon Book Chapter 8.6 (tree-pattern matching), Appel Chapter 9 (instruction selection), and Muchnick Chapter 7 (code selection).

title: Instruction Scheduling Dependence Preservation
tensor_equation: ∀ original IR block B = [i₁, i₂, ..., iₙ]: Scheduled block B' = [i'₁, i'₂, ..., i'ₙ] is a permutation of B such that: ∀ i, j: if i must execute before j (read-after-write, write-after-write, write-after-read): position(i) < position(j) in B' (dependence order preserved)
prompt_injection: You are an instruction scheduling verifier. Build the dependence DAG from the original instruction sequence. Verify that the scheduled sequence is a topological sort of the DAG. Every RAW (true), WAW (output), and WAR (anti) dependence must be preserved in the schedule. Run before and after scheduling; verify identical results for all inputs.
verdict_evidence: INV-ISEL-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 9, Cooper & Torczon Chapter 12, and Dragon Book Chapter 10.

title: Composition of Semantics-Preserving Passes
tensor_equation: ∀ sequence of correct passes P₁, P₂, ..., Pₖ (each preserving semantics individually): ⟦Pₖ(⋯P₂(P₁(π))⋯)⟧ = ⟦π⟧
prompt_injection: You are a pipeline composition verifier. For the full compilation pipeline, run the source program and the final compiled program on identical inputs. They must produce identical outputs. Insert checkpoints between passes: if a pass introduces a bug, bisect the pipeline to find it. The composition invariant holds iff every individual pass invariant holds.
verdict_evidence: INV-CROSS-001 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 10, Dragon Book Chapter 9.1, and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 10.

title: CFG Reducibility and Natural Loop Structure
tensor_equation: ∀ CFG G: G is reducible ⇔ G can be reduced to a single node by repeatedly applying T₁ and T₂ transformations where T₁ removes a self-loop and T₂ removes a node with a single predecessor (folding it in); If G is reducible: all loops are natural loops (single entry, dominated by header)
prompt_injection: You are a CFG reducibility verifier. Apply T₁/T₂ reductions to the CFG. If the graph reduces to a single node, it is reducible. Every back edge must go to a node that dominates its tail (natural loop). If an irreducible CFG is detected, the optimization passes that assume reducibility must conservatively refuse to optimize those regions.
verdict_evidence: INV-CROSS-002 in source-cache/compiler-invariants-oracle.md, derived from Muchnick Chapter 7 and Cooper & Torczon Chapter 9.

title: HTTP/2 Stream ID Parity Assignment
tensor_equation: ∀stream s: (s.initiated_by_client ⇒ s.id MOD 2 = 1) ∧ (s.initiated_by_server ⇒ s.id MOD 2 = 0)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; client-initiated streams must have odd IDs and server-initiated streams must have even IDs.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/2 stream ID respects parity assignment: client-initiated streams carry odd-numbered IDs and server-initiated (push) streams carry even-numbered IDs. A violation means the stream origin is ambiguous and the peer cannot correctly interpret stream ownership, causing PROTOCOL_ERROR.

title: HTTP/2 Monotonic Stream ID Ordering
tensor_equation: ∀endpoint E, ∀streams S = {s_1, s_2, ..., s_n} opened by E in order: s_1.id < s_2.id < ... < s_n.id (stream identifiers MUST be numerically greater than all previously opened or reserved streams)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; stream IDs opened by each endpoint must monotonically increase.
prompt_injection: Verify that stream IDs opened by each endpoint are strictly monotonically increasing — a newly opened stream must have a higher numeric ID than every stream that endpoint previously opened or reserved. Opening stream 5 after stream 3 then attempting stream 3 is forbidden and causes PROTOCOL_ERROR.

title: HTTP/2 No Stream ID Reuse
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: stream_id is used at most once across the entire lifetime of C
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; stream IDs must never be reused within a single connection lifetime.
prompt_injection: Verify that no stream ID is ever reused within the lifetime of a single HTTP/2 connection. Once a stream ID is consumed (opened or reserved), it cannot be recycled. Exhausting the 31-bit stream ID space requires opening a new connection or issuing GOAWAY.

title: HTTP/2 Stream ID Skip Implicit Close
tensor_equation: ∀stream s opened by endpoint E with numeric gap: (∀id' where s.id < id' < next_used_id and id' could have been opened by peer): state(id') = CLOSED
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; skipped stream IDs are implicitly closed and receiving frames on them is an error.
prompt_injection: Verify that when an endpoint opens a stream with an ID that skips intermediate IDs allocable to that endpoint, all skipped IDs are treated as implicitly closed. Receiving a HEADERS frame on a previously-skipped stream ID after a higher one was used constitutes a protocol violation.

title: HTTP/2 Stream State Machine Transitions
tensor_equation: ∀stream s: valid_transitions(s) ⊆ {IDLE → {OPEN, RESERVED_LOCAL, RESERVED_REMOTE, CLOSED}, OPEN → {HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE, CLOSED}, HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL → {HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE, CLOSED}, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE → {CLOSED}, RESERVED_{LOCAL,REMOTE} → {HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE, CLOSED}}
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1 Stream State Machine in oracle/http2-quic.md; every stream transition must follow the defined state machine.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/2 stream obeys the RFC 9113 stream state machine. Sending a HEADERS frame on a stream in HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE state, for instance, triggers STREAM_CLOSED error. No transition outside the defined set is valid.

title: HTTP/2 Frame Type Permissions Per State
tensor_equation: ∀frame f received on stream s in state st: allowed_frame_type(f, st) = {DATA → {OPEN, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE}, HEADERS → {IDLE, OPEN, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE}, PRIORITY → any state including CLOSED, RST_STREAM → {OPEN, HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE}, ...}
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; each frame type is only valid in specific stream states.
prompt_injection: Verify that every received HTTP/2 frame is permitted in the stream's current state. Receiving a DATA frame after the peer has sent END_STREAM (state: HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL) triggers STREAM_CLOSED. Only PRIORITY frames are valid in any state including CLOSED.

title: HTTP/2 Stream Concurrency Limit
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: |active_streams(C)| ≤ SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS where active_streams = streams in {OPEN, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE, HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL}
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.1.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; the peer must not open more concurrent streams than the advertised SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS limit.
prompt_injection: Verify that an endpoint never has more active streams (OPEN, HALF_CLOSED_REMOTE, or HALF_CLOSED_LOCAL) than the peer's advertised SETTINGS_MAX_CONCURRENT_STREAMS value. Exceeding this limit is a protocol error.

title: HTTP/2 RST_STREAM Loop Prevention
tensor_equation: ∀endpoint E: E MUST NOT send RST_STREAM in response to an RST_STREAM
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; endpoints must not echo RST_STREAM frames to prevent infinite reset loops.
prompt_injection: Verify that no endpoint sends an RST_STREAM frame in response to receiving an RST_STREAM. Echoing RST_STREAM frames creates a reset loop where both endpoints continuously reset the same stream, consuming resources without making progress.

title: HTTP/2 Window-Based Credit Semantics
tensor_equation: ∀connection C, ∀stream s in C: bytes_sent(s) ≤ window(s) + Σ(WINDOW_UPDATE increments for s) ∧ bytes_sent_total(C) ≤ connection_window + Σ(WINDOW_UPDATE for C)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; flow control is credit-based and data sent must not exceed the window plus accumulated WINDOW_UPDATE increments.
prompt_injection: Verify that the total DATA bytes sent on a stream never exceeds the initial window plus the sum of all WINDOW_UPDATE increments for that stream, and that total connection-level DATA bytes never exceeds the connection window plus accumulated connection-level WINDOW_UPDATE increments. Violating the flow-control credit budget is a STREAM_FLOW_CONTROL_ERROR or CONNECTION_FLOW_CONTROL_ERROR.

title: HTTP/2 Only DATA Frames Are Flow-Controlled
tensor_equation: ∀non-DATA frame f: f is not subject to flow control
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; only DATA frame payloads consume flow-control credit.
prompt_injection: Verify that flow-control windows only gate DATA frames. SETTINGS, HEADERS, PRIORITY, RST_STREAM, PUSH_PROMISE, PING, GOAWAY, WINDOW_UPDATE, and CONTINUATION frames must never be blocked by a closed flow-control window. Blocking non-DATA frames on flow control would deadlock the connection.

title: HTTP/2 Two-Level Flow Control
tensor_equation: ∀DATA frame f: bytes_consumed(f) ≤ min(stream_window(s(f)), connection_window(C))
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; DATA frames are constrained by both stream-level and connection-level flow-control windows.
prompt_injection: Verify that every DATA frame consumes credit from both the per-stream and connection-level flow-control windows, and the bytes consumed cannot exceed the minimum of the two. A stream with adequate stream-level window but exhausted connection-level window must not send data.

title: HTTP/2 Initial Window Invariant
tensor_equation: ∀new stream s, ∀new connection C: initial_window(s) = initial_window(C) = 65535 octets
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9113 Section 5.2.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; the default initial flow-control window for streams and connections is 65535 octets.
prompt_injection: Verify that every new stream and every new connection starts with an initial flow-control window of exactly 65535 octets (unless SETTINGS_INITIAL_WINDOW_SIZE alters it before stream creation). A stream starting with a smaller window would violate the default contract and could cause spurious flow-control blocking.

title: QUIC Strictly Increasing Packet Numbers
tensor_equation: ∀connection C, ∀packet_number_space pns ∈ {Initial, Handshake, ApplicationData}: pkts(pns) sorted by packet_number ascending ∧ ∀i < j: pkt_i.pn < pkt_j.pn ∧ packet_numbers are NEVER reused in the same space within a connection
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 12.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; packet numbers in each number space must be strictly increasing and never reused.
prompt_injection: Verify that within each QUIC packet number space (Initial, Handshake, ApplicationData), packet numbers strictly increase and are never reused. Retransmitting the same data with the same packet number would create ACK ambiguity — QUIC separates transmission from delivery sequence numbers precisely to avoid this.

title: QUIC Packet Number Space Isolation
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: packet_numbers in Initial space, Handshake space, and ApplicationData space are independent ∧ retransmission boundaries do NOT cross spaces
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 12.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; each packet number space has independent numbering and loss detection.
prompt_injection: Verify that the three QUIC packet number spaces (Initial, Handshake, ApplicationData) maintain fully independent packet number sequences and that ACKs, loss detection, and retransmissions never cross space boundaries. An ACK for the Initial space must never be applied to ApplicationData space packets — the keys differ, and cross-space accounting would corrupt loss recovery.

title: QUIC Packet Number Exhaustion
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: max_packet_number ≥ 2^62 - 1 ⇒ sender MUST close connection without CONNECTION_CLOSE
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 12.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; when the 62-bit packet number space is exhausted, the connection must be terminated without a CONNECTION_CLOSE frame.
prompt_injection: Verify that when a QUIC sender's packet number reaches or exceeds 2^62 - 1, the connection is immediately closed without attempting to send a CONNECTION_CLOSE frame. Wrapping around to packet number 0 would break the strict monotonicity invariant that loss detection depends on.

title: QUIC Packet Number Encoding
tensor_equation: ∀QUIC packet p: p.packet_number ∈ [0, 2^62 - 1) ∧ encoded_length = 1, 2, or 4 bytes (truncated, with largest encoded bit indicating length)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 12.3 and Section 17.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; packet numbers are in [0, 2^62-1) and encoded in 1, 2, or 4 bytes of truncated form.
prompt_injection: Verify that every QUIC packet number falls in the range [0, 2^62-1) and is encoded in exactly 1, 2, or 4 bytes using the truncated encoding scheme where the two high bits of the first byte indicate the encoded length. A packet number requiring more than 4 bytes or encoded with an unsupported truncated length is malformed.

title: QUIC Long Header Form
tensor_equation: ∀QUIC packet with long header: first_byte MSB = 1 ∧ version_field = 32-bit identifier (value 0x00000000 reserved for Version Negotiation)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 8999 Section 4 in oracle/http2-quic.md; long header packets have the MSB set and carry a 32-bit version field, with version 0 reserved for Version Negotiation.
prompt_injection: Verify that every QUIC long-header packet has the most significant bit of the first byte set to 1 and carries a 32-bit version identifier. Version 0x00000000 is reserved exclusively for Version Negotiation packets. A non-VN packet with version 0 is invalid.

title: QUIC Short Header Form
tensor_equation: ∀QUIC packet with short header: first_byte MSB = 0 ∧ destination_connection_id immediately follows first byte (length NOT encoded in packet)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 8999 Section 4 in oracle/http2-quic.md; short header packets have the MSB clear and the Destination Connection ID follows immediately without an encoded length field.
prompt_injection: Verify that every QUIC short-header packet has the most significant bit of the first byte cleared to 0 and the Destination Connection ID immediately follows the first byte. The Connection ID length is not encoded in the short header — it must be known from the connection context. Ambiguity in CID length would cause parsing failures.

title: QUIC Version Negotiation Packet
tensor_equation: ∀Version Negotiation packet VNP: VNP.HeaderForm = 1 (long header) ∧ VNP.Version = 0x00000000 ∧ VNP contains list of Supported Version (32-bit each) ∧ VNP is NOT integrity protected ∧ VNP MUST copy received Source Connection ID into outgoing Destination Connection ID
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 8999 Section 6 in oracle/http2-quic.md; Version Negotiation packets use the long header form, version 0, carry a list of supported versions, lack integrity protection, and must swap Connection IDs.
prompt_injection: Verify that every Version Negotiation packet uses the long header form with version 0x00000000, contains a list of supported 32-bit version identifiers, is not integrity-protected, and copies the received Source Connection ID into the outgoing Destination Connection ID. A VN packet with integrity protection could be discarded by intermediaries that need to inspect it, and failing to swap CIDs breaks routing.

title: QUIC Connection ID Opaqueness
tensor_equation: ∀connection ID cid: cid is opaque (no version-specific semantics required for lower layers) ∧ length ∈ [0, 2040] bits (0-255 bytes) ∧ multiple CID values may be used for the same connection
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 8999 Section 5 in oracle/http2-quic.md; Connection IDs are opaque, 0-255 bytes in length, and multiple may be used for a single connection.
prompt_injection: Verify that Connection IDs are treated as opaque blobs with no version-specific semantics imposed by lower layers, have a length between 0 and 255 bytes (0-2040 bits), and that multiple distinct CIDs may be associated with the same connection. A load balancer parsing CID internals for routing could misroute traffic after a version upgrade that changes the CID structure.

title: QUIC Client-Initiated Migration Only (v1)
tensor_equation: ∀QUIC v1 connection: only client may initiate non-probing migration ∧ server does NOT send non-probing packets to a new client address until receiving a non-probing packet from that address
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 9 in oracle/http2-quic.md; in QUIC v1, only the client initiates non-probing connection migration.
prompt_injection: Verify that in QUIC v1 connections, only the client initiates non-probing connection migration and the server must not send non-probing packets to a new client address until it has first received a non-probing packet from that address. A server proactively migrating to a different client address would violate the protocol and could enable reflection attacks.

title: QUIC Path Validation Before Migration
tensor_equation: ∀new candidate path P: send PATH_CHALLENGE on P ∧ wait for valid PATH_RESPONSE with same data ⇒ P is validated → may migrate
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 8.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; path validation via PATH_CHALLENGE/PATH_RESPONSE is required before migrating to a new path.
prompt_injection: Verify that every QUIC connection migration to a new path is preceded by successful path validation: a PATH_CHALLENGE frame is sent on the candidate path, and a PATH_RESPONSE frame carrying the same data is received before any non-probing application data is sent on that path. Sending application data over an unvalidated path is vulnerable to off-path injection attacks.

title: QUIC Congestion State Reset on Path Change
tensor_equation: ∀connection migrating from old_path to new_path: packets_sent_on(old_path) NOT counted in new_path congestion control ∧ RTT_estimator reset to initial values for new_path (port-only changes MAY retain congestion/RTT state)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 9.4 in oracle/http2-quic.md; congestion control state is reset on path migration except for port-only changes.
prompt_injection: Verify that when a QUIC connection migrates to a new network path involving an address change, the congestion window and RTT estimator are reset to initial values for the new path, and packets sent on the old path are not counted against the new path's congestion budget. Retaining a congestion window calibrated for a high-latency cellular path when switching to low-latency WiFi could cause unnecessary throughput throttling.

title: QUIC Preferred Address Advertisement
tensor_equation: ∀server with preferred_address transport parameter: (server_ip, server_port) is used for initial connection ∧ client MAY migrate to preferred_address after handshake completes ∧ server MUST continue to accept packets at the original address after advertising preferred_address
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 9.6 in oracle/http2-quic.md; a server advertising a preferred address must continue accepting packets at the original address.
prompt_injection: Verify that when a QUIC server advertises a preferred_address transport parameter, it continues to accept packets at its original address even after the client migrates to the preferred address. Abandoning the original address would cause loss of in-flight packets that were already dispatched before the client received the preferred-address advertisement.

title: QUIC No ACK Renege
tensor_equation: ∀ACK frame ack: once a packet number is declared ACKed, it MUST NOT be "un-acked" (reneged) later
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 13.2 and RFC 9002 in oracle/http2-quic.md; QUIC forbids ACK reneging unlike TCP's SACK mechanism.
prompt_injection: Verify that once a QUIC ACK frame declares a packet number as acknowledged, that packet number is never subsequently un-acknowledged (reneged). QUIC deliberately excludes TCP-style SACK reneging where a previously ACKed range could be retracted — this simplifies loss recovery and avoids ambiguity about whether a packet needs retransmission.

title: QUIC Separate Loss Detection Per Space
tensor_equation: ∀packet_number_space pns: loss_detection(pns) is independent of other spaces ∧ ACKs for pns only trigger retransmission within pns
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 Section 12.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; loss detection operates independently per packet number space.
prompt_injection: Verify that QUIC loss detection runs independently within each packet number space (Initial, Handshake, ApplicationData) and that ACKs for one space never trigger retransmission in a different space. An Initial-space ACK confirming Handshake-space delivery would be impossible due to key availability differences between the spaces.

title: QUIC Congestion Window Initialization
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: initial_congestion_window = min(10 * max_datagram_size, max(14720, 2 * max_datagram_size)) ∧ slow_start exits on first packet loss ∧ RTO re-enters slow start
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9002 Section 7 in oracle/http2-quic.md; the initial congestion window is min(10*max_datagram_size, max(14720, 2*max_datagram_size)) and slow start exits on first loss.
prompt_injection: Verify that every QUIC connection initializes its congestion window to min(10 * max_datagram_size, max(14720, 2 * max_datagram_size)), exits slow-start on detecting the first packet loss, and re-enters slow-start upon retransmission timeout. Starting with a TCP-style congestion window of 1 MSS would severely limit QUIC's initial throughput.

title: QUIC Non-Ack-Eliciting Packet Neutrality
tensor_equation: ∀packet p containing only ACK or CONNECTION_CLOSE frames: p is not counted in congestion control limits (bytes_in_flight)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9000 verified errata EID 8240 in oracle/http2-quic.md; pure ACK and CONNECTION_CLOSE packets are not counted in bytes_in_flight for congestion control.
prompt_injection: Verify that QUIC packets containing only ACK frames or only CONNECTION_CLOSE frames are excluded from the bytes_in_flight congestion control counter. Counting pure ACK packets against the congestion window would artificially starve the connection of congestion budget since ACKs themselves do not elicit further ACKs.

title: HTTP/3 Single Request per Stream
tensor_equation: ∀client-initiated bidirectional stream s: client sends exactly one request on s ∧ multiple requests on s ⇒ malformed (H3_INTERNAL_ERROR or similar)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 4.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; each client-initiated bidirectional stream carries exactly one HTTP request.
prompt_injection: Verify that each HTTP/3 client-initiated bidirectional stream carries exactly one HTTP request. Pipelining multiple requests on a single HTTP/3 stream is not allowed — unlike HTTP/1.1 or HTTP/2 pipelining. Sending multiple requests on one stream results in a malformed request error.

title: HTTP/3 Single Control Stream Per Endpoint
tensor_equation: ∀endpoint E, ∀connection C: |control_streams(E, C)| = 1 ∧ control stream type = 0x00 ∧ first frame on control stream MUST be SETTINGS
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 6.2.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; each endpoint opens exactly one control stream, with SETTINGS as its first frame.
prompt_injection: Verify that each endpoint opens exactly one unidirectional control stream (type 0x00) per connection and that the very first frame on that control stream is a SETTINGS frame. Opening a second control stream or omitting SETTINGS as the first frame triggers H3_MISSING_SETTINGS or H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR.

title: HTTP/3 Control Stream Persistence
tensor_equation: ∀control stream cs: sender MUST NOT close cs ∧ receiver MUST NOT request sender to close cs ∧ closure of either endpoint's cs ⇒ connection error H3_CLOSED_CRITICAL_STREAM
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 6.2.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; the control stream must never be closed and closing it is a fatal connection error.
prompt_injection: Verify that neither endpoint ever closes its control stream and that neither endpoint requests the peer to close its control stream. Closure of either direction's control stream is a fatal H3_CLOSED_CRITICAL_STREAM connection error because SETTINGS, GOAWAY, and other critical frames can only be sent on the control stream.

title: HTTP/3 Push Stream Source Restriction
tensor_equation: ∀push stream ps: ps.initiator = server ∧ client → server push stream ⇒ H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 6.2.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; push streams may only be initiated by the server.
prompt_injection: Verify that server push streams are only ever initiated by the server. A client attempting to open a push stream toward the server triggers H3_STREAM_CREATION_ERROR — server push is a server-to-client mechanism and clients cannot push to servers.

title: HTTP/3 Push ID Uniqueness
tensor_equation: ∀push ID pid: pid appears at most once in a push stream header ∧ pid ≤ MAX_PUSH_ID value sent by client
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 4.6 in oracle/http2-quic.md; push IDs must be unique and bounded by the client's MAX_PUSH_ID.
prompt_injection: Verify that every push ID in a push stream header is unique (appearing at most once) and does not exceed the MAX_PUSH_ID limit that the client advertised. A server sending a push ID above the client's MAX_PUSH_ID limit indicates a protocol violation.

title: HTTP/3 Frame Type Permissions by Stream
tensor_equation: ∀frame type ft, ∀stream type st: allowed(ft, st) as per RFC 9114 Table 1: DATA → {request, push}, HEADERS → {request, push}, SETTINGS → {control} (first frame only), GOAWAY → {control}, MAX_PUSH_ID → {control} (client-only), CANCEL_PUSH → {control}, PUSH_PROMISE → {request} (server-only, never on control stream or push stream)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 7.2 Table 1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; each HTTP/3 frame type is only permitted on specific stream types.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/3 frame type is only sent on the stream types permitted by RFC 9114 Table 1. A PUSH_PROMISE frame received on a control stream, for instance, triggers H3_FRAME_UNEXPECTED. SETTINGS is only valid as the first frame on the control stream.

title: HTTP/3 Unidirectional Stream Minimum Requirements
tensor_equation: ∀connection C: transport params allow ≥ 3 unidirectional streams from peer ∧ at least 1024 bytes of flow-control credit per unidirectional stream SHOULD be provided
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 6.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; at least 3 unidirectional streams must be allowed and 1024 bytes of flow-control credit per stream is recommended.
prompt_injection: Verify that each endpoint's transport parameters allow at least 3 unidirectional streams from the peer and that at least 1024 bytes of flow-control credit per unidirectional stream is provided (SHOULD-level requirement). Advertising zero unidirectional stream flow-control credit would prevent the peer from opening its mandatory control stream, deadlocking the connection before it can begin.

title: HTTP/3 Unknown Stream Type Tolerance
tensor_equation: ∀unknown unidirectional stream type unknown_typ: recipient MUST abort reading OR discard data silently ∧ unknown stream types must NOT trigger connection error
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 6.2 in oracle/http2-quic.md; unknown unidirectional stream types must be tolerated and must not cause connection errors.
prompt_injection: Verify that when an HTTP/3 endpoint receives a unidirectional stream with an unknown stream type, it either aborts reading or silently discards the data, but never triggers a connection error. Disconnecting on unknown stream types would break forward compatibility with future HTTP/3 extensions.

title: HTTP/3 ALPN Selection
tensor_equation: ∀HTTP/3 over QUIC connection: ALPN token = "h3" selected in TLS handshake (HTTP/3 relies on QUIC v1 as the underlying transport)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; HTTP/3 over QUIC requires the "h3" ALPN token negotiated during the TLS handshake.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/3-over-QUIC connection negotiates the ALPN token "h3" during the TLS handshake. Negotiating HTTP/2 over QUIC is not a defined protocol — HTTP/2 is defined over TCP/TLS, and HTTP/3 is the QUIC mapping.

title: HTTP/3 0-RTT Setting Compatibility
tensor_equation: ∀0-RTT connection: client uses stored settings from previous session ∧ server MUST NOT accept 0-RTT if stored settings are incompatible with current settings ∧ if server accepts 0-RTT, its SETTINGS MUST NOT reduce limits violated by client 0-RTT data ∧ omission of previously-non-default setting after accepting 0-RTT ⇒ H3_SETTINGS_ERROR
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 3.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; 0-RTT setting compatibility is a hard requirement — settings must be forward-compatible or 0-RTT must be rejected.
prompt_injection: Verify that in 0-RTT connections, the server rejects 0-RTT if its current settings are incompatible with the client's stored settings from the previous session, that accepted 0-RTT SETTINGS frames never reduce limits that would have been violated by the client's 0-RTT data, and that omitting a previously non-default SETTINGS parameter after accepting 0-RTT triggers H3_SETTINGS_ERROR. Accepting 0-RTT data then sending a lower MAX_HEADER_LIST_SIZE than the client observed previously is a protocol violation.

title: HTTP/3 1-RTT SETTINGS Availability
tensor_equation: ∀1-RTT connection: 1-RTT keys become available before SETTINGS is processed by QUIC
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 3.3 in oracle/http2-quic.md; 1-RTT keys must be available before SETTINGS processing.
prompt_injection: Verify that in HTTP/3 connections, 1-RTT encryption keys are available before the QUIC transport processes the server's SETTINGS frame. Server SETTINGS arriving in a 0-RTT packet before 1-RTT keys are established would be unreadable, making the connection impossible to configure correctly.

title: HTTP/3 Server Name Indication
tensor_equation: ∀HTTP/3 client using domain-name-identified authority: client MUST send SNI TLS extension
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 3.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; clients using domain-name-identified authorities must include the SNI TLS extension.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/3 client connecting to an authority identified by a domain name includes the Server Name Indication (SNI) TLS extension in its ClientHello. Omitting SNI for a virtual-hosted origin means the server cannot select the correct certificate, potentially causing connection rejection or serving the wrong origin's content.

title: HTTP/3 Origin Certificate Match
tensor_equation: ∀HTTP/3 connection: client MUST verify server certificate matches the URI's origin server ∧ verification failure ⇒ client MUST NOT consider server authoritative
verdict_evidence: Extracted from RFC 9114 Section 3.1 in oracle/http2-quic.md; clients must verify the server certificate matches the origin and must reject the server as authoritative on failure.
prompt_injection: Verify that every HTTP/3 client validates the server's TLS certificate against the URI's origin server before considering the server authoritative. Accepting data from a server whose certificate does not match the origin constitutes a man-in-the-middle attack vector — the server could be impersonating the intended origin.

title: TLS 1.3 Handshake Message Ordering Invariant
tensor_equation: ∀ TLS 1.3 handshake H: H.messages occur in the order defined in Section 4.4.1: Phase 1 (Key Exchange): ClientHello → ServerHello; Phase 2 (Server Parameters): EncryptedExtensions [CertificateRequest]; Phase 3 (Authentication): [Certificate] [CertificateVerify] Finished; peer receiving message in unexpected order → abort with "unexpected_message" alert
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.4.1 and Section 2, with explicit counterexample: a server sending Certificate before EncryptedExtensions breaks the expected order and MUST be rejected.
prompt_injection: Verify that TLS 1.3 handshake messages occur in the strict order defined in RFC 8446 Section 4.4.1: Phase 1 (Key Exchange) is ClientHello then ServerHello; Phase 2 (Server Parameters) is EncryptedExtensions then optional CertificateRequest; Phase 3 (Authentication) is Certificate, CertificateVerify, Finished. Any peer receiving a message in unexpected order must abort with an "unexpected_message" alert. A server sending Certificate before EncryptedExtensions would break the expected order and must be rejected.

title: Post-Handshake Message Type Restriction
tensor_equation: ∀ post-handshake connection state: only {NewSessionTicket, PostHandshakeAuth, KeyUpdate} messages are permitted
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.6, with explicit counterexample: a server sending a CertificateRequest long after the handshake to a client that did not advertise post-handshake auth.
prompt_injection: Verify that after the TLS 1.3 handshake is complete, only three message types are permitted on the connection: NewSessionTicket, PostHandshakeAuth, and KeyUpdate. Any other handshake message type received post-handshake must be rejected. A server sending a CertificateRequest long after the handshake to a client that did not advertise post-handshake auth is a protocol violation.

title: TLS 1.3 Renegotiation Prohibition
tensor_equation: ∀ server that negotiated TLS 1.3: receiving another ClientHello on the same connection → MUST terminate with "unexpected_message"
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.6, with explicit counterexample: a TLS 1.2 style renegotiation attempt on a TLS 1.3 connection.
prompt_injection: Verify that TLS 1.3 does not support renegotiation. Any server that has negotiated TLS 1.3 and receives another ClientHello on the same connection must terminate the connection with an "unexpected_message" alert. A TLS 1.2-style renegotiation attempt on a TLS 1.3 connection is a protocol violation.

title: TLS 1.3 Encryption Layer Invariant
tensor_equation: ∀ handshake message m: m after ServerHello → encrypted with [sender]_handshake_traffic_secret; m after server Finished → encrypted with [sender]_application_traffic_secret_N; Application data before Finished → forbidden (except 0-RTT, Section 2.3)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 2 and Section 4, with explicit counterexample: an application data record being sent before the server Finished message leaks plaintext.
prompt_injection: Verify that every handshake message after ServerHello is encrypted with the sender's handshake traffic secret, and every message after server Finished is encrypted with the sender's application traffic secret. Application data before Finished is forbidden except for 0-RTT as defined in Section 2.3. An application data record sent before the server Finished message would leak plaintext.

title: TLS 1.3 Three-Phase Handshake Structure
tensor_equation: ∀ TLS 1.3 full handshake: (1) Key Exchange: ClientHello + ServerHello (establishes shared key); (2) Server Parameters: EncryptedExtensions + optional CertificateRequest; (3) Authentication: Certificate + CertificateVerify + Finished (server then client)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 2, with explicit counterexample: skipping the ServerHello and going directly to authentication provides no shared key material.
prompt_injection: Verify that every TLS 1.3 full handshake follows three phases: (1) Key Exchange via ClientHello and ServerHello to establish shared key material; (2) Server Parameters via EncryptedExtensions and optional CertificateRequest; (3) Authentication via Certificate, CertificateVerify, and Finished messages (server first, then client). Skipping the ServerHello to go directly to authentication would provide no shared key material.

title: Finished Message Transcript Authentication
tensor_equation: ∀ handshake: Finished = HMAC(handshake_derived_key, transcript_hash(all_previous_handshake_messages)); client waits for server Finished before sending application data; server waits for client Finished before sending application data
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.4.4, with explicit counterexample: accepting a Finished message whose MAC was computed over a different transcript enables a cut-and-paste attack.
prompt_injection: Verify that every Finished message is computed as HMAC of the handshake derived key and the transcript hash of all previous handshake messages. The client must wait for the server Finished before sending application data, and the server must wait for the client Finished before sending application data. Accepting a Finished message whose MAC was computed over a different transcript enables a cut-and-paste attack.

title: Key Schedule One-Way State Transitions
tensor_equation: ∀ key schedule states S_i, S_j: S_i → S_j (via HKDF-Extract); irreversible: no transition back from S_j to S_i; Stage ordering: 0 → EarlySecret → HandshakeSecret → MasterSecret
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1, with explicit counterexample: deriving EarlySecret from HandshakeSecret would be a backwards extraction breaking forward secrecy.
prompt_injection: Verify that TLS 1.3 key schedule state transitions are one-way: Stage 0 leads to EarlySecret, then to HandshakeSecret, then to MasterSecret, all via HKDF-Extract. No reverse transition is possible. Deriving EarlySecret from HandshakeSecret would be a backwards extraction that breaks forward secrecy.

title: HKDF-Extract Chain Derivation
tensor_equation: Early_Secret = HKDF-Extract(salt=0, IKM=PSK); Handshake_Secret = HKDF-Extract(salt=Derived_Early_Secret, IKM=(EC)DHE); Master_Secret = HKDF-Extract(salt=Derived_Handshake_Secret, IKM=0)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1, with explicit counterexample: skipping the (EC)DHE mixing in the Handshake Secret derivation would lose forward secrecy.
prompt_injection: Verify that the HKDF-Extract chain is derived correctly: Early_Secret from salt=0 and IKM=PSK; Handshake_Secret from salt=Derived_Early_Secret and IKM=(EC)DHE; Master_Secret from salt=Derived_Handshake_Secret and IKM=0. Skipping the (EC)DHE mixing in the Handshake Secret derivation would lose forward secrecy.

title: Label Uniqueness for Domain Separation
tensor_equation: ∀ Derive-Secret calls producing distinct secrets: each uses a unique label from { "tls13 derived", "tls13 c e traffic", "tls13 c hs traffic", "tls13 s hs traffic", "tls13 c ap traffic", "tls13 s ap traffic", "tls13 exp master", "tls13 res master", "tls13 e exp master" }
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1 and Section 4.6.1, with explicit counterexample: two different secrets derived with the same label would produce identical keying material, violating domain separation.
prompt_injection: Verify that every Derive-Secret call producing a distinct secret uses a unique label from the TLS 1.3 label set: "tls13 derived" for state transition chaining, "tls13 c e traffic" for client early traffic, "tls13 c hs traffic" for client handshake traffic, "tls13 s hs traffic" for server handshake traffic, "tls13 c ap traffic" for client app traffic, "tls13 s ap traffic" for server app traffic, "tls13 exp master" for exporter master secret, "tls13 res master" for resumption master secret, "tls13 e exp master" for early exporter master secret. Two different secrets derived with the same label would produce identical keying material, violating domain separation.

title: Transcript Binding in Key Derivation
tensor_equation: ∀ Derive-Secret(secret, label, messages): messages = transcript_hash(all_handshake_messages up to current point); ∀ secrets derived from the same secret but different transcripts: secrets are distinct (with overwhelming probability)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1, with explicit counterexample: a truncated transcript that omitted a handshake message would allow an adversary to swap extensions.
prompt_injection: Verify that every Derive-Secret call binds the transcript hash of all handshake messages up to the current point. Secrets derived from the same secret but different transcripts must be distinct with overwhelming probability. A truncated transcript that omitted a handshake message would allow an adversary to swap extensions.

title: Cipher Suite Hash Determination
tensor_equation: ∀ negotiated cipher suite CS: hash_algorithm(CS) ∈ {SHA-256, SHA-384}; AES_128_GCM_SHA256 → SHA-256; AES_256_GCM_SHA384 → SHA-384; CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 → SHA-256; all subsequent HKDF operations use this hash
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1 and the Cipher Suite registry, with explicit counterexample: deriving keys with SHA-384 after negotiating AES_128_GCM_SHA256 produces mismatched key sizes.
prompt_injection: Verify that the hash algorithm used for all HKDF operations is determined by the negotiated cipher suite: AES_128_GCM_SHA256 uses SHA-256, AES_256_GCM_SHA384 uses SHA-384, CHACHA20_POLY1305_SHA256 uses SHA-256. Deriving keys with SHA-384 after negotiating AES_128_GCM_SHA256 would produce mismatched key sizes.

title: Handshake and Master Secret Separation
tensor_equation: Master_Secret = HKDF-Extract(Derived_Handshake_Secret, 0); handshake traffic keys derived from Handshake_Secret BEFORE server Finished; application traffic keys derived from Master_Secret AFTER handshake complete
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1, with explicit counterexample: deriving application traffic keys from Handshake_Secret directly would reveal them to the handshake encryption context.
prompt_injection: Verify that Master_Secret is derived via HKDF-Extract from Derived_Handshake_Secret with zero IKM (no new entropy). Handshake traffic keys must be derived from Handshake_Secret before server Finished. Application traffic keys must be derived from Master_Secret after the handshake is complete. Deriving application traffic keys from Handshake_Secret directly would reveal them to the handshake encryption context.

title: Forward Secrecy via Ephemeral (EC)DHE
tensor_equation: ∀ full TLS 1.3 session S: Handshake_Secret = HKDF-Extract(Derived_Early_Secret, (EC)DHE); (EC)DHE is ephemeral per-session ⇒ compromise of long-term key does not reveal past session keys
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 7.1 and Appendix E.1, with explicit counterexample: using a static Diffie-Hellman key (TLS 1.2-style) that, when compromised, decrypts all past sessions.
prompt_injection: Verify that every full TLS 1.3 session achieves forward secrecy by using ephemeral (EC)DHE in the Handshake_Secret derivation. The (EC)DHE key is ephemeral per-session, so compromise of a long-term key does not reveal past session keys. Using a static Diffie-Hellman key (TLS 1.2-style) would, when compromised, decrypt all past sessions.

title: Downgrade Detection via Server Random
tensor_equation: ∀ ServerHello SH: SH.negotiated_version = TLS 1.2 ⇒ SH.random[24:] = 44 4F 57 4E 47 52 44 01; SH.negotiated_version ≤ TLS 1.1 ⇒ SH.random[24:] = 44 4F 57 4E 47 52 44 00; TLS 1.3 client checking: SH.random[24:] ∈ {DOWNGRADE_TLS12, DOWNGRADE_TLS11} ⇒ abort with "illegal_parameter"
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.3, with explicit counterexample: a TLS 1.3 client that does not check the last 8 bytes of ServerHello.random against the downgrade sentinels would accept a downgrade to TLS 1.2 without detecting it.
prompt_injection: Verify that a TLS 1.3 client checks the last 8 bytes of ServerHello.random for downgrade sentinels. If the negotiated version is TLS 1.2, the last 8 bytes must be 44 4F 57 4E 47 52 44 01 (DOWNGRADE_TLS12). If the negotiated version is TLS 1.1 or lower, the last 8 bytes must be 44 4F 57 4E 47 52 44 00 (DOWNGRADE_TLS11). If either sentinel is present, the client must abort with "illegal_parameter". A client that does not check would accept a downgrade to TLS 1.2 without detecting it.

title: No Static RSA Key Exchange in TLS 1.3
tensor_equation: ∀ TLS 1.3 ciphersuite: key_exchange_method ∈ {DHE, PSK, PSK_DHE}; static RSA encryption (RFC 5246) is NOT used
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 2, with explicit counterexample: a server offering TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (a TLS 1.2 suite without forward secrecy).
prompt_injection: Verify that every TLS 1.3 ciphersuite uses DHE, PSK, or PSK_DHE as the key exchange method. Static RSA encryption as defined in RFC 5246 is not used in TLS 1.3. A server offering TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA (a TLS 1.2 suite without forward secrecy) would violate this invariant.

title: AEAD Key Usage Limits
tensor_equation: ∀ AEAD key k: AES-GCM: ≤ 2^23 encrypted records; AES-CCM: ≤ 2^23 encrypted records; ChaCha20-Poly1305: ≤ 2^23 encrypted records (AEAD_limit according to Section 5.5)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.5, with explicit counterexample: encrypting 2^24 records under the same AES-GCM key would cause nonce collision near 2^24.5 due to GCM's birthday bound.
prompt_injection: Verify that every AEAD key enforces usage limits: AES-GCM, AES-CCM, and ChaCha20-Poly1305 must each encrypt at most 2^23 records per key. Encrypting 2^24 records under the same AES-GCM key would cause nonce collision near 2^24.5 due to GCM's birthday bound.

title: AEAD-Only Record Protection
tensor_equation: ∀ TLS 1.3 record: encryption_algorithm ∈ AEAD_SUITES; no support for CBC-mode MAC-then-encrypt (removed from TLS 1.3)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.2, with explicit counterexample: a TLS 1.3 implementation supporting TLS_CBC cipher suites would be vulnerable to a Lucky13 attack.
prompt_injection: Verify that every TLS 1.3 record is protected using an AEAD algorithm. CBC-mode MAC-then-encrypt cipher suites are removed from TLS 1.3. An implementation supporting TLS_CBC cipher suites would be vulnerable to a Lucky13 attack.

title: Content Type Obfuscation in TLSCiphertext
tensor_equation: ∀ TLSCiphertext record: opaque_type = 23 (application_data) for all encrypted records; actual content_type is encrypted inside the AEAD payload; inner content_type ∈ {22, 23, 24} (handshake, application_data, alert)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.2, with explicit counterexample: an intermediary seeing "alert" as the outer content type and blocking the connection; the outer content type is always 23.
prompt_injection: Verify that the outer opaque_type field of every TLSCiphertext record is set to 23 (application_data) regardless of the actual content type. The real content type is encrypted inside the AEAD payload and must be one of 22 (handshake), 23 (application_data), or 24 (alert). An intermediary seeing "alert" as the outer content type and blocking the connection would be incorrect; the outer content type is always 23.

title: Record Padding Constraints
tensor_equation: ∀ TLSCiphertext record: zero_padding MAY be appended to obscure plaintext length; padding_len + real_payload_len < 2^14 + 1; padding bytes are zero and stripped before decryption output
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.4, with explicit counterexample: sending padded records that leak length information due to inconsistent padding patterns.
prompt_injection: Verify that zero padding may be appended to TLSCiphertext records to obscure plaintext length. The total length (padding plus real payload) must be less than 2^14 + 1. Padding bytes must be zero and must be stripped before decryption output. Sending padded records with inconsistent padding patterns could leak length information.

title: Sequence Number for AEAD Nonce Construction
tensor_equation: ∀ AEAD encryption with key k: nonce = xor(static_iv(k), write_seq_num); write_seq_num increments by 1 per record; write_seq_num wraps ⇒ connection MUST close
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.3, with explicit counterexample: reusing a nonce for two records under the same key is an AEAD security violation, catastrophic for GCM.
prompt_injection: Verify that the AEAD nonce for each record is constructed by XORing the static IV derived from the key with the 64-bit write sequence number (zero-extended). The write sequence number increments by 1 per record. When the write sequence number wraps, the connection must close. Reusing a nonce for two records under the same key is an AEAD security violation and is catastrophic for GCM.

title: Single Record Per Handshake Message
tensor_equation: ∀ handshake message m: m MAY be fragmented across multiple records; records for different handshake messages MUST NOT be coalesced into a single handshake struct
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 5.1, with explicit counterexample: two separate handshake types (Certificate and CertificateVerify) packed into a single handshake record.
prompt_injection: Verify that a handshake message may be fragmented across multiple records, but records for different handshake messages must not be coalesced into a single handshake struct. Two separate handshake types (e.g., Certificate and CertificateVerify) must not be packed into a single handshake record.

title: HelloRetryRequest Detection via Magic Random
tensor_equation: ∀ ServerHello SH: SH.random = SHA-256("HelloRetryRequest") = CF21AD74E59A6111BE1D8C021E65B891C2A211167ABB8C5E079E09E2C8A8339C ⇒ SH is a HelloRetryRequest, not a real ServerHello
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: a naive implementation comparing bytes instead of the hash would miss the specific HRR pattern.
prompt_injection: Verify that a ServerHello message is identified as a HelloRetryRequest when its random field equals SHA-256("HelloRetryRequest") = CF21AD74E59A6111BE1D8C021E65B891C2A211167ABB8C5E079E09E2C8A8339C. A naive implementation comparing raw bytes instead of the hash would miss the specific HRR pattern.

title: Single HelloRetryRequest Per Connection
tensor_equation: ∀ connection C: |HRR_messages_in_C| ≤ 1; receiving a second HRR (client already responded to first HRR) ⇒ abort with "unexpected_message"
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: a server cycling through key_share groups infinitely by repeatedly sending HRR.
prompt_injection: Verify that at most one HelloRetryRequest is permitted per connection. If a client receives a second HRR after already responding to the first, it must abort with "unexpected_message". A server cycling through key_share groups infinitely by repeatedly sending HRR would violate this invariant.

title: Cipher Suite Consistency Across HRR
tensor_equation: ∀ connection that received HRR: cipher_suite(ServerHello) = cipher_suite(HelloRetryRequest)
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: a server offering AES_128_GCM_SHA256 in HRR but returning AES_256_GCM_SHA384 in ServerHello.
prompt_injection: Verify that when a connection receives a HelloRetryRequest, the cipher suite negotiated in the final ServerHello must match the cipher suite indicated in the HelloRetryRequest. A server offering AES_128_GCM_SHA256 in HRR but returning AES_256_GCM_SHA384 in ServerHello would violate this invariant.

title: Second ClientHello Modifications After HRR
tensor_equation: ∀ second ClientHello after HRR: key_share = single entry from HRR-indicated group; early_data is removed if previously present; cookie extension is included if HRR provided one; pre_shared_key binder and obfuscated_ticket_age are recomputed
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: the client sending the same key_share as the initial ClientHello would result in no change, triggering the HRR idempotency check.
prompt_injection: Verify that the second ClientHello sent after a HelloRetryRequest includes a single key_share entry from the HRR-indicated group, removes early_data if it was previously present, includes the cookie extension if the HRR provided one, and recomputes the pre_shared_key binder and obfuscated_ticket_age. The client sending the same key_share as the initial ClientHello would result in no change, triggering the HRR idempotency check.

title: HRR Idempotency Check
tensor_equation: ∀ initial ClientHello C1, ∀ HRR: if C1 would equal the second ClientHello (no changes needed): client MUST abort with "illegal_parameter"
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: accepting an HRR that requires no change to the ClientHello would create an infinite loop between client and server.
prompt_injection: Verify that if the initial ClientHello would be identical to the second ClientHello after an HRR (meaning no changes are needed), the client must abort with "illegal_parameter". Accepting an HRR that requires no change to the ClientHello would create an infinite loop between client and server.

title: Transcript Persistence Across HRR
tensor_equation: ∀ handshake after HRR: transcript = SHA256(ClientHello1 || HelloRetryRequest || ClientHello2 || ...); transcript is NOT reset between ClientHello1 and ClientHello2
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: using only ClientHello2 for the transcript would allow dropping key_share negotiation from the transcript, undermining downgrade protection.
prompt_injection: Verify that the handshake transcript after a HelloRetryRequest includes the concatenation of ClientHello1, the HRR, ClientHello2, and all subsequent messages. The transcript is not reset between ClientHello1 and ClientHello2. Using only ClientHello2 for the transcript would allow dropping key_share negotiation from the transcript, undermining downgrade protection.

title: HRR Extension Origin Restriction
tensor_equation: ∀ extension ext in HelloRetryRequest: ext was offered in the initial ClientHello, OR ext = "cookie"; HRR MUST NOT introduce novel extensions
verdict_evidence: Derived from RFC 8446 Section 4.1.2, with explicit counterexample: an HRR that offers a new extension the client never advertised would be protocol corruption.
prompt_injection: Verify that every extension in a HelloRetryRequest was either offered by the client in the initial ClientHello or is the "cookie" extension. The HRR must not introduce novel extensions. An HRR that offers a new extension the client never advertised would be protocol corruption.

title: SSA definition dominates all uses
tensor_equation: ∀instruction I, ∀value %x used by I: def(%x) dominates I in the control flow graph (dominators(DT, I) contains def(%x))
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-SSA-001 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Well-Formedness section.
prompt_injection: Verify that for every SSA virtual register %x used by any instruction I in a function, the definition point of %x strictly dominates I in the control-flow graph. A violation — using a value before its definition or referencing a phi value the entry block's predecessors do not provide — breaks the fundamental correctness guarantee of SSA form and can lead to miscompilation.

title: Single static assignment per value
tensor_equation: ∀function F, ∀SSA value v (register or unnamed temporary): v is assigned exactly once in F's IR; unnamed temporaries numbered sequentially per-function counter, starting at 0
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-SSA-002 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Introduction.
prompt_injection: Verify that every SSA virtual register or unnamed temporary is assigned exactly once within a function's IR, and that unnamed temporaries are numbered sequentially from 0 using a per-function counter. Assigning the same register twice in the same function violates SSA and enables miscompilation.

title: No phi nodes in entry block
tensor_equation: ∀function F: predecessors(entry_block(F)) = ∅ ∧ phi_nodes(entry_block(F)) = ∅
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-SSA-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Phi node instruction.
prompt_injection: Verify that the entry block of every function has no predecessors and contains no phi instructions. A phi node in the entry block would refer to non-existent predecessors, making it undefined and breaking SSA well-formedness.

title: Verifier runs before and after optimization
tensor_equation: ∀valid Module M: verifyModule(M) = true (parser runs verifier after parsing; optimizer runs verifier before bitcode emit)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-SSA-004 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Well-Formedness section.
prompt_injection: Verify that every valid LLVM module passes the module verifier. The parser must run the verifier after parsing, and the optimizer must run the verifier before bitcode emission. A malformed module that passes the verifier would silently produce incorrect codegen.

title: Function types are not first-class
tensor_equation: ∀instruction I that produces a value: result_type(I) ∈ {integer, floating-point, pointer, vector, struct, array, ...} ∧ result_type(I) ≠ FunctionType (function types are NOT first-class)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-TYPE-001 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Type System.
prompt_injection: Verify that no instruction produces a function type as its result. Function types are not first-class values in LLVM IR; functions are only addressable via pointers. Returning a function type value directly from an instruction breaks the type system.

title: Void type restricted to return operand
tensor_equation: ∀use of void type v: v appears only as function return or ret operand with no value ∧ v is never the result of a non-terminator instruction
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-TYPE-002 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Void Type.
prompt_injection: Verify that the void type only appears as a function return type or as the operand of a ret instruction with no value. Void must never be the result type of a non-terminator instruction. Using void as a stored or loaded type violates LLVM IR semantics.

title: Literal types uniqued structurally, identified types by name
tensor_equation: ∀literal type T1, T2: structurally_equal(T1, T2) ⇒ T1 = T2 (literal types are uniqued structurally); ∀identified type T1, T2: T1.name ≠ T2.name ⇒ T1 ≠ T2 (identified types are never uniqued)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-TYPE-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Type System.
prompt_injection: Verify that literal types are uniqued structurally — two structurally identical literal types must be the same type. Identified types are uniqued by name — two identified types with different names must never be treated as equivalent. Treating differently-named identified types as equivalent breaks type safety.

title: Pointer and vector element type restrictions
tensor_equation: ∀PointerType P: element_type(P) is a first-class TYPE (not void or function directly); ∀VectorType V: element_type(V) is a first-class or integer TYPE ∧ vector_length(V) is a power of 2 or 1 for scalable vectors
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-TYPE-004 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Pointer Type and Vector Type.
prompt_injection: Verify that pointer element types are first-class types (never void or function directly). Verify that vector element types are first-class or integer types, and that fixed vector lengths are powers of two (or 1 for scalable vectors). A vector of void or a non-power-of-2 fixed vector length violates LLVM IR type well-formedness.

title: Every basic block must end with a terminator
tensor_equation: ∀BasicBlock B in function F: last_instruction(B) ∈ {ret, br, switch, indirectbr, invoke, callbr, resume, catchswitch, catchret, cleanupret, unreachable}
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-CFG-001 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Terminator Instructions.
prompt_injection: Verify that every basic block in a function ends with a terminator instruction from the set {ret, br, switch, indirectbr, invoke, callbr, resume, catchswitch, catchret, cleanupret, unreachable}. A block that falls through without a proper terminator cannot be assembled and represents malformed IR.

title: Branch targets must be in same function
tensor_equation: ∀terminator T with label operand L: function_containing(L) = function_containing(T)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-CFG-002 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef br instruction.
prompt_injection: Verify that every terminator instruction with a label operand targets a basic block within the same function. Branching to a label in a different function is undefined and breaks CFG well-formedness.

title: Single entry block per function
tensor_equation: ∀function F: |entry_blocks(F)| = 1 ∧ entry_block(F) is defined by the first label in F's definition
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-CFG-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Structure.
prompt_injection: Verify that every function has exactly one entry block, defined as the first label in the function's definition. Multiple entry points that can be jumped to directly from outside the function violate LLVM IR function structure.

title: Volatile ops not reordered or eliminated
tensor_equation: ∀volatile op v ∈ {load, store}: v NOT: optimized away, reordered with other volatile ops, or combined with adjacent volatile ops; volatile accesses model memory-mapped I/O behavior
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-MEM-001 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Volatile Memory Accesses.
prompt_injection: Verify that volatile loads and stores are never optimized away, never reordered relative to other volatile operations, and never combined with adjacent volatile operations. Volatile accesses model memory-mapped I/O; violating these constraints can cause incorrect hardware interaction.

title: Load/store alignment must divide access address
tensor_equation: ∀load/store with alignment parameter A: access_address MOD A = 0; if A omitted: A = target_ABI_alignment(accessed_type)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-MEM-002 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Memory Access Operations.
prompt_injection: Verify that the access address for any load or store with an explicit alignment parameter A is divisible by A. If alignment is omitted, it defaults to the target ABI alignment for the accessed type. Misaligned access can cause faults on strict-alignment architectures.

title: Atomic ordering enforces happens-before ordering
tensor_equation: ∀ordering o ∈ {monotonic, acquire, release, acq_rel, seq_cst}: acquire ⇒ all subsequent memory operations by this thread happen after the atomic op; release ⇒ all preceding memory operations by this thread happen before the atomic op; seq_cst ⇒ single total order consistent with happens-before of all seq_cst ops
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-MEM-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Atomic Instructions.
prompt_injection: Verify that acquire ordering ensures all subsequent memory operations happen after the atomic operation, release ordering ensures all preceding memory operations happen before the atomic operation, and sequentially consistent ordering provides a single total order across all seq_cst operations. Reordering an acquire load's dependent load before the acquire itself breaks concurrency correctness.

title: cmpxchg failure ordering constraints
tensor_equation: ∀cmpxchg(success_ordering, failure_ordering): failure_ordering ≠ release ∧ failure_ordering ≠ acq_rel ∧ failure_ordering ≤ success_ordering
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-MEM-004 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef cmpxchg instruction.
prompt_injection: Verify that for every cmpxchg instruction, the failure ordering is neither release nor acq_rel (which would be semantically meaningless on a failed store), and the failure ordering is not stronger than the success ordering.

tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "readonly": F.does_not_write_memory_visible_to_caller() = true ∧ F.may_read_memory_visible_to_caller() = true
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-ATTR-001 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Attributes.
prompt_injection: Verify that functions marked readonly do not write to any memory visible to the caller, though they may read such memory. A readonly function writing to a global variable would allow the optimizer to incorrectly eliminate or reorder stores.

title: readnone functions must not access visible memory
tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "readnone": F.does_not_read_memory_visible_to_caller() = true ∧ F.does_not_write_memory_visible_to_caller() = true
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-ATTR-002 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Attributes.

tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "nounwind": unwinding does not propagate through F; F MUST return normally or call abort/trap
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-ATTR-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Attributes.

title: willreturn functions always terminate
tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "willreturn": F always terminates (does not infinite-loop, does not call noreturn)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-ATTR-004 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Attributes.

title: convergent calls must not be control-modified
tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "convergent": control_flow_of(call) must not be changed by optimization (F cannot be made control-dependent on additional values)
verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-ATTR-005 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Function Attributes.

tensor_equation: ∀call to function F with attribute "speculatable": F.has_side_effects() = false ∧ F.does_not_depend_on_undef_or_poison_behavior() = true

verdict_evidence: Extracted from INV-LLVM-INT-003 block in source-cache/llvm-ir.md, sourced from LLVM LangRef Standard Intrinsics.

title: Lifetime intrinsics bound stack slot accessibility

title: Resource identification: endpoints identify nouns, methods identify verbs

Flag any endpoint where the path contains a verb like /getUsers, /createUser, /deleteUserById.

prompt_injection: ∀method M ∈ {GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE}: M has a single, consistent semantic across all endpoints. GET is safe+idempotent+cacheable. POST is neither safe nor idempotent. PUT is idempotent (replace). PATCH is not necessarily idempotent. DELETE is idempotent.

title: HTTP 500 responses must never be HTML stack traces
prompt_injection: ∀HTTP 500 response R: body(R) is a structured JSON error, never an HTML stack trace, never a plain text panic, never a debug page. The server must sanitize internal error details before returning them to the client.

redteam_pattern: For every endpoint, assert that a request that results in an error returns a 4xx or 5xx status code, never 200 with an error field in the body. Send a malformed request to each endpoint and verify the status code is 4xx, not 200.

title: Canonical error code taxonomy maps codes to HTTP statuses
prompt_injection: ∀error response R: error.code(R) maps to the correct HTTP status per the canonical taxonomy: INVALID_ARGUMENT→400, FAILED_PRECONDITION→400, OUT_OF_RANGE→400, UNAUTHENTICATED→401, PERMISSION_DENIED→403, NOT_FOUND→404, ABORTED→409, ALREADY_EXISTS→409, RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED→429, CANCELLED→499, INTERNAL→500, UNAVAILABLE→503, DEADLINE_EXCEEDED→504.
tensor_equation: ∀error response R: http_status(R) = canonical_map(error.code(R))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 2: Error Responses — Structured, Consistent, Actionable (Google AIP-193 taxonomy). Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For each error code in the API, assert that the HTTP status code matches the canonical AIP-193 mapping. For example, if any endpoint returns error.code=NOT_FOUND with status 500, or PERMISSION_DENIED with status 401, that is a violation. Verify the mapping exhaustively across all error paths.

title: gRPC error responses use canonical google.rpc.Status codes
prompt_injection: ∀gRPC error response R: R uses canonical gRPC error codes (google.rpc.Code) matching the HTTP equivalents. The server returns a google.rpc.Status proto with code, message, and details fields. Error codes map to HTTP statuses in gRPC-HTTP transcoding.
tensor_equation: ∀gRPC error response R: R = google.rpc.Status{code, message, details} ∧ code ∈ canonical gRPC codes
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 2: Error Responses — Structured, Consistent, Actionable. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect every gRPC handler's error return path. Assert that all errors are returned as canonical gRPC status codes (google.rpc.Code) with code, message, and details. Flag any use of status.Errorf with a non-canonical code, or any raw error returned without a gRPC status wrapper.

title: MCP error responses follow JSON-RPC 2.0 error format
prompt_injection: ∀MCP error response R: R follows JSON-RPC 2.0 error format: {"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": N, "error": {"code": int, "message": string, "data": optional}}. Error codes: -32700 (parse error), -32600 (invalid request), -32601 (method not found), -32602 (invalid params), -32603 (internal error).
tensor_equation: ∀MCP error response R: R = JSON-RPC 2.0 error with code ∈ {-32700, -32600, -32601, -32602, -32603}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 2: Error Responses — Structured, Consistent, Actionable and Section 8: MCP Protocol. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send an invalid MCP request (e.g., unknown method, malformed params, parse error) to the MCP server. Assert that the response follows JSON-RPC 2.0 error format with the correct error code, a message string, and the id field echoing the request id.

title: Backwards-compatible changes stay in current version; incompatible changes need new version
prompt_injection: ∀API change C: compatible(C) → C is deployed in the current version. ∀API change C: ¬compatible(C) → C requires a new version. Backwards-compatible: adding endpoints, adding optional request fields, adding response fields, widening input types, loosening validation, changing implementation. Backwards-incompatible: removing endpoints, removing response fields, making optional fields required, narrowing input types, tightening validation, changing field semantics, changing URL structure.
tensor_equation: ∀API change C: compatible(C) ⇒ version(C) = current ∧ ¬compatible(C) ⇒ version(C) = new
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Review the API changelog and git history for any breaking change (removed endpoint, removed field, required field added, type narrowed) that was deployed without a version bump. Assert that every breaking change corresponds to a new version prefix (e.g., /v1/ → /v2/).

title: Version is explicit in every client request
prompt_injection: ∀client request R: the API version is explicit in the request (URL prefix /v1/, Accept header, or query parameter). The version must be visible to the client without out-of-band knowledge. The version is part of the service contract.
tensor_equation: ∀client request R: version(R) is explicit ∧ version(R) ∈ {URL prefix, Accept header, query param}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For every API endpoint, assert that the version is visible in the URL path (e.g., /api/v1/...), the Accept header, or a query parameter. Assert that there is no endpoint where the version is implicit (e.g., /api/users without a version prefix).

title: Version is a monotonically increasing identifier
prompt_injection: ∀version V: V is a monotonically increasing integer or date string (e.g., /v1/, /v2/, /v2023-01-01/). Version identifiers must be comparable so that clients can determine which version is newer. Never use semantic labels like 'stable', 'latest', 'beta' as version identifiers.
tensor_equation: ∀version V: V ∈ {monotonically increasing integer, date string}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Examine all version identifiers used in the API. Assert that they are monotonically increasing integers (v1, v2, v3) or date strings (v2023-01-01). Flag any version labels that are non-comparable (e.g., 'stable', 'latest', 'beta', 'next').

title: Adding new endpoints and optional fields is backwards-compatible
prompt_injection: ∀API change C: adding a new endpoint, adding an optional request field, adding a response field, widening an input type (e.g., int32→int64), or loosening validation (e.g., required→optional) is backwards-compatible and may be deployed in the current version. Clients must ignore unknown fields in responses (robustness principle).
tensor_equation: ∀change C ∈ {add_endpoint, add_optional_field, add_response_field, widen_type, loosen_validation}: compatible(C) = true
