verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Propose adding a new optional field to a request or response. Verify that the change is deployed without a version bump. Assert that existing clients continue to work (old requests without the new field are accepted, old clients ignore the new field in responses).

title: Removing endpoints or fields, changing semantics is backwards-incompatible
prompt_injection: ∀API change C: removing an endpoint, removing a response field, making an optional field required, narrowing an input type, tightening validation, changing the semantics of an existing field, or changing the URL structure is backwards-incompatible and requires a new API version.
tensor_equation: ∀change C ∈ {remove_endpoint, remove_field, make_required, narrow_type, tighten_validation, change_semantics, change_url}: compatible(C) = false
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Check the git diff between the last deployment and the current code. For any removed endpoint, removed response field, newly required field, or type narrowing, assert that a new version prefix exists (e.g., /v2/ endpoints) and the old version is still served.

title: Query parameter versioning is fragile and should be avoided
prompt_injection: ∀API versioning scheme: query parameter versioning (GET /users?version=1) is fragile — easy to miss, hard to enforce in middleware, and not cache-friendly. URL prefix versioning (/v1/users) is preferred for production APIs.
tensor_equation: versioning_scheme ∈ {URL_prefix, Accept_header} for production APIs
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 3: Versioning — Backwards-Incompatible Changes Require a New Version. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: If the API uses query parameter versioning, flag it as a fragility risk. Assert that production APIs use URL prefix versioning (/v1/, /v2/) or Accept header content negotiation, not query parameters.

title: Every list endpoint must paginate its results
prompt_injection: ∀list endpoint E: E must paginate its results. No endpoint returns an unbounded result set. Pagination is a hard requirement for reliability, not a performance optimization. A list endpoint that returns all results works fine with 10 rows but crashes the server with 10 million.
tensor_equation: ∀list endpoint E: paginated(E) = true
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For every list endpoint in the API, send a request without pagination parameters and assert that the response is bounded (returns a subset of results with a next_page_token). Assert that no endpoint returns all results in a single response without pagination controls.

title: List response includes next_page_token for pagination
prompt_injection: ∀list endpoint E: response(E) includes a next_page_token field (or next cursor) that the client can use to request the next page. When there are no more results, next_page_token is null or empty.
tensor_equation: ∀list endpoint E: response(E).next_page_token ≠ null
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For each list endpoint, call it with a small page_size. Assert that the response includes a next_page_token field (possibly null/empty for the last page). Assert that providing the next_page_token as a query parameter returns the next page of results.

title: Page size has a documented maximum value
prompt_injection: ∀list endpoint E: request(E) includes a page_size field with a documented max value. ∀request: page_size ≤ server-defined max_page_size. The server must cap page_size to the maximum even if the client requests a larger value.
tensor_equation: ∀list endpoint E: request(E).page_size ∈ [1, max_page_size] ∧ server_clamp(page_size, max_page_size)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For each list endpoint, send a request with page_size set to a very large value (e.g., 999999). Assert that the response is capped at the documented max_page_size. Assert that the server does not return more results than max_page_size or crash.

title: Cursor-based pagination is preferred over offset-based
prompt_injection: ∀list endpoint E: prefer cursor-based pagination over offset-based. Cursor-based is stable under concurrent writes — the cursor points to a specific row, not a position that can shift. Offset-based pagination may miss or duplicate rows under concurrent writes (inserting a row shifts subsequent pages).
tensor_equation: ∀list endpoint E: pagination_type(E) = cursor-based (preferred) | offset-based (acceptable for small datasets only)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For each list endpoint using offset-based pagination, insert a new row while the client is paginating through results. Assert that the client either misses the new row or sees it duplicated (demonstrating the instability). Verify that cursor-based pagination does not have this problem.

title: Pagination cursor is opaque to the client
prompt_injection: ∀cursor-based pagination: the cursor is opaque to the client — typically a base64-encoded row ID or timestamp tuple. The client must not inspect, modify, or construct cursors. The server decodes the cursor and translates it to a database query (WHERE (id) > (cursor_id) ORDER BY id LIMIT page_size).
tensor_equation: ∀cursor C: opaque(C) = true ∧ client must not inspect C
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the cursor format. Assert that cursors are opaque (e.g., base64-encoded, hashed, or encrypted) and the client cannot derive the underlying row ID or timestamp from the cursor value. Assert that the server handles malformed cursors gracefully (returns INVALID_ARGUMENT, not 500).

title: Unbounded list queries are a reliability risk
prompt_injection: ∀list endpoint E: unbounded queries are a reliability risk. A list endpoint that returns all results works fine with 10 rows but crashes the server with 10 million. Pagination must be enforced at the API layer, not left to the client to request responsibly.
tensor_equation: ∀list endpoint E: ¬unbounded(E) — pagination is mandatory
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 4: Pagination — Cursor-Based Preferred, No Unbounded Lists. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Attempt to access a list endpoint without pagination parameters. Assert that the server either returns a default page_size-limited result, or returns an error requiring pagination parameters. Assert that no endpoint returns all rows from a database table without a LIMIT clause.

title: Idempotency keys enable safe retry of POST and PATCH requests
prompt_injection: ∀idempotent request R (POST/PATCH with Idempotency-Key header): server stores (key, response) pair for a configured TTL. Server checks for existing key before executing the operation. Replay with same key returns the stored response, does not re-execute. Replay with different key creates a new operation.
tensor_equation: ∀request R with Idempotency-Key K: ∃K in store ⇒ return stored response ∧ ¬∃K in store ⇒ execute and store (K, response)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 5: Idempotency — Safe Replay for POST and PATCH. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send a POST request with an Idempotency-Key header. Receive the response. Send the same POST with the same Idempotency-Key but a different body. Assert that the server returns 422 UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY (key locked to original body). Send the same POST with the same Idempotency-Key and same body. Assert that the server returns the stored response without re-executing.

title: Idempotency key with different body returns 422 UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY
prompt_injection: ∀replay with same Idempotency-Key K but different request body: server returns 422 UNPROCESSABLE_ENTITY because the key is locked to the original request body. This prevents a client from accidentally reusing a key for a different operation.
tensor_equation: ∀replay R with same(K) ∧ different(body): status(R) = 422 ∧ error.code = INVALID_ARGUMENT
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 5: Idempotency — Safe Replay for POST and PATCH. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Execute a POST request with Idempotency-Key=abc123. Then send a different POST request (different body) with the same Idempotency-Key=abc123. Assert that the server returns 422 or 400, and that the original operation was not re-executed or overwritten.

title: Idempotency check and store must be atomic to prevent race conditions
prompt_injection: ∀idempotency implementation: the check (key exists?) and store (save key+response) must be atomic. Use Redis SETNX, database INSERT with ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING, or a distributed lock. Without atomicity, two concurrent requests with the same key can both pass the check and both execute the operation.
tensor_equation: ∀idempotency operation: atomic(check_existence(K), store(K, response)) = true
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 5: Idempotency — Safe Replay for POST and PATCH. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send two concurrent POST requests with the same Idempotency-Key and same body. Assert that only one operation is executed (the second returns the cached response). Without proper atomicity, both requests would execute. Test with a race-condition detector or by inspecting the idempotency store after concurrent requests.

title: GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent by HTTP semantics; idempotency key is for POST/PATCH
prompt_injection: ∀idempotency mechanism: GET, PUT, DELETE are already idempotent by HTTP semantics. The Idempotency-Key mechanism is for POST and PATCH, which are not inherently idempotent. gRPC: idempotency key is a field in the request proto. MCP: JSON-RPC request IDs serve as idempotency keys for tools/call.
tensor_equation: ∀method M ∈ {GET, PUT, DELETE}: idempotent(M) = true (by HTTP spec) ∧ Idempotency-Key is for M ∈ {POST, PATCH}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 5: Idempotency — Safe Replay for POST and PATCH. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Verify that GET, PUT, and DELETE endpoints do not require Idempotency-Key headers (they are idempotent by definition). Verify that POST and PATCH endpoints that have side effects either require or support Idempotency-Key for safe retry.

title: Every API response includes rate limit headers
prompt_injection: ∀response R from a rate-limited endpoint: R includes X-RateLimit-Limit (max requests per window), X-RateLimit-Remaining (requests remaining in current window), and X-RateLimit-Reset (Unix timestamp when window resets). The headers give the client enough information to implement backoff without guessing.
tensor_equation: ∀response R: R.headers contains {X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 6: Rate Limiting Headers — Inform the Client, Enforce the Limit. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Call every rate-limited endpoint and inspect the response headers. Assert that X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, and X-RateLimit-Reset are present. Assert that X-RateLimit-Limit is a positive integer, X-RateLimit-Remaining is a non-negative integer ≤ limit, and X-RateLimit-Reset is a valid Unix timestamp in the future.

title: 429 response includes Retry-After header
prompt_injection: ∀429 Too Many Requests response R: R includes the Retry-After header (seconds to wait, or HTTP-date). The client uses this to implement exponential backoff. Without Retry-After, the client must guess when to retry, which can cause thundering herd problems.
tensor_equation: ∀429 response R: Retry-After ∈ R.headers
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 6: Rate Limiting Headers — Inform the Client, Enforce the Limit. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Exhaust the rate limit for an endpoint (send requests until 429). Assert that the 429 response includes the Retry-After header with a positive integer value. Assert that the structured error body is also present with error.code=RESOURCE_EXHAUSTED.

title: Rate limiting enforced at gateway/middleware before handler
prompt_injection: ∀rate limit: the limit is enforced at the gateway or middleware layer, not in individual handlers. The handler must never see the rate limit check — it is enforced before the request reaches the handler. This ensures consistent enforcement across all endpoints and prevents handlers from bypassing the limit.
tensor_equation: ∀rate limit enforcement: layer = gateway ∨ middleware ∧ check performed before handler execution
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 6: Rate Limiting Headers — Inform the Client, Enforce the Limit. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the codebase for rate limiting implementation. Assert that rate limiting is implemented as middleware or gateway-level interceptor, not as per-handler logic. Verify that a new endpoint added without explicit rate limiting code is still covered by the gateway/middleware rate limiter.

title: gRPC: every RPC must have a client-set deadline
prompt_injection: ∀RPC R: the client sets a deadline using context.WithTimeout or context.WithDeadline. gRPC without deadlines is a memory leak — a client that crashes without a deadline leaves the server running the RPC indefinitely. The server should also enforce a maximum server-side timeout as a safety net.
tensor_equation: ∀RPC R: deadline(R) is set by client ∧ deadline(R) < ∞
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect all gRPC client code. Assert that every RPC call uses a context with a deadline or timeout (context.WithTimeout or context.WithDeadline). Flag any RPC call that uses context.Background() or a bare context without a deadline. Also verify that the server has a maximum timeout configured to catch clients that don't set deadlines.

title: gRPC: server must check ctx.Err() in streaming and long-running RPCs
prompt_injection: ∀RPC R: server checks ctx.Err() at least once per iteration in streaming RPCs and long-running operations. Without ctx.Err() checks, a streaming RPC can run forever even after the client disconnects. The server must detect client cancellation and stop processing.
tensor_equation: ∀streaming RPC S: server checks ctx.Err() per iteration ∨ checks ctx.Done() in select
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Examine all gRPC server-side streaming handler implementations. Assert that each streaming loop checks ctx.Err() or ctx.Done() between sends or iterations. Simulate a client disconnect mid-stream and verify that the server stops processing within a bounded time (not running until the natural end of the data stream).

title: gRPC: server must not hold a mutex across a blocking I/O call
prompt_injection: ∀RPC R: server does not hold a mutex across a blocking I/O call (network, disk, channel send). Holding a mutex across a blocking I/O call causes contention that can deadlock the server. The goroutine should release the mutex before performing I/O and re-acquire it afterward.
tensor_equation: ∀mutex M, ∀I/O call IO: held(M) during IO ⇒ potential deadlock — must release before IO
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Analyze the gRPC server code for any mutex.Lock() that is not released before a blocking I/O call (network request, database query, file write, channel send). Use Go's race detector or static analysis to find lock-holding across I/O. Flag any occurrence as a potential deadlock.

title: gRPC: server handles client cancellation and closes stream on completion
prompt_injection: ∀streaming RPC S: server handles client cancellation (ctx.Done()) within the stream loop and closes the stream when the iteration is complete. The server must not leak goroutines or open streams when the client disconnects. The server calls stream.Close() or returns from the handler when done.
tensor_equation: ∀streaming RPC S: handler detects ctx.Done() ∧ handler closes stream on completion
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect all streaming RPC handler implementations. Assert that the stream loop has a select on ctx.Done() or checks ctx.Err(). Assert that the handler returns cleanly (no goroutine leak) when the context is cancelled. Verify that the stream is closed on normal completion by checking for the Send call pattern.

title: gRPC without deadlines is a memory leak
prompt_injection: ∀RPC R: ¬deadline(R) ⇒ server resource leak. A client that crashes without a deadline leaves the server running the RPC indefinitely, consuming memory, goroutines, and connections. Deadlines are the primary mechanism for resource cleanup in gRPC. A server-side maximum timeout is a safety net for misconfigured clients.
tensor_equation: ∀RPC R: ¬deadline(R) ⇒ memory_leak(R). Server must enforce max_timeout as safety net.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Configure a gRPC client without a deadline and send an RPC that takes a long time. Kill the client process. Assert that the server goroutine handling that RPC terminates within a bounded time (via server-side timeout), not running indefinitely. Check the server's goroutine count before and after to verify no leak.

title: Protobuf naming conventions: Request/Response messages, snake_case fields, UNSPECIFIED enum zero
prompt_injection: ∀protobuf message M: request messages are named {Resource}{Action}Request (e.g., GetUserRequest), response messages are named {Resource}{Action}Response. Fields are snake_case in proto, translated to camelCase in JSON. Enums start with ENUM_TYPE_UNSPECIFIED as the zero value (required by protobuf semantics). Standard fields: name (resource name), parent (parent resource), request_id (idempotency key).
tensor_equation: ∀protobuf message M: naming_convention(M) ∈ {Request/Response, snake_case, UNSPECIFIED zero}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 7: gRPC Patterns — Protobuf Contracts, Deadlines, Streaming (AIP-121, AIP-122). Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect all .proto files. Assert that every RPC has a corresponding {Action}Request and {Action}Response message. Assert that all field names use snake_case. Assert that every enum has an UNSPECIFIED zero value. Assert that request messages use standard field names (name, parent, request_id) where applicable.

title: Every MCP server implements tools/list and tools/call
prompt_injection: ∀MCP server S: S implements tools/list (returns available tools) and tools/call (executes a tool with given arguments). The tools/list endpoint lets the LLM discover available tools dynamically. tools/call returns the tool execution result.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP server S: S implements tools/list ∧ S implements tools/call
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Connect to the MCP server and call tools/list. Assert that the response is a valid JSON-RPC result with a tools array. Call tools/call with a valid tool name and arguments. Assert that the response is a valid JSON-RPC result. Verify that unknown tool names return JSON-RPC error code -32601 (method not found).

title: Every MCP server implements at least one of resources or prompts
prompt_injection: ∀MCP server S: S implements at least one of resources (resources/list, resources/read) or prompts (prompts/list, prompts/get). Resources provide data content. Prompts provide conversation templates. Tools alone are not sufficient for a complete MCP server.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP server S: S implements resources ∨ S implements prompts
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Connect to the MCP server and attempt to call resources/list and prompts/list. Assert that at least one of these returns a non-empty result or is implemented. If the server only implements tools and neither resources nor prompts, flag the violation.

title: All MCP messages use JSON-RPC 2.0 framing
prompt_injection: ∀MCP server S: all messages use JSON-RPC 2.0 framing: {"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": N, "method": "M", "params": {...}}. The transport is either stdio (subprocess) or SSE (HTTP). Notifications (no id field) do not expect a response.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP message M: M = JSON-RPC 2.0 frame ∧ M.transport ∈ {stdio, SSE}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the raw bytes sent over the MCP transport. Assert that every message from the server is valid JSON-RPC 2.0 with jsonrpc field set to "2.0". Assert that request messages have id, method, and optional params. Assert that responses have id and either result or error.

title: Every MCP tool has name, description, and inputSchema
prompt_injection: ∀MCP tool T: T has a name (string identifier), description (human-readable explanation of what the tool does), and inputSchema (JSON Schema defining the valid arguments). Tool arguments are validated against the inputSchema before execution.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP tool T: T = {name: string, description: string, inputSchema: JSON Schema}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Call tools/list on the MCP server. For each tool in the response, assert that the tool object has a name (non-empty string), description (non-empty string), and inputSchema (valid JSON Schema object). Assert that tools/call validates arguments against the inputSchema and returns JSON-RPC error -32602 for invalid params.

title: Every MCP resource has a URI pattern and name
prompt_injection: ∀MCP resource R: R has a URI pattern in the form {scheme}://{path} (e.g., file:///path/to/file, db://users/123, api://weather/current) and a name (human-readable label). Resources can be dynamic — the same URI can return different data on different reads.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP resource R: R = {uri: scheme://path, name: string}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Call resources/list on the MCP server. For each resource in the response, assert that the resource has a uri field matching scheme://path pattern and a name field (non-empty string). Call resources/read with a valid URI and assert that the response contains the resource content.

title: Every MCP prompt has a name and message list
prompt_injection: ∀MCP prompt P: P has a name (string identifier) and a list of messages (role and content). Prompts are deterministic — same arguments produce the same messages. Prompts provide conversation templates for the LLM.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP prompt P: P = {name: string, messages: [{role: string, content: string}]} ∧ deterministic(P) = true
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Call prompts/list on the MCP server. For each prompt, assert that it has a name and a list of messages. Call prompts/get with the same arguments twice and assert that the returned messages are identical (deterministic).

title: MCP tools are stateless; server manages state
prompt_injection: ∀MCP tool T: T is stateless. The server manages state; the tool call is a stateless operation that receives input and returns output. The client (LLM) should not need to manage state between tool calls. The server is responsible for persisting and retrieving state across calls.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP tool T: stateless(T) = true ∧ state management is server responsibility
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Examine each MCP tool handler. Assert that the tool does not depend on caller-maintained state between invocations. The same tool call with the same arguments should produce the same result (or the server should be the single source of truth for state). Flag any tool that requires the caller to pass state accumulated from previous calls.

title: JSON-RPC id field must be present in request and echoed in response
prompt_injection: ∀MCP request R: id(R) is present and unique. ∀MCP response R: id(R) echoes the request id. A notification (no id field in request) does not expect a response. The id field enables the client to correlate requests and responses in asynchronous transports.
tensor_equation: ∀MCP request R with id: id(response) = id(request) ∧ ∀notification without id: no response expected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 8: MCP Protocol — Tools, Resources, and Prompts. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send an MCP request with a specific id. Assert that the response has the same id. Send an MCP notification (no id field). Assert that the server does not send a response. Send a request with a duplicate id and verify the server handles it gracefully (returns the result for the original request or an error).

title: Protected endpoints require valid authentication credential
prompt_injection: ∀request R to a protected endpoint E: R includes a valid auth header (Authorization: Bearer <token> or X-API-Key: <key>). The server validates the credential before executing the handler. Invalid/missing credential → 401 UNAUTHENTICATED. Valid credential, insufficient scope → 403 PERMISSION_DENIED.
tensor_equation: ∀request R to protected endpoint E: auth(R) ∈ {Bearer token, API key} ∧ valid(auth(R)) ⇒ execute ∧ ¬valid(auth(R)) ⇒ 401
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 9: Authentication — Bearer Tokens, API Keys, OAuth2. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: For each protected endpoint, send a request without any auth header, send a request with an invalid auth header, and send a request with a valid auth header. Assert that missing auth returns 401, invalid auth returns 401, and valid auth returns the expected response (not 401).

title: Consistent authentication mechanism across all endpoints
prompt_injection: ∀protected endpoint E: the authentication mechanism is consistent across all endpoints. All endpoints use the same auth header format (Authorization: Bearer or X-API-Key). All endpoints validate using the same middleware. Inconsistent auth mechanisms force clients to implement multiple auth strategies and increase the attack surface.
tensor_equation: ∀protected endpoints E1, E2: auth_mechanism(E1) = auth_mechanism(E2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 9: Authentication — Bearer Tokens, API Keys, OAuth2. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect all protected endpoints. Assert that they all use the same auth mechanism (e.g., all use Authorization: Bearer or all use X-API-Key, not a mix). Flag any endpoint that uses a different auth mechanism than the rest of the API.

title: Auth is middleware, not per-endpoint logic
prompt_injection: ∀authentication: the auth layer is middleware applied to route groups, not per-endpoint logic in each handler. The middleware reads the auth header, validates the credential, and sets the user/identity in the request context. Handlers should never contain auth validation logic — they should read the identity from the context.
tensor_equation: ∀auth: layer = middleware ∧ auth logic is not in individual handlers
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 9: Authentication — Bearer Tokens, API Keys, OAuth2. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the codebase for authentication logic. Assert that auth is implemented as middleware (applied to a route group or router) and not as inline code in individual handler functions. Flag any handler that reads and validates auth headers directly instead of reading an identity from the request context.

title: 401 for missing/invalid credentials, 403 for insufficient scope
prompt_injection: ∀request R: invalid/missing credential → 401 UNAUTHENTICATED. Valid credential, insufficient scope → 403 PERMISSION_DENIED. 401 means 'who are you?' 403 means 'I know who you are but you can't do that.' These must not be conflated — returning 403 for missing credentials leaks information about valid routes.
tensor_equation: ∀request R: ¬valid_auth ⇒ 401 ∧ valid_auth ∧ ¬authorized ⇒ 403
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 9: Authentication — Bearer Tokens, API Keys, OAuth2. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send a request without auth credentials to a protected endpoint. Assert status is 401, not 403. Send a request with valid credentials but insufficient permissions (e.g., a read-only token to a write endpoint). Assert status is 403, not 401. Verify that the error codes match UNAUTHENTICATED and PERMISSION_DENIED respectively.

title: API keys are long-lived and require secure storage
prompt_injection: ∀API key K: K is a random string stored in the database (hashed or encrypted). API keys are long-lived and require secure storage. The server looks up the key, checks the rate limit, and identifies the client. Unlike JWTs, API keys cannot be self-validated — they require a database lookup but can be revoked immediately.
tensor_equation: ∀API key K: stored_securely(K) = true ∧ K is revocable immediatement
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 9: Authentication — Bearer Tokens, API Keys, OAuth2. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the API key storage implementation. Assert that API keys are hashed (bcrypt, argon2, or SHA-256 with salt) before storage, not stored in plaintext. Assert that there is a mechanism to revoke a key immediately (not just set an expiry date in the future). Assert that key lookup is rate-limited to prevent brute force.

title: Every request has a unique traceable request ID
prompt_injection: ∀request R: X-Request-ID or X-Correlation-ID is generated at the entry point (gateway) or propagated from the client. If the client provides a request ID, it is propagated. If not, the gateway generates one (UUID or ULID). The request ID is the single thread that connects all log lines across all services.
tensor_equation: ∀request R: request_id(R) is generated at entry point or propagated from client
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 10: Observability — Request ID Propagation, Structured Logging, Metrics. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Send a request to the API without an X-Request-ID header. Assert that the response includes an X-Request-ID header with a non-empty value. Send a request with a specific X-Request-ID header. Assert that the response echoes the same value. Verify that the request ID appears in the server logs for that request.

title: Request ID is propagated to all downstream services
prompt_injection: ∀request R: the request ID is propagated to all downstream services. REST: X-Request-ID header. gRPC: X-Request-ID in gRPC metadata. MCP: the request ID is sent as part of the notification or request params. Without propagation, correlating logs across services requires matching timestamps and guessing.
tensor_equation: ∀request R: request_id(R) is propagated via protocol-appropriate mechanism to all downstream services
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 10: Observability — Request ID Propagation, Structured Logging, Metrics. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Trace a request through the full service chain (gateway → gRPC → downstream). Assert that the request ID from the entry point is present in the gRPC metadata and in the logs of every downstream service. If a service receives a request without a request ID, it should generate one and propagate it.

title: Every log line includes the request_id field
prompt_injection: ∀log line L: L includes the request_id field. The structured logger adds the field automatically from the context. Every log line should be traceable to a specific request. Error logs additionally include: error, stack_trace (at ERROR level), and code (error code).
tensor_equation: ∀log line L: request_id ∈ fields(L) ∧ ∀error log L: {error, stack_trace, code} ⊆ fields(L)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 10: Observability — Request ID Propagation, Structured Logging, Metrics. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Generate a request and capture all log lines emitted by all services during that request. Assert that every log line (at every level: INFO, WARN, ERROR, DEBUG) includes the request_id field. Assert that ERROR-level log lines include error and stack_trace fields.

title: Every service exports metrics for request count, latency, and error rate
prompt_injection: ∀service S: S exports metrics for request count (requests_total), latency histogram (request_duration_seconds with p50/p95/p99), and error rate (errors_total by error code). Metrics are aggregated by endpoint, method, and status code. Prometheus format: http_requests_total{method="GET", endpoint="/users", status="200"}.
tensor_equation: ∀service S: S exports metrics {requests_total, request_duration_seconds, errors_total} by endpoint/method/status
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 10: Observability — Request ID Propagation, Structured Logging, Metrics. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Examine the metrics endpoint of each service. Assert that requests_total, request_duration_seconds (histogram), and errors_total metrics are present. Assert that these metrics are labeled by endpoint, method, and status code. Assert that the histogram has buckets configured for typical latency ranges (e.g., 1ms to 10s).

title: Log in JSON format, not plain text
prompt_injection: ∀log output: log in JSON format, not plain text. JSON is parseable by log aggregation tools (ELK, Loki, CloudWatch). Every log line includes: timestamp, level, request_id, service, message, and any operation-specific fields. No fmt.Println or console.log for logging — use a structured logger.
tensor_equation: ∀log line L: format(L) = JSON ∧ L contains {timestamp, level, request_id, service, message}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/api-design-oracle.md Section 10: Observability — Request ID Propagation, Structured Logging, Metrics. Full citation in source-cache/api-design-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Inspect the log output configuration. Assert that the log format is JSON (not plain text, not a custom format). Assert that every log line is valid JSON with fields: timestamp, level, request_id, service, message. Search the codebase for fmt.Println, console.log, or logger.Info without structured fields — flag any occurrence.

title: Xcode build reproducibility
prompt_injection: ∀config ∈ {Debug, Release}: build(config) = f(source, settings) where f is deterministic and reproducible. The same source and settings always produce the same binary.
tensor_equation: ∀config: build(config) = f(source, settings) : f deterministic
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.1 Build System. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Build the same project twice on the same machine with identical settings and compare binary hashes. Different hashes violate the invariant.

title: Scheme action configuration must match build configuration
prompt_injection: ∀scheme S: ∀action A ∈ {Build, Test, Run, Profile, Analyze, Archive}: S specifies a build configuration for A. If the scheme's action configuration is mismatched, bugs appear only in release builds that were not caught in debug.
tensor_equation: ∀scheme: ∀action: ∃build_config(scheme, action) ∧ match(build_config, action_purpose)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.2 Schemes. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Set Test action to Release build configuration. Run unit tests. If tests pass but release-only bugs exist in production, the invariant is violated. Verify by checking scheme XML for mismatched build configurations.

title: Code signing chain of trust at launch
prompt_injection: ∀launch L of binary B: signature_chain(B) is valid (Developer Certificate ← Apple CA ← Apple Root CA) ∧ provisioning_profile(B) includes this_device ∧ entitlements(B) match provisioning_profile(B). If any link fails, the launch is denied by Gatekeeper or AMFI.
tensor_equation: ∀launch: valid(chain(binary)) ∧ device_in_profile(profile, device) ∧ entitlements_match(profile, entitlements)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.3 Code Signing. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Sign a binary with an expired provisioning profile and attempt to launch it. Gatekeeper should block the launch. If the launch succeeds, the invariant is violated.

title: CodeResources integrity in signed bundle
prompt_injection: ∀signed bundle B: CodeResources contains hashes of every file in B. If any file in B is modified after signing, the signature becomes invalid. ∀modified_file: hash(modified_file) ≠ hash_in_CodeResources → invalid_signature.
tensor_equation: ∀modified_file ∈ bundle: hash(modified_file) ≠ CodeResources_hash → signature_invalid
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.3 Code Signing. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Modify a resource file inside a signed .app bundle after codesigning. Verify that codesign -v fails. If it passes, the invariant is violated.

title: Instruments trace data is runtime evidence
prompt_injection: ∀Instruments trace T: T is evidence of actual runtime behavior, not speculative behavior. Leak speculation without Instruments trace data is not actionable. ∀trace: the heaviest stack trace in the allocation trace is the primary code path allocating memory.
tensor_equation: ∀trace: evidence(trace) = actual_runtime_behavior ∧ heaviest_stack(trace) = primary_allocation_path
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.4 Instruments. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Profile an app with Instruments Allocations while the app is idle. If the heaviest stack trace is not the primary allocation path, the invariant is violated. Correlate with VM Tracker data.

title: Package.resolved lockfile reproducibility
prompt_injection: ∀dependency D in package graph G: exact version of D is recorded in Package.resolved. Any change to Package.resolved changes the dependency graph. CI and developer machines must agree on the same Package.resolved commit. ∀mismatch: 'It works on my machine' due to different resolved version.
tensor_equation: ∀dep: exact_version(dep) ∈ Package.resolved ∧ change(Package.resolved) → change(dep_graph) ∧ CI_version = dev_version
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.5 Swift Package Manager. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Delete Package.resolved, run swift package update, and compare the new resolved versions to the original. If versions differ, the invariant is violated. CI must fail if Package.resolved is not committed.

title: SPM version constraint semantics
prompt_injection: ∀version_constraint C: 'upToNextMajor(from: x)' enforces x ≤ version < next_major(x). 'exact(v)' enforces version = v exactly. 'branch(name)' enforces latest commit on branch, which is non-reproducible. ∀non-reproducible constraint: use only in development.
tensor_equation: ∀constraint: semantics(constraint) = (upToNextMajor → x ≤ v < x+1) ∨ (exact → v = x) ∨ (branch → non_reproducible)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.5 Swift Package Manager. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Set a dependency to 'branch("main")' and build on two different days. If the resolved version differs between builds, the invariant is violated. Run 'xcodebuild -resolvePackageDependencies' and check for non-reproducible resolution.

title: XCTest isolation and lifecycle
prompt_injection: ∀test T: setUp() runs before T, tearDown() runs after T. Tests are isolated — the state of one test must not leak into another test. ∀state_leak: test_order_dependency → flaky tests.
tensor_equation: ∀test: setUp_precedes(test) ∧ tearDown_follows(test) ∧ state(test_n) ∩ state(test_{n+1}) = ∅
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.6 XCTest. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Write two XCTest tests where the first modifies a shared static variable and the second reads it. Run the tests in reverse order. If the second test produces different results depending on order, test isolation is violated.

title: ARC retain count invariant
prompt_injection: ∀reference R to object O: retain_count(O) ≥ number_of_strong_references(O). ∀retain_cycle: if A retains B and B retains A, then neither A nor B is freed → leak. strong references increment retain count; weak references do not; unowned references do not but crash on post-deallocation access.
tensor_equation: ∀R, O: retain_count(O) ≥ |{strong_refs(O)}| ∧ retain_cycle(A,B) → ¬freed(A) ∧ ¬freed(B)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.1 Memory Management — ARC. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create two classes that hold strong references to each other. Allocate them and verify that deinit is never called using Instruments Leaks or Memory Graph Debugger. If both are deallocated, the invariant is violated.

title: ARC memory deallocation determinism
prompt_injection: ∀object O with ARC: O is deallocated deterministically when retain_count(O) reaches zero. Unlike garbage collection, ARC deallocation timing is predictable and known at the point the last strong reference is released. This is NOT garbage collection.
tensor_equation: ∀O: dealloc(O) ↔ retain_count(O) = 0 ∧ deterministic_timing(O) ≠ GC_timing
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.1 Memory Management — ARC. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create an object and release all references to it in a tight loop. Verify that deinit is called immediately (synchronously) after the last reference is released. If deinit is delayed, the deterministic timing invariant is violated.

title: MainActor thread enforcement
prompt_injection: ∀@MainActor function f: f executes on the main thread. The compiler enforces this — calling a MainActor function from a background context requires await. The compiler rejects direct calls from non-main-actor contexts.
tensor_equation: ∀f annotated @MainActor: thread(f) = main_thread ∧ compiler_rejects(direct_call(f, background_context))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.2 Concurrency. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Write a @MainActor function and call it directly (without await) from a non-MainActor context. The compiler should reject this with a build error. If it compiles, the invariant is violated.

title: Actor mutual exclusion
prompt_injection: ∀Actor A: at most one task executes on A at any time. This is mutual exclusion by construction — the compiler prevents data races by making actor state access go through the actor's serial executor. All actor state access is async.
tensor_equation: ∀Actor A: |{tasks_executing_on(A)}| ≤ 1 ∧ all_access_async(A)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.2 Concurrency. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create an actor with a mutable property. Spawn two concurrent tasks that both mutate the property. Run Thread Sanitizer. If a data race is detected, the actor mutual exclusion invariant is violated.

title: Swift task cooperative cancellation
prompt_injection: ∀Task T: T can be cancelled. Cancellation is cooperative — the child task must check Task.isCancelled or call Task.checkCancellation(). Cancellation does not force-stop; it requests. ∀task_ignoring_cancellation: task continues until completion.
tensor_equation: ∀Task: cancellable(T) ∧ cooperative(T) ∧ checkCancellation(T) → stop(T) ∧ ¬checkCancellation(T) → continue(T)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.2 Concurrency. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create a long-running task that does not check for cancellation. Cancel the task from the parent. If the task continues running to completion despite cancellation, the cooperative cancellation invariant is demonstrated. If it stops, the OS enforces forced cancellation, which violates cooperativity.

title: Sendable type safety across concurrency domains
prompt_injection: ∀type T crossing concurrency domains: T must conform to Sendable. Value types (structs, enums) are Sendable by default. Classes must be explicitly marked @unchecked Sendable or be immutable. The compiler prevents passing non-Sendable types across actor boundaries.
tensor_equation: ∀T: cross_concurrency(T) → Sendable(T) ∧ (value_type(T) → Sendable_default(T)) ∧ (class(T) → explicit_Sendable(T) ∨ immutable(T))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.2 Concurrency. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Attempt to pass a non-Sendable class instance across an actor boundary. The compiler should reject it. If it compiles and runs, the Sendable invariant is violated.

title: Protocol compile-time contract enforcement
prompt_injection: ∀protocol P: the set of requirements in P is the minimum contract for conformance. Protocol conformance is checked at compile time. No runtime type-checking is needed for protocol conformance (unlike Go interface conversion which can panic at runtime).
tensor_equation: ∀protocol P: requirements(P) = minimum_contract ∧ compile_time_check(conformance) ∧ ¬runtime_panic(conversion)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.3 Protocols. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Define a protocol with requirements and create a type that claims conformance without implementing all requirements. The compiler should produce a build error. If it compiles, the compile-time contract enforcement invariant is violated.

title: Typed error handling completeness
prompt_injection: ∀throwing function f (Swift 6+): f's error type is part of the interface. Callers must handle or propagate every error case. 'try?' converts errors to nil, silently dropping error information — use only for best-effort operations. 'try!' crashes on error — use only when error is provably impossible.
tensor_equation: ∀f throws E: error_type(E) ∈ interface(f) ∧ handled_or_propagated(E) ∧ (try? → silent_drop) ∧ (try! → crash_on_error)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.4 Error Handling. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Define a function that throws NetworkError. Call it with 'try?' and do not handle the error. If the error is silently dropped and the program continues with nil instead of propagating the error, the invariant is violated. Verify with a network operation that returns nil on error.

title: Property wrapper compile-time guarantee
prompt_injection: ∀property wrapper W: W.wrappedValue access goes through W's get/set. The compiler synthesizes the backing storage. The property wrapper's semantics are compile-time guaranteed — the transformation is applied before runtime.
tensor_equation: ∀W: access(wrappedValue) → get/set(W) ∧ compiler_synthesizes(storage) ∧ compile_time_guarantee(semantics(W))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 2.5 Property Wrappers. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create a custom property wrapper with a non-trivial get/set. Assign a value and read it back. If the read value bypasses the wrapper's getter, the compile-time guarantee is violated. Verify by inspecting the SIL (Swift Intermediate Language) output.

title: SwiftUI View body is a pure function of state
prompt_injection: ∀View V: V.body is a pure function of the view's state. Side effects in body are forbidden — body can be called multiple times during layout. Network calls or file I/O in body result in N redundant calls, where N is the number of body invocations.
tensor_equation: ∀View V: body(V) = f(state(V)) ∧ pure(f) ∧ side_effects_in_body → redundant_calls
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.1 SwiftUI. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Add a print() or network call inside a SwiftUI body property. Run the app and observe how many times the body is called during a single state change. If body is called multiple times, the side-effect-is-bad invariant is demonstrated. If body is called exactly once, the pure function invariant may be violated.

title: SwiftUI incremental view tree diffing
prompt_injection: ∀state_change: SwiftUI recomputes body and diffs the view tree. ∀diff: only changed views are re-rendered, not the entire tree. The framework identifies the minimal set of views that need updating based on the diff between old and new view tree.
tensor_equation: ∀state_change: recompute(body) ∧ diff(old_tree, new_tree) ∧ re_render(changed_views_only) ∧ ¬re_render(unchanged_views)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.1 SwiftUI. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Add a custom view with a print in its body initializer. Change a state variable that only affects a sibling view. If the print fires for the unchanged view, the incremental diffing invariant is violated. Use Instruments signposts to confirm view tree diffing scope.

title: UIKit/AppKit main thread requirement
prompt_injection: ∀UI operation in UIKit/AppKit: the operation MUST be executed on the main thread. UIKit and AppKit are not thread-safe. Any UI operation from a background thread causes undefined behavior, including crashes, visual corruption, or data inconsistency.
tensor_equation: ∀ui_op: thread(ui_op) = main_thread ∧ ¬thread_safe(UIKit) ∧ background_ui_op → undefined_behavior
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.2 UIKit/AppKit. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Dispatch a UI frame/bounds update from a background queue using DispatchQueue.global(). Run Thread Sanitizer. If a warning is produced, the main thread invariant is enforced. If no warning and the app does not crash, UIKit may be more thread-safe than claimed.

title: UIKit view lifecycle sequence
prompt_injection: ∀view in UIKit lifecycle: sequence is init → loadView → viewDidLoad → viewWillAppear → viewDidAppear → viewWillDisappear → viewDidDisappear → deinit. This sequence is deterministic and guaranteed by the framework. Any deviation breaks the view lifecycle contract.
tensor_equation: ∀view: lifecycle(view) = [init, loadView, viewDidLoad, viewWillAppear, viewDidAppear, viewWillDisappear, viewDidDisappear, deinit] ∧ deterministic(sequence)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.2 UIKit/AppKit. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Override all view lifecycle methods with print statements. Push and pop a view controller. If the lifecycle methods are called out of order, or if any method is called multiple times without its counterpart, the deterministic lifecycle invariant is violated.

title: Background task finite time budget
prompt_injection: ∀background task T: T has a finite time budget. When the budget expires, T is suspended by the OS. ∀BackgroundTasks framework task: must call setTaskCompleted() when done. Failure to call setTaskCompleted() results in the task being killed or the budget being exhausted.
tensor_equation: ∀background_task T: finite_budget(T) ∧ expire(budget) → suspended(T) ∧ ∀BT_task: setTaskCompleted() → done(BT_task) ∧ ¬setTaskCompleted() → killed_or_exhausted(BT_task)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.3 Background Tasks. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Register a background task that does not call setTaskCompleted(). Run the app, background it, and measure whether the task is killed after the budget expires. If the task runs indefinitely, the finite budget invariant is violated.

title: Accessibility attributes on every view
prompt_injection: ∀view: has an accessibilityLabel, accessibilityHint, and accessibilityValue. If VoiceOver cannot navigate the app, the app is broken. Accessibility is not optional — it is a platform contract enforced by the framework and App Store review.
tensor_equation: ∀view: has(view, accessibilityLabel) ∧ has(view, accessibilityHint) ∧ has(view, accessibilityValue) ∧ ¬navigable_by_VoiceOver → broken(app)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 3.4 Accessibility. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Enable VoiceOver and navigate through the app. If any interactive element is not reachable or does not announce its purpose, the accessibility invariant is violated. Use Xcode Accessibility Inspector to programmatically verify all views have labels.

title: Sandboxed app filesystem access constraint
prompt_injection: ∀file_access from sandboxed app A to file F: A has an entitlement for the path of F OR the user explicitly granted access via NSOpenPanel powerbox. Without either condition, the access is denied by the sandbox kernel extension. The sandbox limits filesystem access to the app's container.
tensor_equation: ∀A, F: access(A, F) ↔ entitlement(A, path(F)) ∨ powerbox_grant(A, F) ∧ ¬entitlement ∧ ¬powerbox → denied(access)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 4.1 Sandbox. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Create a sandboxed app that attempts to read a file outside its container without an entitlement or powerbox grant. The read should fail with EPERM. If the read succeeds, the sandbox constraint is violated. Verify with sandbox-exec or by running the app with App Sandbox enabled.

title: Capability three-part gate
prompt_injection: ∀capability C (camera, microphone, location, health data, network): compile-time entitlement in Entitlements.plist AND build-time usage description string in Info.plist AND runtime permission granted via system dialog → capability available. Missing any of these three = capability denied.
tensor_equation: ∀capability C: available(C) ↔ entitlement(C) ∧ plist_string(C) ∧ runtime_grant(C). Missing any one → denied(C)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 4.2 Entitlements. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Add an entitlement for camera access but omit the usage description string from Info.plist. Build and run the app. If the app can access the camera without the Info.plist string, the three-part gate invariant is violated. Test each missing part independently.

title: Provisioning profile structure as trust document
prompt_injection: ∀provisioning_profile P: P = (app_id, team_id, devices, entitlements, expiration, certificate_chain). The provisioning profile is a signed ASN.1 document that encodes the complete set of permissions for a build. Each field is a constraint — the app must match all of them at launch.
tensor_equation: ∀profile P: fields(P) = {app_id, team_id, devices, entitlements, expiration, cert_chain} ∧ launch_matches(P) → allowed ∧ mismatch_any → denied
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.3 Code Signing. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Decode a provisioning profile with 'security cms -D -i profile.mobileprovision'. Modify the profile's device list to remove the current device. Attempt to launch the app. If the launch succeeds, the provisioning profile trust invariant is violated.

title: Build settings inheritance chain
prompt_injection: ∀build_setting S: inheritance_order is .xcconfig → Target → Project → Default (Xcode defaults). The resolved value of S is determined by the first match in this chain. Understanding this chain explains why a setting has the value it does.
tensor_equation: ∀S: resolved_value(S) = first_match(.xcconfig, Target, Project, Default) ∧ precedence(xcconfig) > precedence(Target) > precedence(Project) > precedence(Default)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.1 Build System. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Set the same build setting at all four levels (xcconfig, target, project, default) with different values. Run 'xcodebuild -showBuildSettings'. If the resolved value does not match the xcconfig value, the inheritance chain invariant is violated.

title: Xcode build pipeline stage sequence
prompt_injection: ∀Xcode build: pipeline_sequence = [Source → Swift Compiler → LLVM IR → Machine Code → Linker → Binary → Code Sign → Package → Deploy]. Each stage is a transform with specific inputs and outputs. The pipeline is deterministic and sequential.
tensor_equation: ∀build: pipeline = [Source, swiftc, LLVM_IR, llc, Machine_Code, ld, Binary, codesign, Package, Deploy] ∧ deterministic_and_sequential(pipeline)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.1 Build System. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Run xcodebuild with verbose logging (-verbose). Verify that the pipeline stages execute in the documented order. If any stage is skipped or executed out of order, the pipeline sequence invariant is violated.

title: Code signing certificate chain trust hierarchy
prompt_injection: ∀signed binary B: the trust chain is Developer Certificate ← Apple CA (intermediate) ← Apple Root CA. The Developer Certificate is issued by the Apple CA, which is issued by the Apple Root CA. This forms a directed acyclic graph of trust ending at the Apple Root CA.
tensor_equation: ∀binary: chain(binary) = Developer_Cert → Apple_CA → Apple_Root_CA ∧ acyclic(chain) ∧ root(Apple_Root_CA)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 1.3 Code Signing. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Self-sign a binary with a certificate not issued by the Apple CA chain. Attempt to launch it. Gatekeeper should block the launch. If the launch succeeds, the trust chain invariant is violated.

title: Launch watchdog timeout
prompt_injection: ∀app launch: the app must finish launching within approximately 20 seconds or the OS kills it via watchdog. This applies to both iOS and macOS. The watchdog is a kernel-level enforcement mechanism that prevents hung apps from blocking the user indefinitely.
tensor_equation: ∀launch: finish_launching(app) ≤ 20s ∧ ¬finish_within_20s → killed_by_watchdog(app)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md Part 4.3 Launch Constraints. Full citation in source-cache/apple-platform-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Insert a 30-second sleep in application(_:didFinishLaunchingWithOptions:). Launch the app. If the app is not killed by the OS after 20 seconds, the watchdog timeout invariant is violated. Verify by checking crash logs for watchdog termination.

title: No syscall passthrough
prompt_injection:
∀ syscall s issued by sandboxed application A:
  s is intercepted by the Sentry, handled entirely within Sentry code,
  and does NOT result in a host syscall of the same type being issued with A's arguments.
tensor_equation:
∀ syscall s issued by sandboxed application A:
  s is intercepted by the Sentry, handled entirely within Sentry code,
  and does NOT result in a host syscall of the same type being issued with A's arguments.
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'No system call is passed through directly to the host' (Intro page, Security Model Principle #1)
redteam_pattern: Run a sandboxed binary that invokes ioctl(2) and ptrace(2) with known-valid arguments. Trace host syscalls with strace on the sandbox process. If either syscall appears in the host trace with the sandboxed application's arguments, the invariant is violated — the host kernel's attack surface for those syscalls is reachable from within the sandbox.

title: Sentry host syscall surface is exhaustively enumerated and minimized
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'The sandbox is not permitted to open new files, create new sockets.' (Security Model, Goals: Limiting Exposure, Principle #3)
redteam_pattern: Read the Sentry's seccomp-bpf filter at startup via /proc/<pid>/seccomp. Decompile the BPF bytecode and verify that socket(2), connect(2), exec(2), accept(2), and open(2) are explicitly denied (return SECCOMP_RET_KILL or ERRNO). If any of these syscalls are absent from the filter (i.e., not trapped), they would default to allow on older kernels, violating the invariant.

title: Privilege drop before untrusted code execution
prompt_injection:
∀ sandbox lifecycle:
  setup phases may use elevated privileges,
  but before_any_untrusted_code_runs => privileges_dropped ∧ user_namespace_isolated
tensor_equation:
∀ sandbox lifecycle:
  setup phases may use elevated privileges,
  but before_any_untrusted_code_runs => privileges_dropped ∧ user_namespace_isolated
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'Once the sandbox setup is complete, gVisor re-executes itself and drops all privileges in the process. This takes place before any untrusted code runs.' (Intro page)
redteam_pattern: Inject a ptrace breakpoint or uprobe at the function that drops privileges (e.g., setuid/setgid or user-namespace-creation call). Attach to the Sentry process and log the timestamp of privilege drop vs. the timestamp of the first application-level instruction. If application code is observed executing before the privilege drop completes, the invariant is violated.

title: Unsafe code isolation in Go
prompt_injection:
∀ Go source file f in the Sentry:
  f uses package "unsafe" ⟹ f.ends_with("unsafe.go") ∧
  f does NOT use package "unsafe" ⟹ f does NOT end with "unsafe.go"
tensor_equation:
∀ Go source file f in the Sentry:
  f uses package "unsafe" ⟹ f.ends_with("unsafe.go") ∧
  f does NOT use package "unsafe" ⟹ f does NOT end with "unsafe.go"
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'All unsafe code is isolated in files that end with "unsafe.go", in order to facilitate validation and auditing. No file without the unsafe suffix may import the unsafe package.' (Security Model)
redteam_pattern: Run `grep -r 'import.*"unsafe"'` across the Sentry source tree, then inspect each matching file's name. If any file that imports unsafe does not end with 'unsafe.go', or any file ending with 'unsafe.go' does not import unsafe at all, the invariant is violated. Also grep for `unsafe.Pointer` or `reflect.SliceHeader` usages in non-unsafe.go files which would indicate the invariant is bypassed.

title: No CGo in Sentry
prompt_injection:
∀ Go packages in the Sentry core:
  import "C" is forbidden ∧ CGo is disabled
tensor_equation:
∀ Go packages in the Sentry core:
  import "C" is forbidden ∧ CGo is disabled
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'No CGo is allowed. The Sentry must be a pure Go binary.' (Security Model, Principles: Defense-in-Depth)
redteam_pattern: Run `grep -r 'import "C"'` across the Sentry core package directory. If any Go file in the core Sentry packages contains 'import "C"', the invariant is violated. Additionally, inspect the binary for CGo symbols with `go tool nm sentry-binary | grep -E '_(CGo|_cgo_)'` — if any CGo symbols are present, the pure-Go claim is false.

title: External import control in Sentry core
prompt_injection:
∀ Go package p in Sentry core packages:
  p.imports ∩ external_dependencies = ∅
tensor_equation:
∀ Go package p in Sentry core packages:
  p.imports ∩ external_dependencies = ∅
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'External imports are not generally allowed within the core packages. Only limited external imports are used within the setup code.' (Security Model)
redteam_pattern: Run `go list -m all` on the Sentry module to enumerate all external dependencies. Then for each core Sentry package, run `go list -json` and inspect the `Imports` field. If any core package imports a path that resolves to an external module (not in the gvisor.dev/go/gvisor root), the invariant is violated. For setup/bootstrap packages, verify they are excluded from the 'core' definition.

title: Single memfd backing all application memory
prompt_injection:
∀ sandbox application memory region m:
  m is backed by exactly one host memfd ∧
  host sees all application anonymous memory as shmem (not anon) for that cgroup
tensor_equation:
∀ sandbox application memory region m:
  m is backed by exactly one host memfd ∧
  host sees all application anonymous memory as shmem (not anon) for that cgroup
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'A single memfd backs all application memory.' (Resource Model, Memory)
redteam_pattern: Read /proc/<sentry-pid>/maps and /proc/<sentry-pid>/smaps. Count the number of memfd-backed regions (/memfd:). If multiple distinct memfd inodes are backing application memory, or if there are anonymous (anon) regions of comparable size that should be application memory, the invariant is violated. Also check memory.stat in the sandbox's cgroup — verify that shmem accounts for the application memory and anon does not.

title: Sandbox time isolation
prompt_injection:
∀ time value t returned to sandboxed application:
  t originates from Sentry's own vDSO/time-keeping implementation ∧
  t does NOT share state with host clock after initialization
tensor_equation:
∀ time value t returned to sandboxed application:
  t originates from Sentry's own vDSO/time-keeping implementation ∧
  t does NOT share state with host clock after initialization
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'Time in the sandbox is provided by the Sentry, through its own vDSO and time-keeping implementation. This is distinct from the host time, and no state is shared with the host' (Resource Model, Time)
redteam_pattern: Run a sandboxed application that calls clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC) in a tight loop and logs the values. Simultaneously, on the host, adjust the system clock (e.g., via adjtimex or clock_settime). If the sandboxed application observes the host clock adjustment, the invariant is violated — time state is shared. Also check if the sandboxed application can write to /dev/ptp or adjtimex to adjust time; if it can, the isolation is bidirectional and broken.

title: Tickless idle — zero CPU when no application threads are active
prompt_injection:
∀ sandbox state where all_application_threads_idle:
  Sentry disables all timers ∧ Sentry CPU usage ≈ 0
tensor_equation:
∀ sandbox state where all_application_threads_idle:
  Sentry disables all timers ∧ Sentry CPU usage ≈ 0
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'When all application threads are idle, the Sentry disables timers until an event occurs that wakes either the Sentry or an application thread, similar to a tickless kernel.' (Resource Model, Time)
redteam_pattern: Run a sandbox, start an application thread that pauses (e.g., pause() syscall or sleep(1000)), then sample the Sentry host process CPU usage every 100ms for 30 seconds using `ps -p <pid> -o %cpu`. If CPU usage is consistently above 0.5% when the application thread is blocked, the invariant is violated. Also check /proc/<pid>/timers to see if any timers remain armed after the application thread goes idle.

title: Sentry cannot open host sockets
prompt_injection:
∀ socket operations:
  Sentry.may_create_socket() = false (unless host networking mode is enabled) ∧
  Sentry receives file descriptors only via SCM_RIGHTS from Gofer
tensor_equation:
∀ socket operations:
  Sentry.may_create_socket() = false (unless host networking mode is enabled) ∧
  Sentry receives file descriptors only via SCM_RIGHTS from Gofer
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'The sentry, which for security cannot open host sockets of its own, is initialized with a single AF_PACKET socket.' (Networking Guide, Resource Model)
redteam_pattern: Trace the Sentry process with strace -e socket,connect,bind,listen,accept and run a sandboxed application that attempts to create a network connection. If any socket(2), connect(2), bind(2), listen(2), or accept(2) syscall appears on the Sentry's host syscall trace (except for the single AF_PACKET socket at initialization), the invariant is violated. Also inspect the Sentry's file descriptors via /proc/<pid>/fd and check for socket inodes that were not received via SCM_RIGHTS from the Gofer.

title: Process isolation — sandboxed processes invisible to host
prompt_injection:
∀ process p running inside sandbox S:
  p does NOT appear in host's process table (e.g., /proc, ps, top)
tensor_equation:
∀ process p running inside sandbox S:
  p does NOT appear in host's process table (e.g., /proc, ps, top)
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'Processes within the sandbox do not manifest as processes on the host system, and process-level interactions within the sandbox require entering the sandbox.' (Resource Model, Processes)
redteam_pattern: Run a sandbox that spawns multiple application processes (e.g., fork-bomb inside the sandbox, or simply a shell that runs `sleep 3600 &`). On the host, run `ps auxf` and `ls /proc/ | grep -E '^[0-9]+$'` before and after. If any new process appears in the host process table that corresponds to the sandboxed workload, the invariant is violated. Also attempt `kill -SIGSTOP <potential-pid>` from the host to verify no host PID corresponds to sandboxed processes.

title: Network resource containment
prompt_injection:
∀ network state s (sockets, routing tables, connection tracking, port bindings):
  s exists only within the sandbox's netstack ∧
  s does NOT appear in host network state (except packets in flight on virtual devices)
tensor_equation:
∀ network state s (sockets, routing tables, connection tracking, port bindings):
  s exists only within the sandbox's netstack ∧
  s does NOT appear in host network state (except packets in flight on virtual devices)
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'All network resources, other than packets in flight on the host, exist only inside the sandbox.' (Resource Model, Networking)
redteam_pattern: From inside a sandbox, create a raw socket, bind to a port, and establish a TCP connection. On the host, run `ss -tlnp`, `ss -ulnp`, `ip route show`, and `conntrack -L`. If any of the sandbox's sockets, routing table entries, or connection tracking state appears in the host output (beyond the single virtual device endpoint), the invariant is violated. Run two sandboxes concurrently and verify from each that the other sandbox's network state is invisible.

title: Memory release on madvise
prompt_injection:
∀ memory region r where application calls madvise(DONTNEED/FREE):
  Sentry releases r back to host immediately ∧
  r can be reclaimed by host for other uses
tensor_equation:
∀ memory region r where application calls madvise(DONTNEED/FREE):
  Sentry releases r back to host immediately ∧
  r can be reclaimed by host for other uses
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'When an application marks a region of memory as no longer needed, for example via a call to madvise, the Sentry releases this memory back to the host.' (Resource Model, Physical memory)
redteam_pattern: Inside a sandbox, allocate a large memory region (e.g., 1GB mmap), then call madvise(MADV_DONTNEED) on it. Measure the sandbox cgroup's memory usage via memory.stat (or /proc/<sentry-pid>/smaps) before allocation, after allocation, and after madvise. If the post-madvise RSS is not within 5% of the pre-allocation baseline, the memory is not being released back to the host and the invariant is violated.

title: Dual-kernel exploit requirement
prompt_injection:
∀ sandbox escape:
  attacker must exploit BOTH the Sentry (Go) ∧ the host Linux kernel (C) ∧
  these attack surfaces share zero code paths
tensor_equation:
∀ sandbox escape:
  attacker must exploit BOTH the Sentry (Go) ∧ the host Linux kernel (C) ∧
  these attack surfaces share zero code paths
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'In order to break out of a gVisor sandbox, an attacker would need to simultaneously exploit the gVisor Sentry kernel AND the host Linux kernel, which do not share any code.' (Intro page)
redteam_pattern: Audit all shared library dependencies of the Sentry binary with `ldd` and `nm -D`. If any shared library (e.g., libc, libpthread) is used by both the Sentry and the host kernel (via a kernel module), identify the shared code paths. Additionally, verify that the Sentry's Go runtime does not call into any C library functions that the host kernel also uses (e.g., vsyscall page entries). If a single vulnerability could compromise both the Sentry and the host kernel via shared code, the invariant is violated.

title: Specialized API non-implementation
prompt_injection:
∀ kernel feature f that is specialized (raw sockets, ioctls, extended attributes, module-specific):
  Sentry does NOT implement f ∧ Sentry does NOT pass through f to host
tensor_equation:
∀ kernel feature f that is specialized (raw sockets, ioctls, extended attributes, module-specific):
  Sentry does NOT implement f ∧ Sentry does NOT pass through f to host
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'Only common, universal functionality is implemented... we do not implement or pass through these specialized APIs.' (Security Model, Principle #2)
redteam_pattern: Enumerate all syscall numbers the Sentry handles by cross-referencing the Sentry syscall table. Verify that ioctl, the raw socket family (socket(AF_PACKET/SOCK_RAW)), and extended attribute syscalls (setxattr, getxattr, listxattr, removexattr) are either unimplemented (return ENOSYS) or blocked. Then from inside a sandbox, attempt each of these specialized syscalls and verify the return value is ENOSYS (or the process is killed). If any specialized syscall succeeds or is passed through to the host, the invariant is violated.

title: Platform interception completeness
prompt_injection:
∀ sandboxed execution on platform P:
  P intercepts ALL syscalls AND page faults from sandboxed code ∧
  no execution path reaches host kernel directly from sandboxed code
tensor_equation:
∀ sandboxed execution on platform P:
  P intercepts ALL syscalls AND page faults from sandboxed code ∧
  no execution path reaches host kernel directly from sandboxed code
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'gVisor requires a platform to implement interception of syscalls, basic context switching, and memory mapping functionality.' (Platforms page, Intro page)
redteam_pattern: From inside a sandbox, invoke every syscall number from 0 to 450 (the Linux syscall table range) using a custom assembly program that directly triggers `syscall` or `int 0x80` instructions. Use `perf trace` on the host to monitor which syscalls reach the host kernel.
