If any syscall number that the Sentry claims to handle reaches the host kernel directly (rather than being intercepted by the Sentry's platform), the invariant is violated. Also attempt to trigger page faults (e.g., by accessing unmapped memory) and verify they are handled by the Sentry, not by the host kernel's SIGSEGV handler.

title: Sentry cannot open host sockets — single AF_PACKET socket
prompt_injection:
∀ socket s created by sentry process:
  s.type = AF_PACKET
  ∧ s is the socket the sentry was initialized with
  ∧ sentry does not call socket(), accept(), or connect() syscalls on the host

Exception: when --network=host is enabled, hostinet bypasses this entirely
tensor_equation:
∀ socket s created by sentry process:
  s.type = AF_PACKET
  ∧ s is the socket the sentry was initialized with
  ∧ sentry does not call socket(), accept(), or connect() syscalls on the host

Exception: when --network=host is enabled, hostinet bypasses this entirely
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'The sentry, which for security cannot open host sockets of its own, is initialized with a single AF_PACKET socket.' (Networking guide)
redteam_pattern: Attacker compromises sentry and attempts to call socket(2) with AF_INET to create a TCP socket directly on the host, bypassing the AF_PACKET proxy. Verify that the seccomp-bpf filter or sentry syscall table blocks this.

title: All non-loopback traffic must transit the AF_PACKET socket
prompt_injection:
∀ packet p ingressed by or egressed from the sandbox:
  is_loopback(p) ∨ traversed(p, single_AF_PACKET_socket)
tensor_equation:
∀ packet p ingressed by or egressed from the sandbox:
  is_loopback(p) ∨ traversed(p, single_AF_PACKET_socket)
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'gVisor ingresses and egresses all non-loopback traffic across that socket.' (Networking guide)
redteam_pattern: Pass a connected TCP socket FD into the sandboxed application via SCM_RIGHTS and verify that the application can send/receive traffic without the data ever traversing the sentry's AF_PACKET socket, thereby bypassing network policy enforcement.

title: Address/route mirroring — sentry routing table ≡ VETH device config
prompt_injection:
∀ address a on virtual network device in sentry's netns:
  a ∈ sentry_internal_routing_table
∀ route r on virtual network device in sentry's netns:
  r ∈ sentry_internal_routing_table

The converse is NOT guaranteed: sentry may have internal-only routes
for virtual networks not exposed on the VETH.
tensor_equation:
∀ address a on virtual network device in sentry's netns:
  a ∈ sentry_internal_routing_table
∀ route r on virtual network device in sentry's netns:
  r ∈ sentry_internal_routing_table

The converse is NOT guaranteed: sentry may have internal-only routes
for virtual networks not exposed on the VETH.
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'gVisor scrapes addresses, routes, and the like from those devices and configures the sentry to use those same addresses and routes.' (Networking guide)
redteam_pattern: Add a secondary IP address to the VETH device after sentry startup (e.g., via CNI plugin) and verify that the sentry cannot route to or bind to this new address, proving the route table is a startup-time snapshot not a live mirror.

title: No system call pass-through — sentry reimplements every syscall
prompt_injection:
∀ syscall σ that the application invokes:
  handler(σ) ∈ sentry_implementation
  ∧ handler(σ) ∉ {direct_host_passthrough}

Consequence: if a kernel feature has no sentry implementation, it is UNAVAILABLE
to the sandboxed application.
tensor_equation:
∀ syscall σ that the application invokes:
  handler(σ) ∈ sentry_implementation
  ∧ handler(σ) ∉ {direct_host_passthrough}

Consequence: if a kernel feature has no sentry implementation, it is UNAVAILABLE
to the sandboxed application.
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'No system call is passed through directly to the host. Every supported call has an independent implementation in the Sentry' (Security Model)
redteam_pattern: From within the sandboxed application, invoke a syscall (e.g., kexec_load, bpf, or finit_module) known to be unimplemented in sentry and verify that it returns ENOSYS rather than being passed through to the host kernel. Then attempt a TOCTOU attack: race the sentry's argument validation against the host kernel's argument processing to confirm the sentry's reimplementation prevents the race window.

title: Outbound packets pass through a queueing discipline before link egress
prompt_injection:
∀ outbound packet p:
  enqueued(p, qdisc) → dequeued(p, qdisc) → written(p, link_endpoint)

Default qdisc = FIFO ⇒ ∀ packets p₁, p₂ where enqueue(p₁) happens-before enqueue(p₂):
  dequeue(p₁) happens-before dequeue(p₂)
tensor_equation:
∀ outbound packet p:
  enqueued(p, qdisc) → dequeued(p, qdisc) → written(p, link_endpoint)

Default qdisc = FIFO ⇒ ∀ packets p₁, p₂ where enqueue(p₁) happens-before enqueue(p₂):
  dequeue(p₁) happens-before dequeue(p₂)
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'Outgoing packets...until typically reaching a queueing discipline. There another goroutine writes batches of queued packets out the link endpoint. The default qdisc is FIFO' (Networking guide)
redteam_pattern: Send multiple packets from different goroutines to the same destination and instrument the egress path to detect if any packet is written directly to the link endpoint without going through the qdisc. Verify FIFO ordering by sequencing outbound packets with monotonically increasing IDs and checking for reordering on the receiver.

title: Host networking bypasses ALL netstack isolation
prompt_injection:
network_mode = host ⇒
  ¬INV-NET-001 ∧ ¬INV-NET-002 ∧ ¬INV-NET-004

i.e., with --network=host:
  - sentry CAN open host sockets (hostinet)
  - traffic does NOT transit AF_PACKET socket
  - syscalls use native Linux networking, not sentry emulation
tensor_equation:
network_mode = host ⇒
  ¬INV-NET-001 ∧ ¬INV-NET-002 ∧ ¬INV-NET-004

i.e., with --network=host:
  - sentry CAN open host sockets (hostinet)
  - traffic does NOT transit AF_PACKET socket
  - syscalls use native Linux networking, not sentry emulation
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'gVisor can also be run with host networking via the --network=host flag. This uses the hostinet package, which trades the security and isolation of netstack for the performance of native Linux networking.' (Networking guide, Security Model)

title: TCP async processing ≠ other protocols inline processing
prompt_injection:
∀ incoming packet p:
  protocol(p) = TCP ⇒ handled_async(p)  // enqueued, processed by TCP's own goroutines
  protocol(p) ≠ TCP ⇒ handled_inline(p)  // processed in dispatcher goroutine

This is an architectural choice, not a correctness invariant:
TCP requires complex state machines (congestion control, retransmit timers);
other protocols (UDP, ICMP, raw IP) are stateless enough for inline dispatch.

Measure head-of-line blocking: saturate TCP processing and verify that non-TCP packets are not delayed by the TCP processing pipeline.

  compromise(L_i) ⟹ ¬compromise(L_j) for j ≠ i

title: ptrace SYSEMU: never-execute-in-host guarantee
prompt_injection:
Ptrace_platform:
  Uses PTRACE_SYSEMU (NOT PTRACE_SYSCALL)
  PTRACE_SYSEMU semantics:
    When tracee reaches a syscall instruction → kernel stops tracee BEFORE executing syscall
    Tracer reads tracee registers, determines intended syscall, emulates it
    Tracer modifies tracee registers to reflect emulated result
    Tracer resumes tracee in userspace (past the syscall instruction)
    The syscall instruction NEVER executes in the host kernel
  TOCTOU_race_impossible: no window between check and execution

  Example: read(pipe) from sandbox → futex() host syscall (Go runtime synchronization)
  Example: write(file) from sandbox → pwrite64() host syscall (on donated FD)

title: No direct system call passthrough to host

For each syscall, compare the behavior (e.g., error type, file descriptor created on host vs. sandbox) to determine if passthrough occurred. Use strace on the Sentry process to detect any unhandled syscalls reaching the host kernel.

Specifically look for open(), openat(), creat(), socket(), connect() — if any appear without host_networking or directfs mode enabled, the invariant is violated.

title: Application only manipulates virtualized resources, never host resources

  ∧ ¬∃ path(app_syscall) = app → host_kernel  [layer 1]
  ∧ surface(Sentry → host_kernel) ⊆ minimal_syscall_set  [layer 2]
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'First, the application's direct interactions with the host System API are intercepted by the Sentry... Second, the System API accessible to the Sentry itself is minimized to a safer, restricted set.' (Security Model, Goals: Limiting Exposure)
redteam_pattern: Test Layer 1: From inside the sandbox application, attempt every syscall and use strace on the host to detect if any syscall bypasses the Sentry and reaches the host kernel directly. Test Layer 2: From the Sentry process itself (via GDB attach or by compromising the Sentry in a test), enumerate all syscalls the Sentry can make and verify they are within the minimal set. If any app syscall reaches the host kernel (layer 1 failure) or the Sentry can make unvetted syscalls (layer 2 failure), the invariant is violated.

title: Sandbox is not a substitute for secure architecture
prompt_injection:
secure_system ⇒ (secure_architecture ∧ (sandbox ⇒ defense_in_depth))
sandbox ¬⇒ secure_system
tensor_equation:
secure_system ⇒ (secure_architecture ∧ (sandbox ⇒ defense_in_depth))
sandbox ¬⇒ secure_system
verdict_evidence: Source text confirms: 'An attacker need not escalate privileges within a container if there's an exploitable network-accessible service on the host or some other API path. A sandbox is not a substitute for a secure architecture.' (Security Model, Other Vectors)
redteam_pattern: Set up a host with an unpatched network-accessible service (e.g., a vulnerable HTTP server on port 443) alongside a gVisor sandboxed application. Demonstrate that the host service can be exploited remotely without ever interacting with the sandbox. This confirms that the sandbox provides no protection against host-level attack vectors. For bridge/orbit: demonstrate that a vulnerable host service can compromise bridge data even if the bridge sandbox is perfectly secure.

title: Kernighan Test — Generality: does requirement change modify code or just arguments?
prompt_injection: ∀code C, requirement change R: general(C) → change(arguments, C) ∧ specific(C) → change(code, C)
tensor_equation: ∀code C, requirement change R: general(C) → change(arguments, C) ∧ specific(C) → change(code, C)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md — The Kernighan Test, line 247. Full citation in source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Given a function that hardcodes a retry count of 3, change the requirement to retry 5 times. If the code changes (not just the argument), the function is too specific. Refactor to make retry count a parameter.

title: Kernighan Test — Interface: what does the caller NOT need to know? Is it hidden?
prompt_injection: ∀interface I: kernighan_interface(I) ↔ hidden(implementation(I), caller)
tensor_equation: ∀interface I: kernighan_interface(I) ↔ hidden(implementation(I), caller)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md — The Kernighan Test, line 248. Full citation in source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Review a public API. For each method, ask: 'Does the caller need to know about this?' If the answer is no, the method should be private or the interface should be split.

title: Kernighan Test — Debugging: if this code has a bug, how would I find it?
prompt_injection: ∀code C: debuggable(C) ↔ ∃method M: find_bug(C, M) ∧ deterministic(M)
tensor_equation: ∀code C: debuggable(C) ↔ ∃method M: find_bug(C, M) ∧ deterministic(M)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md — The Kernighan Test, line 249. Full citation in source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Given a function with side effects, globals, and complex state, ask: 'If this has a bug, how would I find it?' If the answer is 'I'd add printf everywhere', the function needs restructuring for debuggability.

title: Kernighan Test — Testing: is there a test that would catch the bug I'm about to write?
prompt_injection: ∀code change C: test_exists(C) → bug_caught(C, before_deploy)
tensor_equation: ∀code change C: test_exists(C) → bug_caught(C, before_deploy)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md — The Kernighan Test, line 250. Full citation in source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Before writing a new function, write the test first. Then write the function. If the function passes the test on the first try, the test was the right one. If not, the test caught a bug early.

title: Kernighan Test — Portability: does this depend on something that might change?
prompt_injection: ∀code C: portable(C) ↔ ¬∃dependency D: depends(C, D) ∧ changes(D)
tensor_equation: ∀code C: portable(C) ↔ ¬∃dependency D: depends(C, D) ∧ changes(D)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Extracted from source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md — The Kernighan Test, line 251. Full citation in source-cache/kernighan-plaughter-oracle.md.
redteam_pattern: Review code for hardcoded paths, OS-specific calls, environment variables, and implicit network dependencies. For each, ask: 'Could this change?' If yes, abstract it.

title: Kernighan Test — Notation: am I thinking in the right language?
prompt_injection: ∀problem P: right_language(P) → solution_is_obvious(P) ∧ wrong_language(P) → solution_is_opaque(P)

redteam_pattern: Given a state machine implemented as nested if-else in Go, rewrite it as a state diagram, then as a Go struct with explicit state transitions. Verify the struct-based approach is clearer than the if-else maze.

title: Side-Effect-Free Read Detection — Allow() must not mutate state during filtering
prompt_injection: ∀function f: name(f) = Allow ∧ called_during_filtering → side_effect_free(f)
tensor_equation: ∀function f: name(f) = Allow ∧ called_during_filtering → side_effect_free(f)

redteam_pattern: In a circuit breaker Pick() function, call Allow() to filter backends. Verify that Allow() does not mutate breaker state during the filtering pass — state mutations should only happen on the selected backend after filtering completes.

title: Lazy Expiry Must Explicitly Check — expired state must be zeroed, not deferred
prompt_injection: ∀expired state E: lazy(E) → check(expiry, E) ∧ zero(E, on_acquisition)
tensor_equation: ∀expired state E: lazy(E) → check(expiry, E) ∧ zero(E, on_acquisition)

redteam_pattern: In a token bucket rate limiter with lazy expiry, Acquire() must check the expiry of each bucket before returning it. If the bucket is expired, zero it and return 0, not the stale value. Write a test that proves the stale value is never returned.

title: Path Resolution Must Verify Containment — symlink resolution must be checked against root
prompt_injection: ∀path P, root R: resolve(P) → verify(contained(resolved(P), R))
tensor_equation: ∀path P, root R: resolve(P) → verify(contained(resolved(P), R))

redteam_pattern: Create a sandbox root with a symlink pointing outside the root. Call resolve() on the symlink path. Verify that resolve() returns an error, not a path outside the root. The fix: call filepath.EvalSymlinks AND verify the resolved path is within root.

title: Specification Before Implementation
prompt_injection: ∀system S: spec(S) is written before code(S). The spec describes WHAT the system does (its behavior), not HOW it does it. The implementation is a refinement of the spec. If the spec is wrong, the implementation is wrong by definition.
tensor_equation: ∀system S: spec(S) is written before code(S). The spec describes WHAT the system does (its behavior), not HOW it does it. The implementation is a refinement of the spec. If the spec is wrong, the implementation is wrong by definition.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Write the specification FIRST. The spec describes WHAT the system does (its behavior), not HOW it does it. The implementation is a refinement of the spec. If the spec is wrong, the implementation is wrong by definition.' — Section 1: Specification Before Implementation
redteam_pattern: A developer writes code for a distributed lock without first writing a TLA+ spec. The code contains a subtle deadlock that only manifests under specific interleavings. Model-checking the spec would have found the deadlock, but because no spec exists, the bug is only discovered in production.

title: Spec Updated Before Code Change
prompt_injection: ∀change C: spec(S) is updated before code(S) is changed. If the spec is not updated first, the spec and implementation diverge, and refinement is lost.
tensor_equation: ∀change C: spec(S) is updated before code(S) is changed. If the spec is not updated first, the spec and implementation diverge, and refinement is lost.

redteam_pattern: A developer changes the circuit breaker state machine logic in Go code to add a new 'Degraded' state, but does not update the formal spec. The spec still describes a 3-state machine. The TestAX gates pass because they match the old spec, but the actual behavior has diverged. A model checker would detect the mismatch.

title: Safety as Always Invariant
prompt_injection: □P — a safety property is an invariant that must hold in every state of every behavior. Safety: □Invariant. If it is violated, the violation happens at a specific point in time. Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions.
tensor_equation: □P — a safety property is an invariant that must hold in every state of every behavior. Safety: □Invariant. If it is violated, the violation happens at a specific point in time. Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'A safety property is an invariant: it must hold in every reachable state. □P — always P — P holds in every state of every behavior. Safety: □Invariant. Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions.' — Section 2: Safety
redteam_pattern: A circuit breaker implementation allows Allow() to return true while state = Open and timeout is still active. The invariant □(state = Open ∧ timeout_active → Allow() = false) is violated. TLC model checker would produce a trace: Init → Open → Open(timeout_active) → Allow()=true → violation.

title: Safety Violation is Finite Counterexample
prompt_injection: A safety violation occurs at a specific point in time. It is a finite counterexample — a sequence of states leading to the violation state. The violation is observable at a specific, identifiable step in the behavior.
tensor_equation: A safety violation occurs at a specific point in time. It is a finite counterexample — a sequence of states leading to the violation state. The violation is observable at a specific, identifiable step in the behavior.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'A safety violation occurs at a specific point in time. It is a finite counterexample — a sequence of states leading to the violation state.' — Section 2: Safety. Also Lamport, Specifying Systems (2002)
redteam_pattern: A sandbox path resolution allows a symlink traversal to escape the worktree root. The safety violation is observable at a single step: resolve('/worktree/../etc/passwd') returns '/etc/passwd'. The invariant □(resolve(path) is within worktree root) fails at a specific call.

title: Safety is Minimum Correctness Condition
prompt_injection: Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions. If a safety property is violated, the system is broken at a specific, observable point. Every P0 and P1 finding is a safety violation. Without safety, liveness guarantees are meaningless.
tensor_equation: Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions. If a safety property is violated, the system is broken at a specific, observable point. Every P0 and P1 finding is a safety violation. Without safety, liveness guarantees are meaningless.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Safety properties are the MINIMUM correctness conditions. If a safety property is violated, the system is broken at a specific, observable point. Every P0 and P1 finding is a safety violation. Without safety, liveness guarantees are meaningless.' — Section 2: Safety
redteam_pattern: A system claims to be 'correct' because all requests eventually get responses (liveness), but the system also allows data corruption (safety violation). The liveness property is satisfied, but the system is broken because the minimum correctness condition (no data corruption) is violated.

title: Liveness as Eventually Property
prompt_injection: ◇P — a liveness property says that something eventually happens. It cannot be violated at a single point in time; it can only be violated over an infinite behavior. 'Every request eventually gets a response' is a liveness property.
tensor_equation: ◇P — a liveness property says that something eventually happens. It cannot be violated at a single point in time; it can only be violated over an infinite behavior. 'Every request eventually gets a response' is a liveness property.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'A liveness property says that something eventually happens. ◇P — eventually P — P holds at some point in every behavior. It cannot be violated at a single point in time; it can only be violated over an infinite behavior.' — Section 3: Liveness
redteam_pattern: A dispatch system never completes a specific request because the retry loop is infinite but the good event (dispatch result) never occurs. The liveness property ◇(dispatch → result) is violated over an infinite behavior. No single snapshot shows the problem — the system appears to be 'still working' forever.

title: Liveness Cannot Be Tested at a Single Point
prompt_injection: A liveness violation cannot be observed at a single point in time — it can only be observed over an infinite behavior. You cannot test for 'eventually' — you can only test for 'within N seconds'. Liveness requires temporal reasoning, not point-in-time assertions.
tensor_equation: A liveness violation cannot be observed at a single point in time — it can only be observed over an infinite behavior. You cannot test for 'eventually' — you can only test for 'within N seconds'. Liveness requires temporal reasoning, not point-in-time assertions.

You cannot test for eventually — you can only test for within N seconds. Liveness requires temporal reasoning, not point-in-time assertions.' — Section 3: Liveness
redteam_pattern: A test asserts 'the circuit breaker eventually transitions to HalfOpen' by checking the state after 5 seconds. The test passes, but under scheduler contention, the transition never happens. The test cannot distinguish between 'eventually happened' and 'happened within 5 seconds in this particular run'.

title: Weak Fairness
prompt_injection: WF_v(A) = (◇□(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). Weak Fairness: if an action is eventually always enabled, it must be executed infinitely often. If the scheduler eventually keeps the action continuously enabled, the action must eventually execute.
tensor_equation: WF_v(A) = (◇□(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). Weak Fairness: if an action is eventually always enabled, it must be executed infinitely often. If the scheduler eventually keeps the action continuously enabled, the action must eventually execute.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Weak Fairness: WF_v(A) = (◇□(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). If an action is eventually always enabled, it must be executed infinitely often.' — Section 4: Fairness
redteam_pattern: A token router has a goroutine that is continuously runnable (acquiring a key) but the Go scheduler never schedules it because other goroutines are always ready too. Under weak fairness, this should not happen — a continuously enabled action must eventually execute. Go's scheduler provides weak fairness, but if the goroutine is blocked on a mutex that is never released, the action is not enabled, so weak fairness does not apply.

title: Strong Fairness
prompt_injection: SF_v(A) = (□◇(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). Strong Fairness: if an action is repeatedly enabled (infinitely often), it must be executed infinitely often. Unlike weak fairness, strong fairness requires execution even if the action is not continuously enabled, as long as it keeps becoming enabled again.
tensor_equation: SF_v(A) = (□◇(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). Strong Fairness: if an action is repeatedly enabled (infinitely often), it must be executed infinitely often. Unlike weak fairness, strong fairness requires execution even if the action is not continuously enabled, as long as it keeps becoming enabled again.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Strong Fairness: SF_v(A) = (□◇(A enabled) ⇒ □◇(A executed)). If an action is repeatedly enabled (infinitely often), it must be executed infinitely often. Unlike weak fairness, strong fairness requires execution even if the action is not continuously enabled.' — Section 4: Fairness
redteam_pattern: A retry-with-backoff mechanism repeatedly enables a retry action (the backoff timer fires, making the retry possible), but the retry action is never executed because another goroutine always preempts it. The action is repeatedly enabled (□◇ enabled) but never executed — a violation of strong fairness. The retry never happens, violating liveness.

title: Fairness is a Scheduler Assumption
prompt_injection: Fairness is an assumption about the scheduler, not a property of the system. Without fairness, a liveness property can be violated by the scheduler simply never choosing to execute the action that would satisfy it. Fairness rules out 'the scheduler is infinitely unlucky' as a counterexample.
tensor_equation: Fairness is an assumption about the scheduler, not a property of the system. Without fairness, a liveness property can be violated by the scheduler simply never choosing to execute the action that would satisfy it. Fairness rules out 'the scheduler is infinitely unlucky' as a counterexample.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Fairness is an assumption about the scheduler, not a property of the system. Without fairness, a liveness property can be violated by the scheduler simply never choosing to execute the action that would satisfy it.' — Section 4: Fairness
redteam_pattern: A distributed system spec assumes strong fairness for a leader election action. The actual deployment uses a scheduler that does not guarantee strong fairness (e.g., a non-FIFO mutex). The liveness property 'every candidate eventually becomes leader' fails because the scheduler can repeatedly skip the candidate. The model checker would validate the spec under the fairness assumption, but the real system violates the assumption.

title: Liveness Requires Fairness
prompt_injection: Most liveness properties require at least weak fairness. Without it, any system with a loop can be 'stuck' by the scheduler never choosing the action that breaks the loop. A liveness proof without fairness assumptions is almost always incomplete.
tensor_equation: Most liveness properties require at least weak fairness. Without it, any system with a loop can be 'stuck' by the scheduler never choosing the action that breaks the loop. A liveness proof without fairness assumptions is almost always incomplete.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Most liveness properties require at least weak fairness. Without it, any system with a loop can be stuck by the scheduler never choosing the action that breaks the loop. A liveness proof without fairness assumptions is almost always incomplete.' — Section 4: Fairness
redteam_pattern: A circuit breaker spec claims ◇(Open → HalfOpen) without assuming fairness. The timeout goroutine that transitions to HalfOpen is never scheduled because the main goroutine is always busy. The liveness property fails. Adding a weak fairness assumption (the timeout goroutine is eventually always enabled) closes the gap, but the spec must document this assumption.

title: Refinement Definition
prompt_injection: ∀I,∀S: I refines S ⇔ ∀behavior b: b ∈ behaviors(I) → b ∈ behaviors(S). An implementation refines a specification if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification. The implementation can be more deterministic (fewer behaviors) but cannot introduce new behaviors that the spec forbids.
tensor_equation: ∀I,∀S: I refines S ⇔ ∀behavior b: b ∈ behaviors(I) → b ∈ behaviors(S). An implementation refines a specification if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification. The implementation can be more deterministic (fewer behaviors) but cannot introduce new behaviors that the spec forbids.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'I refines S ⇔ ∀behavior b: b ∈ behaviors(I) → b ∈ behaviors(S). An implementation refines a specification if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification.' — Section 5: Refinement
redteam_pattern: A Go implementation of the circuit breaker introduces a new behavior: Allow() returns true when state = Open if a special 'override' flag is set. The spec forbids this (□(Open → Allow() = false)). The implementation has a behavior that the spec does not allow. Refinement is violated: behaviors(I) ⊈ behaviors(S).

title: Refinement Abstraction Function
prompt_injection: The abstraction function maps the implementation's concrete state to the spec's abstract state. ∀reachable state s of I: f(s) is a spec-valid state. The invariant: the abstraction function maps reachable states to spec-valid states. This is the mechanism for proving refinement.
tensor_equation: The abstraction function maps the implementation's concrete state to the spec's abstract state. ∀reachable state s of I: f(s) is a spec-valid state. The invariant: the abstraction function maps reachable states to spec-valid states. This is the mechanism for proving refinement.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The abstraction function maps the implementation's concrete state to the spec's abstract state. ∀reachable state s of I: f(s) is a spec-valid state. This is the mechanism for proving refinement.' — Section 5: Refinement
redteam_pattern: A token router's concrete implementation has a complex bucket structure with multiple counters per key. The abstraction function maps (bucket.tokens, bucket.lastRefill) to a single 'available tokens' value. If the abstraction function is incorrect (e.g., it doesn't account for cooldown), the implementation appears to refine the spec but actually violates it under specific interleavings.

title: Stuttering Step
prompt_injection: Stuttering step: s → s (the state doesn't change). A specification allows stuttering steps at any point. This is critical: the implementation might take 3 steps to do what the spec does in 1 step. The spec allows those extra steps as stuttering. Without stuttering, refinement would be impossible (step counts would never match).
tensor_equation: Stuttering step: s → s (the state doesn't change). A specification allows stuttering steps at any point. This is critical: the implementation might take 3 steps to do what the spec does in 1 step. The spec allows those extra steps as stuttering. Without stuttering, refinement would be impossible (step counts would never match).
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Stuttering step: s → s (the state doesn't change). A specification allows stuttering steps at any point. Without stuttering, refinement would be impossible (step counts would never match).' — Section 6: Stuttering Steps
redteam_pattern: A refinement checker that requires exact step-for-step correspondence between spec and implementation would reject all real implementations. For example, the spec says 'Acquire transitions from Idle to Acquired' in one step. The implementation does: check bucket → decrement counter → update timestamp → return key. Four concrete steps for one spec step. Without stuttering, this is a refinement violation.

title: Stuttering Enables Refinement
prompt_injection: Stuttering makes refinement possible: the implementation can take more steps than the spec, as long as the extra steps don't change the observable state. ∀implementation I, ∀spec S: I refines S under stuttering if the observable behavior of I matches S after removing stuttering steps.
tensor_equation: Stuttering makes refinement possible: the implementation can take more steps than the spec, as long as the extra steps don't change the observable state. ∀implementation I, ∀spec S: I refines S under stuttering if the observable behavior of I matches S after removing stuttering steps.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Stuttering makes refinement possible: the implementation can take more steps than the spec, as long as the extra steps don't change the observable state.' — Section 6: Stuttering Steps
redteam_pattern: A developer adds logging, metrics collection, and caching to a circuit breaker implementation. These are stuttering steps — they don't change the observable state (state transitions, Allow() return values). A refinement checker that does not account for stuttering would incorrectly reject the implementation. The TestAX gates correctly check only the observable behavior, not the internal steps.

title: Spec Describes Observable Behavior
prompt_injection: The spec describes OBSERVABLE behavior, not internal steps. The implementation can add logging, metrics, caching — as long as the observable state is unchanged. A test checks the observable state, not the internal steps. TestAX tests check the invariant, not the implementation path.
tensor_equation: The spec describes OBSERVABLE behavior, not internal steps. The implementation can add logging, metrics, caching — as long as the observable state is unchanged. A test checks the observable state, not the internal steps. TestAX tests check the invariant, not the implementation path.

redteam_pattern: A TestAX test for the circuit breaker checks that the implementation calls log.Printf() before transitioning state. This is testing an internal step, not the observable behavior. The spec only requires Allow() returns false when Open. If the implementation is refactored to use structured logging, the test breaks even though the observable behavior is unchanged. The test should check the state transition, not the logging.

title: TLC Exhaustive State Exploration
prompt_injection: ∀finite model M: TLC(M) = {states reachable from Init | all transitions}. TLC (the TLA+ model checker) explores all reachable states of a finite model of the specification. This is exhaustive testing for specifications — it finds bugs that human review misses.
tensor_equation: ∀finite model M: TLC(M) = {states reachable from Init | all transitions}. TLC (the TLA+ model checker) explores all reachable states of a finite model of the specification. This is exhaustive testing for specifications — it finds bugs that human review misses.

redteam_pattern: A circuit breaker spec has 3 states and 3 events (9 transitions). A human review misses the case where timeout fires exactly at the same moment as a probe request. TLC explores all reachable states and finds the race condition: the state machine transitions to HalfOpen and receives Allow() simultaneously, causing a state inconsistency. The counterexample trace shows the exact sequence.

title: TLC Invariant Check
prompt_injection: ∀invariant I: TLC checks I in every reachable state. If the invariant is violated, TLC produces a counterexample: a sequence of states leading to the violation. ∀counterexample: TLC produces a trace from Init to the state where I fails. This is a BUG in the specification — a case the spec didn't handle.

redteam_pattern: A token router spec has the invariant □(∀k,t: RequestBuckets[k][t] ≤ RPM/60). TLC explores a model with 2 keys and 3 time windows. It finds a counterexample: KeyA sends 2 requests in the same sub-second window (both decrement from the same bucket before the refill). The invariant is violated. The trace shows: Init → Request(k=A, t=0) → Request(k=A, t=0) → violation. The spec didn't handle concurrent requests within the same time window.

title: Model-Check Spec Before Implementing
prompt_injection: Model-check the spec before implementing. TLC counterexample → fix the spec → model-check again → repeat until clean. The counterexample from TLC is a concrete scenario where the spec is wrong — the most valuable kind of bug report. Every iteration catches a bug before any code is written.

The counterexample from TLC is a concrete scenario where the spec is wrong — the most valuable kind of bug report. Every iteration catches a bug before any code is written.

redteam_pattern: A team writes a TLA+ spec for a distributed lock service, then immediately implements in Go without model-checking the spec. The spec has a bug: two clients can acquire the lock simultaneously if their requests arrive in the same clock tick. TLC would have found this in 2 seconds. Instead, the bug is discovered in production after a data corruption incident.

title: Spec is the Acceptance Criterion
prompt_injection: 'The code is correct' means 'the code refines the spec'. The spec is the acceptance criterion. The implementation is correct if and only if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification. There is no other definition of correctness.
tensor_equation: 'The code is correct' means 'the code refines the spec'. The spec is the acceptance criterion. The implementation is correct if and only if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification. There is no other definition of correctness.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The code is correct means the code refines the spec. The spec is the acceptance criterion. The implementation is correct if and only if every behavior of the implementation is allowed by the specification.' — Section 8: Model Checking and Section 5: Refinement
redteam_pattern: A team claims the circuit breaker is 'correct' because all unit tests pass. But the spec says Allow() must return false when state = Open and timeout is active. The tests never check this specific case. The code returns true in this case. The tests pass, but the code does not refine the spec. The acceptance criterion is violated: the 'correctness' claim is meaningless without refinement proof.

title: PlusCal Semantics
prompt_injection: PlusCal is an algorithm language that compiles to TLA+. It looks like a programming language (while loops, if-then-else, variables) but has the semantics of TLA+ (states, transitions, stuttering). It is the bridge between 'I can write pseudocode' and 'I can prove properties'. Workflow: write PlusCal → translate to TLA+ → model-check with TLC → find counterexamples → fix → repeat.

redteam_pattern: A developer writes a lock algorithm in Go directly without writing the PlusCal spec first. The Go implementation has a race condition where two goroutines both pass the 'await ~locked' check before either sets locked := TRUE. The PlusCal version would have the same bug if the check and assignment are in separate atomic steps. Writing PlusCal with the correct atomicity annotation (either a single atomic block or a label per atomic step) would make the atomicity requirement explicit.

title: Circuit Breaker Safety Invariant
prompt_injection: □(state = Open ∧ timeout_active → Allow() = false). When the circuit breaker is in the Open state and the timeout is still active, the Allow() function must return false. This is a safety property: the bad thing (traffic through an open breaker) must never happen.
tensor_equation: □(state = Open ∧ timeout_active → Allow() = false). When the circuit breaker is in the Open state and the timeout is still active, the Allow() function must return false. This is a safety property: the bad thing (traffic through an open breaker) must never happen.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: '□(state = Open ∧ timeout_active → Allow() = false). When the circuit breaker is in the Open state and the timeout is still active, the Allow() function must return false.' — Section 2: Safety, applied to circuit breaker state machine
redteam_pattern: A circuit breaker implementation has a race condition: the timeout goroutine clears timeout_active, then sets state = HalfOpen. Between these two operations, a concurrent Allow() call sees timeout_active = false (good) but also state = Open (bad because timeout is cleared but state hasn't transitioned). The Allow() call returns true because it checks only timeout_active, not state. The invariant □(state = Open ∧ timeout_active → Allow() = false) is violated because the intermediate state (Open, !timeout_active) allows traffic.

title: Circuit Breaker Liveness Property
prompt_injection: ◇(state = Open → state = HalfOpen). The circuit breaker eventually transitions from Open to HalfOpen after the timeout expires. This is a liveness property: the good thing (allowing a probe request) must eventually happen. The timeout is the enforcement mechanism.
tensor_equation: ◇(state = Open → state = HalfOpen). The circuit breaker eventually transitions from Open to HalfOpen after the timeout expires. This is a liveness property: the good thing (allowing a probe request) must eventually happen. The timeout is the enforcement mechanism.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: '◇(state = Open → state = HalfOpen). The circuit breaker eventually transitions from Open to HalfOpen after the timeout expires. This is a liveness property: the good thing (allowing a probe request) must eventually happen.' — Section 3: Liveness, applied to circuit breaker state machine
redteam_pattern: A circuit breaker enters Open state and the timeout is set to 30 seconds. The timeout goroutine is blocked by a concurrent operation (e.g., a mutex held by a slow request handler). The state never transitions to HalfOpen. The liveness property ◇(Open → HalfOpen) is violated. Under weak fairness, the timeout goroutine must eventually execute, but if the mutex is never released, the action is not enabled and weak fairness does not apply.

title: TLA+ Test Framework
prompt_injection: ∀system S: (1) ∃written spec spec(S) describing what S SHOULD do; (2) □Invariant(S) — safety is proven; (3) ◇Goal(S) — liveness is proven; (4) fairness assumptions are valid for the target scheduler; (5) implementation I refines spec(S) — refinement is proven; (6) spec(S) describes observable behavior, not internal steps; (7) spec(S) is model-checkable for a finite model. These are the seven questions to ask for any system.
tensor_equation: ∀system S: (1) ∃written spec spec(S) describing what S SHOULD do; (2) □Invariant(S) — safety is proven; (3) ◇Goal(S) — liveness is proven; (4) fairness assumptions are valid for the target scheduler; (5) implementation I refines spec(S) — refinement is proven; (6) spec(S) describes observable behavior, not internal steps; (7) spec(S) is model-checkable for a finite model. These are the seven questions to ask for any system.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The TLA+ Test: (1) ∃written spec; (2) □Invariant (safety); (3) ◇Goal (liveness); (4) fairness assumptions valid; (5) refinement proven; (6) spec describes observable behavior; (7) spec is model-checkable.' — The TLA+ Test section
redteam_pattern: A system passes all seven TLA+ Test questions on paper, but the fairness assumption (weak fairness of Go's scheduler) is violated because the system uses a non-FIFO mutex that can starve a goroutine. The liveness proof assumed weak fairness, but the actual scheduler does not provide it for this specific mutex. The TLA+ Test framework catches this: question 4 (are fairness assumptions valid for the target scheduler?) fails.

title: Scope-Based Resource Release

Then fix the data race with a mutex but introduce a race condition (e.g., check-then-act without holding the lock across both operations). Show that -race does not catch the race condition.

∀ thread t, ∀ capability c:

title: execve() Capability Transformation Rules
prompt_injection:
∀ thread T, executable file F, after execve(F):
  P'_ambient     = (F_is_privileged) ? ∅ : P_ambient
  P'_permitted   = (P_inheritable ∩ F_inheritable) ∪ (F_permitted ∩ P_bounding) ∪ P'_ambient
  P'_effective   = F_effective ? P'_permitted : P'_ambient
  P'_inheritable = P_inheritable     [unchanged]
  P'_bounding    = P_bounding        [unchanged]
tensor_equation:
∀ thread T, executable file F, after execve(F):
  P'_ambient     = (F_is_privileged) ? ∅ : P_ambient
  P'_permitted   = (P_inheritable ∩ F_inheritable) ∪ (F_permitted ∩ P_bounding) ∪ P'_ambient
  P'_effective   = F_effective ? P'_permitted : P'_ambient
  P'_inheritable = P_inheritable     [unchanged]
  P'_bounding    = P_bounding        [unchanged]
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Transformation of capabilities during execve()' section — these are the formal kernel equations governing capability transitions across execve, as documented in the capabilities(7) man page.

title: UID Transition Capability Clearing
prompt_injection:
∀ thread t:
  (is_root_before(t) ∧ ¬is_root_after(t)) ⟹ P_permitted(t) = P_effective(t) = P_ambient(t) = ∅
  (euid(t) = 0 → euid'(t) ≠ 0) ⟹ P_effective(t) = ∅
  (euid(t) ≠ 0 → euid'(t) = 0) ⟹ P_effective(t) = P_permitted(t)
tensor_equation:
∀ thread t:
  (is_root_before(t) ∧ ¬is_root_after(t)) ⟹ P_permitted(t) = P_effective(t) = P_ambient(t) = ∅
  (euid(t) = 0 → euid'(t) ≠ 0) ⟹ P_effective(t) = ∅
  (euid(t) ≠ 0 → euid'(t) = 0) ⟹ P_effective(t) = P_permitted(t)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Effect of user ID changes on capabilities' section — three sub-rules: (1) All real/effective/saved UIDs go from 0→nonzero clears permitted, effective, ambient; (2) Effective UID goes 0→nonzero clears effective; (3) Effective UID goes nonzero→0 copies permitted to effective.
redteam_pattern: Start as root with full capabilities; call setuid(65534) to drop all UIDs to nonzero; verify /proc/PID/status shows CapPrm=CapEff=CapAmb=0000000000000000; start as root, set euid to nonzero while keeping ruid=0; verify CapEff cleared but CapPrm may survive (rule 2 only clears effective); verify SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS can preserve CapPrm across UID drop but is cleared on execve

title: SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS Cleared on execve
prompt_injection:
∀ thread t, after execve(F):
  SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS ∉ securebits(t)
tensor_equation:
∀ thread t, after execve(F):
  SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS ∉ securebits(t)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The securebits flags' section — 'This flag is always cleared on an execve(2).'
redteam_pattern: Set SECBIT_KEEP_CAPS via prctl(PR_SET_SECUREBITS); drop UIDs to nonzero so CapPrm should survive; execve /bin/true; inspect securebits in parent via wait+ptrace or /proc/PID/status; verify KEEP_CAPS flag is cleared after execve

title: Securebits Locked Flag Irreversibility
prompt_injection:
∀ thread t, ∀ flag f ∈ {KEEP_CAPS, NO_SETUID_FIXUP, NOROOT, NO_CAP_AMBIENT_RAISE}:
  f_LOCKED ∈ securebits(t) ⟹ f is immutable for t and all descendants
tensor_equation:
∀ thread t, ∀ flag f ∈ {KEEP_CAPS, NO_SETUID_FIXUP, NOROOT, NO_CAP_AMBIENT_RAISE}:
  f_LOCKED ∈ securebits(t) ⟹ f is immutable for t and all descendants
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The securebits flags' section — 'Setting any of the locked flags is irreversible, and has the effect of preventing further changes to the corresponding base flag.'
redteam_pattern: Set SECBIT_NOROOT | SECBIT_NOROOT_LOCKED via prctl(PR_SET_SECUREBITS); attempt to clear SECBIT_NOROOT; verify EPERM; fork a child and attempt the same in the child; verify both parent and child cannot unset; attempt execve of a setuid-root binary and verify no root privilege escalation occurs

title: Capability-Dumb Binary All-or-Nothing Check on execve
prompt_injection:
∀ file F with F_effective = true:
  execve(F) succeeds ⟹ F_permitted ⊆ P'_permitted
  execve(F) fails with EPERM ⟺ F_permitted ⊈ P'_permitted
tensor_equation:
∀ file F with F_effective = true:
  execve(F) succeeds ⟹ F_permitted ⊆ P'_permitted
  execve(F) fails with EPERM ⟺ F_permitted ⊈ P'_permitted
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Safety checking for capability-dumb binaries' section — 'If the process did not obtain the full set of file permitted capabilities, then execve(2) fails with the error EPERM.'
redteam_pattern: Set file capabilities on a binary with F_effective=true; drop one of those capabilities from the bounding set; attempt execve of that binary; verify it fails with EPERM; add all capabilities back to bounding set and verify execve succeeds

title: File Effective Bit Propagation Constraint
prompt_injection:
∀ file F, ∀ c ∈ F_permitted ∪ F_inheritable with c's effective flag set:
  ∀ d ∈ F_permitted ∪ F_inheritable with d's permitted/inheritable flag set:
    d's effective flag must also be set
tensor_equation:
∀ file F, ∀ c ∈ F_permitted ∪ F_inheritable with c's effective flag set:
  ∀ d ∈ F_permitted ∪ F_inheritable with d's permitted/inheritable flag set:
    d's effective flag must also be set
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'File capabilities' section — 'if we specify the effective flag as being enabled for any capability, then the effective flag must also be specified as enabled for all other capabilities for which the corresponding permitted or inheritable flag is enabled.'
redpattern: Use setcap(8) to set a file with CAP_NET_RAW=ep and CAP_SYS_ADMIN=p (no effective for SYS_ADMIN) and verify setcap rejects it with an error; verify that hand-writing the VFS xattr with this invalid combination causes execve to either fail or grant both as effective

title: Bounding Set Masks Inheritable Set Addition via capset(2)
prompt_injection:
∀ thread t, ∀ capability c:
  c ∉ P_bounding(t) ⟹ c cannot be added to P_inheritable(t) via capset(2)
tensor_equation:
∀ thread t, ∀ capability c:
  c ∉ P_bounding(t) ⟹ c cannot be added to P_inheritable(t) via capset(2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Capability bounding set' section — 'if a capability is not in the bounding set, then a thread cant add this capability to its inheritable set.' Note: existing inheritable capabilities can persist without bounding set membership — a subtle asymmetry also documented in the source.
redteam_pattern: Drop CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE from bounding set; verify current P_inheritable = ∅; call capset(2) to add CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE to P_inheritable; verify it fails; note: if CAP_DAC_OVERRIDE was already in P_inheritable before the drop, verify it persists — the bounding set only prevents NEW additions, not existing ones

title: Namespaced File Capability Containment (VFS_CAP_REVISION_3)
prompt_injection:
∀ file F with VFS_CAP_REVISION_3 xattr, ∀ process p:
  (p executes F ∧ capabilities conferred) ⟹
    (uid 0 in p's user namespace maps to the root user ID stored in F's xattr)
    ∨ (p's user namespace is a descendant of such a namespace)
tensor_equation:
∀ file F with VFS_CAP_REVISION_3 xattr, ∀ process p:
  (p executes F ∧ capabilities conferred) ⟹

    ∨ (p's user namespace is a descendant of such a namespace)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Namespaced file capabilities' section — 'capabilities are conferred only if the binary is executed by a process that resides in a user namespace whose UID 0 maps to the root user ID that is saved in the extended attribute, or when executed by a process that resides in a descendant of such a namespace.'
redteam_pattern: Create two disjoint user namespaces; set file capabilities with VFS_CAP_REVISION_3 xattr in namespace A; attempt to execute that file from namespace B; verify no capabilities are conferred; execute the same file from namespace A and verify capabilities are conferred

title: Capability-Bounding Execve Gate
prompt_injection:
∀ thread t, ∀ file F, after execve(F):
  P'_permitted ⊆ P_bounding(t)
tensor_equation:
∀ thread t, ∀ file F, after execve(F):
  P'_permitted ⊆ P_bounding(t)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Derived from the execve transformation rules in 'Transformation of capabilities during execve()' — the P_bounding AND with F_permitted term enforces this. The source cache notes this is 'the defining property of the bounding set.' Medium confidence as it is a derived invariant, not a standalone statement in the man page.
redteam_pattern: Drop CAP_SYS_ADMIN from bounding set; execve a file with F_permitted containing CAP_SYS_ADMIN; verify CapPrm in /proc/PID/status does NOT contain CAP_SYS_ADMIN; repeat with CAP_NET_RAW in inheritable set via a file with F_inheritable set; verify it also gets masked by the empty bounding set

title: Every process belongs to exactly one cgroup
prompt_injection: ∀ p ∈ Processes, ∃! c ∈ Cgroups: p ∈ c
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ Processes, ∃! c ∈ Cgroups: p ∈ c
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Terminology' and 'Processes' sections — 'cgroups form a tree structure and every process in the system belongs to one and only one cgroup. All threads of a process belong to the same cgroup.'
redteam_pattern: Create a process and verify /proc/self/cgroup shows membership in exactly one cgroup. Attempt to place a process into two sibling cgroups simultaneously and verify the kernel rejects the operation.

title: No Internal Process Constraint (domain mode)
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ NonRootCgroups, (∃ d ∈ DomainControllers: enabled(c, d)) ⇒ (∄ p ∈ Processes: p ∈ c)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ NonRootCgroups, (∃ d ∈ DomainControllers: enabled(c, d)) ⇒ (∄ p ∈ Processes: p ∈ c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'No Internal Process Constraint' section — 'This guarantees that, when a domain controller is looking at the part of the hierarchy which has it enabled, processes are always only on the leaves. This rules out situations where child cgroups compete against internal processes of the parent.'
redteam_pattern: Create a non-root cgroup with a process running in it, then attempt to enable a domain controller (e.g., +cpu) in cgroup.subtree_control. Verify the kernel rejects the write with -EBUSY.

title: Top-Down Enablement Constraint
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ NonRootCgroups, ∀ d ∈ Controllers: enabled(c, d) ⇒ enabled(parent(c), d)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ NonRootCgroups, ∀ d ∈ Controllers: enabled(c, d) ⇒ enabled(parent(c), d)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Top-down Constraint' section — 'Resources are distributed top-down and a cgroup can further distribute a resource only if the resource has been distributed to it from the parent.'
redteam_pattern: Create a child cgroup and attempt to enable a controller (e.g., +cpu) in its cgroup.subtree_control without first enabling it in the parent. Verify the kernel rejects the write with -EINVAL.

title: Restrictions are strictly tightening (hierarchical nesting)
prompt_injection: ∀ c₁, c₂ ∈ Cgroups: is_descendant(c₁, c₂) ⇒ restrictions(c₁) ⊆ restrictions(c₂)
tensor_equation: ∀ c₁, c₂ ∈ Cgroups: is_descendant(c₁, c₂) ⇒ restrictions(c₁) ⊆ restrictions(c₂)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'What is cgroup?' section — 'When a controller is enabled on a nested cgroup, it always restricts the resource distribution further. The restrictions set closer to the root in the hierarchy can not be overridden from further away.'
redteam_pattern: Create a parent cgroup with memory.max=100M and a child cgroup. Attempt to set child's memory.max to 200M. Verify the kernel silently caps the child's effective max to the parent's value.

title: Memory ownership is static (no migration on process move)
prompt_injection: ∀ m ∈ MemoryAreas, ∀ p ∈ Processes: charged_to(m, cgroup_at_instantiation(m)) ∧ (migrate(p, c₁, c₂) ⇒ still_charged_to(m, cgroup_at_instantiation(m)))
tensor_equation: ∀ m ∈ MemoryAreas, ∀ p ∈ Processes: charged_to(m, cgroup_at_instantiation(m)) ∧ (migrate(p, c₁, c₂) ⇒ still_charged_to(m, cgroup_at_instantiation(m)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Memory Ownership' section — 'A memory area is charged to the cgroup which instantiated it and stays charged to the cgroup until the area is released. Migrating a process to a different cgroup doesnt move the memory usages that it instantiated while in the previous cgroup to the new cgroup.'
redteam_pattern: Allocate 64MB of memory in cgroup A, then migrate the process to cgroup B. Read memory.current on cgroup A to verify the memory is still charged there, and cgroup B's memory.current shows no increase from the allocation.

title: Delegation containment (no cross-delegation moves)
prompt_injection: ∀ p ∈ DelegatedSubhierarchy(D), ∀ q ∈ Processes: migrate(q, src, dst) succeeds ∧ delegatee_has_write_access(D) ⇒ (src ∈ D ∧ dst ∈ D)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ DelegatedSubhierarchy(D), ∀ q ∈ Processes: migrate(q, src, dst) succeeds ∧ delegatee_has_write_access(D) ⇒ (src ∈ D ∧ dst ∈ D)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Delegation Containment' section — 'A delegated sub-hierarchy is contained in the sense that processes cant be moved into or out of the sub-hierarchy by the delegatee.'
redteam_pattern: As a non-root user delegated a cgroup sub-hierarchy, attempt to write a PID from outside the delegated subtree into the subtree's cgroup.procs. Verify the write fails with -EACCES. Similarly, attempt to move a process out of the subtree to a parent cgroup and verify failure.

title: Allocation model -- no over-commitment
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups, ∀ r ∈ AllocatableResources: Σ_{child ∈ children(c)} allocation(child, r) ≤ available(c, r)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups, ∀ r ∈ AllocatableResources: Σ_{child ∈ children(c)} allocation(child, r) ≤ available(c, r)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Resource Distribution Models / Allocations' section — 'Allocations cant be over-committed - the sum of the allocations of children can not exceed the amount of resource available to the parent.'
redteam_pattern: Create a parent cgroup with 4 CPUs available. Attempt to configure two child cgroups each requesting 3 exclusive CPUs via cpuset.cpus.exclusive. Verify the kernel rejects the second write because the sum of allocations (6) exceeds available (4).

title: Cpuset exclusivity (one CPU to at most one child)
prompt_injection: ∀ cpu ∈ ExclusiveCPUs, ∀ parent ∈ Cgroups: |{child ∈ children(parent) : cpu ∈ exclusive(child)}| ≤ 1
tensor_equation: ∀ cpu ∈ ExclusiveCPUs, ∀ parent ∈ Cgroups: |{child ∈ children(parent) : cpu ∈ exclusive(child)}| ≤ 1
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'cpuset.cpus.exclusive' section — 'For a parent cgroup, any one of its exclusive CPUs can only be distributed to at most one of its child cgroups. Having an exclusive CPU appearing in two or more of its child cgroups is not allowed (the exclusivity rule).'
redteam_pattern: Create a parent cgroup with exclusive CPU 0. Configure two child cgroups, both listing CPU 0 in their cpuset.cpus.exclusive. Verify the second write returns an error rejecting the overlapping exclusive CPU assignment.

title: Cpuset effective subset (child sees parent's effective set)
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ CpusetCgroups: cpuset.cpus.effective(c) ⊆ cpuset.cpus.effective(parent(c))
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ CpusetCgroups: cpuset.cpus.effective(c) ⊆ cpuset.cpus.effective(parent(c))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Cpuset' section — 'The cpuset controller is hierarchical. That means the controller cannot use CPUs or memory nodes not allowed in its parent.'
redteam_pattern: Set parent cgroup's cpuset.cpus to '0-1'. Set child cgroup's cpuset.cpus to '0-3'. Read child's cpuset.cpus.effective and verify it shows '0-1' (intersection with parent) not '0-3'.

title: memory.min -- hard reclaim protection
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) ≤ effective_min(c) ⇒ ¬reclaimed(c)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) ≤ effective_min(c) ⇒ ¬reclaimed(c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'memory.min' section — 'Hard memory protection. If the memory usage of a cgroup is within its effective min boundary, the cgroups memory wont be reclaimed under any conditions. If there is no unprotected reclaimable memory available, OOM killer is invoked.'
redteam_pattern: Set memory.min=50M for a cgroup with a process actively using 30M of memory. Create global memory pressure by allocating memory in other cgroups. Verify via memory.stat that the protected cgroup's memory is not reclaimed (no pgscan events for that cgroup) and that the OOM killer is invoked instead.

title: memory.high -- throttle, never OOM
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) > memory.high(c) ⇒ throttled(c) ∧ ¬OOM(c)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) > memory.high(c) ⇒ throttled(c) ∧ ¬OOM(c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'memory.high' section — 'Going over the high limit never invokes the OOM killer and under extreme conditions the limit may be breached.'
redteam_pattern: Set memory.high=10M for a cgroup and run a process that allocates 20M. Verify the process is throttled (observable via memory.events showing high events) but not killed (no OOM events in memory.events).

title: memory.max -- hard limit, OOM on failure
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) ≥ memory.max(c) ∧ ¬reclaimable(c) ⇒ OOM(c)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: usage(c) ≥ memory.max(c) ∧ ¬reclaimable(c) ⇒ OOM(c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'memory.max' section — 'Memory usage hard limit. If a cgroups memory usage reaches this limit and cant be reduced, the OOM killer is invoked in the cgroup.'
redteam_pattern: Set memory.max=10M for a cgroup and run a process that allocates 20M of non-reclaimable memory (e.g., anonymous pages). Verify via memory.events that the OOM event fires and the process receives SIGKILL. Verify sibling cgroups are unaffected.

title: oom.group -- atomic kill
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: memory.oom.group(c) = 1 ⇒ (OOM(c) ⇒ (∀ p ∈ subtree(c): killed(p) ∨ oom_protected(p)))
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: memory.oom.group(c) = 1 ⇒ (OOM(c) ⇒ (∀ p ∈ subtree(c): killed(p) ∨ oom_protected(p)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'memory.oom.group' section — 'Determines whether the cgroup should be treated as an indivisible workload by the OOM killer. If set, all tasks belonging to the cgroup or to its descendants are killed together or not at all.'
redteam_pattern: Create a cgroup with memory.oom.group=1, spawn two processes in it, set memory.max to a low value. Confirm both processes are killed (not just one) when OOM fires. Verify that a process with oom_score_adj=-1000 survives the atomic kill.

title: Thread mode is irreversible
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: type(c) = threaded ⇒ type(c) = threaded forever
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: type(c) = threaded ⇒ type(c) = threaded forever
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Threads' section — 'Once threaded, the cgroup cant be made a domain again.'
redteam_pattern: Write 'threaded' to a cgroup's cgroup.type file. Then attempt to write 'domain' to the same file. Verify the kernel rejects the write and the cgroup remains threaded.

title: Dying cgroup is immutable and terminal
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: dying(c) ⇒ (∄ p ∈ Processes: migrate(p, _, c)) ∧ ¬revive(c)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: dying(c) ⇒ (∄ p ∈ Processes: migrate(p, _, c)) ∧ ¬revive(c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'cgroup.stat' section — 'A process cant enter a dying cgroup under any circumstances, a dying cgroup cant revive. A dying cgroup can consume system resources not exceeding limits, which were active at the moment of cgroup deletion.'
redteam_pattern: Create a cgroup, rmdir it to initiate the dying state. Attempt to write a PID into the cgroup's cgroup.procs file. Verify the write fails with -ENODEV. Attempt to recreate the same cgroup directory and verify it succeeds as a fresh cgroup (not a revival).

title: Controller enable/disable is atomic
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups, ∀ ops ∈ ControllerOperations: |ops| > 1 ⇒ (all_succeed(ops) ∨ all_fail(ops))
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups, ∀ ops ∈ ControllerOperations: |ops| > 1 ⇒ (all_succeed(ops) ∨ all_fail(ops))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Enabling and Disabling' section — 'When multiple operations are specified as above, either they all succeed or fail.'
redteam_pattern: Write '+cpu +memory' to cgroup.subtree_control and verify both controllers become enabled. Then write a mixed batch with an invalid controller name (e.g., '+cpu +invalid_controller') and verify neither cpu nor the invalid controller is enabled — the entire write fails atomically.

title: Fork obeys PID limits (not migration)
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: pids.current(c) > pids.max(c) is possible via migration or limit-lowering, but fork()/clone() ⇒ (pids.current(c) < pids.max(c) ∨ returns -EAGAIN)
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: pids.current(c) > pids.max(c) is possible via migration or limit-lowering, but fork()/clone() ⇒ (pids.current(c) < pids.max(c) ∨ returns -EAGAIN)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'PID' section — 'Organisational operations are not blocked by cgroup policies, so it is possible to have pids.current > pids.max. ... However, it is not possible to violate a cgroup PID policy through fork() or clone().'
redteam_pattern: Set pids.max=5 in a cgroup with 5 running processes. Attempt to fork() from within the cgroup and verify fork() returns -EAGAIN. Then migrate a 6th process into the cgroup from outside and verify the migration succeeds despite pids.current > pids.max.

title: Freezer is hierarchical and OR-composed
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: frozen(c) ⇔ (frozen_self(c) ∨ (∃ a ∈ ancestors(c): frozen(a)))
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: frozen(c) ⇔ (frozen_self(c) ∨ (∃ a ∈ ancestors(c): frozen(a)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'cgroup.freeze' section — 'A cgroup can be frozen either by its own settings, or by settings of any ancestor cgroups. If any of ancestor cgroups is frozen, the cgroup will remain frozen.'
redteam_pattern: Create a parent cgroup with two child cgroups, each running a process. Write '1' to the parent's cgroup.freeze. Verify all child cgroup processes are frozen (cgroup.events shows 'frozen 1'). Then unfreeze only the parent and verify all children are unfrozen.

title: Cgroup kill is atomic and migration-safe
prompt_injection: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: write("1", cgroup.kill(c)) ⇒ (∀ p ∈ subtree(c): eventually_receives_SIGKILL(p))
tensor_equation: ∀ c ∈ Cgroups: write("1", cgroup.kill(c)) ⇒ (∀ p ∈ subtree(c): eventually_receives_SIGKILL(p))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'cgroup.kill' section — 'Killing a cgroup tree will deal with concurrent forks appropriately and is protected against migrations.'
redteam_pattern: Spawn a process in a cgroup, then write '1' to cgroup.kill. Verify the process receives SIGKILL (exit code 137). Then create a race test: spawn a process that continuously forks, write '1' to cgroup.kill, and verify all processes in the subtree are killed with no survivors.

title: Writeback cgroup ownership is inode-based (not page-based)
prompt_injection: ∀ inode ∈ Inodes: ∃! c ∈ Cgroups: owner(inode, c) ∧ ∀ bio ∈ WritebackIOs(inode): attributed_to(bio, c)
tensor_equation: ∀ inode ∈ Inodes: ∃! c ∈ Cgroups: owner(inode, c) ∧ ∀ bio ∈ WritebackIOs(inode): attributed_to(bio, c)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Writeback' section — 'Memory is tracked per page while writeback per inode. For the purpose of writeback, an inode is assigned to a cgroup and all IO requests to write dirty pages from the inode are attributed to that cgroup.'
redteam_pattern: Create two cgroups. From cgroup A, write to a file. From cgroup B, write to the same file. Check IO statistics (io.stat) on each cgroup. Verify that all writeback IO for the shared file is attributed to a single cgroup (the inode owner), not split between both cgroups as per-page accounting would suggest.

title: Namespace-scoped IDs are not valid across namespace boundaries
prompt_injection:
For any namespace-scoped identifier id obtained in namespace N,
and any namespace M where M != N:
  resolve(id, M) is undefined — it may resolve to a different object,
  or to no object.
tensor_equation:
For any namespace-scoped identifier id obtained in namespace N,
and any namespace M where M != N:
  resolve(id, M) is undefined — it may resolve to a different object,
  or to no object.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: compatibility-list.rst, item 1 — 'this ID is only valid within the namespace it was obtained in and may refer to some other object in another namespace'
redteam_pattern: Spawn two processes in separate PID namespaces. Process A writes its PID to a shared tmpfs file. Process B reads that PID and attempts to send a signal via kill(). Verify that the signal reaches the wrong process or fails with EINVAL/ESRCH, confirming the cross-namespace ID leak is unsafe.

title: Cross-namespace ID exposure via shared resources is prohibited
prompt_injection:
For any two tasks t1 in namespace N1 and t2 in namespace N2 where N1 != N2,
and any shared resource R (filesystem, IPC shmem/message queue):
  t1 must not expose a namespace-scoped ID obtained in N1 to t2 via R.
tensor_equation:
For any two tasks t1 in namespace N1 and t2 in namespace N2 where N1 != N2,
and any shared resource R (filesystem, IPC shmem/message queue):
  t1 must not expose a namespace-scoped ID obtained in N1 to t2 via R.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: compatibility-list.rst, item 1 — 'tasks shouldnt try exposing this ID to some other task living in a different namespace via a shared filesystem or IPC shmem/message'
redteam_pattern: Run two containers sharing a tmpfs mount and IPC namespace. Container A creates a System V semaphore, writes its ID to a shared file. Container B reads the ID and calls semctl(). Verify that the operation acts on a different semaphore or fails with EINVAL, proving the ID should never cross the boundary.

title: User namespace UID isolation for VFS (INTENDED BUT BROKEN)
prompt_injection:
For any two user namespaces U1 and U2 where U1 != U2,
and any numeric UID u:
  VFS permission check for uid=u in U1 must not grant access to
  resources owned by uid=u in U2.
tensor_equation:
For any two user namespaces U1 and U2 where U1 != U2,
and any numeric UID u:
  VFS permission check for uid=u in U1 must not grant access to
  resources owned by uid=u in U2.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: compatibility-list.rst, item 2 — 'two equal user IDs in different user namespaces should not be equal from the VFS point of view' with status 'But currently this is not so.' The intended invariant is correctly captured; the source explicitly documents it as BROKEN in the current kernel.
redteam_pattern: Create two user namespaces with overlapping UID mappings so both have UID 1000 mapped. Create a file owned by UID 1000 in user namespace A on a shared filesystem. Verify that a process in user namespace B running as UID 1000 can access that file, confirming the kernel's broken UID isolation in VFS.

title: User namespace UID isolation for IPC (INTENDED BUT BROKEN)
prompt_injection:
For any two user namespaces U1 and U2 where U1 != U2,
and any numeric UID u, and any IPC object O:
  if O is owned by (u, U1), then a process with credentials (u, U2)
  must not be able to access O.
tensor_equation:
For any two user namespaces U1 and U2 where U1 != U2,
and any numeric UID u, and any IPC object O:
  if O is owned by (u, U1), then a process with credentials (u, U2)
  must not be able to access O.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: compatibility-list.rst, item 2 — 'two users from different user namespaces should not access the same IPC objects even having equal UIDs' with status 'currently this is not so.' The intended invariant is correctly captured; the source explicitly documents it as BROKEN in the current kernel.
redteam_pattern: Create two user namespaces with overlapping UID mappings. In user namespace A, create an IPC object (semaphore, message queue, or shared memory) owned by UID 1000. In user namespace B, as UID 1000, attempt to access that IPC object. Verify the access succeeds despite different user namespaces, confirming the broken UID isolation for IPC.

title: User namespaces without resource limits are unsafe for untrusted code
prompt_injection:
For any system S that enables user namespaces and hosts untrusted users:
  S must enable memory control groups (cgroups) to bound per-user
  resource consumption.
  Without this bound, a user can exhaust kernel resources by
  creating objects that lack per-instance limits or whose limits
  are bypassed by UID switching.
tensor_equation:
For any system S that enables user namespaces and hosts untrusted users:
  S must enable memory control groups (cgroups) to bound per-user
  resource consumption.
  Without this bound, a user can exhaust kernel resources by
  creating objects that lack per-instance limits or whose limits
  are bypassed by UID switching.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: resource-control.rst — 'On a system where the admins dont trust their users or their users programs, user namespaces expose the system to potential misuse of resources. In order to mitigate this, we recommend that admins enable memory control groups on any system that enables user namespaces.' Note: this is a recommendation from the kernel docs, not a kernel-enforced invariant; medium confidence.

Verify that kernel memory exhaustion (DoS) occurs, demonstrating that memory cgroups are a necessary safeguard.

title: Action precedence ordering is total and deterministic
prompt_injection:
∀ f1,f2 ∈ installed_filters, ∀ s ∈ syscalls:
  action(f1, s) = a1 ∧ action(f2, s) = a2 ⇒
  effective_action(s) = max_precedence(a1, a2)

∀ f1,f2 ∈ installed_filters, ∀ s ∈ syscalls:

Call write() and verify that the process is killed (KILL_PROCESS wins), not allowed to proceed. Also test that a later KILL_PROCESS overrides an earlier ALLOW for the same syscall.

title: NO_NEW_PRIVS is a hard prerequisite for unprivileged filter installation
prompt_injection:
∀ task t: install_filter(t, prog) succeeds ⇒

redteam_pattern: Create an unprivileged process (no CAP_SYS_ADMIN) that does NOT call prctl(PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS, 1). Attempt seccomp(SECCOMP_SET_MODE_FILTER, ...) and verify it returns -EACCES. Then call prctl(PR_SET_NO_NEW_PRIVS, 1) and retry the same seccomp call — verify it succeeds. Finally, as root with CAP_SYS_ADMIN, verify the check is bypassed even without NO_NEW_PRIVS.

title: BPF programs cannot dereference pointers (structural TOCTOU prevention)

∀ bpf_prog p in seccomp context, ∀ instruction i ∈ p:

∀ bpf_prog p in seccomp context, ∀ instruction i ∈ p:
  i is not a pointer-dereference operation
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Introduction' section — 'BPF makes it impossible for users of seccomp to fall prey to time-of-check-time-of-use (TOCTOU) attacks that are common in system call interposition frameworks. BPF programs may not dereference pointers which constrains all filters to solely evaluating the system call arguments directly.'
redteam_pattern: Write a seccomp BPF program that attempts to dereference a pointer argument (e.g., load the value at [arg0] rather than arg0 itself). Submit it via seccomp(SECCOMP_SET_MODE_FILTER, ...) and verify the kernel rejects the filter or the BPF verifier refuses to load the program. Then confirm that reads from seccomp_data registers (syscall number, argument registers) work correctly since these are stack-snapshotted values.

title: seccomp filter evaluation is not re-run after ptrace notification
prompt_injection: ∀ syscall s: trace_notification_fired(s) ⇒ ¬evaluate_seccomp_filters(s, again)

verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'SECCOMP_RET_TRACE' section — 'The seccomp check will not be run again after the tracer is notified. (This means that seccomp-based sandboxes MUST NOT allow use of ptrace, even of other sandboxed processes, without extreme care; ptracers can use this mechanism to escape.)'
redteam_pattern: Write a seccomp filter that returns SECCOMP_RET_TRACE for write() syscalls. Attach a ptracer that, upon receiving the PTRACE_EVENT_SECCOMP notification, changes the syscall to read() using PTRACE_SET_SYSCALL. Resume execution and verify that read() is executed without seccomp re-evaluation — demonstrating the ptrace bypass of seccomp filtering. Then verify the documented warning: sandboxes MUST NOT allow ptrace.

title: Filter inheritance is total — children are always equally or more constrained

∀ parent p, ∀ child c of p:

∀ parent p, ∀ child c of p:

Then fork another child that tries to install a more permissive filter (e.g., SECCOMP_SET_MODE_FILTER with SECCOMP_FILTER_FLAG_TSYNC to remove restrictions) — verify the kernel rejects attempts to loosen constraints on inherited filters. Also verify the child CAN add additional stricter filters on top of parent's.

title: Architecture value must be checked before syscall number in any correct filter

∀ filter f that is safe against syscall-number-spoofing:

On any architecture that supports multiple system call invocation conventions, the system call numbers may vary based on the specific invocation. If the numbers in the different calling conventions overlap, then checks in the filters may be abused. Always check the arch value!'
redteam_pattern: Write a seccomp filter that allows syscall number 4 (write on x86-64) WITHOUT checking the architecture first. Execute a 32-bit x86 binary that calls stat() (syscall number 4 in x86-32 ABI) and verify the syscall is incorrectly allowed. Then add an architecture check (AUDIT_ARCH_X86_64) as the first instruction and verify the same 32-bit stat() call is now correctly denied. This proves the arch check is necessary for correctness.

title: Multiple same-precedence return values resolve to most-recently-installed filter's data
prompt_injection:
∀ f_i, f_j ∈ installed_filters where i > j (f_i installed later):

∀ f_i, f_j ∈ installed_filters where i > j (f_i installed later):

verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Return values' section — 'Precedence is only determined using the SECCOMP_RET_ACTION mask. When multiple filters return values of the same precedence, only the SECCOMP_RET_DATA from the most recently installed filter will be returned.'

Call write() on a valid fd and verify the errno is EACCES (from later filter), not EPERM. Then reverse installation order and confirm the tiebreaker always resolves to the most recently installed filter's SECCOMP_RET_DATA.

title: seccomp notification fd reads/writes are synchronized

∀ notification_fd fd:

∀ notification_fd fd:

Simultaneously trigger the same syscall from multiple sandboxed processes. Verify that: (1) each notification is delivered to exactly one reader thread, (2) no notification is delivered twice, (3) each response (by id) is applied to the correct notification. Also verify that a thread blocking on read() does not prevent other threads from read() — i.e., synchronization is per-reader, not per-fd.

title: actions_logged sysctl rejects SECCOMP_RET_ALLOW with EINVAL

redteam_pattern: Write 'allow' to /proc/sys/kernel/seccomp/actions_logged and verify the write returns EINVAL. Write 'kill_process,kill_thread,trap,errno,user_notif,trace,log' and verify it succeeds. Then attempt 'KILL_PROCESS' (uppercase) to confirm case sensitivity. Write 'allow' mixed with valid tokens (e.g., 'kill_process,allow') and verify the entire write fails with EINVAL. Confirm that reading the sysctl after a rejected write shows the previous valid configuration is preserved.

title: SECCOMP_RET_USER_NOTIF without attached listener returns -ENOSYS

∀ filter f returning SECCOMP_RET_USER_NOTIF for syscall s:

Call fcntl() from the sandboxed process and verify it returns -ENOSYS.
