Finally kill the supervisor, trigger the syscall, and confirm it returns ENOSYS (graceful degradation on supervisor death).

title: Limited Direct Execution — Timer Preemption
prompt_injection: ∀process P: P cannot prevent the OS from regaining control → timer interrupt fires within bounded slice duration
tensor_equation: ∀p∈Processes: timer_interrupt(p) → os_regains_control(p) ∧ bounded(slice_duration)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'The OS gives each process the illusion of its own CPU by time-slicing. The OS regains control via timer interrupts. Invariant: ∀process P: P cannot prevent the OS from regaining control.' — Section 1: CPU Virtualization
redteam_pattern: Start a goroutine with an infinite loop and no preemption point. Verify the orchestrator's context deadline still terminates the enclosing operation. If the loop blocks shutdown, the preemption invariant is violated.

title: Limited Direct Execution — Memory and Device Isolation
prompt_injection: ∀process P: P cannot access memory or devices it does not own
tensor_equation: ∀p∈Processes, ∀m∈Memory: owns(p,m) ∨ accessible(p,m) → permission(p,m)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Invariant: ∀process P: P cannot access memory or devices it does not own. Two modes: user mode (restricted) and kernel mode (privileged).' — Section 1: CPU Virtualization
redteam_pattern: Spawn a sandboxed process that attempts to read /etc/shadow or /proc/1/mem. If the read succeeds or escapes the worktree, the memory isolation invariant is violated.

title: Bounded Time Slice — Timer Interrupt Fires Within Slice Duration

tensor_equation: ∀t∈TimeSlice: timer_interrupt_fires(t) ∧ duration(t) ≤ max_slice_duration
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Invariant: ∀time slice: timer interrupt fires within the slice duration (bounded).' — Section 1: CPU Virtualization invariants
redteam_pattern: Create a dispatch task with a 5ms timeout that runs a 10ms CPU-bound loop. If the task is not preempted within the bounded slice, the timer-interrupt invariant is violated.

title: Address Translation — Virtual to Physical Mapping

tensor_equation: ∀p∈Processes, ∀a∈VA(p): translate(a) ∈ {valid_phys_addr(p, a), fault(p)}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Each process sees its own virtual address space. The OS + MMU translate virtual addresses to physical addresses via page tables. Invariant: ∀process P: ∀address a in P's VA, translate(a) is either a valid physical address or a fault.' — Section 2: Memory Virtualization
redteam_pattern: From a sandboxed process, attempt to dereference a pointer to another process's virtual address. If the read succeeds instead of faulting, the address translation invariant is violated.

title: Physical Page Isolation — No Cross-Process Memory Access

tensor_equation: ∀p,q∈Processes, p≠q: phys_pages(p) ∩ phys_pages(q) = ∅ ∨ shared_memory(p,q)

redteam_pattern: Run two processes where one writes a known pattern to memory and the other reads all its accessible pages. If the pattern appears in the second process's address space without explicit shared memory setup, page isolation is violated.

title: Mutex Mutual Exclusion — At Most One Thread Holds a Lock
prompt_injection: ∀lock L: at most one thread holds L at any time
tensor_equation: ∀t1,t2∈Threads, ∀L∈Locks: holds(t1, L) ∧ holds(t2, L) → t1 = t2
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Locks (mutexes): mutual exclusion — only one thread in the critical section at a time. Invariant: ∀lock L: at most one thread holds L at any time.' — Section 3: Concurrency
redteam_pattern: Spawn two goroutines that both attempt to acquire the same sync.Mutex and enter a critical section writing to a shared counter. Use -race to detect the data race. If both enter the critical section simultaneously, the mutual exclusion invariant is violated.

title: Lock Release — No Deadlock

redteam_pattern: Acquire lock A, then acquire lock B in goroutine 1 while goroutine 2 acquires B then A (circular wait). If both goroutines block indefinitely, the lock-release invariant is violated.

prompt_injection: ∀thread T: T eventually acquires L (no starvation, if fair)
tensor_equation: ∀t∈Threads, ∀L∈Locks: fair(L) → waiting(t, L) ⇒ ∃t'>t: holds(t, L, t')

verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Invariant: ∀condition variable CV: wait(CV, L) atomically releases L and blocks until signaled.' — Section 3: Concurrency invariants

title: Semaphore Bound — Counting Permits

title: Scheduling — Interactive Latency

title: Scheduling — Priority Weighted Sharing
prompt_injection: ∀priority p₁ > p₂: process with p₁ gets more CPU share than p₂, but p₂ is not starved

tensor_equation: ∀p∈Processes, ∀r∈Resources: access(p, r) → permission(p, r)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Invariant: ∀process P: P can only access resources for which it has explicit permission. Access control: file permissions (rwx), capabilities (CAP_SYS_*), user IDs.' — Section 7: Security

title: seccomp Policy — Syscall Filtering
prompt_injection: ∀syscall S: seccomp policy(S) ∈ {allow, trap, kill}
tensor_equation: ∀S∈Syscalls: seccomp_policy(S) ∈ {allow, trap, kill_permit}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Invariant: ∀syscall S: seccomp policy(S) ∈ {allow, trap, kill}. System call filtering: seccomp restricts which syscalls a process can make.' — Section 7: Security

title: Context Cancellation — Caller Can Always Cancel
prompt_injection: ∀blocking operation O: the caller can always cancel O (context cancellation)
tensor_equation: ∀O∈BlockingOps, ∀c∈Callers: cancel(c, O) → terminated(O) — bounded cancellation latency
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Context cancellation: the caller can always cancel. Enforcement: context.Context with WithTimeout/WithDeadline — CPU time bounded.' — Section 1: CPU Virtualization, Enforcement patterns

title: Parallel Composition Rule
prompt_injection: ∀programs S₁, S₂: ({P₁} S₁ {Q₁} ∧ {P₂} S₂ {Q₂} ∧ IF(S₁, S₂)) ⇒ {P₁ ∧ P₂} S₁ ∥ S₂ {Q₁ ∧ Q₂} where IF(S₁, S₂) means S₁ and S₂ are interference-free proof outlines
tensor_equation: ∀programs S₁, S₂: ({P₁} S₁ {Q₁} ∧ {P₂} S₂ {Q₂} ∧ IF(S₁, S₂)) ⇒ {P₁ ∧ P₂} S₁ ∥ S₂ {Q₁ ∧ Q₂}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'If you prove (a) each thread correct in isolation and (b) all cross-thread assertions interference-free, the parallel composition is correct.' — Section 3: The Composition Rule
redteam_pattern: Write two goroutines where each is sequentially correct (each Hoare triple holds in isolation) but T2's assignment statement invalidates T1's intermediate assertion. The parallel composition should be rejected by Owicki-Gries, but no data race exists (properly synchronized). Demonstrate that sequential correctness alone is insufficient for concurrent correctness.

title: Owicki-Gries Atomicity Definition
prompt_injection: ∀statement a: is_atomic(a) ⇔ (a is an assignment to a shared variable) ∨ (a is a read of a shared variable) ∨ (a is a Lock() call) ∨ (a is an Unlock() call). ∀statement s in Go between synchronization points: ¬is_atomic(s) UNLESS explicitly protected by a mutex or atomic operation
tensor_equation: ∀statement a: is_atomic(a) ⇔ (a is an assignment to a shared variable) ∨ (a is a read of a shared variable) ∨ (a is a Lock() call) ∨ (a is an Unlock() call)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'An assignment to a shared variable, a read of a shared variable, a Lock()/Unlock() call — any statement between synchronization points is NOT atomic unless explicitly protected by a mutex or atomic operation.' — Section 2: What counts as atomic in Owicki-Gries
redteam_pattern: Write Go code where a goroutine reads a shared variable, computes a result, and then writes the result back without a mutex. The sequence read-compute-write is treated implicitly as atomic, but Owicki-Gries requires explicit atomicity declaration. Demonstrate that without explicit mutex protection, the intermediate state between read and write allows interference from another thread.

title: Race Detector C-Consistent Race Coverage
prompt_injection: ∀access a₁, a₂: (conflicting(a₁, a₂) ∧ ¬happens-before(a₁, a₂) in current execution) ⇒ race detected by TSan
tensor_equation: ∀access a₁, a₂: (conflicting(a₁, a₂) ∧ ¬happens-before(a₁, a₂) in current execution) ⇒ race detected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Go's race detector (ThreadSanitizer/TSan) detects happens-before violations dynamically. It instruments memory accesses at runtime and reports pairs of conflicting accesses that are not ordered by happens-before in the CURRENT execution.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector CATCHES
redteam_pattern: Write two goroutines with a data race on an unprotected variable. Run once with `go test -race` — the race should be detected. Then write a test where the same race exists but in a code path that is not executed by the test — confirm the race detector does NOT report it.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Unexecuted Code Paths
prompt_injection: ∀race in code_path: ¬executed(code_path) ⇒ race NOT detected by TSan
tensor_equation: ∀race in code_path: ¬executed(code_path) ⇒ race NOT detected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Races in unexecuted code paths — only instrumented code that actually runs is checked. If a test doesn't cover a branch, races there are invisible.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #1
redteam_pattern: Write a function with a conditional branch: if debug_mode { unprotectedSharedWrite() }. The test does not set debug_mode=true. Run `go test -race` — the race in the dead branch is NOT detected. This demonstrates that `go test -race` passing does NOT imply race-freedom.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Non-Occurring Interleavings
prompt_injection: ∀race: ¬occurs_in_current_schedule(race) ⇒ race NOT detected by TSan
tensor_equation: ∀race: ¬occurs_in_current_schedule(race) ⇒ race NOT detected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Races whose problematic interleaving does not occur — TSan only sees ONE schedule. A race that requires a specific interleaving not exercised is invisible.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #2
redteam_pattern: Write two goroutines where a data race exists but requires a specific thread interleaving (e.g., goroutine A must be preempted after reading but before writing). Run 100 times with `go test -race` — the race may never be detected because the timing never triggers the problematic schedule. This is the fundamental difference from Owicki-Gries: TSan proves presence in ONE interleaving, O-G proves absence across ALL interleavings.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Synchronized Non-Data-Race Assertion Violations
prompt_injection: ∀violation: synchronized_but_assertion_broken(violation) ⇒ TSan does NOT detect violation
tensor_equation: ∀violation: synchronized_but_assertion_broken(violation) ⇒ TSan silent
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Owicki-Gries interference-freedom violations that are NOT data races — two threads can be properly synchronized (no happens-before violation) but still violate an assertion.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #3
redteam_pattern: Thread T1 sets x=1 under mutex, T2 reads x=1 under mutex and sets y=2 under a different mutex, T1 reads y expecting 0 under its first mutex. Each access is properly synchronized, so TSan sees no race. But the assertion 'y=0' in T1 is invalidated by T2 — this is a PROVABLE Owicki-Gries violation but NOT a data race.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Unmodeled Synchronization Primitives
prompt_injection: ∀pattern: ¬tracked_by_tsan(pattern) ⇒ races in pattern NOT detected by TSan
tensor_equation: ∀pattern: ¬tracked_by_tsan(pattern) ⇒ races in pattern NOT detected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Races hidden by synchronization primitives TSan doesn't model — condition variables, lost signals, spurious wakeups. TSan models mutex, channel, and atomic happens-before but not all synchronization patterns.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #4
redteam_pattern: Use sync.Cond for thread synchronization with a shared variable. Signal one goroutine that a shared variable has been updated, but the wakeup goroutine reads the variable without acquiring the associated mutex. TSan may not model the sync.Cond pattern and fail to detect the race between the signaling write and the woken read.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Goroutine Count Limit
prompt_injection: ∀race: goroutine_count > 8192 ⇒ race may NOT be detected by TSan
tensor_equation: ∀race: goroutine_count > 8192 ⇒ race may NOT be detected
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Races beyond the 8192 goroutine limit — the Go race detector has a finite shadow memory and goroutine ID space.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #5
redteam_pattern: Spawn 10000 goroutines in a tight loop, each performing a racy increment on a shared variable. The race detector's 8192-goroutine shadow memory limit may cause it to miss races among the goroutines beyond its tracking capacity. The test string 'goroutine_count > 8192' in the source definitively marks this as a known limit.

title: Race Detector Limitation: Static Potential Races
prompt_injection: ∀race: (∃schedule: occurs_in_schedule(race, schedule) ∧ ¬occurs_in_current_schedule(race)) ⇒ Owicki-Gries can detect, TSan cannot
tensor_equation: ∀race: ∃schedule_where(race_occurs) ∧ ¬occurs_in_current_schedule ⇒ O-G can detect, TSan cannot
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Static/potential races — Owicki-Gries can prove that a race COULD happen even if it didn't in a given run. The race detector cannot do this; it only sees what happened.' — Section 4: What the Go race detector DOES NOT catch #6
redteam_pattern: Write a program where a race is possible under a specific interleaving (e.g., thread A checks a condition, thread B changes the condition, thread A acts on the stale check). Run 1000 times with `go test -race` — the race never manifests. Owicki-Gries static analysis proves it COULD happen. TSan's dynamic analysis misses it.

title: Partial Correctness
prompt_injection: ∀execution e of program P with input x: terminates(e) ⇒ postcondition(result(e), x)
tensor_equation: ∀execution e of program P with input x: terminates(e) ⇒ postcondition(result(e), x)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'If the program terminates, the result satisfies the postcondition. Partial Correctness: ∀execution e of program P with input x: terminates(e) ⇒ postcondition(result(e), x). Go provides nothing for this.' — Section 6.1: Partial Correctness
redteam_pattern: Write a function that correctly computes a result when it terminates, but has an infinite loop bug for a specific input. Under partial correctness, the function is still 'correct' because the postcondition only needs to hold when termination occurs. Demonstrate that partial correctness alone is insufficient — total correctness (termination + partial correctness) is needed.

title: Mutual Exclusion
prompt_injection: ∀threads t₁, t₂, ∀time τ: t₁ ≠ t₂ ⇒ ¬(in_critical(t₁, τ) ∧ in_critical(t₂, τ))
tensor_equation: ∀threads t₁, t₂, ∀time τ: t₁ ≠ t₂ ⇒ ¬(in_critical(t₁, τ) ∧ in_critical(t₂, τ))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'At most one thread is executing in a critical section at any time. Mutual Exclusion: ∀threads t₁, t₂, ∀time τ: t₁ ≠ t₂ ⇒ ¬(in_critical(t₁, τ) ∧ in_critical(t₂, τ))' — Section 6.2: Mutual Exclusion
redteam_pattern: Implement custom synchronization using CAS operations (sync/atomic.CompareAndSwap) instead of sync.Mutex. Write a test where two goroutines both CAS-loop into what they believe is a critical section, but a third goroutine's intervening CAS causes both to be 'in_critical' simultaneously. Demonstrate that custom CAS-based locking can violate mutual exclusion without proper loop conditions.

title: Deadlock Freedom
prompt_injection: ∀reachable state s: ∃thread t: can_make_progress(t, s)
tensor_equation: ∀reachable state s: ∃thread t: can_make_progress(t, s)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'In every reachable state, at least one thread can make progress. Equivalently: no state where ALL threads are blocked waiting for each other. Deadlock Freedom: ∀reachable state s: ∃thread t: can_make_progress(t, s)' — Section 6.3: Deadlock Freedom. Note: Go runtime detects TOTAL deadlock only, not partial deadlock
redteam_pattern: Create a partial deadlock: goroutine A holds mutex M1 and waits for channel C, goroutine B holds mutex M2 and waits for M1, goroutine C holds channel C send and waits for M2. Some goroutines can still make progress (those not in the cycle), so Go's runtime does NOT panic with 'all goroutines are asleep'. But the cycle is a deadlock — goroutines A, B, C are indefinitely blocked.

title: Starvation Freedom
prompt_injection: ∀thread t: attempts_to_enter_critical_section(t) ⇒ eventually(enters_critical_section(t))
tensor_equation: ∀thread t: attempts_to_enter_critical_section(t) ⇒ eventually(enters_critical_section(t))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Every thread that attempts to enter a critical section eventually succeeds. No thread is indefinitely postponed. Starvation Freedom: ∀thread t: attempts_to_enter_critical_section(t) ⇒ eventually(enters_critical_section(t))' — Section 6.4: Starvation Freedom. Note: Go's sync.Mutex is NOT starvation-free
redteam_pattern: Spawn 100 goroutines all contending for a single sync.Mutex. Under high contention, one goroutine may be repeatedly preempted by the scheduler while holding or attempting the lock, allowing others to keep progressing ahead of it. Go's mutex starvation mode (Go 1.9+) mitigates after 1ms, but does not prove starvation freedom. Demonstrate that with enough goroutines and tight loops, a specific goroutine can be starved for an unbounded duration.

title: TLA+ Invariant Preservation Model Checking
prompt_injection: ∀invariant I, ∀action a in Next-state relation: (I ∧ pre(a)) ⇒ I' where I' is the invariant evaluated over the state after executing action a. TLC checks: Invariant ∧ Action ⇒ Invariant'
tensor_equation: ∀invariant I, ∀action a: (I ∧ pre(a)) ⇒ I'
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'TLC checks: Invariant ∧ Action ⇒ Invariant'. TLA+ model-checks for Owicki-Gries violations at the spec level. TLC exhaustively explores all interleavings.' — Section 5: TLA+ Relationship, Invariant Preservation
redteam_pattern: Write a TLA+ spec for a circuit breaker state machine with a wrong invariant (e.g., asserting that state transitions from Open directly to Closed without HalfOpen). TLC will generate a counterexample trace showing the invariant violation. Now implement the same bug in Go — the TestAX gate should catch the same violation. This demonstrates the TLA+ spec-code gap and validates the Owicki-Gries to TLA+ mapping.

title: TLA+ Spec-Code Gap
prompt_injection: ∀TLA+ spec S, ∀Go implementation code C: (S is correct in TLA+) ∧ (C implements S faithfully) ⇒ C is correct. However: (S is correct in TLA+) ∧ ¬(C implements S faithfully) ⇒ C may be incorrect. The spec-code gap means TLA+ proves the SPEC is correct, but the code must still faithfully implement the spec
tensor_equation: ∀TLA+ spec S, ∀Go code C: (correct(S) ∧ implements(C, S)) ⇒ correct(C)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'TLA+ proves the SPEC is correct. The code must still faithfully implement the spec. This is the spec-code gap. Both TLA+ and TestAX gates are needed: TLA+ for the design, TestAX for the implementation.' — Section 5: What TLA+ provides that Go cannot #4
redteam_pattern: Write a TLA+ spec that proves a concurrent counter is correct under all interleavings. Then write a Go implementation that has a subtle bug: the counter increment uses atomic.AddUint64 but the read uses a non-atomic load. The TLA+ spec is correct, the Go code has a spec-code gap bug, and the TestAX gate that runs concurrent increments and reads should catch the invariant violation.

title: Proof Template: Invariant Declaration
prompt_injection: ∀shared_state S, ∀new concurrent component C: invariant(S) must be declared as a tensor equation before proving correctness of C
tensor_equation: ∀shared_state S: invariant(S) must be declared
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Step 1: Declare the invariant — ∀shared_state S: invariant(S). Express as a tensor equation before proceeding with correctness proofs.' — Section 9: Owicki-Gries Proof Template, Step 1
redteam_pattern: Add a new concurrent component to orbit (e.g., a concurrent job queue) WITHOUT first declaring the invariant tensor equation. Demonstrate that without an explicit invariant, it is impossible to write an interference-freedom TestAX gate, because there is no predicate to assert. The missing invariant declaration propagates as a gap across all Owicki-Gries proof obligations.

title: Race Detector as Necessary but Not Sufficient Condition
prompt_injection: ∀program P: (P passes `go test -race`) ⇏ concurrently_correct(P). Equivalently: (P passes `go test -race`) is a NECESSARY but NOT SUFFICIENT condition for concurrent correctness under Owicki-Gries
tensor_equation: ∀program P: passes_race_detector(P) ⇏ concurrently_correct(P)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'go test -race passes is a NECESSARY condition for any TestAX gate that uses concurrent goroutines. It is NOT sufficient.' — Section 3 (race detector insufficient) and Section 9 Step 5. Also Section 4 summary table
redteam_pattern: Write a program that passes `go test -race` but has a provable Owicki-Gries interference-freedom violation: two threads are properly synchronized (no happens-before violation) but thread T2's statement invalidates the assertion 'state == INITIALIZING' in thread T1. The race detector passes. The Owicki-Gries proof catches the violation. Demonstrate the gap.

title: Concurrent Correctness Equivalence
prompt_injection: ∀program P: ConcurrentlyCorrect(P) ⇔ (∀thread t ∈ P: SequentiallyCorrect(t) ∧ ∀thread t₁, t₂ ∈ P, t₁ ≠ t₂: InterferenceFree(t₁, t₂))
tensor_equation: ∀program P: ConcurrentlyCorrect(P) ⇔ (∀t: SequentiallyCorrect(t) ∧ ∀t₁≠t₂: InterferenceFree(t₁, t₂))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'ConcurrentCorrectness(program) ⇔ ∀thread t ∈ program: SequentiallyCorrect(t) ∧ ∀thread t₁, t₂, t₁≠t₂: InterferenceFree(t₁, t₂)' — Section 3: The Composition Rule, central definitional equivalence
redteam_pattern: Write a program where all threads are sequentially correct but one pair fails interference freedom. Show that concurrent correctness fails. Then write a second program where both sequential correctness and interference freedom hold for all threads. Show that concurrent correctness is proven. This demonstrates the equivalence is both necessary and sufficient.

title: Table-level lock conflict exclusion
prompt_injection:
∀ t1, t2 ∈ active_transactions, ∀ table T, ∀ lock_modes m1, m2:
  (holds(t1, T, m1) ∧ holds(t2, T, m2) ∧ t1 ≠ t2)
  ⇒ ¬conflicts(m1, m2)
tensor_equation:
∀ t1, t2 ∈ active_transactions, ∀ table T, ∀ lock_modes m1, m2:
  (holds(t1, T, m1) ∧ holds(t2, T, m2) ∧ t1 ≠ t2)
  ⇒ ¬conflicts(m1, m2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Open two concurrent transactions, acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on table X in txn A, attempt ACCESS SHARE on table X in txn B, verify B blocks until A commits. Then test the inverse: ACCESS SHARE held first, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE requested second, verify B blocks.

title: Row-level lock conflict exclusion
prompt_injection:
∀ t1, t2 ∈ active_transactions, ∀ row R, ∀ row_lock_modes m1, m2:
  (holds(t1, R, m1) ∧ holds(t2, R, m2) ∧ t1 ≠ t2)
  ⇒ ¬row_conflicts(m1, m2)
tensor_equation:
∀ t1, t2 ∈ active_transactions, ∀ row R, ∀ row_lock_modes m1, m2:
  (holds(t1, R, m1) ∧ holds(t2, R, m2) ∧ t1 ≠ t2)
  ⇒ ¬row_conflicts(m1, m2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Open two concurrent transactions, SELECT FOR UPDATE on same row R in both, verify second transaction blocks. Then test all row-lock-mode combinations from Table 13.3 (FOR KEY SHARE, FOR SHARE, FOR NO KEY UPDATE, FOR UPDATE) against each other and verify the conflict matrix holds.

title: Row locks only block writers, never readers
prompt_injection:
∀ txn_reader, txn_writer, ∀ row R:
  (holds(txn_writer, R, FOR_UPDATE) ∧ txn_reader executes SELECT R)
  ⇒ txn_reader proceeds without blocking
tensor_equation:
∀ txn_reader, txn_writer, ∀ row R:
  (holds(txn_writer, R, FOR_UPDATE) ∧ txn_reader executes SELECT R)
  ⇒ txn_reader proceeds without blocking
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Open txn A with FOR UPDATE on row R, then in txn B execute a plain SELECT on the same row R while txn A holds the lock. Measure query completion time — verify B returns immediately without waiting for A to commit.

title: Lock held until transaction end
prompt_injection:
∀ locks l acquired by transaction t:
  release_time(l) ∈ {commit(t), rollback(t), rollback_to_savepoint(t, sp) where sp ≤ acquisition(l)}
tensor_equation:
∀ locks l acquired by transaction t:
  release_time(l) ∈ {commit(t), rollback(t), rollback_to_savepoint(t, sp) where sp ≤ acquisition(l)}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on table T in a transaction, then attempt to acquire ACCESS SHARE on T from another session before committing — verify second session blocks. Then commit and verify the lock is released. Repeat with savepoint: acquire lock after savepoint, rollback to savepoint, verify lock is released.

title: Self-conflict is impossible
prompt_injection:
∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ lock_mode m1, m2:
  (holds(t, T, m1) ⇒ can_acquire(t, T, m2))
tensor_equation:
∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ lock_mode m1, m2:
  (holds(t, T, m1) ⇒ can_acquire(t, T, m2))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: In a single transaction, acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on table T, then immediately acquire ACCESS SHARE on the same table T. Verify no error or blocking occurs. Repeat for all combinations of held lock mode and requested lock mode from Table 13.2 within a single transaction.

title: FOR UPDATE on changed row errors under SERIALIZABLE
prompt_injection:
∀ t ∈ transactions with isolation ∈ {REPEATABLE_READ, SERIALIZABLE},
  ∀ row R read by t in a SELECT FOR UPDATE:
  (version(R, t.snapshot_start) ≠ version(R, now))
  ⇒ error_raised(t, "could not serialize access")
tensor_equation:
∀ t ∈ transactions with isolation ∈ {REPEATABLE_READ, SERIALIZABLE},
  ∀ row R read by t in a SELECT FOR UPDATE:
  (version(R, t.snapshot_start) ≠ version(R, now))
  ⇒ error_raised(t, "could not serialize access")
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Set isolation to REPEATABLE READ. In txn A, update row R and commit. In txn B (started before A committed), SELECT FOR UPDATE on row R. Verify error 'could not serialize access' is raised. Repeat with SERIALIZABLE isolation. Also test that no error occurs for unchanged rows.

title: Deadlock detection is guaranteed
prompt_injection:
∀ wait-for-graph G across transactions:
  (G contains a cycle) ⇒ (postgresql detects ∧ aborts at least one t in cycle)
tensor_equation:
∀ wait-for-graph G across transactions:
  (G contains a cycle) ⇒ (postgresql detects ∧ aborts at least one t in cycle)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Create classic bank-transfer deadlock: txn A locks row 1 then waits for row 2; txn B locks row 2 then waits for row 1. Verify PostgreSQL detects the cycle and aborts exactly one of the two transactions within a bounded time (deadlock_timeout). Verify the surviving transaction's locks remain intact.

title: Lock wait is unbounded in absence of deadlock
prompt_injection:
∀ t ∈ transactions waiting for lock L, ∀ time τ:
  (¬deadlock_detected(t)) ⇒ t continues waiting
tensor_equation:
∀ t ∈ transactions waiting for lock L, ∀ time τ:
  (¬deadlock_detected(t)) ⇒ t continues waiting
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Hold ACCESS SHARE on table T in txn A for a long duration. In txn B, attempt ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on T. Verify B waits indefinitely (no timeout, no error). Set lock_timeout in txn B to a finite value and verify the wait is interrupted when timeout expires. Also verify `nowait` causes immediate failure instead of waiting.

title: Advisory lock self-grant always succeeds
prompt_injection:
∀ session s, ∀ advisory_lock_id id:
  holds(s, id) ⇒ acquire(s, id) == immediate_success
tensor_equation:
∀ session s, ∀ advisory_lock_id id:
  holds(s, id) ⇒ acquire(s, id) == immediate_success
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Acquire a session-level advisory lock `pg_advisory_lock(42)` in session s. Then from the same session, attempt `pg_advisory_lock(42)` again. Verify immediate success with no blocking. Also test transaction-level: acquire `pg_advisory_xact_lock(42)` in a transaction, then acquire it again in the same transaction — verify immediate success.

title: Lock memory is finite and bounded
prompt_injection:
∀ granted_locks G:
  |G| ≤ max_locks_per_transaction × max_connections + additional_shared_memory
tensor_equation:
∀ granted_locks G:
  |G| ≤ max_locks_per_transaction × max_connections + additional_shared_memory
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Set `max_locks_per_transaction` to a low value (e.g., 64) and exhaust the lock table by acquiring many distinct advisory locks in a single session. Verify that lock acquisition fails with 'out of shared memory' once the limit is reached. Then verify that PostgreSQL refuses all new lock acquisition (including regular table locks) after exhaustion.

title: Session-level advisory locks survive rollback
prompt_injection:
∀ session s, ∀ advisory_lock_id id, ∀ transaction t in s:
  acquire_session_level(s, id) during t ∧ rollback(t)
  ⇒ holds(s, id) after rollback
tensor_equation:
∀ session s, ∀ advisory_lock_id id, ∀ transaction t in s:
  acquire_session_level(s, id) during t ∧ rollback(t)
  ⇒ holds(s, id) after rollback
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Acquire `pg_advisory_lock(42)` in a session, then rollback the current transaction. From a second session, try to acquire `pg_advisory_lock(42)` — verify it blocks because the first session still holds the lock. Then verify that `pg_advisory_xact_lock(42)` (transaction-level) is released on rollback, so the second session can acquire it immediately.

title: Consistent lock ordering prevents deadlock
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Create two transactions both locking rows in ascending order (e.g., row 1 then row 2). Run them concurrently and verify no deadlock occurs. Then reverse the order in one transaction (row 2 then row 1) and verify deadlock is detected. Test with advisory locks, row locks, and table locks to confirm ordering principle applies universally.

title: Most restrictive lock first
prompt_injection:
∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ object O, ∀ lock_modes m1 first, m2 later:
  acquire(t, O, m1) followed by acquire(t, O, m2)
  ⇒ mode_strength(m1) ≥ mode_strength(m2)
  (recommended: take the strongest lock you'll need up front)
tensor_equation:
∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ object O, ∀ lock_modes m1 first, m2 later:
  acquire(t, O, m1) followed by acquire(t, O, m2)
  ⇒ mode_strength(m1) ≥ mode_strength(m2)
  (recommended: take the strongest lock you'll need up front)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: 'Two transactions cannot hold locks of conflicting modes on the same table at the same time.' (Section 13.3.1)
redteam_pattern: Acquire ACCESS SHARE on table T, then attempt to upgrade to ACCESS EXCLUSIVE while another transaction holds ACCESS SHARE on T. Verify the upgrade blocks. Then create two transactions both holding ACCESS SHARE and both attempting to upgrade to ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — verify deadlock is detected. As a positive test, acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE first and then ACCESS SHARE — verify it succeeds.

title: MVCC non-blocking read/write guarantee
prompt_injection:
∀ readers r, writers w operating on same relation:
  r acquires no lock that conflicts with w's lock ∧
  w acquires no lock that conflicts with r's lock
⇒ reading never blocks writing ∧ writing never blocks reading
tensor_equation:
∀ readers r, writers w operating on same relation:
  r acquires no lock that conflicts with w's lock ∧
  w acquires no lock that conflicts with r's lock
⇒ reading never blocks writing ∧ writing never blocks reading

title: Subclass must weaken precondition and strengthen postcondition (Liskov)

title: Hazard analysis must precede code; safety constraints must be formalized
