  AND the parser is thread-safe (multiple instances can run concurrently)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Lemon also generates a parser which is reentrant and thread-safe.
redteam_pattern: Spawn 64 concurrent threads, each creating a separate parser instance and parsing a different SQL statement. Verify no race conditions, data corruption, or crashes. Then verify that a single parser instance can be paused mid-parse (via interruption) and a new SQL statement parsed in a different instance without interference.

title: No memory leak on parse error
prompt_injection:
∀ syntax errors encountered during parsing:
  all memory allocated for the parse tree up to the error point is freed
  (no leak; non-terminal destructor runs for all partially-constructed nodes)
tensor_equation:
∀ syntax errors encountered during parsing:
  all memory allocated for the parse tree up to the error point is freed
  (no leak; non-terminal destructor runs for all partially-constructed nodes)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Lemon defines the concept of a non-terminal destructor so that it does not leak memory when syntax errors are encountered.
redteam_pattern: Fuzz the SQL parser with 100,000 intentionally malformed SQL statements, tracking heap memory before and after each parse attempt. Verify that the heap delta is zero or bounded (within allocator overhead) across all parse failures, proving no memory accumulates from partial parse trees.

title: Per-table, per-index B-tree isolation
prompt_injection:
∀ tables T and indices I in a database D:
  T has a distinct B-tree BT
  AND each index i ∈ I has a distinct B-tree Bi
  AND BT ≠ Bi for all i
  AND all B-trees {BT} ∪ {Bi} are stored in the same disk file
tensor_equation:
∀ tables T and indices I in a database D:
  T has a distinct B-tree BT
  AND each index i ∈ I has a distinct B-tree Bi
  AND BT ≠ Bi for all i
  AND all B-trees {BT} ∪ {Bi} are stored in the same disk file
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Separate B-trees are used for each table and each index in the database. All B-trees are stored in the same disk file.
redteam_pattern: Create a database with 10 tables, each with 2 indices. Use sqlite3_db_filename() and the pager's page-ownership map to verify that each table and index has a unique root page number, and that all pages reside in the same single database file. Verify that corrupting one table's B-tree root page does not affect other tables' B-tree traversals.

title: File format backward compatibility
prompt_injection:
∀ database files created by SQLite version V:
  the file is readable and writable by all SQLite versions V' where V' ≥ V
  (the file format is stable and guaranteed forward-compatible)
tensor_equation:
∀ database files created by SQLite version V:
  the file is readable and writable by all SQLite versions V' where V' ≥ V
  (the file format is stable and guaranteed forward-compatible)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The file format details are stable and well-defined and are guaranteed to be compatible moving forward.
redteam_pattern: Create a database file using SQLite 3.0.0 (the first version 3 release). Open it in the latest SQLite version, perform a write transaction, close, and reopen. Verify the file is readable and data integrity is maintained across version boundaries. Repeat for at least 5 major version pairs spanning the full release history.

title: Page size is a power of two in [512, 65536]
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The default page_size is 4096 bytes but can be any power of two between 512 and 65536 bytes.
redteam_pattern: Attempt to create a database with page size 1000 (not a power of two). Attempt page sizes 256 and 131072 (outside [512, 65536]). Verify sqlite3_errcode() returns SQLITE_ERROR for invalid page sizes. Attempt every valid power of two: 512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384, 32768, 65536 and verify all succeed.

title: Atomic commit via pager abstraction
prompt_injection:
∀ transactions T:
  either all pages modified by T are durably written to disk (commit)
  OR no pages modified by T persist (rollback)
  (the pager provides atomic commit)
tensor_equation:
∀ transactions T:
  either all pages modified by T are durably written to disk (commit)
  OR no pages modified by T persist (rollback)
  (the pager provides atomic commit)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The page cache also provides the rollback and atomic commit abstraction.
redteam_pattern: Use a VFS test double that injects a crash (SIGKILL or fsync failure) at every page write during a multi-page transaction. After recovery, verify the database is in a consistent state: either all pages from the transaction are present (verified by checksum) or none are. Repeat 10,000 times with random crash injection points using the SQLite fossil test harness.

title: All disk I/O through VFS
prompt_injection:
∀ file operations (open, read, write, close, sync, lock, unlink):
  the operation goes through the VFS abstraction layer
  NO component below the VFS interface makes direct OS syscalls
tensor_equation:
∀ file operations (open, read, write, close, sync, lock, unlink):
  the operation goes through the VFS abstraction layer
  NO component below the VFS interface makes direct OS syscalls
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In order to provide portability across operating systems, SQLite uses an abstract object called the VFS.
redteam_pattern: Install a VFS shim that logs every file operation. Run a full SQLite test suite (all TH3 tests) and verify that every open, read, write, close, sync, lock, and unlink call is captured by the VFS shim. Use dtruss/strace to confirm zero direct syscalls bypassing the VFS layer.

title: sqlite3_step() termination guarantees
prompt_injection:
∀ calls to sqlite3_step(S) where S is a prepared statement:
  the call returns within finite time with exactly one of:
  - SQLITE_ROW (a result row is available)
  - SQLITE_DONE (execution complete)
  - SQLITE_ERROR (fatal error)
  - SQLITE_INTERRUPT (externally interrupted)
tensor_equation:
∀ calls to sqlite3_step(S) where S is a prepared statement:
  the call returns within finite time with exactly one of:
  - SQLITE_ROW (a result row is available)
  - SQLITE_DONE (execution complete)
  - SQLITE_ERROR (fatal error)
  - SQLITE_INTERRUPT (externally interrupted)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The sqlite3_step() interface passes a bytecode program into the virtual machine, and runs the program until it either completes, or forms a row of result to be returned, or hits a fatal error, or is interrupted.
redteam_pattern: Construct a prepared statement known to trigger a long-running query (e.g., a recursive CTE that generates a large result set). Call sqlite3_step() in a loop with a 1-second timeout via sqlite3_progress_handler(). Verify that every call to sqlite3_step() returns one of the four allowed status codes and never hangs indefinitely. Also verify that no return value outside the allowed set is ever produced.

title: No test code in production builds
prompt_injection:
∀ source files in src/ whose name begins with "test":
  the file is excluded from standard (non-test) builds
tensor_equation:
∀ source files in src/ whose name begins with "test":
  the file is excluded from standard (non-test) builds
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Files in the 'src/' folder of the source tree whose names begin with test are for testing only and are not included in a standard build of the library.
redteam_pattern: Build SQLite with the standard (non-test) build configuration. Verify that the resulting binary/symbol table contains no symbols from files in src/ whose names begin with 'test'. Use nm or objdump to enumerate all compiled symbols and grep for test-related function names — confirm zero matches.

title: Page size is uniform and power-of-two bounded
prompt_injection: For all pages p in pages(db): size(p) = page_size(db) and page_size(db) is an element of {2^k | 512 <= 2^k <= 65536}
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ pages(db): size(p) = page_size(db) ∧ page_size(db) ∈ {2^k | 512 ≤ 2^k ≤ 65536}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: All pages within the same database are the same size. The size of a page is a power of two between 512 and 65536 inclusive.
redteam_pattern: Create a SQLite database, then modify a single page to have a different size than the declared page_size. Verify that b-tree navigation fails (wrong offset calculations) or that page-level reads produce garbage data.

title: Every valid database begins with a 16-byte magic string
prompt_injection: For all valid_sqlite_db: first_16_bytes(db) = 0x53514C69746520666F726D6174203300
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ valid_sqlite_db: first_16_bytes(db) = 0x53514C69746520666F726D6174203300
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Every valid SQLite database file begins with the following 16 bytes (in hex): 53 51 4c 69 74 65 20 66 6f 72 6d 61 74 20 33 00.
redteam_pattern: Take a valid SQLite 3 database file and zero out the first 16 bytes. Pass it to sqlite3_open() and verify that SQLITE_NOTADB is returned rather than the file being silently opened with garbage data.

title: B-tree page type flag is restricted to 4 valid values
prompt_injection: For all btree_pages p in db: page_type_byte(p) is an element of {0x02, 0x05, 0x0a, 0x0d}
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ btree_pages(db): page_type_byte(p) ∈ {0x02, 0x05, 0x0a, 0x0d}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A value of 2 (0x02) means the page is an interior index b-tree page. A value of 5 (0x05) means the page is an interior table b-tree page. A value of 10 (0x0a) means the page is a leaf index b-tree page. A value of 13 (0x0d) means the page is a leaf table b-tree page. Any other value for the b-tree page type is an error.
redteam_pattern: Flip a single bit in the page type byte of a b-tree page (e.g., 0x02 to 0x03) in a valid SQLite database file. Verify that SQLite's integrity_check detects the invalid page type or that reading from that table produces an error.

title: Interior b-tree pages hold at least 2 keys (except page 1)
prompt_injection: For all interior_btree_pages p in db minus page_1: key_count(p) >= 2
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ interior_btree_pages(db) \ {page_1}: key_count(p) ≥ 2
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The number of keys on an interior b-tree page, K, is almost always at least 2... The only exception is when page 1 is an interior b-tree page.
redteam_pattern: Craft a database file where an interior b-tree page (not page 1) has exactly 0 or 1 keys. Run PRAGMA integrity_check and verify that the checker reports the structural violation. Confirm that a naive search on this page degenerates to linear scan.

title: All children of an interior b-tree have the same depth
prompt_injection: For all interior_btree_pages p in db: for all children c1, c2 of p: depth(c1) = depth(c2)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ interior_btree_pages(db): ∀ c1, c2 ∈ children(p): depth(c1) = depth(c2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In a well-formed database, all children of an interior b-tree have the same depth.
redteam_pattern: Construct a database where one branch of a b-tree is deeper than another (e.g., manually link a page into the tree at a different depth). Run PRAGMA integrity_check and verify the balance violation is detected. Confirm that range scans produce incomplete results.

title: Keys within a b-tree page are unique and ascending
prompt_injection: For all btree_pages p in db: for all i, j in [0, cell_count(p)-1] where i < j: key(cell_i) < key(cell_j)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ btree_pages(db): ∀ i, j ∈ [0, cell_count(p)-1], i < j: key(cell_i) < key(cell_j)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: All keys within the same page are unique and are logically organized in ascending order from left to right.
redteam_pattern: Insert a duplicate key or an out-of-order key into a b-tree page's cell pointer array. Execute a binary search for a value that should be between the two keys and verify that the wrong result (or no result) is returned.

title: Child-pointer key range is partitioned
prompt_injection: For all interior_btree_pages p in db and for all keys X in keys(p): for all k in keys(left_child(X)): k <= X, and for all k in keys(right_child(X)): k > X
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ interior_btree_pages(db): ∀ key X ∈ keys(p): (∀ k ∈ keys(left_child(X)): k ≤ X) ∧ (∀ k ∈ keys(right_child(X)): k > X)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: For any key X, pointers to the left of X refer to b-tree pages on which all keys are less than or equal to X. Pointers to the right of X refer to pages where all keys are greater than X.
redteam_pattern: Modify a child page in an index b-tree to contain a key that violates the parent's partition separator (e.g., place key 42 in the left child when the separator is 40). Issue a point query for key 42 and verify that the row is silently missed.

title: Every b-tree page has at most one parent
prompt_injection: For all btree_pages p in db: the cardinality of parents(p) is less than or equal to 1
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ btree_pages(db): |parents(p)| ≤ 1
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Every b-tree page has at most one parent b-tree page.
redteam_pattern: Create a database where a single b-tree page is referenced by two different parent pages (a DAG instead of a tree). Run PRAGMA integrity_check and verify that the cycle/DAG is detected. Confirm that auto-vacuum would leave a dangling reference.

title: All pages in a b-tree are of the same type (table or index)
prompt_injection: For all btree bt in btree_set(db): for all pages p1, p2 in pages(bt): type(p1) = type(p2) and type(p) is an element of {table_btree, index_btree}
tensor_equation: ∀ btree bt ∈ btree_set(db): ∀ p1, p2 ∈ pages(bt): type(p1) = type(p2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: All pages within each complete b-tree are of the same type: either table or index.
redteam_pattern: Modify a page pointer in a table b-tree interior page to point to an index b-tree leaf page. Read from the table and verify that rowid extraction produces garbage or that the query fails with a corruption error.

title: sqlite_schema root page is always page 1
prompt_injection: root_page(sqlite_schema_table) = 1
tensor_equation: root_page(sqlite_schema_table) = 1
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The b-tree corresponding to the sqlite_schema table is always a table b-tree and always has a root page of 1.
redteam_pattern: Move the sqlite_schema root page to page 2 and set page 1 to a non-table-b-tree page type. Attempt to open the database with sqlite3_open() and verify that SQLITE_CORRUPT or an error is returned rather than silent failure.

title: Payload fraction header bytes are fixed constants
prompt_injection: For all valid_sqlite_db: max_embedded_payload_frac(db) = 64 and min_embedded_payload_frac(db) = 32 and leaf_payload_frac(db) = 32
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ valid_sqlite_db: max_embedded_payload_frac(db) = 64 ∧ min_embedded_payload_frac(db) = 32 ∧ leaf_payload_frac(db) = 32
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The maximum and minimum embedded payload fractions and the leaf payload fraction values must be 64, 32, and 32.
redteam_pattern: Modify the max_embedded_payload_frac byte in the database header from 64 to 128. Insert a row with a payload that should overflow. Verify that the overflow threshold calculation changes and the reader interprets wrong byte ranges as cell content.

title: Freeblock chain is ordered by increasing offset
prompt_injection: For all btree_pages p in db: for all freeblocks fb_i, fb_j in freeblocks(p): if fb_i precedes fb_j in the chain then offset(fb_i) < offset(fb_j)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ btree_pages(db): ∀ fb_i, fb_j ∈ freeblocks(p): fb_i precedes fb_j in chain ⇒ offset(fb_i) < offset(fb_j)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Freeblocks are always connected in order of increasing offset.
redteam_pattern: Swizzle the freeblock pointers on a b-tree page so that the chain is out of order (e.g., a freeblock at offset 200 points to one at offset 100). Trigger a page defragmentation via INSERT/DELETE operations and verify that overlapping allocations occur.

title: Fragment bytes per b-tree page bounded at 60
prompt_injection: For all well_formed_btree_pages p in db: fragment_bytes(p) <= 60
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ well_formed_btree_pages(db): fragment_bytes(p) ≤ 60
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In a well-formed b-tree page, the total number of bytes in fragments may not exceed 60.
redteam_pattern: Repeatedly insert and delete variable-length rows on a single page to create more than 60 fragment bytes. Run PRAGMA integrity_check and verify that the excessive fragmentation is flagged. Confirm that subsequent inserts on the page eventually fail due to unusable space.

title: At least one cell precedes the first freeblock on a well-formed page
prompt_injection: For all well_formed_btree_pages p in db: if cell_count(p) > 0 then min_cell_offset(p) < first_freeblock_offset(p)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ well_formed_btree_pages(db): cell_count(p) > 0 ⇒ min_cell_offset(p) < first_freeblock_offset(p)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In a well-formed b-tree page, there will always be at least one cell before the first freeblock.
redteam_pattern: Modify a b-tree page so that the first freeblock starts at an offset lower than the minimum cell pointer offset. Insert a new cell and verify that the cell pointer array writes into freeblock space, causing overlapping regions.

title: No page appears more than once in a single rollback journal
prompt_injection: For all journals j: for all page_records pr1, pr2 in j: if pr1 != pr2 then page_number(pr1) != page_number(pr2)
tensor_equation: ∀ journal j: ∀ pr1, pr2 ∈ page_records(j): pr1 ≠ pr2 ⇒ page_number(pr1) ≠ page_number(pr2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The same page may not appear more than once within a single rollback journal.
redteam_pattern: Craft a rollback journal where the same page number appears twice with different content. Simulate a crash recovery by replaying the journal and verify that the second (older) entry overwrites the first (newer), producing a database state that was never committed.

title: WAL frame validity requires matching salts and cumulative checksum
prompt_injection: For all frames f in wal: valid(f) is true if and only if salt1(f) = salt1(wal_header) and salt2(f) = salt2(wal_header) and checksum(f) = cumulative_checksum(wal_header, frames_up_to(f))
tensor_equation: ∀ f ∈ frames(wal): valid(f) ⇔ (salt1(f) = salt1(wal_header) ∧ salt2(f) = salt2(wal_header) ∧ checksum(f) = cumulative_checksum(wal_header, frames_up_to(f)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A frame is considered valid if and only if the salt-1 and salt-2 values in the frame-header match salt values in the wal-header, and the checksum values in the final 8 bytes of the frame-header exactly match the checksum computed consecutively
redteam_pattern: Append a frame from a prior WAL epoch (with old salt values) to the end of a current WAL file. Open the database in WAL mode and verify that the stale frame is rejected due to salt mismatch. Corrupt the checksum of the last frame and verify that the entire WAL suffix is invalidated.

title: WAL salt-1 increments on reset, invalidating old frames
prompt_injection: For all wal w: after_reset(w) implies salt1(w) > salt1(w_before_reset)
tensor_equation: ∀ wal w: after_reset(w) ⇒ salt1(w) > salt1(w_before_reset)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: At the start of the first new write transaction, the WAL header salt-1 value is incremented and the salt-2 value is randomized. These changes to the salts invalidate old frames in the WAL
redteam_pattern: Force a WAL checkpoint and examine the WAL header afterward. Verify that salt-1 has incremented and salt-2 has been randomized. Attempt to replay old frames from before the checkpoint and confirm they are rejected by salt mismatch.

title: Checkpoint xSync provides write barrier ordering
prompt_injection: For all checkpoints c: all_writes_before(xSync_1) happens_before all_writes_after(xSync_1) and all_writes_before(xSync_2) happens_before all_writes_after(xSync_2)
tensor_equation: ∀ checkpoint c: all_writes_before(xSync_1) happens_before all_writes_after(xSync_1) ∧ all_writes_before(xSync_2) happens_before all_writes_after(xSync_2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: On a checkpoint, the WAL is first flushed to persistent storage using the xSync method of the VFS. Then valid content of the WAL is transferred into the database file. Finally, the database is flushed to persistent storage using another xSync method call.
redteam_pattern: Inject a crash at each of the three phases of a checkpoint: (1) after WAL flush but before copy, (2) after copy but before DB flush, (3) after DB flush but before WAL reset. Verify that phase 1 loses the WAL (no copy occurred), phase 2 loses committed transactions (DB not durable), and phase 3 is safe.

title: WAL reader snapshot isolation via mxFrame
prompt_injection: For all readers r and transactions t_start: for all reads by r after t_start: data_seen(r) = db_state_at_frame(mxFrame(r))
tensor_equation: ∀ reader r, transaction t_start: ∀ reads by r after t_start: data_seen(r) = db_state_at_frame(mxFrame(r))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The reader uses this recorded mxFrame value for all subsequent read operations. New transactions can be appended to the WAL, but as long as the reader uses its original mxFrame value and ignores subsequently appended content, the reader will see a consistent snapshot
redteam_pattern: Open two connections in WAL mode. On connection 1, begin a transaction and read the mxFrame value. On connection 2, insert a row and commit. On connection 1, issue a new query and verify that the new row is NOT visible (snapshot isolation holds). If the mxFrame advances mid-transaction, verify that the reader sees a partial commit.

title: Ptrmap back-pointers cover every page following the ptrmap page
prompt_injection: For all ptrmap_pages P at page_number N: for all i in [1, J]: entry_i(P) describes back_link(page_{N+i})
tensor_equation: ∀ ptrmap_page P at page_number N: ∀ i ∈ [1, J]: entry_i(P) describes back_link(page_{N+i})
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Each 5-byte entry on a ptrmap page provides back-link information about one of the pages that immediately follow the pointer map.
redteam_pattern: Corrupt a single ptrmap entry to point to the wrong parent page. Trigger an auto-vacuum operation and verify that the wrong parent pointer is updated, leaving the correct parent with a dangling reference. Run PRAGMA integrity_check and confirm the corruption is detected.

title: All b-tree root pages precede non-root pages in ptrmap databases
prompt_injection: For all databases with ptrmap_enabled: for all root_pages rp and non_root_pages nrp: page_number(rp) < page_number(nrp)
tensor_equation: ∀ db with ptrmap_enabled(db): ∀ rp ∈ root_pages(db), ∀ nrp ∈ non_root_pages(db): page_number(rp) < page_number(nrp)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In any database file that contains ptrmap pages, all b-tree root pages must come before any non-root b-tree page, cell payload overflow page, or freelist page.
redteam_pattern: Place a non-root b-tree page at a page number lower than a root page in a ptrmap-enabled database. Attempt auto-vacuum and verify that the root page is moved (since auto-vacuum cannot update sqlite_schema), making the table or index permanently inaccessible.

title: Schema cookie change detection guarantees prepared statement consistency
prompt_injection: For all prepared statements s compiled against schema_cookie C: if schema_cookie(db) != C then s must be reprepared before execution, else s aborts with SQLITE_SCHEMA
tensor_equation: ∀ prepared_stmt s compiled against schema_cookie C: if schema_cookie(db) ≠ C then s must be reprepared before execution, else s aborts with SQLITE_SCHEMA
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: When the database schema changes, the statement must be reprepared. When a prepared statement runs, it first checks the schema cookie to ensure the value is the same as when the statement was prepared.
redteam_pattern: Prepare a SELECT * statement against a table with 3 columns. Execute ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN to add a 4th column. Attempt to execute the original prepared statement without reprepare and verify that SQLITE_SCHEMA is returned, preventing wrong-column-offset reads.

title: Text encoding is restricted to exactly 3 values
prompt_injection: For all valid_sqlite_db: text_encoding(db) is an element of {1, 2, 3}
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ valid_sqlite_db: text_encoding(db) ∈ {1, 2, 3}
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A value of 1 means UTF-8. A value of 2 means UTF-16le. A value of 3 means UTF-16be. No other values are allowed.
redteam_pattern: Change the text encoding byte in the database header from 1 (UTF-8) to 4 (invalid). Open the database and query a text column. Verify that SQLite returns an error or that string comparisons produce wrong ordering, breaking index lookups.

title: Reserved header bytes must be zero
prompt_injection: For all valid_sqlite_db: for all reserved_header_bytes b in db: b = 0
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ valid_sqlite_db: ∀ b ∈ reserved_header_bytes(db): b = 0
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: All other bytes of the database file header are reserved for future expansion and must be set to zero.
redteam_pattern: Set a reserved byte in the database header to a non-zero value. Attempt to open the database with a future version of SQLite that assigns meaning to that byte and verify forward-compatibility hazards. Open with current SQLite and verify it does not reject the file (latent hazard).

title: Minimum usable page size is 480 bytes
prompt_injection: For all valid_sqlite_db: usable_page_size(db) >= 480
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ valid_sqlite_db: usable_page_size(db) ≥ 480
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The usable size is not allowed to be less than 480. In other words, if the page size is 512, then the reserved space size cannot exceed 32.
redteam_pattern: Create a database with page size 512 and set the reserved space to 33 (usable = 479). Attempt to create a table and insert rows. Verify that SQLite rejects the configuration or that b-tree operations fail due to insufficient space for the minimum page structure.

title: Index b-tree key overflow guarantees minimum fanout of 4
prompt_injection: For all interior_index_btree_pages p in db: key_count(p) >= 4
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ interior_index_btree_pages(db): key_count(p) ≥ 4
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Large keys on index b-trees are split up into overflow pages so that no single key uses more than one fourth of the available storage space on the page and hence every internal page is able to store at least 4 keys.
redteam_pattern: Create an index with a very large key (e.g., 1500-byte VARCHAR) that exceeds 1/4 of the usable page space. Verify that the key is split into overflow pages and that the interior index page still holds at least 4 keys, maintaining O(log_4 n) fanout.

title: Overflow page chain terminated by zero pointer
prompt_injection: For all overflow_chains c: last_page(c).next_page_number = 0
tensor_equation: ∀ overflow_chain c: last_page(c).next_page_number = 0
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The first four bytes of each overflow page are a big-endian integer which is the page number of the next page in the chain, or zero for the final page in the chain.
redteam_pattern: Create a circular overflow chain where page A points to B, B points to A. Attempt to read a row with a large payload that traverses the overflow chain. Verify that the reader hangs or exhausts memory. Create a non-terminated chain where the last page's next_page_number is non-zero and points to a non-existent page.

title: AUTOINCREMENT keys strictly exceed the sequence maximum
prompt_injection: For all tables t with AUTOINCREMENT: for all new rows r inserted into t: rowid(r) > sqlite_sequence.seq(t)
tensor_equation: ∀ table t with AUTOINCREMENT: ∀ new_row r inserted into t: rowid(r) > sqlite_sequence.seq(t)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: New automatically generated integer primary keys for AUTOINCREMENT tables are guaranteed to be larger than the sqlite_sequence.seq field for that table.
redteam_pattern: Create an AUTOINCREMENT table, insert rows up to rowid 100, then manually set sqlite_sequence.seq to 50. Insert a new row and verify that the new rowid is > 50 (not > 100), demonstrating that a regressed sequence counter produces a reused rowid. Confirm that foreign key references break.

title: WAL file always grows forward; checksums detect stale frames
prompt_injection: For all wal w: append_position(w) is monotonically non-decreasing across the lifetime of w
tensor_equation: ∀ wal w: append_position(w) is monotonically non-decreasing across the lifetime of w
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A WAL always grows from beginning toward the end. Checksums and counters attached to each frame are used to determine which frames within the WAL are valid and which are leftovers from prior checkpoints.
redteam_pattern: Force a WAL checkpoint that resets the WAL file. After the reset, examine the WAL file and verify that new frames are appended at the new start position, not overwriting old frames. Verify that old frame data is cryptographically invalidated by salt-1 increment rather than by zeroing.

title: Ptrmap page placement is deterministic and exclusive
prompt_injection: For all databases with ptrmap_enabled: ptrmap_pages(db) = {page_{k*(U/5)+3} | k >= 0} minus lock_byte_page_if_collision
tensor_equation: ∀ db with ptrmap_enabled(db): ptrmap_pages(db) = {page_{k*(U/5)+3} | k ≥ 0} \ lock_byte_page_if_collision
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: In a database that uses ptrmap pages, all pages at locations identified by the computation in the previous paragraph must be ptrmap page and no other page may be a ptrmap page.
redteam_pattern: Create a ptrmap-enabled database and compute the expected ptrmap page positions using the formula k*(U/5)+3. Verify that every computed position contains a ptrmap page and no other page does. Modify a non-ptrmap page to occupy a ptrmap slot and verify that auto-vacuum reads garbage as ptrmap entries.

title: Lock state machine — mutual exclusion
prompt_injection: ∀ p, q ∈ processes, ∀ db ∈ databases: (lock_state(p, db) = EXCLUSIVE) ⇒ ¬∃ q ≠ p : lock_state(q, db) ≠ UNLOCKED
tensor_equation: ∀ p, q ∈ processes, ∀ db ∈ databases: (lock_state(p, db) = EXCLUSIVE) ⇒ ¬∃ q ≠ p : lock_state(q, db) ≠ UNLOCKED
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Only one EXCLUSIVE lock is allowed on the file and no other locks of any kind are allowed to coexist with an EXCLUSIVE lock.
redteam_pattern: Open two connections to the same SQLite database. Acquire EXCLUSIVE lock on the first connection via BEGIN IMMEDIATE. Attempt to acquire SHARED or EXCLUSIVE lock on the second connection. Verify the second connection returns SQLITE_BUSY. Then release the first connection and verify the second can acquire its lock.

title: RESERVED lock uniqueness
prompt_injection: ∀ t1, t2 ∈ transactions, ∀ db ∈ databases: lock_state(t1, db) = RESERVED ⇒ ¬∃ t2 ≠ t1 : lock_state(t2, db) = RESERVED
tensor_equation: ∀ t1, t2 ∈ transactions, ∀ db ∈ databases: lock_state(t1, db) = RESERVED ⇒ ¬∃ t2 ≠ t1 : lock_state(t2, db) = RESERVED
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Only a single RESERVED lock may be active at one time, though multiple SHARED locks can coexist with a single RESERVED lock.
redteam_pattern: Open two connections to the same database. Begin a write transaction (INSERT) on connection A to acquire RESERVED. On connection B, begin another write transaction (INSERT) that requires RESERVED. Verify connection B gets SQLITE_BUSY. Commit connection A, then verify connection B's write proceeds.

title: PENDING lock blocks new readers
prompt_injection: ∀ p ∈ processes, ∀ db ∈ databases: lock_state(p, db) = PENDING ⇒ ¬∃ q : can_acquire(q, db, SHARED)
tensor_equation: ∀ p ∈ processes, ∀ db ∈ databases: lock_state(p, db) = PENDING ⇒ ¬∃ q : can_acquire(q, db, SHARED)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: No new SHARED locks are permitted against the database if a PENDING lock is active, though existing SHARED locks are allowed to continue.
redteam_pattern: Open connection A with a long-running write transaction that has reached PENDING (after RESERVED, during COMMIT attempt with existing readers). Open connection B and try a SELECT (which needs SHARED). Verify connection B gets SQLITE_BUSY. Then close the existing readers so A acquires EXCLUSIVE and commits, then verify B can read.

title: Lock upgrade ordering
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ db ∈ databases: can_write(t, db) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) must follow path: UNLOCKED → SHARED → RESERVED → PENDING → EXCLUSIVE
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ db ∈ databases: can_write(t, db) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) must follow path: UNLOCKED → SHARED → RESERVED → PENDING → EXCLUSIVE
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: To write to a database, a process must first acquire a SHARED lock... After a SHARED lock is obtained, a RESERVED lock must be acquired... A PENDING lock is always just a temporary stepping stone on the path to an EXCLUSIVE lock.
redteam_pattern: Use the SQLite C API to attempt lock escalation via sqlite3_file_control() or direct OS-level locking. Try jumping from UNLOCKED directly to EXCLUSIVE by acquiring a POSIX file lock without first establishing a RESERVED lock. Verify the pager refuses the operation or the database becomes corrupt after a crash. Then verify that normal escalation through SHARED→RESERVED→PENDING→EXCLUSIVE succeeds and maintains journal integrity.

title: Hot journal detection — RESERVED lock as hotness signal
prompt_injection: ∀ db ∈ databases: is_hot(journal(db)) ⇔ exists(journal(db)) ∧ size(journal(db)) > 512 ∧ has_well_formed_header(journal(db)) ∧ (super_journal_name(journal(db)) = "" ∨ exists(super_journal(journal(db)))) ∧ lock_state(db) ≠ RESERVED
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ databases: is_hot(journal(db)) ⇔ exists(journal(db)) ∧ size(journal(db)) > 512 ∧ has_well_formed_header(journal(db)) ∧ (super_journal_name(journal(db)) = "" ∨ exists(super_journal(journal(db)))) ∧ lock_state(db) ≠ RESERVED
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A journal is hot if... It exists, and... Its size is greater than 512 bytes, and... The journal header is non-zero and well-formed, and... Its super-journal exists or the super-journal name is an empty string, and... There is no RESERVED lock on the corresponding database file.
redteam_pattern: Create a database and begin a write transaction. Kill the process (simulate crash with SIGKILL) before commit. On the next open, verify SQLite detects the hot journal by checking journal file exists, size > 512 bytes, well-formed header, no RESERVED lock. Then verify SQLite performs rollback before allowing any read. Also verify that an active RESERVED lock prevents the journal from being treated as hot.

title: Hot journal recovery before read
prompt_injection: ∀ db ∈ databases, ∀ p ∈ processes: before(p, reads(db)) ⇒ (is_hot(journal(db)) ⇒ rollback(p, journal(db)) ∧ delete(journal(db)))
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ databases, ∀ p ∈ processes: before(p, reads(db)) ⇒ (is_hot(journal(db)) ⇒ rollback(p, journal(db)) ∧ delete(journal(db)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Before reading from a database file, SQLite always checks to see if that database file has a hot journal. If the file does have a hot journal, then the journal is rolled back before the file is read.
redteam_pattern: Create a database, insert a row, then use BEGIN + INSERT to start a write transaction. Manually truncate or corrupt the database file. Crash the process mid-write. Reopen the database — verify the hot journal is rolled back and the database returns to the state before the crashed transaction. Attempt to read a row that was only partially inserted — it should not exist. Verify the journal file is deleted after rollback.

title: Hot journal recovery uses PENDING, not RESERVED
prompt_injection: ∀ db ∈ databases, ∀ p ∈ processes: is_hot(journal(db)) ∧ rollback(p, journal(db)) ⇒ lock_state(p, db) transitions UNLOCKED → SHARED → PENDING → EXCLUSIVE (NOT through RESERVED)
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ databases, ∀ p ∈ processes: is_hot(journal(db)) ∧ rollback(p, journal(db)) ⇒ lock_state(p, db) transitions UNLOCKED → SHARED → PENDING → EXCLUSIVE (NOT through RESERVED)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Acquire a PENDING lock then an EXCLUSIVE lock on the database file. (Note: Do not acquire a RESERVED lock because that would make other processes think the journal was no longer hot.)
redteam_pattern: Place a hot journal on a database (by simulating a crash mid-write). Open a new connection that triggers recovery. Use an strace/dtrace/tbfs hook to capture lock syscalls during recovery. Verify the lock acquisition sequence is SHARED → PENDING → EXCLUSIVE, and that RESERVED is NOT acquired at any point during recovery. Then open a second connection during recovery and verify it does not incorrectly see the journal as non-hot.

title: Journal flush before commit
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions: commit(t) ⇒ fsync_complete(journal(t)) ∧ after(fsync_complete(journal(t)), delete(journal(t)))
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions: commit(t) ⇒ fsync_complete(journal(t)) ∧ after(fsync_complete(journal(t)), delete(journal(t)))
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Make sure all rollback journal data has actually been written to the surface of the disk... Flush all database file changes to the disk... Delete the journal file. This is the instant when the changes are committed.
redteam_pattern: Open a SQLite database on a filesystem where fsync can be traced (e.g., using a FUSE layer or strace). Start a write transaction and COMMIT. Use strace to verify that fsync() or fdatasync() is called on the journal file descriptor BEFORE the journal file is unlinked. Also verify that on a system with write-back cache disabled, a power-failure simulation (SIGKILL + discard disk cache) does not leave the database in an unrecoverable state.

title: Commit point = journal deletion
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions: is_committed(t) ⇔ (journal(t) does not exist ∨ journal(t) has zero header)
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions: is_committed(t) ⇔ (journal(t) does not exist ∨ journal(t) has zero header)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Delete the journal file. This is the instant when the changes are committed. Prior to deleting the journal file, if a power failure or crash occurs, the next process to open the database will see that it has a hot journal and will roll the changes back.
redteam_pattern: Start a write transaction on a SQLite database. Before committing, manually inspect the journal file on disk — it should exist with the rollback data. Commit the transaction and immediately check for the journal file — it should be deleted or have a zero header. Simulate a crash by sending SIGKILL to the process between writing db pages and deleting the journal, then verify on next open that the hot journal rolls back the partially-written changes.

title: Multi-database commit atomicity via super-journal
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ db1, db2 ∈ databases(t): is_committed(t, db1) ⇔ is_committed(t, db2)
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions, ∀ db1, db2 ∈ databases(t): is_committed(t, db1) ⇔ is_committed(t, db2)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Delete the super-journal file. This is the instant when the changes are committed... Prior to deleting the super-journal file, if a power failure or crash occurs, the individual file journals will be considered hot and will be rolled back.
redteam_pattern: Create two SQLite databases and ATTACH one to the other. Start a transaction that writes to both databases. Before committing, crash the process at various points (after writing to db1 but before writing to db2, after writing super-journal but before deleting it). On restart, verify both databases are either both committed or both rolled back — never one committed and the other not. Test with databases on different filesystem volumes to verify the invariant degrades correctly.

title: Writer starvation prevention
prompt_injection: ∀ db ∈ databases: ∃ writer w : lock_state(w, db) = PENDING ⇒ eventually ∃ t : lock_state(w, db) = EXCLUSIVE assuming all existing readers eventually release their SHARED locks
tensor_equation: ∀ db ∈ databases: ∃ writer w : lock_state(w, db) = PENDING ⇒ eventually ∃ t : lock_state(w, db) = EXCLUSIVE assuming all existing readers eventually release their SHARED locks
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The PENDING lock allows existing readers to continue but prevents new readers from connecting to the database... Assuming existing readers do eventually complete, all SHARED locks will eventually clear and the writer will be given a chance to make its changes.
redteam_pattern: Open 10 reader connections and start SELECT queries on each. While readers are active, open a writer connection and begin a write transaction. Verify the writer can acquire RESERVED (shared with readers). Start a new reader during the writer's PENDING phase — verify the new reader gets SQLITE_BUSY. Close all readers one by one and verify the writer eventually acquires EXCLUSIVE and commits. Then verify the writer starvation never occurs when readers eventually terminate.

title: Autocommit state machine — lock acquisition is lazy
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions: BEGIN ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = UNLOCKED ∧ first_SELECT(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = SHARED ∧ first_INSERT_UPDATE_DELETE(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = RESERVED ∧ cache_spill(t) ∨ COMMIT(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = EXCLUSIVE
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions: BEGIN ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = UNLOCKED ∧ first_SELECT(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = SHARED ∧ first_INSERT_UPDATE_DELETE(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = RESERVED ∧ cache_spill(t) ∨ COMMIT(t) ⇒ lock_state(t, db) = EXCLUSIVE
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Note that the BEGIN command does not acquire any locks on the database. After a BEGIN command, a SHARED lock will be acquired when the first SELECT statement is executed. A RESERVED lock will be acquired when the first INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement is executed.
redteam_pattern: Open a SQLite connection and BEGIN a transaction. Use an OS-level file lock monitor to verify no locks are held on the database file. Issue a SELECT and verify a SHARED lock is acquired. Issue an INSERT and verify the lock escalates to RESERVED. Finally COMMIT and verify the lock escalates to EXCLUSIVE then releases. Also verify that a large INSERT filling the page cache triggers EXCLUSIVE before COMMIT via cache spill.

title: COMMIT retry on SHARED conflict
prompt_injection: ∀ t ∈ transactions: autocommit_on(t) ∧ commit_attempt(t) ∧ (∃ q : lock_state(q, db) = SHARED) ∧ commit_fails(t) ⇒ autocommit_off(t)
tensor_equation: ∀ t ∈ transactions: autocommit_on(t) ∧ commit_attempt(t) ∧ (∃ q : lock_state(q, db) = SHARED) ∧ commit_fails(t) ⇒ autocommit_off(t)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: If the SQL COMMIT command turns autocommit on and the autocommit logic then tries to commit change but fails because some other process is holding a SHARED lock, then autocommit is turned back off automatically.
redteam_pattern: Open connection A and begin a SELECT (acquiring SHARED lock). Open connection B and begin a write transaction with INSERT (acquiring RESERVED). On connection B, attempt COMMIT (which tries to escalate to EXCLUSIVE but is blocked by A's SHARED). Verify connection B's COMMIT fails with SQLITE_BUSY and that connection B's transaction remains open (autocommit turned off). Close connection A's transaction, then retry COMMIT on connection B and verify it succeeds.

title: Atomic commit across crash or power-loss
prompt_injection:
∀ write-transaction T, ∀ crash/power-loss during T:
  after restart, ∃ exactly one of:
    (a) T's changes are fully committed and visible, OR
    (b) T's changes are completely rolled back and not visible
  ∧ PRAGMA integrity_check reports no corruption
tensor_equation:
∀ write-transaction T, ∀ crash/power-loss during T:
  after restart, ∃ exactly one of:
    (a) T's changes are fully committed and visible, OR
    (b) T's changes are completely rolled back and not visible
  ∧ PRAGMA integrity_check reports no corruption
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: After the child dies, the original test process opens and reads the test database and verifies that the changes attempted by the child either completed successfully or else were completely rolled back. The integrity_check PRAGMA is used to make sure no database corruption occurs.
redteam_pattern: Kill a sandboxed process mid-write-transaction, then verify the database is either fully consistent with the transaction or unchanged, with integrity_check passing.

title: Write-ahead journal ordering
prompt_injection:
∀ write to database file at address A:
  the data at A must have been written AND synced to the rollback journal
  BEFORE the write to A occurs
tensor_equation:
∀ write to database file at address A:
  the data at A must have been written AND synced to the rollback journal
  BEFORE the write to A occurs
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The journal-test VFS monitors all disk I/O traffic between the database file and rollback journal, checking to make sure that nothing is written into the database file which has not first been written and synced to the rollback journal.
redteam_pattern: Craft a journal-test VFS that monitors disk I/O: write data directly to the database file before the journal entry is synced, crash, and verify integrity_check fails.

title: No memory leak under any failure mode
prompt_injection:
∀ operation O, ∀ failure mode F ∈ {OOM, disk-I/O-error, malformed-input}:
  after O completes (with error or success),
  memory allocated during O's execution == memory freed after O's completion
  (i.e., no leak)
tensor_equation:
∀ operation O, ∀ failure mode F ∈ {OOM, disk-I/O-error, malformed-input}:
  after O completes (with error or success),
  memory allocated during O's execution == memory freed after O's completion
  (i.e., no leak)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: SQLite is designed to never leak memory, even after an exception such as an OOM error or disk I/O error. The test harnesses are zealous to enforce this.
redteam_pattern: Simulate OOM at each allocation site during a B-tree split; verify that after each failure the process's total allocated memory returns to the pre-operation baseline.

title: Query optimizer semantic preservation
prompt_injection:
∀ SQL query Q, ∀ optimization flag configuration C (on/off):
  result(Q, optimizations=C_on) = result(Q, optimizations=C_off)
tensor_equation:
∀ SQL query Q, ∀ optimization flag configuration C (on/off):
  result(Q, optimizations=C_on) = result(Q, optimizations=C_off)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: SQLite should always generate exactly the same answer with optimizations enabled and with optimizations disabled; the answer simply arrives quicker with the optimizations turned on.
redteam_pattern: Run the same SQL query with each optimization flag toggled individually; assert the result set is identical across all configurations.

title: Malformed input must produce defined error, not undefined behavior
prompt_injection:
∀ malformed database file D (bytes changed by non-SQLite means):
  SQLite's operations on D must return SQLITE_CORRUPT
  ∧ must NOT: overflow buffers, dereference NULL, or invoke UB
tensor_equation:
∀ malformed database file D (bytes changed by non-SQLite means):
  SQLite's operations on D must return SQLITE_CORRUPT
  ∧ must NOT: overflow buffers, dereference NULL, or invoke UB
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The malformed database tests verify that SQLite finds the file format errors and reports them using the SQLITE_CORRUPT return code without overflowing buffers, dereferencing NULL pointers, or performing other unwholesome actions.
redteam_pattern: Craft a database file with an impossibly large page count causing integer overflow, feed it to SQLite, and verify the return is SQLITE_CORRUPT with no buffer overflow or segfault.

title: Graceful degradation under resource exhaustion
prompt_injection:
∀ operation O, ∀ allocation site i ∈ [0, N] in O's call graph:
  if malloc fails at site i (and all subsequent sites if continuous-fail mode),
  O must return an error code
  ∧ the database must remain consistent
  ∧ no crash, no undefined behavior
tensor_equation:
∀ operation O, ∀ allocation site i ∈ [0, N] in O's call graph:
  if malloc fails at site i (and all subsequent sites if continuous-fail mode),
  O must return an error code
  ∧ the database must remain consistent
  ∧ no crash, no undefined behavior
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Some SQLite operation is carried out and checks are done to make sure SQLite handled the OOM error correctly. (Plus: continuous-failure mode confirmed in same section)
redteam_pattern: Instrument malloc to fail at each allocation site sequentially (single-failure mode) and in all subsequent calls (continuous-failure mode) during a commit; verify error return and integrity_check passes.

title: I/O error must not introduce corruption
prompt_injection:
∀ operation O, ∀ I/O operation site i ∈ [0, N]:
  if I/O fails at site i,
  O must return an error code
  ∧ after failure simulation is disabled, PRAGMA integrity_check must pass
  (no corruption introduced by the failed I/O)
tensor_equation:
∀ operation O, ∀ I/O operation site i ∈ [0, N]:
  if I/O fails at site i,
  O must return an error code
  ∧ after failure simulation is disabled, PRAGMA integrity_check must pass
  (no corruption introduced by the failed I/O)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: After the I/O error simulation failure mechanism is disabled, the database is examined using PRAGMA integrity_check to make sure that the I/O error has not introduced database corruption.
redteam_pattern: Simulate a disk-full or partial-write I/O error at the halfway point of a B-tree page write; disable the fault injector and run integrity_check to verify no corruption.

title: Test coverage meta-invariant — 100% branch coverage plus 100% MC/DC
prompt_injection:
∀ branch instruction B in core SQLite (excluding extensions):
  ∃ at least one test case that executes B-taken AND
  ∃ at least one test case that executes B-not-taken
tensor_equation:
∀ branch instruction B in core SQLite (excluding extensions):
  ∃ at least one test case that executes B-taken AND
  ∃ at least one test case that executes B-not-taken
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The SQLite core, including the unix VFS, has 100% branch test coverage under TH3 in its default configuration as measured by gcov. SQLite also achieves 100% MC/DC in addition to 100% branch coverage.
redteam_pattern: Run gcov over the test suite; identify any branch that is only ever taken (or only ever not-taken) — the untaken path has unknown runtime behavior.

title: Compound failure resilience
prompt_injection:
∀ {crash_event, I/O_error, OOM_fault} in sequence:
  recovery from crash_event must succeed even if
  recovery itself encounters I/O_error or OOM_fault
tensor_equation:
∀ {crash_event, I/O_error, OOM_fault} in sequence:
  recovery from crash_event must succeed even if
  recovery itself encounters I/O_error or OOM_fault
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Tests are run to ensure correct behavior when an I/O error or OOM fault occurs while trying to recover from a prior crash.
redteam_pattern: Crash the process mid-transaction, then inject an I/O error during WAL replay recovery; verify the database still reaches a consistent state after handling the nested failure.

title: Regression test for every fixed bug
prompt_injection:
∀ bug B reported and fixed:
  ∃ a test case T that would have exhibited B before the fix
  ∧ T is permanently added to the test suite
  ∧ T passes on every subsequent release
tensor_equation:
∀ bug B reported and fixed:
  ∃ a test case T that would have exhibited B before the fix
  ∧ T is permanently added to the test suite
  ∧ T passes on every subsequent release
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Whenever a bug is reported against SQLite, that bug is not considered fixed until new test cases that would exhibit the bug have been added to either the TCL or TH3 test suites.
redteam_pattern: Audit the bug tracker: for each closed bug, verify a regression test exists that would have caught the original bug. Any bug without a matching regression test is a process gap.

title: Mutation testing — every branch must affect output
prompt_injection:
∀ branch instruction B in core SQLite (excluding /*OPTIMIZATION-IF-{TRUE,FALSE}*/):
  mutating B (flip to unconditional jump or no-op) must cause
  at least one test case to fail
tensor_equation:
∀ branch instruction B in core SQLite (excluding /*OPTIMIZATION-IF-{TRUE,FALSE}*/):
  mutating B (flip to unconditional jump or no-op) must cause
  at least one test case to fail
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: SQLite strives to verify that every branch instruction makes a difference using mutation testing... The script steps through the generated assembly language and, one by one, changes each branch instruction into either an unconditional jump or a no-op, compiles the result, and verifies that the test suite catches the mutation.
redteam_pattern: Step through assembly, flip each branch instruction to unconditional jump or no-op, compile, run the test suite; any mutation that survives (no test failure) indicates dead code or a test gap.

title: Snapshot Isolation Per Reader
prompt_injection: ∀ reader r, ∀ transaction t opened by r: end_mark(r, t) = last_valid_commit_record(WAL) at t_open ∧ end_mark(r, t) is constant for duration(t) ⇒ visible_pages(r, t) = { p ∈ database | p.last_modified ≤ end_mark(r, t) }
tensor_equation: ∀ reader r, ∀ transaction t opened by r: end_mark(r, t) = last_valid_commit_record(WAL) at t_open ∧ end_mark(r, t) is constant for duration(t) ⇒ visible_pages(r, t) = { p ∈ database | p.last_modified ≤ end_mark(r, t) }
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: When a read operation begins on a WAL-mode database, it first remembers the location of the last valid commit record in the WAL... for any particular reader, the end mark is unchanged for the duration of the transaction, thus ensuring that a single read transaction only sees the database content as it existed at a single point in time.
redteam_pattern: Open a read transaction in WAL mode, then commit a write from another connection. Verify that the reader's subsequent queries return the pre-write snapshot, not the post-write state. If the reader sees the write, the end-mark is not immutable.

title: Single Writer Constraint
prompt_injection: ∀ points in time τ, |{ writers active at τ }| ≤ 1
tensor_equation: ∀ points in time τ, |{ writers active at τ }| ≤ 1
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Writers merely append new content to the end of the WAL file... however, since there is only one WAL file, there can only be one writer at a time.
redteam_pattern: Spawn two concurrent threads each attempting to BEGIN IMMEDIATE on the same WAL-mode database. If both succeed and both commit, the invariant is violated. SQLite should return SQLITE_BUSY on the second writer.

title: Checkpoint Must Not Overwrite Active Reader Pages
prompt_injection: ∀ checkpoint c, ∀ reader r active during c: max_page_checkpointed(c) ≤ min({ end_mark(r) | r active })
tensor_equation: ∀ checkpoint c, ∀ reader r active during c: max_page_checkpointed(c) ≤ min({ end_mark(r) | r active })
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: A checkpoint can run concurrently with readers, however the checkpoint must stop when it reaches a page in the WAL that is past the end mark of any current reader.
redteam_pattern: Start a long-running read transaction, write and commit a page that advances the WAL past the reader's end mark, then trigger a checkpoint. If the reader subsequently sees the new page content (instead of the snapshot at its end mark), the checkpoint violated the invariant.

title: WAL-Sync Ordering for Checkpoint Durability
prompt_injection: checkpoint_state_machine: IDLE → WAL_SYNCED → DB_UPDATED → DB_SYNCED → WAL_RESET → IDLE. order_constraint: WAL_sync MUST precede db_write ∧ db_sync MUST precede WAL_reset
tensor_equation: checkpoint_state_machine: IDLE → WAL_SYNCED → DB_UPDATED → DB_SYNCED → WAL_RESET → IDLE. order_constraint: WAL_sync MUST precede db_write ∧ db_sync MUST precede WAL_reset
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The WAL must be synced to persistent storage prior to moving content from the WAL into the database and the database file must be synced prior to resetting the WAL.
redteam_pattern: Simulate a power failure mid-checkpoint by sending SIGKILL to the process at each state transition. Recover and verify the database integrity with PRAGMA integrity_check. If any checkpoint run leaves the database corrupt, the sync ordering was violated.

title: WAL-Database File Atomicity
prompt_injection: ∀ database db: db_file(db) and WAL_file(db) form an atomic unit. If db_file and WAL_file are separated: ⇒ data_loss ∨ corruption
tensor_equation: ∀ database db: db_file(db) and WAL_file(db) form an atomic unit. If db_file and WAL_file are separated: ⇒ data_loss ∨ corruption
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: The WAL file is part of the persistent state of the database and should be kept with the database if the database is copied or moved. If a database file is separated from its WAL file, then transactions that were previously committed to the database might be lost, or the database file might become corrupted.
redteam_pattern: Commit a transaction in WAL mode, copy only the database file (without the WAL file) to a new location, then open the copy. If the committed transaction is present in the copy, the invariant is false — the WAL was not needed. If the transaction is missing, the invariant holds.

title: Single-Host Shared Memory Constraint
prompt_injection: ∀ processes p₁, p₂ accessing same WAL-mode database: host(p₁) = host(p₂)
tensor_equation: ∀ processes p₁, p₂ accessing same WAL-mode database: host(p₁) = host(p₂)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: All processes using a database must be on the same host computer; WAL does not work over a network filesystem. This is because WAL requires all processes to share a small amount of memory and processes on separate host machines obviously cannot share memory with each other.
redteam_pattern: Mount a WAL-mode database directory via NFS from two separate hosts. Open the database on both hosts concurrently, write a row on host A, then query on host B. If host B sees the write (or the database becomes corrupted), the invariant is violated — two hosts shared the same WAL-mode database.

title: WAL-Reset Precondition
prompt_injection: WAL_reset_permitted ⇔ checkpoint_progress = complete(WAL) ∧ no_readers_active(WAL)
tensor_equation: WAL_reset_permitted ⇔ checkpoint_progress = complete(WAL) ∧ no_readers_active(WAL)
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: Whenever a write operation occurs, the writer checks how much progress the checkpointer has made, and if the entire WAL has been transferred into the database and synced and if no readers are making use of the WAL, then the writer will rewind the WAL back to the beginning.
redteam_pattern: Start a read transaction, then write and commit many transactions to fill the WAL. Trigger a checkpoint while the reader is still active. If the WAL is reset (rewound to beginning) before the reader closes, the reader will read stale or corrupted pages from the WAL — verify by reading a page the reader should have pinned.

title: Format Version Guard Against Downgrade Corruption
prompt_injection: ∀ db in WAL mode: db.header.bytes[18:20] = 2 ∧ ∀ SQLite version < 3.7.0: open(db) → error("file is encrypted or is not a database")
tensor_equation: ∀ db in WAL mode: db.header.bytes[18:20] = 2 ∧ ∀ SQLite version < 3.7.0: open(db) → error("file is encrypted or is not a database")
verdict_evidence: Source confirms: To prevent older versions of SQLite (prior to version 3.7.0, 2010-07-22) from trying to recover a WAL-mode database... the database file format version numbers (bytes 18 and 19 in the database header) are increased from 1 to 2 in WAL mode.
redteam_pattern: Create a WAL-mode database, read bytes 18-19 of the header. If they are not [0x00, 0x02], the format version is not 2. Then attempt to open the same database file with a pre-3.7.0 SQLite build — if it opens without error or corrupts the database, the downgrade guard failed.

title: Enterprise patterns are architectural decisions, not refactorings
prompt_injection: ∀enterprise pattern p (Domain Model, Service Layer, Repository, Unit of Work, Lazy Load, Identity Map, Data Mapper, Table Module, Transaction Script): p is an ARCHITECTURAL decision made once at the architecture level. Getting the pattern wrong means the entire codebase is organized around the wrong abstraction. Refactoring across architectural patterns is the hardest kind.

Also: 'Getting them wrong means the entire codebase is organized around the wrong abstraction. Refactoring across architectural patterns is the hardest kind of refactoring.'

title: Domain Model: business logic in domain objects, not services
prompt_injection: ∀Domain Model dm: business logic MUST live in domain objects, not services. An anemic domain model where all logic is in services and the domain is just data is a violation. The domain objects must encapsulate both state AND behavior.

title: Repository pattern mediates domain and data mapping layers
prompt_injection: ∀Repository r: r MUST mediate between the domain layer and the data mapping layer. Direct database access in domain logic is a violation — domain code must not contain SQL or database API calls.
tensor_equation: ∀Repository r: r MUST mediate between the domain layer and the data mapping layer. Direct database access in domain logic is a violation — domain code must not contain SQL or database API calls.

redteam_pattern: Write domain logic that directly calls a database driver; verify repository-pattern audit flags the leak.

title: Identity Map: each object loaded once per transaction
prompt_injection: ∀transaction t: each logical database row MUST be represented by at most one in-memory object within t. Duplicate objects for the same row violate the Identity Map pattern and can lead to lost updates (one copy modified, the other stale).
tensor_equation: ∀transaction t: each logical database row MUST be represented by at most one in-memory object within t. Duplicate objects for the same row violate the Identity Map pattern and can lead to lost updates (one copy modified, the other stale).

redteam_pattern: Load the same database row into two objects in the same transaction, modify one, then commit; verify the other object's stale state causes a lost update.

title: Data Mapper: domain objects don't know about the database
prompt_injection: ∀Data Mapper m: domain objects MUST NOT contain SQL or DB API calls. The mapper layer translates between domain objects and the database. Domain objects should be persistence-ignorant. SQL in domain code is a violation.

title: The Fowler Test: six questions before any change
prompt_injection: ∀change c: c MUST answer YES to all six Fowler Test questions before proceeding: (1) Am I adding function or refactoring? (one hat at a time) (2) Do tests pass before and after? (behavior preserved) (3) Is there a smell that justifies this? (don't refactor 'just because') (4) Is this step small enough? (can be undone with Ctrl-Z) (5) Is there a catalog name for what I'm doing? (6) Is the test suite fast enough? (< 1 second per step)

title: Service Layer is a thin facade over domain model
prompt_injection: ∀Service Layer s: s MUST be a THIN facade over the domain model. If the service layer contains all the business logic and the domain model is anemic, the architecture is wrong — 'Fat Service, Anemic Domain' is an anti-pattern.

title: Unit of Work: atomic commit of tracked changes
prompt_injection: ∀Unit of Work u: ALL tracked changes within u MUST commit atomically. Partial commits (some objects saved, others not) violate the Unit of Work pattern and leave the database in an inconsistent state. Either all changes persist or none do.
tensor_equation: ∀Unit of Work u: ALL tracked changes within u MUST commit atomically. Partial commits (some objects saved, others not) violate the Unit of Work pattern and leave the database in an inconsistent state. Either all changes persist or none do.
verdict_evidence: Source confirms in 'Key patterns' table: 'Unit of Work | Tracks changes to objects, commits atomically | Partial commit (some objects saved, others not)'.
redteam_pattern: Save two related entities in separate write operations without a transaction; verify atomicity audit detects the non-atomic write pattern.

title: I/O error injection sweep catches every missing error check
prompt_injection: ∀I/O operation op: test MUST sweep failAt from 0 upward through every Write call using a faulting writer that injects errors at each successive write position. This catches every missing error check in I/O code paths. The faulting writer returns error when n == failAt.
tensor_equation: ∀I/O operation op: test MUST sweep failAt from 0 upward through every Write call using a faulting writer that injects errors at each successive write position. This catches every missing error check in I/O code paths. The faulting writer returns error when n == failAt.

redteam_pattern: Implement a Write call without an error check; sweep I/O fault injection from 0 upward — verify the missing check is detected as a failure.

title: Journal-Test Pattern: ordering assertions on storage
prompt_injection: ∀storage interface s with journal/wal: test MUST interpose on s and assert that every Write to the primary was preceded by Write+Sync to the journal. This is how SQLite proves crash recovery. The same pattern applies to any pipeline: assert ordering of operations.
tensor_equation: ∀storage interface s with journal/wal: test MUST interpose on s and assert that every Write to the primary was preceded by Write+Sync to the journal. This is how SQLite proves crash recovery. The same pattern applies to any pipeline: assert ordering of operations.

redteam_pattern: Write to primary without writing to journal first; verify journal-test interposer catches the ordering violation.

title: Four-level assertion typology: invariant, always, boundary, corrupt
prompt_injection: ∀assertion a: a MUST be classified into exactly one of four levels — invariant (proven, panics in debug), always (believed, graceful fallback in release), boundary (known to vary, coverage marker), or corrupt (pre-condition, defensive check for bad input). Each level implies different runtime behavior and testing rigor.

verdict_evidence: Source confirms the four-level typology table: invariant (proven, panic in debug), always (believed, graceful fallback in release), boundary (known to vary, coverage marker), corrupt (pre-condition, defensive check). The four levels and their Go patterns match exactly.
redteam_pattern: Use an invariant-level assertion in a place that can fail under valid input; verify the four-level classification audit flags the mismatch.

title: Every bug gets a test BEFORE the fix
prompt_injection: ∀bug b: the test for b MUST be written BEFORE the fix for b. The test MUST fail on the unfixed code and pass on the fixed code. This proves the fix works, prevents regression, and documents the bug as a test case.
tensor_equation: ∀bug b: the test for b MUST be written BEFORE the fix for b. The test MUST fail on the unfixed code and pass on the fixed code. This proves the fix works, prevents regression, and documents the bug as a test case.

redteam_pattern: Fix a bug without writing a test first; verify bug-to-test regression audit flags it.

title: Resource leak detection via goroutine/thread counting
prompt_injection: ∀test t involving goroutines or resources: before := runtime.NumGoroutine(); run t; after := runtime.NumGoroutine(); if after > before: t.Errorf('leaked %d goroutines', after-before). Goroutine leaks, thread leaks, and handle leaks must be detected in tests. Same pattern for open file descriptors.

verdict_evidence: Source confirms: exact Go code pattern using runtime.NumGoroutine() with before/after comparison and Errorf with 'leaked %d goroutines' message.
redteam_pattern: Write a test that spawns goroutines without cleaning them up; verify NumGoroutine comparison detects the leak.

title: Cross-implementation differential testing
prompt_injection: ∀critical algorithm a: a MUST be tested via differential testing — run identical inputs through two independent implementations and diff the outputs. This catches logic bugs that pass all other tests. The reference implementation can be slower, simpler, or known-correct.
tensor_equation: ∀critical algorithm a: a MUST be tested via differential testing — run identical inputs through two independent implementations and diff the outputs. This catches logic bugs that pass all other tests. The reference implementation can be slower, simpler, or known-correct.

redteam_pattern: Implement two versions of a sort function, make one buggy on edge cases; verify differential testing catches the divergence.

title: Test-to-code ratio target: 590:1 for critical infrastructure
prompt_injection: ∀critical software system: test-to-code ratio SHOULD approach 590:1 (test KSLOC / code KSLOC) as practiced by SQLite. For a 155.8 KSLOC core, this means 92,053 KSLOC of test code. This ratio enables 100% branch coverage and exhaustive fault injection.

title: 100% branch coverage (every conditional both ways)
prompt_injection: ∀test suite T: T MUST achieve 100% branch coverage (not line coverage) — every conditional branch exercised both true and false. Line coverage is insufficient; a line with a conditional expression is only partially tested if only one branch is covered.

title: Assertion density target: 4.3% of lines contain an assertion
prompt_injection: ∀critical software system: assertion density SHOULD reach 4.3% of code lines containing an assertion (SQLite benchmark). Assertions in the code prove invariants at runtime in debug builds and document assumptions for readers.

title: Property-based tests for invariant verification across random inputs
prompt_injection: ∀critical invariant i: i MUST be tested with property-based testing (QuickCheck, Hedgehog) — verify the invariant holds across random inputs, not just specific examples. The invariant survives refactoring because it is about properties, not implementation.

title: Concurrent code needs stress tests and race detector
prompt_injection: ∀concurrent code c: c MUST be stress-tested AND run with race detector (go test -race, ThreadSanitizer, loom). Stress tests surface timing-dependent failures; race detector catches data races statically. One without the other is insufficient — races can hide under low concurrency, and stress tests without race detector can pass despite data races.

redteam_pattern: Write concurrent code with a data race that doesn't manifest under low load; verify race detector catches it while stress tests alone might pass.
